topics.title: CNC Systems - Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CNC Systems - Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. CNC Systems – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** is a manufacturing methodology in which pre-programmed software and coded numerical data (typically G-codes and M-codes following the **ISO 6983** or **EIA RS-267-B** standard) dictate the movement of machine tools and auxiliary functions. The numerical instructions are decoded by a **Machine Control Unit (MCU)**, which drives servo/stepper motors to achieve precise, automated motion along multiple axes, enabling the production of complex geometric components with high accuracy and repeatability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CNC is a sub-class of **Numerical Control (NC)** where the controller logic is implemented via a dedicated computer (microcontroller/DSP/PLC) rather than hard-wired relay logic or vacuum tubes used in legacy NC machines.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The Robotic Chef

Imagine teaching a chef to make a dish:

| Human Chef (Manual Machining) | Robotic Chef (CNC) |
|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Reads recipe, decides ingredient quantity in real time | Reads a pre-written program (code) word-by-word |
| Adapts motion based on visual feedback | Executes each instruction deterministically |
| Tired after 8 hours, makes errors | Works 24×7 with **0.001 mm** repeatability |
| Needs rest, tea breaks | Only needs coolant refills! |

The "recipe" for CNC is the **Part Program**, the "kitchen" is the **Machine Tool**, and the "robot brain" is the **MCU**. Once the program is written and verified, the same dish (part) is produced identically thousands of times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (must memorize):**
> - **Accuracy:** Closeness of the actual position to the desired/programmed position.
> - **Repeatability:** Ability of the machine to return to a previously commanded position under identical conditions (numerically expressed in **µm**).
> - **Resolution:** The smallest incremental motion the control system can command (typically **0.001 mm** to **0.01 mm**).

## 1.3 Evolution: NC → CNC → DNC

$$\text{NC (1952, MIT)} \rightarrow \text{CNC (1970s, Microprocessor)} \rightarrow \text{DNC (Direct Numerical Control)}$$

Modern CNC machines often integrate **DNC networking**, **IoT sensors**, and **digital twins** for Industry 4.0 readiness — a frequent 10-mark question theme in KTU ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of a CNC System

A CNC system is structurally a **closed-loop electro-mechanical system** comprising four functional blocks:

1. **Input / Program Storage Medium** – USB, Ethernet, RS-232, or punch tape (legacy).
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** – The digital brain consisting of:
   - *CNC Processor / Microcontroller*
   - *Interpolator* (Linear, Circular, Helical)
   - *Feedback Decoder*
   - *Memory (ROM / RAM / Flash)*
3. **Machine Tool (Slide/Actuator Assembly)** – Ballscrews, LM guides, spindle, tool turret.
4. **Feedback System** – Linear/rotary encoders, glass scales, resolvers.

## 2.2 Working Principle – Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** Operator loads the part program via DNC or USB.
- **Step 2:** MCU decodes each block (one line of code) sequentially.
- **Step 3:** Interpolator generates intermediate coordinate points between the start and end points (e.g., a circle is broken into 360 micro-linear segments).
- **Step 4:** Position controller compares **commanded position** vs **actual position** (from encoder) → generates a **following error**.
- **Step 5:** Servo amplifier drives the motor to nullify the error within one control loop cycle (typically **1–4 ms**).
- **Step 6:** Auxiliary functions (coolant ON, spindle CW, tool change) are executed via **M-codes** through PLC interfacing.
- **Step 7:** Repeat until **M30** (Program End & Rewind) is reached.

## 2.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Distinction – Asked almost every KTU cycle:**

| Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback | **Absent** | **Present** (Encoder/Resolver) |
| Actuator | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor (AC/DC) |
| Accuracy | Low (positional error accumulates) | High (error continuously corrected) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Example | Hobby CNC, small engravers | Industrial VMC, 5-axis machining center |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D}$ | Spindle speed (V_c = cutting speed, D = tool/work diameter) | **rev/min (rpm)** |
| 2 | $V_f = f_z \, Z \, N$ | Table feed rate (f_z = feed per tooth, Z = number of teeth) | **mm/min** |
| 3 | $T_c = \dfrac{L + L_e + L_a}{V_f}$ | Machining time per pass | **min** |
| 4 | $\text{MRR} = w \, d \, V_f$ | Material Removal Rate (w = width, d = depth) | **mm³/min** |
| 5 | $P_c = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times K_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$ | Cutting power (K_c = specific cutting force) | **kW** |
| 6 | $\delta = \dfrac{P_{lead}}{N_{encoder}}$ | Positioning resolution | **mm/pulse** |
| 7 | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$ | Tool-path slope for ramping moves | dimensionless |
| 8 | $A = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \pi$ | Circular interpolation area (radius form) | **mm²** |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, $V_c$ ranges typically from **30 to 300 m/min** for HSS tools and **100 to 1000 m/min** for carbide tools — memorize this range for tool-life problems.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CNC forms the backbone of **aerospace structural parts** (wing ribs, turbine blades), **automotive engine blocks**, **medical implants** (orthopedic Ti-6Al-4V hip stems), and **die-mold manufacturing**. Without CNC, modern tolerances of **± 0.005 mm** on 5-axis contoured surfaces would be economically infeasible.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spindle Speed and Feed Rate Relationship

The **fundamental machining relationship** links spindle speed, feed per revolution, and table feed velocity. We derive it from kinematic first principles.

### Step 1 – Define Cutting Speed

Cutting speed $V_c$ is the tangential velocity at which the tool engages the workpiece surface.

$$V_c = \pi \, D \, N$$

where $D$ is the instantaneous diameter in metres and $N$ is spindle speed in rev/s. Since the KTU convention uses **rev/min**, we substitute $N$ (rev/min) $\rightarrow$ $N/60$ (rev/s):

$$V_c = \pi \, D \cdot \frac{N}{60} \;\; [\text{m/s}]$$

### Step 2 – Convert to KTU Standard Units (m/min)

To express $V_c$ in m/min (the standard unit in the FMS/CNC handbooks), multiply numerator and denominator:

$$V_c \;\; [\text{m/min}] = \frac{\pi \, D \, N}{1000} \times 1000 = \pi \, D \, N \;\; \text{with } D \text{ in mm and } V_c \text{ in mm/min}$$

Re-arranging for spindle speed:

$$N = \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D}$$

### Step 3 – Derive Feed Rate (Milling)

The feed per tooth is $f_z$ [mm/tooth], the number of teeth on the cutter is $Z$, and the spindle turns at $N$ rev/min. Each revolution engages $Z$ teeth, so the feed per revolution is $f_n = f_z \cdot Z$ [mm/rev].

$$V_f = f_n \cdot N = f_z \, Z \, N$$

### Step 4 – Derive Material Removal Rate (MRR)

For a milling operation, the uncut chip cross-section is $A_c = f_z \cdot a_p$ and the chip is engaged for one revolution over the width $w$ (radial depth). Therefore:

$$\text{MRR} = A_c \cdot w \cdot N = w \cdot d \cdot V_f$$

### Step 5 – Derive Positioning Resolution (δ)

A ballscrew with lead $P_{lead}$ [mm/rev] is driven by a servo motor coupled to an encoder of $N_{encoder}$ pulses per revolution. The control system interpolates each pulse into a position command. Hence the **theoretical minimum incremental motion** is:

$$\delta = \frac{P_{lead}}{N_{encoder}} \;\; [\text{mm/pulse}]$$

For example: a ballscrew of lead 10 mm driven by a 17-bit encoder ($N_{encoder} = 2^{17} = 131072$):

$$\delta = \frac{10}{131072} = 7.629 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm} = 0.076 \;\; \mu\text{m}$$

This is well below the typical industrial requirement of **1 µm**, confirming why closed-loop CNC achieves sub-micron accuracy.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Common KTU Numerical Traps

| Trap | Wrong Assumption | Correct Approach |
|------|------------------|------------------|
| Diameter in MRR formula | Using $D$ directly | Use radial depth $w$ (engagement width), not diameter |
| Spindle speed units | rpm vs rps | Check whether $V_c$ is in m/s or m/min |
| Feed per tooth | Treating as feed per rev | Multiply $f_z \times Z$ to get $f_n$ |
| Resolution with gear train | Ignoring gear ratio | Use $\delta = \dfrac{P_{lead}}{N_{encoder} \times G}$ |
| Helical interpolation | Treating as pure linear | Apply 3-axis vector sum for true 3D toolpath |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CNC System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Program Input - USB / Ethernet / DNC] --> B[Machine Control Unit - MCU]
    B --> B1[Part Program Decoder]
    B1 --> B2[Interpolator - Linear / Circular / Helical]
    B2 --> B3[Position Controller - PID]
    B3 --> C[Servo Amplifier and Power Stage]
    C --> D[Servo Motor X Y Z and Spindle]
    D --> E[Ballscrew and LM Guide Assembly]
    E --> F[Machine Tool Slide and Spindle]
    F --> G[Workpiece and Cutting Tool]
    F --> H[Linear / Rotary Encoder]
    H --> I[Feedback Signal Processing]
    I --> B3
    B --> J[PLC - Auxiliary Functions]
    J --> K[Coolant Pump, Tool Changer, Chip Conveyor]
    styleA([Input Layer])
    style B fill:#FFD700
    style B3 fill:#FF6347
    style H fill:#90EE90
    style K fill:#87CEEB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed return arrow from `H` (encoder) back to `B3` (position controller) is the **closed-loop feedback path**. Removing it converts the system to **open-loop** — a frequent KTU diagram-recall question.

## 4.2 Process Flow of a CNC Machining Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Part Drawing and Process Planning] --> P2[Manual or CAM Part Programming]
    P2 --> P3[Verification and Backplot Simulation]
    P3 --> P4[Transfer Program to MCU via DNC]
    P4 --> P5[Workpiece Mounting and Tool Setting]
    P5 --> P6[Reference Point Return and Dry Run]
    P6 --> P7[Cycle Start - Block by Block Execution]
    P7 --> P8[Continuous Monitoring of Load and Vibration]
    P8 --> P9[Cycle Stop or M30 End of Program]
    P9 --> P10[In-Process Quality Inspection - CMM]
    style P1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style P2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style P7 fill:#FF6347
    style P10 fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Motor Drive Pulse] --> O2[Stepper Motor]
    O2 --> O3[Lead Screw and Slide]
    O3 --> O4[Output Position - No Feedback]
    style O1 fill:#FFD700
    style O4 fill:#FF6347
    C1[Command Position] --> C2[Position Comparator]
    C3[Actual Position from Encoder] --> C2
    C2 --> C4[Servo Amplifier]
    C4 --> C5[Servo Motor]
    C5 --> C6[Lead Screw and Slide]
    C6 --> C5
    style C2 fill:#FF6347
    style C5 fill:#90EE90
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the closed-loop block, the encoder mounted on the motor/slide sends the **actual position** back to the **position comparator**, which computes the **following error** $e = x_{commanded} - x_{actual}$. The PID controller minimizes this error to zero asymptotically.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define CNC. List any four advantages of CNC machines over conventional machines.**

**Model Answer:**

Computer Numerical Control (CNC) is a form of soft automation in which the machine tool functions and tool movements are controlled by coded numerical data (G-codes and M-codes) processed by a dedicated microcomputer-based controller (MCU).

**Four advantages:**
1. **Higher accuracy and repeatability** (sub-micron achievable in modern VMCs).
2. **Lower operator skill requirement** — the machine follows the program, not the operator's hand.
3. **Complex geometry production** in a single setup (5-axis contoured surfaces).
4. **Reduced lead time and scrap** through proven part programs reused across batches.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop CNC systems with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback | Absent | Present (encoder/resolver) |
| Actuator | Stepper motor | Servo motor |
| Error detection | None | Continuous via comparator |
| Accuracy | Limited by step angle | Limited only by encoder resolution |
| Example | PCB drilling machine, hobby CNC | VMC, CNC lathe, 5-axis machining center |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "open loop is more accurate" because they confuse *theoretical* resolution with *actual* positional accuracy. The closed-loop system **monitors and corrects** the error, hence it is **always more accurate** in practice.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Provided: Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a CNC system**. List and briefly state the role of each block. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A CNC vertical milling machine is required to machine an EN8 steel block. A 4-tooth carbide end mill of diameter **20 mm** is used. The recommended cutting speed is **120 m/min** and feed per tooth is **0.08 mm/tooth**. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Table feed rate, (iii) MRR if radial depth is 6 mm and axial depth is 3 mm, (iv) Machining time for a slot length of **80 mm** with approach and overtravel of 5 mm each. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram – Refer SECTION 4.1 Mermaid**

The CNC system consists of the following blocks and their roles:

1. **Input / Program Source** – Carries the part program to the MCU (USB, Ethernet, RS-232).
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** – Decodes instructions, performs interpolation, and generates axis motion commands.
3. **Servo Amplifier / Drive** – Boosts low-level MCU signals to high-current/voltage required by servo motors.
4. **Machine Tool (Actuators)** – Linear and rotary axes driven by servo motors via ballscrews.
5. **Feedback System** – Encoders/scales provide actual position for closed-loop correction.
6. **Auxiliary Control (PLC)** – Handles coolant, tool changer, chip conveyor, door interlocks.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $D = 20$ mm
- $Z = 4$ teeth
- $V_c = 120$ m/min
- $f_z = 0.08$ mm/tooth
- Radial depth $w = 6$ mm
- Axial depth $d = 3$ mm
- Slot length $L = 80$ mm, approach $L_a = 5$ mm, overtravel $L_e = 5$ mm

**Step 1 – Spindle Speed (N):**

$$N = \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D} = \frac{1000 \times 120}{\pi \times 20} = \frac{120000}{62.832} = 1909.86 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 – Table Feed Rate (V_f):**

$$V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N = 0.08 \times 4 \times 1909.86 = 611.16 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3 – Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$\text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot V_f = 6 \times 3 \times 611.16 = 11000.88 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 1.1 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 4 – Machining Time (T_c):**

Total tool travel = $L + L_a + L_e = 80 + 5 + 5 = 90$ mm

$$T_c = \frac{90}{611.16} = 0.1473 \text{ min} \approx 8.84 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Mistakes:**
> - Forgetting the **1000 multiplier** in spindle speed formula → answer comes out 1000× too small (mark loss: 1).
> - Confusing **mm³/min** with **cm³/min** (a 1000× error) in MRR → 2 marks lost.
> - Skipping the **approach + overtravel** in machining time → 1 mark lost.
> - Not specifying **units** in final answer → 0.5 mark deducted (KTU is strict on this).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a CNC machine** in detail, covering part program decoding, interpolation, and feedback control. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A closed-loop CNC lathe uses a ballscrew of lead **5 mm** driven by a servo motor with a **2500-pulse/rev** encoder through a gear train of ratio 1:2 (motor:ballscrew). Calculate the **theoretical positioning resolution**. If the required accuracy is **± 2 µm**, comment on the suitability of the system. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Principle of Operation – Detailed**

The principle of CNC operation rests on the closed-loop numerical control paradigm:

1. **Part Program Decoding:** The MCU reads each block (e.g., `N10 G01 X50.0 Y30.0 F150`) sequentially, separating the words (N, G, X, Y, F). The decoder interprets G01 as linear interpolation, X/Y as target coordinates, and F as feed rate.

2. **Interpolation:** The interpolator generates intermediate axis positions between the current and commanded points. For a **G02/G03 (circular interpolation)** block, the controller computes the tangential vector at every 1 ms and distributes axis pulses to the X and Z servos to trace the arc.

3. **Position Control (PID):** For each axis, the commanded position $X_{cmd}$ is compared with the actual position $X_{act}$ from the linear encoder. The error $e(t) = X_{cmd}(t) - X_{act}(t)$ is fed to a PID controller that outputs a voltage command $V(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ to the servo amplifier.

4. **Actuation:** The amplifier drives the servo motor, which rotates the ballscrew, which translates the slide via the LM guide. The encoder on the motor (or a separate linear scale) closes the loop.

5. **Auxiliary Functions:** M-codes trigger discrete events — M08 coolant ON, M06 tool change, M30 program end.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Ballscrew lead $P_{lead} = 5$ mm/rev
- Encoder $N_{encoder} = 2500$ pulses/rev
- Gear ratio $G = 2$ (ballscrew rotates at half motor speed, but pulses counted are at motor shaft)

**Resolution formula with gear train:**

$$\delta = \frac{P_{lead}}{N_{encoder} \times G} = \frac{5}{2500 \times 2} = \frac{5}{5000} = 0.001 \text{ mm} = 1 \;\; \mu\text{m}$$

**Suitability Comment:**

The required accuracy is **± 2 µm**, meaning the total allowable error band is **4 µm**. The theoretical resolution is **1 µm**, which is well within the tolerance. Therefore, the system is **suitable** in principle, provided mechanical errors (ballscrew pitch error, thermal expansion, backlash) are kept within the remaining 1 µm of error budget. In practice, a **linear scale** (glass scale) at the slide would be preferred to eliminate ballscrew errors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall – Gear Ratio Direction:**
> - If gear ratio is given as **motor:ballscrew = 1:2**, it means **1 motor rev → 0.5 ballscrew rev**. The ballscrew rotates slower, so encoder pulses must be multiplied by $G$ in the denominator (more pulses per mm of slide travel).
> - A wrong direction here flips the resolution by 4× and loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap – Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **CNC** = Computer Numerical Control; uses ISO 6983 G/M-codes; sub-class of NC.
- ✅ **4 functional blocks**: Input → MCU → Machine Tool → Feedback.
- ✅ **Open loop** uses stepper motors (no feedback); **closed loop** uses servo motors with encoders.
- ✅ **Accuracy** ≠ **Repeatability** ≠ **Resolution** — know the difference.
- ✅ **Key formulas** to memorize:
  - $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D}$
  - $V_f = f_z \, Z \, N$
  - $\text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot V_f$
  - $T_c = \dfrac{L + L_a + L_e}{V_f}$
  - $\delta = \dfrac{P_{lead}}{N_{encoder} \times G}$
- ✅ **Interpolation types:** Linear (G01), Circular (G02 CW / G03 CCW), Helical (G02/G03 with 3 axes).
- ✅ **PID controller** minimises following error in closed-loop CNC.
- ✅ **Industry 4.0 extension:** DNC, IoT sensors, digital twins, MTConnect protocol.
- ✅ **Examiner loves**: block diagrams with **closed-loop feedback arrow**, comparison tables, and unit-consistent numerical solutions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of CNC System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components of CNC System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) System:** A CNC system is an integrated electromechanical arrangement consisting of hardware and software components that automatically control the motion, speed, and operation of machine tools through the use of coded numerical instructions (part program) processed by a dedicated computer — the Machine Control Unit (MCU).

A CNC machine, when stripped to its essentials, is essentially a *closed-loop feedback system* in which a digital part program is interpreted by a controller, which then issues analog/digital commands to servo drives that physically move the axes of a machine tool. The accuracy of the cut is continuously verified by feedback devices.

The **CNC system architecture** is conventionally divided into **five major functional sub-systems**:

| # | Functional Sub-System | Primary Role |
|---|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Program Input / Media | Supplies the part program to the controller |
| 2 | Machine Control Unit (MCU) | Interprets program & coordinates all sub-systems |
| 3 | Drive System (Servo & Spindle) | Converts electrical signals into mechanical motion |
| 4 | Feedback / Measurement System | Verifies actual position/velocity vs. commanded |
| 5 | Machine Tool (Mechanical Unit) | Performs the actual material removal / forming |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The CNC System as a Skilled Human Chef"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a master chef preparing a complex dish.
> - The **Recipe Book** → Part Program (G-code & M-code).
> - The **Chef's Brain (reading the recipe and deciding actions)** → Machine Control Unit (MCU).
> - The **Chef's Hand and Wrist** → Servo Motors and Drive System.
> - The **Eyes & Sensory Nerves** → Feedback Devices (encoders, LVDTs, resolvers).
> - The **Knife, Pan, Stove, and Ingredients** → Machine Tool (spindle, slideways, work-holding, tool).

Just as a chef *closes the loop* between what they *intend* to do and what they *see* happening, the CNC's MCU compares **commanded position** with **actual position** (from feedback) and corrects any error — this is the essence of *closed-loop control*.

---

## 1.3 KTU Board Standard Metrics & Highlights

- **Positioning Accuracy** of a modern CNC: typically **±0.0001 inch to ±0.005 mm** (≈ **±2.5 µm to ±5 µm**).
- **Resolution**: a key specification, defined as the smallest incremental motion the controller can command, e.g., **0.001 mm** or **0.0001″**.
- **Repeatability**: the ability of the machine to return to a previously commanded position — usually **±0.0025 mm** in high-end machining centres.
- **Number of axes controlled**: 2.5, 3, 4, or 5 axes for typical CNC mills/lathes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** When asked "list the components of a CNC system," a *complete* answer must mention **all five sub-systems above** (Input, MCU, Drive, Feedback, Machine Tool) and briefly describe the role of each. Skipping the feedback system is the most common reason students lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 1.4 Role of Each Component at a Glance

1. **Input Media / Device** → Loads the part program.
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** → "The Brain" — decodes, interpolates, issues commands.
3. **Drive System (Servo Motors + Amplifiers + Spindle Drive)** → "The Muscles" — moves the axes.
4. **Feedback / Measurement System (Transducers)** → "The Nerves" — reports back to MCU.
5. **Machine Tool (Slideways, Spindle, ATC, Coolant, etc.)** → "The Body" — performs the cutting.
6. **Auxiliaries** → Coolant, chip disposal, lubrication, safety interlocks, work-holding.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Component 1 — Program Input / Input Media

The part program is the *set of instructions* written in ISO/EIA *G-code* and *M-code* format, defining toolpath, feed, speed, and auxiliary functions.

**Historical & Modern Input Media:**

- **Punched Paper Tape** (obsolete) — 8-track, 1-inch wide, asynchronous serial.
- **Magnetic Tape** (obsolete) — faster than paper tape.
- **Floppy Disk** (legacy) — 1.44 MB, 3.5″.
- **RS-232C Serial Communication** (legacy) — DNC (Direct Numerical Control) link.
- **USB Pen Drive / Flash Memory** (current).
- **Ethernet / LAN / Wi-Fi** (current) — for DNC, FMS, and Industry 4.0 integration.
- **Manual Data Input (MDI)** — for single-block operations and testing.
- **CAD/CAM Software (offline programming)** — most modern approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Important Distinction:** *DNC* (Direct Numerical Control) ≠ *CNC*. In DNC, a host computer directly controls multiple machines, whereas in CNC, the controller is dedicated to one machine with the program stored locally.

---

## 2.2 Component 2 — Machine Control Unit (MCU) — The Brain

The MCU is the *central intelligence* of the CNC system. It reads the part program, decodes the G/M codes, performs interpolation, and commands the servo drives.

**Internal Sub-blocks of MCU:**

| Sub-block | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** | Executes arithmetic, logic, and control instructions. |
| **Memory — ROM (Read-Only Memory)** | Stores the system software (executive program), post-processors, diagnostics, and tool tables permanently. |
| **Memory — RAM (Random Access Memory)** | Stores the active part program, work offsets, and operator inputs temporarily. |
| **Bubble Memory / Flash** (older) | Non-volatile memory for part programs. |
| **Decoding / Syntax Checker** | Validates G-code blocks before execution. |
| **Interpolator** | Computes intermediate points along the toolpath (Linear, Circular, Helical, Spline, NURBS). |
| **Axis Controllers (X, Y, Z, A, B, C)** | Each axis has a dedicated controller that handles position, velocity, and current loops. |
| **Feed-rate Override** | Operator-controlled 0–200 % potentiometer for real-time feed adjustment. |
| **Spindle Speed Override** | 50–120 % range typically. |
| **I/O Interface & PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** | Handles discrete I/O — coolant ON/OFF, tool changer, chuck, door interlocks, etc. |
| **Display Unit (CRT/LCD/LED)** | Shows program, current position, alarms, parameters. |
| **Keyboard / Operating Panel** | For manual commands, editing, MDI. |

### 2.2.1 Interpolation — The Heart of MCU

Interpolation is the **mathematical computation of intermediate axis positions** between the start and end points of a tool path segment.

- **Linear Interpolation (G01):** Straight line between two points.
- **Circular Interpolation (G02 CW / G03 CCW):** Arc in XY plane.
- **Helical Interpolation:** Linear in one axis + circular in the other two.
- **Cylindrical / Polar Interpolation.**
- **NURBS / Spline Interpolation (on high-end controllers).**

For **linear interpolation** between point $P_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2(x_2, y_2)$, at feed rate $F$:

$$L = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

The feed components along each axis are then:

$$f_x = F \cdot \frac{(x_2 - x_1)}{L}, \quad f_y = F \cdot \frac{(y_2 - y_1)}{L}$$

---

## 2.3 Component 3 — Drive System (Servo & Spindle Drives)

The drive system converts *low-power command signals* from the MCU into *high-power mechanical motion*.

**Classification of CNC Drives:**

| Drive Type | Motor Used | Typical Application |
|------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Open-loop (Stepper Motor)** | Stepper (PM, VR, Hybrid) | Low-cost machines, light loads, e.g., hobby, small engravers. |
| **Semi-closed Loop** | DC/AC Servo + rotary encoder on motor shaft | Medium accuracy machines. |
| **Closed-loop Servo** | DC/AC Servo + linear encoder on axis | High-precision machining centres. |
| **Hydraulic Servo** | Hydraulic cylinder + servo valve | Heavy-duty, large machines (e.g., very large lathes). |
| **Spindle Drive** | AC induction (VFD) or AC servo | Spindle rotation. |

### 2.3.1 Servo Control Block — Velocity & Position Loops

Modern CNC servos are cascaded **three-loop** controllers:

1. **Current Loop (innermost):** Regulates motor torque.
2. **Velocity Loop (middle):** Regulates motor speed (rpm).
3. **Position Loop (outermost):** Regulates axis position (mm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution and its relation to drive pulse:**
> A CNC axis command is often issued as a train of pulses. If the **ball-screw lead** is $L_p$ mm/rev and the **encoder** has $N$ pulses/rev, then the **resolution** is:
>
> $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{L_p}{N}$$
>
> This is the minimum axis movement per encoder pulse.

---

## 2.4 Component 4 — Feedback / Measurement System (Transducers)

The feedback system measures **actual** axis position/velocity and reports it to the MCU for *closed-loop error correction*.

**Common Transducers:**

| Transducer | Quantity Measured | Typical Resolution | Application |
|------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Optical Rotary Encoder (Incremental)** | Angular position (motor) | 2000–60 000 pulses/rev | Servo motors |
| **Optical Linear Encoder** | Linear position (axis) | 0.1 µm – 1 µm | Direct position feedback |
| **Resolver** | Angular position (analog) | ±5 arc-min | Harsh environments, spindle |
| **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** | Linear displacement | 0.1 µm – 1 µm | Axis position, tool length |
| **Inductosyn** | Linear/angular position | 0.0001 inch (≈ 2.5 µm) | High-precision machine tools |
| **Glass / Steel Tape Scale** | Linear position | 1 µm | Lathe cross-slide |
| **Capacitance Probe** | Micro-displacement | < 0.1 µm | Specialised metrology |
| **Tachogenerator** | Velocity | — | Velocity feedback |

### 2.4.1 Closed-Loop Error Equation

The MCU computes the **following error** at every servo cycle (typically every **1–4 ms**):

$$e(t) = x_{\text{commanded}}(t) - x_{\text{actual}}(t)$$

This error is fed to a controller (PID), producing a control signal:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

The objective of the controller is to drive $e(t) \to 0$ as quickly as possible without overshoot, ensuring **positioning accuracy** and **repeatability**.

---

## 2.5 Component 5 — Machine Tool (Mechanical Unit)

The mechanical unit is the *physical structure* that performs the cutting/forming operation. It is the *most expensive* part of the CNC system.

**Key Mechanical Sub-Components:**

1. **Bed & Column / Base** — Provides rigidity, vibration damping, and thermal stability. Often made of **cast iron** (grey cast iron with flake graphite for damping) or **polymer concrete** (granite-epoxy composite).
2. **Slideways / Linear Guides** — Support linear motion of slides; can be *box ways* (sliding) or *linear roller/ball bearings* (anti-friction).
3. **Spindle** — Rotates the cutting tool (or workpiece in lathe). Driven by AC servo or VFD-controlled induction motor; speeds up to **15 000–60 000 rpm** in high-speed machining centres.
4. **Feed Drives (Ball Screws / Linear Motors)** —
   - **Ball Screw** lead $L_p$ (mm/rev), efficiency **≥ 90 %**, preloaded to remove backlash.
   - **Linear Motor** — direct drive, no backlash, higher speed & acceleration, no ball-screw wear.
5. **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** — Carousel, arm-type, or chain-type. Tool change time 1–5 s typical.
6. **Work-Holding Devices** — Chucks, vises, fixtures, pallets, magnetic chucks.
7. **Coolant System** — Flood coolant, MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication), or through-spindle coolant.
8. **Chip Disposal / Conveyor** — Auger, conveyor belt, or chip pan.
9. **Lubrication System** — Auto-grease or oil-mist for slideways and ball screws.
10. **Enclosure / Safety Guards** — Interlocked doors, light curtains, e-stops.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Key Note |
|---|--------------------|----------------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Distance (Linear Interpol.) | $L = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ | mm | Used for feed distribution |
| 2 | Feed component along X | $f_x = F \cdot \dfrac{\Delta x}{L}$ | mm/min | $F$ = programmed feed |
| 3 | Feed component along Y | $f_y = F \cdot \dfrac{\Delta y}{L}$ | mm/min | — |
| 4 | Resolution (encoder-based) | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{L_p}{N}$ | mm/pulse | $L_p$ = lead, $N$ = pulses/rev |
| 5 | Following Error | $e(t) = x_c(t) - x_a(t)$ | mm | Drives PID controller |
| 6 | PID Output | $u = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | V (or A) | — |
| 7 | Spindle speed | $N_{sp} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | $V_c$ = cutting speed, $D$ = tool/workpiece dia. |
| 8 | Feed rate (lathe) | $f_r = N_{sp} \cdot f$ | mm/min | $f$ = feed per rev |
| 9 | MRR (Material Removal Rate) | $\text{MRR} = a_p \cdot f \cdot V_c$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ = depth of cut |
| 10 | Positioning accuracy | $A = \pm k \cdot L^{0.5}$ | µm | Empirical (ISO 230/2) |
| 11 | Repeatability | $R \approx \pm 2\sigma_x$ | µm | Statistical (ISO 230/2) |
| 12 | Velocity (linear axis) | $v = N_{mot} \cdot L_p$ | mm/min | $N_{mot}$ = motor rpm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note on Tables:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ in formulas above has been replaced with $\text{Res}$ or words to avoid breaking markdown tables. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside cells if absolute-value bars are essential.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace** — 5-axis CNC mills with linear motors and LVDT feedback machine monolithic wing ribs and turbine blades from titanium/Inconel.
- **Automotive** — CNC lathes with servo spindles produce crankshafts, pistons, and brake discs with repeatability < 5 µm.
- **Medical Implants** — Closed-loop CNC produces hip stems, dental crowns (e.g., zirconia) with bio-compatible tolerances.
- **Mould & Die** — High-speed CNC with NURBS interpolation carves hardened-steel moulds.
- **Electronics / Semiconductor** — PCB drilling/routing CNC uses stepper (open-loop) for speed; precision milling uses closed-loop.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resolution of a CNC Axis (Closed-Loop Ball-Screw Drive)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A CNC vertical machining centre uses a **ball screw** with **lead** $L_p$ mm per revolution. The **servo motor** carries an **incremental rotary encoder** that produces $N$ pulses per revolution. The controller operates in *electronic gearing mode* where 1 command pulse corresponds to 1 encoder pulse (gear ratio = 1). Determine the **smallest linear axis movement per command pulse** (the *axis resolution*).

**Step 1 — Define the parameters**

Let:
- $L_p$ = ball-screw lead (mm/rev).
- $N$ = encoder pulses per revolution.
- $\Delta x$ = smallest axis movement per pulse (mm/pulse).

**Step 2 — Establish the geometric relationship**

One complete revolution of the ball screw advances the nut (and the slide) by exactly one lead $L_p$. During that single revolution, the encoder outputs $N$ pulses, one per unit angular displacement.

**Step 3 — Set up the ratio**

Because motion and pulses are linear in this relationship:

$$\frac{\Delta x}{1 \text{ pulse}} = \frac{L_p \text{ mm}}{N \text{ pulses}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve**

$$\boxed{\Delta x = \frac{L_p}{N}}$$

This is the **theoretical axis resolution**. If the controller applies *electronic gear ratio* $G$ (command pulses per encoder pulse), then:

$$\Delta x_{\text{effective}} = \frac{L_p \cdot G}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU board-style)**

A CNC lathe has ball-screw lead $L_p = 10$ mm/rev. Encoder has $N = 2500$ pulses/rev. Electronic gear ratio $G = 1$.

Substituting:

$$\Delta x = \frac{10}{2500} = 0.004 \text{ mm per pulse} = 4 \,\mu\text{m}$$

So the axis can be commanded in **4 µm increments**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feed Components for Linear Interpolation

**Step 1 — Set up the problem**

A milling centre must move from $P_1(x_1, y_1)$ to $P_2(x_2, y_2)$ in a straight line at a programmed feed rate $F$ (mm/min). We need the individual feed rates $f_x$ and $f_y$ along the X and Y axes.

**Step 2 — Total path length (Pythagoras)**

The total length of the linear segment is:

$$L = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Time to traverse**

Time to complete the segment:

$$t = \frac{L}{F} \text{ (minutes)}$$

**Step 4 — Individual axis displacements**

Each axis must move a fraction of the total displacement equal to its share of the *unit vector* along the path.

Direction cosines:

$$\cos\theta_x = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{L}, \quad \cos\theta_y = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Individual feed rates**

Since each axis must complete its displacement in the same total time $t$:

$$f_x = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{t} = \frac{(x_2 - x_1) \cdot F}{L}$$

$$f_y = \frac{(y_2 - y_1) \cdot F}{L}$$

Generalised to 3 axes:

$$f_z = \frac{(z_2 - z_1) \cdot F}{\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Vector verification**

The vector sum must equal the programmed feed:

$$\sqrt{f_x^{\,2} + f_y^{\,2} + f_z^{\,2}} = F \quad \text{(must hold true)}$$

This is verified by substituting and squaring.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Following Error Dynamics (Open-Loop First-Order Approximation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Modelling intent:** Show the *relationship* between commanded position, actual position, and the gain of the closed-loop position controller. This is the analytical basis for *servo tuning*.

**Step 1 — Define variables**

Let:
- $X_c(s)$ = commanded position (Laplace).
- $X_a(s)$ = actual position.
- $E(s)$ = following error $= X_c(s) - X_a(s)$.
- $K_v$ = velocity gain (s⁻¹).
- $G(s)$ = position-loop controller (here approximated as unity for simplicity).

**Step 2 — Plant model**

A simple first-order model of the axis (motor + ball-screw + slide) treated as a position integrator with gain:

$$\frac{X_a(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K_v}{s}$$

where $U(s)$ is the velocity command output of the MCU.

**Step 3 — Closed-loop transfer function**

With unity feedback and controller $G(s)=1$:

$$\frac{X_a(s)}{X_c(s)} = \frac{K_v}{s + K_v}$$

**Step 4 — Error transfer function**

$$E(s) = X_c(s) - X_a(s) = X_c(s) \left[ 1 - \frac{K_v}{s + K_v} \right]$$

$$E(s) = X_c(s) \cdot \frac{s}{s + K_v}$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state error for ramp input**

For a *constant velocity* (ramp) command $X_c(s) = \dfrac{V}{s^2}$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{V}{s^2} \cdot \frac{s}{s + K_v}$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{V}{s + K_v} = \frac{V}{K_v}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (KTU high-yield):** The steady-state **following error** during a constant-velocity move is inversely proportional to the **velocity gain** $K_v$. Hence, *higher $K_v$ ⇒ smaller following error*, but *too high $K_v$ ⇒ overshoot and instability*. This is why CNC tuning aims to maximise $K_v$ without violating the stability margin (typically phase margin ≥ 45°).

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop CNC

| Feature | Open-Loop (Stepper) | Closed-Loop (Servo) |
|---------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Feedback | None | Encoder / Resolver / LVDT |
| Motor | Stepper (PM / VR / Hybrid) | DC or AC Servo |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Accuracy | Lower (lost steps possible) | Very high |
| Speed | Moderate | High (with linear motor: very high) |
| Holding torque at standstill | High (detent torque) | Active (servo current) |
| Heat generation | High (always energised) | Lower (only when needed) |
| Typical use | Hobby machines, low-cost engravers, EDM wire feed | Machine tools, robots, FMS cells |
| Reversal backlash compensation | Not possible | Possible (e.g., dual-loop) |

---

|------------|-----------|-------------|------------|------------|------|
| Incremental Optical Encoder | Slit + photo-diode | Pulses (TTL) | 0.1–1 µm (linear) | Moderate | Medium |
| Absolute Optical Encoder | Gray code disc | Multi-bit digital | 16–25 bit | Moderate | Medium-High |
| Resolver | Electromagnetic coupling | sin/cos analog | ±5 arc-min | Very high (oil, heat) | Medium |
| LVDT | Mutual induction | Analog AC | 0.1 µm | High | Medium |
| Inductosyn | Transformer with fine scale | Analog | 0.0001″ | Very high | High |
