# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper Pattern]**
**"List the major components of a CNC system and state the function of the Machine Control Unit (MCU)." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major components of a CNC system are:

1. **Program input device** (tape, USB, Ethernet, MDI)
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** — the controller
3. **Drive system** (servo motors + amplifiers + spindle drive)
4. **Feedback / measurement system** (encoders, LVDTs, resolvers)
5. **Machine tool** (mechanical structure, spindle, slideways, ATC, coolant)
6. **Auxiliaries** (chip conveyor, lubrication, safety interlocks)

**Function of MCU:** The MCU is the *brain* of the CNC machine. It (a) reads the part program from the input device, (b) decodes the NC codes (G/M), (c) performs interpolation to compute intermediate axis positions, (d) commands the servo drives for each axis, (e) regulates spindle speed, (f) handles auxiliary functions via the built-in PLC, and (g) compares actual position (from feedback) with commanded position to compute the following error and correct it.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**"Distinguish between open-loop and closed-loop CNC systems. Give one example of each." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Loop CNC | Closed-Loop CNC |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Feedback | Absent | Present (position & velocity) |
| Motor | Stepper motor | DC/AC servo motor |
| Error detection | Cannot detect lost steps | Detects and corrects error |
| Accuracy | Lower (limited by step size) | High (encoder resolution) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Example | Small engraving CNC, low-cost EDM wire-cut | Vertical machining centre, CNC lathe with servo drive |

In an **open-loop** system, the MCU sends command pulses to the stepper motor and *assumes* the axis has moved exactly as commanded. In a **closed-loop** system, the encoder/LVDT measures the *actual* position and the MCU continuously computes the following error, applying a correction signal via a PID controller.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (14 Marks):** Each Module has *internal choice* between two full questions of 14 marks. Each 14-mark question has *two sub-parts* of **7 marks each** (typically (a) descriptive + (b) analytical/derivation/comparison).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Module 1 Q1]**

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a CNC system. Describe the role of the Machine Control Unit (MCU) and its major sub-blocks.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b) Describe the various types of feedback transducers used in CNC machines. Compare an LVDT with a Resolver.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q1(a):**

The **functional architecture** of a CNC system consists of the following sub-systems (as shown in Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Part Program / Input Device** — supplies the NC coded instructions.
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** — interprets the program.
3. **Servo Drive System** — moves the axes.
4. **Feedback / Measurement System** — provides actual position data.
5. **Machine Tool** — performs the operation.

**Major Sub-blocks of the MCU (with description):**

- **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Executes system software, processes the NC code line by line. The CPU is typically a 32-bit or 64-bit microprocessor.
- **Memory (ROM & RAM):** ROM holds the *executive program* and *post-processor data* permanently. RAM holds the *active part program* and *parameter settings* temporarily (volatile).
- **Decoder & Syntax Checker:** Parses each block of NC code; raises an *alarm* (e.g., "syntax error") if the block is invalid.
- **Interpolator:** Computes intermediate axis positions between the start and end points of a cut. Supports linear, circular, helical, and NURBS interpolation.
- **Axis Controllers (X, Y, Z, A, B, C):** Dedicated controllers for each axis that close the position/velocity/current loops.
- **Spindle Speed Module:** Controls the spindle rpm via VFD or spindle drive.
- **Feed-rate Override & Spindle Override:** Allow the operator to scale F and S by 0–200 % in real time.
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** Handles *discrete I/O* — coolant pump, ATC, chuck, door interlock, lubrication.
- **Display & Keyboard / Operating Panel:** For program display, alarms, and manual input.

> **Conclusion:** The MCU coordinates all sub-systems in real time with a deterministic *servo cycle* (typically 1–4 ms) to deliver accurate, repeatable motion.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q1(b):**

**Types of Feedback Transducers in CNC Machines:**

1. **Optical Incremental Encoder** — produces pulses as the disc rotates; for motor position/velocity.
2. **Optical Absolute Encoder** — outputs a unique multi-bit code for every position; for tool magazine and C-axis.
3. **Linear Encoder (Glass/Steel Scale)** — direct linear position feedback along an axis.
4. **Resolver** — rugged electromagnetic rotary transducer for spindle and harsh environments.
5. **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** — linear position via mutual induction; used for tool length and axis position.
6. **Inductosyn** — high-precision linear/rotary scale based on inductive coupling.
7. **Tachogenerator** — analog velocity feedback.
8. **Capacitance Probe** — sub-micron displacement.

**Comparison — LVDT vs Resolver:**

| Parameter | LVDT | Resolver |
|-----------|------|----------|
| Quantity | Linear displacement | Angular displacement |
| Principle | Mutual induction between primary & two secondaries | Rotary transformer with sine/cosine windings |
| Output | Analog AC voltage (differential) | Analog sin/cos voltages |
| Resolution | 0.1 µm typical | ±5 arc-min typical |
| Robustness | High (sealed) | Very high (oil, heat, vibration) |
| Electronics | Requires carrier + demodulator | Requires R/D converter |
| Cost | Moderate | Moderate-High |
| Use in CNC | Axis position, tool length sensor | Spindle position, C-axis in lathe, harsh zones |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Module 1 Q2]**

**(a) Explain with a block diagram the closed-loop servo control of a CNC axis. Discuss the role of the position, velocity, and current loops.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b) A CNC milling machine uses a ball screw with lead 8 mm and a rotary encoder of 2500 pulses/rev. Calculate (i) the axis resolution, and (ii) the actual axis movement if the controller outputs 3125 command pulses. Take the electronic gear ratio as 1.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q2(a):**

A **closed-loop CNC axis** uses *feedback* to compare the commanded position with the actual position and reduce the error to near zero.

**Block Diagram** (as shown in Section 4.3):

The cascaded structure has three nested control loops, executed every servo cycle (1–4 ms):

1. **Position Loop (outermost):**
   - Compares **commanded position** with **actual position** (from linear encoder).
   - Computes **following error** $e = x_c - x_a$.
   - Outputs a *velocity command* to the velocity loop.
   - Tuned via **proportional gain $K_p$** and sometimes integral action.

2. **Velocity Loop (middle):**
   - Compares **commanded velocity** with **measured velocity** (from rotary encoder on the motor, or derived by differentiation of position).
   - Outputs a *current/torque command* to the current loop.
   - Tuned via **PI controller** ($K_{pv}$, $K_{iv}$).

3. **Current Loop (innermost):**
   - Compares **commanded current** with **measured motor current** (via current sensors / shunt).
   - Operates at the *highest bandwidth* (typically 2–5 kHz).
   - Produces the **PWM gate signals** for the servo amplifier's IGBTs.

**Why this cascade?** Each inner loop must be *much faster* (10× bandwidth) than the outer loop. This allows:
- High disturbance rejection (current loop is fastest).
- Smooth velocity (velocity loop is medium).
- Accurate final position (position loop is slowest, most accurate).

> **Conclusion:** The cascade ensures high stiffness, low following error, and good dynamic response — essential for contouring accuracy in CNC.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q2(b):**

**Given data:**

- Ball-screw lead $L_p = 8$ mm/rev.
- Encoder pulses $N = 2500$ pulses/rev.
- Electronic gear ratio $G = 1$.
- Command pulses $= n = 3125$ pulses.

**Step 1 — Axis resolution per pulse**

Using the formula from Section 3.1:

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{L_p \cdot G}{N}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{8 \times 1}{2500} = \frac{8}{2500} \text{ mm/pulse}$$

$$\text{Resolution} = 0.0032 \text{ mm/pulse} = 3.2 \,\mu\text{m/pulse}$$

**Step 2 — Actual axis movement for 3125 pulses**

$$x = \text{Resolution} \times n$$

$$x = 0.0032 \text{ mm} \times 3125$$

$$x = 10.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Verification (alternative method)**

The motor will turn $3125 / 2500 = 1.25$ revolutions.
Axis travel = $1.25 \times 8 = 10.0$ mm. ✔

> **Final Answer:** (i) Axis resolution = **3.2 µm/pulse**; (ii) Axis movement for 3125 pulses = **10.0 mm**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Omitting the feedback system** when listing CNC components — the feedback loop is *the defining feature* of a closed-loop CNC; forgetting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing DNC and CNC** — DNC = distributed control from a host computer; CNC = standalone controller with stored program. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Mixing up "accuracy" and "repeatability"** — Accuracy = closeness to *true* position; Repeatability = closeness to a *previously commanded* position when repeated.
> 4. **Wrong units in resolution calculation** — The ball-screw lead must be in **mm** (not cm) and the answer in **µm**. Always double-check unit consistency.
> 5. **Forgetting electronic gear ratio** $G$ in the resolution formula — if $G \ne 1$, the effective resolution changes.
> 6. **Using LVDT for rotational measurement** — LVDT is a *linear* transducer. For rotation, use resolver or rotary encoder. Examiners catch this.
> 7. **Skipping a labelled block diagram** in 7-mark descriptive answers — diagrams are *expected* and earn 1–2 marks.
> 8. **Stating "stepper motors are accurate"** — Stepper motors have *no feedback*; lost-step error is undetectable. Use a servo for high accuracy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **CNC = Computer Numerical Control**; 5 major sub-systems: **Input → MCU → Drive → Feedback → Machine Tool**.
- **MCU** is the *brain* — contains CPU, ROM, RAM, Decoder, Interpolator, Axis Controllers, PLC, Display.
- **Interpolation** types: **Linear (G01), Circular (G02/G03), Helical, NURBS** — interpolator outputs per-axis pulses.
- **Drives:** Stepper (open-loop), DC/AC Servo (closed-loop), Hydraulic (heavy duty), Spindle Drive (VFD or servo).
- **Ball-screw resolution:** $\Delta x = L_p / N$ (mm/pulse). Always with unit check.
- **Following error** $e = x_c - x_a$; PID controller drives $e \to 0$.
- **Velocity gain $K_v$** inversely determines steady-state ramp error $e_{ss} = V / K_v$.
- **Feedback transducers:** Linear/Rotary encoder (digital), LVDT (linear analog), Resolver (rotary analog), Inductosyn (high precision), Tachogenerator (velocity).
- **Open-loop vs Closed-loop:** Open = stepper, no feedback, low cost; Closed = servo with encoder, high accuracy.
- **Machine tool parts:** Bed/column, slideways, spindle, ATC, ball screws, work-holding, coolant, chip conveyor.
- **High-end features:** Linear motors (no backlash), direct-drive spindles, NURBS interpolation, thermal-error compensation.
- **Standards:** ISO 6983 (G-code), ISO 230/2 (accuracy/repeatability testing), EIA RS-267 (legacy).
- **Key CNC advantages:** Flexibility, repeatability, complex 3D contours, reduced lead time, lower scrap.
- **Key CNC limitations:** High initial cost, skilled operator/programmer required, vibration/thermal issues at high speed.
- **Mnemonic for CNC components:** **"I-MDF"** → **I**nput, **M**CU, **D**rive, **F**eedback (then **Machine Tool**).
- **Mnemonic for Feedback types:** **"EL-RLI"** → **E**ncoder, **L**VDT, **R**esolver, **L**inear scale, **I**nductosyn.
- **Remember:** A CNC system is fundamentally a **closed-loop, multi-axis, real-time, computer-controlled positioning system** — every component exists to support this.

> **End of Module 1 Topic — Components of CNC System | KTU 2024 Scheme | PCMET502**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coordinate Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coordinate Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coordinate System (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **coordinate system** is a mathematical framework used to uniquely define the position and orientation of a point, line, or body in two-dimensional (2D) or three-dimensional (3D) space using an ordered set of numerical values (coordinates) referenced to a set of mutually perpendicular or angular reference axes.

In the context of **Advanced Manufacturing Engineering**, coordinate systems form the foundational language for:
- **CNC (Computer Numerical Control)** programming (G-code, M-code)
- **CNC machining centers** (lathes, milling machines, EDM)
- **Robotics** (kinematic modeling, end-effector positioning)
- **CAD/CAM/CAE** integration (geometry transfer between software)
- **Metrology & CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)** inspection

The universally adopted standard for machine tool axis nomenclature is **ISO 841:2001** (Industrial automation systems and integration — Numerical control of machines — Program format and definitions of address words), which is also equivalent to **EIA RS-267-B** in North America.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Treasure Map":**
> Imagine you are giving directions to a friend to find a buried treasure in a large field. If you say *"Go 10 steps forward, 5 steps right, and dig 2 feet down"*, you are essentially using a **Cartesian coordinate system**. The starting point is the **origin (0, 0, 0)**, and the three numbers describe exactly where to dig.
>
> Now imagine instead you say *"Walk 15 meters at a 45° angle from the north pole of the field, then walk 8 meters toward the center"*. This is akin to a **spherical coordinate system**, where we describe position by **distance and angles** rather than straight-line distances.
>
> In CNC machining, the **machine tool is the treasure**, and **coordinate systems** tell the cutting tool precisely where to move in space.

## 1.3 Fundamental Reference Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Right-Hand Rule (RHR) — KTU High-Yield Concept:**
> The standard **3-axis Cartesian coordinate system** in manufacturing follows the **right-hand rule**:
> - **Point the index finger** along the positive **+X axis**.
> - **Point the middle finger** along the positive **+Y axis**.
> - **The thumb** then automatically points along the positive **+Z axis**.
>
> This convention ensures unambiguous orientation across all global manufacturing standards.

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Coordinate Systems

The major coordinate systems used in advanced manufacturing are:

1. **Cartesian (Rectangular) Coordinate System** — Uses three mutually perpendicular linear axes: **X, Y, Z**
2. **Polar Coordinate System (2D)** — Uses one linear distance (**r**) and one angle (**θ**)
3. **Cylindrical Coordinate System (3D)** — Uses two linear distances (**r, z**) and one angle (**θ**)
4. **Spherical Coordinate System (3D)** — Uses one linear distance (**r**) and two angles (**θ, φ**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Cartesian (Rectangular) Coordinate System

The Cartesian system is the **default reference** for virtually all CNC, robotics, and CAD systems. A point **P** in 3D space is described as:

$$P(x, y, z)$$

**Characteristics:**
- **Origin (O)**: The reference point where all three axes intersect, defined as **(0, 0, 0)**.
- **Axes**: **X, Y, Z** are mutually perpendicular (orthogonal).
- **Planes**: **XY-plane, YZ-plane, XZ-plane** are the three principal reference planes.
- **Orientation**: Determined by the **right-hand rule**.
- **Octants**: The three axes divide 3D space into **8 octants**.

### Industrial Application of Cartesian Systems
- **3-axis CNC milling machines** (X = table longitudinal, Y = table cross, Z = spindle vertical)
- **Cartesian robots** (gantry robots, pick-and-place machines)
- **3D printers** (FDM/FFF printers)

## 2.2 Polar Coordinate System (2D)

A point in the 2D plane is described by a radial distance **r** from the origin and an angular measure **θ** from a reference axis (typically the +X axis):

$$P(r, \theta)$$

where:
- $r \geq 0$ is the radial distance from origin
- $\theta$ is measured in radians or degrees

**Real-world analogy**: The hands of a clock — distance from center and angle from the 12 o'clock position.

## 2.3 Cylindrical Coordinate System (3D)

A natural extension of polar coordinates to 3D space, used extensively in **CNC lathes** because the workpiece is rotationally symmetric about a principal axis:

$$P(r, \theta, z)$$

where:
- $r$ = radial distance from the Z-axis
- $\theta$ = angular position around the Z-axis
- $z$ = height along the Z-axis

### Conversion: Cylindrical ↔ Cartesian

$$x = r \cos\theta$$
$$y = r \sin\theta$$
$$z = z$$

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

## 2.4 Spherical Coordinate System (3D)

Used in **5-axis machining** where the tool orientation is described by two angles (azimuth and elevation) plus a radial distance:

$$P(r, \theta, \phi)$$

where (using physics convention):
- $r$ = radial distance from origin
- $\theta$ = azimuthal angle (in XY-plane from +X axis)
- $\phi$ = polar angle (measured from +Z axis downward)

### Conversion: Spherical ↔ Cartesian

$$x = r \sin\phi \cos\theta$$
$$y = r \sin\phi \sin\theta$$
$$z = r \cos\phi$$

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$$
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$
$$\phi = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{z}{r}\right)$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **all critical formulas** for the KTU examination on coordinate systems. The vertical bar `|` has been intentionally replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Coordinate System | Parameters | Cartesian Conversion | Inverse Conversion | Engineering Application |
|-------------------|-----------|----------------------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| **Cartesian (3D)** | $x, y, z$ | $P(x, y, z)$ | Direct | CNC mills, 3D printers, CMMs |
| **Polar (2D)** | $r, \theta$ | $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$ | $r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2}$, $\theta = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$ | Tool radius compensation |
| **Cylindrical (3D)** | $r, \theta, z$ | $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$, $z = z$ | $r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2}$, $\theta = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$ | CNC lathes (turning centers) |
| **Spherical (3D)** | $r, \theta, \phi$ | $x = r\sin\phi\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\phi\sin\theta$, $z = r\cos\phi$ | $r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}$, $\phi = \cos^{-1}(z/r)$ | 5-axis CNC tool orientation |

**Other Critical KTU Relations:**

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Distance between two points $P_1(x_1,y_1,z_1)$ and $P_2(x_2,y_2,z_2)$ | $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$ | Used for linear interpolation, distance check |
| Mid-point of segment $P_1P_2$ | $M = \left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}, \frac{z_1+z_2}{2}\right)$ | Used in hole pattern programming |
| Direction cosines of vector $\vec{V}(a,b,c)$ | $\cos\alpha = a/\vert V \vert$, $\cos\beta = b/\vert V \vert$, $\cos\gamma = c/\vert V \vert$ | Used for tool vector definition |
| Magnitude of vector $\vec{V}(a,b,c)$ | $\vert V \vert = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}$ | Tool length, vector magnitude |

## 2.6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) and Machine Tool Axes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Definition:**
> A rigid body in 3D space has a maximum of **6 Degrees of Freedom**: **3 translational** (along X, Y, Z) and **3 rotational** (about X, Y, Z — denoted as A, B, C respectively per ISO 841).

| Axis Designation | Per ISO 841 | Motion Type | Example Machine |
|-----------------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **X** | Primary linear axis (longest travel) | Translation | Mill table left-right |
| **Y** | Secondary linear axis (perpendicular to X) | Translation | Mill table in-out |
| **Z** | Tertiary axis (parallel to spindle) | Translation | Spindle up-down |
| **A** | Rotation about X | Rotation | Tilt table around X |
| **B** | Rotation about Y | Rotation | Tilt table around Y |
| **C** | Rotation about Z | Rotation | Spindle rotation / 4th axis indexer |

**Machine Classification:**
- **3-axis machine**: X, Y, Z only (most common milling machines)
- **4-axis machine**: + A or B rotation (indexing tables)
- **5-axis machine**: + two rotations (simultaneous A+B, B+C, or A+C)
- **6-axis machine**: Full 6 DOF (advanced robotics)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Cylindrical to Cartesian Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation rigorously demonstrates the mathematical relationship between cylindrical and Cartesian coordinates — a **guaranteed question** in KTU university examinations.

**Given:** A point in cylindrical coordinates $P(r, \theta, z)$ where:
- $r$ = radial distance from Z-axis
- $\theta$ = angle measured from +X axis in the XY-plane (counterclockwise positive)
- $z$ = vertical height along Z-axis

**To Derive:** Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$

### Step 1: Visualize the Projection onto XY-Plane

Project the point $P$ vertically downward onto the **XY-plane**. The projected point $P'$ has polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ in the XY-plane.

### Step 2: Apply Trigonometric Decomposition

The vector $\vec{OP'}$ in the XY-plane has magnitude $r$ and angle $\theta$ from the +X axis. Decomposing this vector:

The **X-component** is the projection onto the X-axis:

$$x = r \cos\theta$$

The **Y-component** is the projection onto the Y-axis:

$$y = r \sin\theta$$

The **Z-component** remains unchanged because the projection from P to P' was purely vertical:

$$z = z$$

### Step 3: Combined Conversion Equations

$$\boxed{x = r \cos\theta, \quad y = r \sin\theta, \quad z = z}$$

### Step 4: Inverse Conversion (Cartesian to Cylindrical)

Starting with $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$, square and add:

$$x^2 + y^2 = r^2\cos^2\theta + r^2\sin^2\theta$$

$$x^2 + y^2 = r^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta)$$

Using the **Pythagorean trigonometric identity** $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$x^2 + y^2 = r^2$$

Taking the positive square root (since $r \geq 0$ by definition):

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

To find $\theta$, divide the equation for $y$ by the equation for $x$:

$$\frac{y}{x} = \frac{r\sin\theta}{r\cos\theta} = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \tan\theta$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the angle calculation**: The quadrant of the angle $\theta$ must be determined by examining the signs of $x$ and $y$. In programming, the function **ATAN2(y, x)** is preferred over $\tan^{-1}(y/x)$ because it correctly handles all four quadrants.

### Step 5: Numerical Verification

**Problem:** Convert the cylindrical point $P(r=5, \theta=60°, z=3)$ to Cartesian.

**Solution:**

$$x = 5 \cos(60°) = 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5$$

$$y = 5 \sin(60°) = 5 \times 0.866 = 4.330$$

$$z = 3$$

Therefore: $P(2.5, 4.330, 3)$ in Cartesian coordinates.

**Inverse Verification:**

$$r = \sqrt{2.5^2 + 4.330^2} = \sqrt{6.25 + 18.75} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \checkmark$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4.330}{2.5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.732) = 60° \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Spherical to Cartesian Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is critical for **5-axis CNC tool vector programming** and is a high-weightage topic.

**Given:** A point in spherical coordinates $P(r, \theta, \phi)$ where:
- $r$ = radial distance from origin
- $\theta$ = azimuthal angle (in XY-plane, from +X axis)
- $\phi$ = polar angle (from +Z axis)

### Step 1: Project onto Z-Axis

The vertical (Z) component is the projection of vector $\vec{r}$ onto the Z-axis:

$$z = r \cos\phi$$

### Step 2: Project onto XY-Plane

The magnitude of the projection of $\vec{r}$ onto the XY-plane is:

$$\rho = r \sin\phi$$

where $\rho$ is the radial distance in the XY-plane (equivalent to the cylindrical $r$).

### Step 3: Decompose the XY-Plane Projection

Using the azimuthal angle $\theta$:

$$x = \rho \cos\theta = r \sin\phi \cos\theta$$

$$y = \rho \sin\theta = r \sin\phi \sin\theta$$

### Step 4: Combined Equations

$$\boxed{x = r\sin\phi\cos\theta, \quad y = r\sin\phi\sin\theta, \quad z = r\cos\phi}$$

### Step 5: Inverse Conversion

Squaring and adding all three equations:

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = r^2\sin^2\phi\cos^2\theta + r^2\sin^2\phi\sin^2\theta + r^2\cos^2\phi$$

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = r^2\sin^2\phi(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) + r^2\cos^2\phi$$

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = r^2\sin^2\phi + r^2\cos^2\phi = r^2$$

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$$

For $\phi$, divide $z$ by $r$:

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{z}{r}\right)$$

For $\theta$:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \quad \text{(or ATAN2 function)}$$

### Step 6: Numerical Example

**Problem:** Convert the spherical point $P(r=10, \theta=30°, \phi=45°)$ to Cartesian.

$$x = 10 \sin(45°)\cos(30°) = 10 \times 0.7071 \times 0.8660 = 6.124$$

$$y = 10 \sin(45°)\sin(30°) = 10 \times 0.7071 \times 0.5 = 3.536$$

$$z = 10 \cos(45°) = 10 \times 0.7071 = 7.071$$

Therefore: $P(6.124, 3.536, 7.071)$ in Cartesian.

## 3.3 Direction Cosines and Tool Vector Definition

For **5-axis CNC programming**, the tool orientation vector is often specified using **direction cosines** $(\cos\alpha, \cos\beta, \cos\gamma)$ where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the angles the tool vector makes with the X, Y, Z axes respectively.

**Key Constraint:**

$$\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$$

This identity arises directly from the unit vector property — for a unit vector $\hat{u} = (a, b, c)$:

$$a^2 + b^2 + c^2 = 1$$

where $a = \cos\alpha$, $b = \cos\beta$, $c = \cos\gamma$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Cartesian Coordinate System Architecture (Block-Level Functional View)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the Cartesian coordinate system as applied to a CNC machining center, including the relationship between axes, planes, and machine motion components.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OriginNode["ORIGIN POINT 0,0,0"]
    XAxis["X AXIS Linear Long Travel"]
    YAxis["Y AXIS Linear Cross Travel"]
    ZAxis["Z AXIS Parallel to Spindle"]
    XYPlane["XY PLANE Horizontal Working Plane"]
    YZPlane["YZ PLANE Side Reference Plane"]
    XZPlane["XZ PLANE Vertical Reference Plane"]
    ToolPoint["TOOL TIP POSITION X, Y, Z"]
    RHRNode["RIGHT HAND RULE INDEX MIDDLE THUMB"]
    Q1["OCTANT 1 X POS Y POS Z POS"]
    Q2["OCTANT 2 X NEG Y POS Z POS"]
    Q3["OCTANT 3 X NEG Y NEG Z POS"]
    Q4["OCTANT 4 X POS Y NEG Z POS"]
    Q5["OCTANT 5 X POS Y POS Z NEG"]
    Q6["OCTANT 6 X NEG Y POS Z NEG"]
    Q7["OCTANT 7 X NEG Y NEG Z NEG"]
    Q8["OCTANT 8 X POS Y NEG Z NEG"]

    OriginNode --> XAxis
    OriginNode --> YAxis
    OriginNode --> ZAxis
    OriginNode --> XYPlane
    OriginNode --> YZPlane
    OriginNode --> XZPlane
    OriginNode --> RHRNode
    OriginNode --> Q1
    OriginNode --> Q2
    OriginNode --> Q3
    OriginNode --> Q4
    OriginNode --> Q5
    OriginNode --> Q6
    OriginNode --> Q7
    OriginNode --> Q8

    XAxis --> ToolPoint
    YAxis --> ToolPoint
    ZAxis --> ToolPoint
```

## 4.2 CNC Machine Axis Identification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MachineStart["CNC MACHINE TOOL"]
    Step1["Identify Spindle Axis Z"]
    Step2["Set X Axis Longest Travel Perpendicular to Z"]
    Step3["Set Y Axis Completing Right Hand Rule"]
    Step4["Add Rotational Axis A About X if Present"]
    Step5["Add Rotational Axis B About Y if Present"]
    Step6["Add Rotational Axis C About Z if Present"]
    DOF3["3 AXIS MACHINE XYZ"]
    DOF4["4 AXIS MACHINE XYZ plus A"]
    DOF5["5 AXIS MACHINE XYZ plus AB or BC or AC"]
    DOF6["6 AXIS MACHINE XYZ plus ABC"]

    MachineStart --> Step1
    Step1 --> Step2
    Step2 --> Step3
    Step3 --> DOF3
    Step3 --> Step4
    Step4 --> DOF4
    Step4 --> Step5
    Step5 --> DOF5
    Step5 --> Step6
    Step6 --> DOF6
```

## 4.3 Coordinate System Conversion Flow Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Cartesian["CARTESIAN X Y Z"]
    Polar["POLAR R THETA 2D"]
    Cylindrical["CYLINDRICAL R THETA Z 3D"]
    Spherical["SPHERICAL R THETA PHI 3D"]
    CNCmill["CNC MILLING MACHINES 3D"]
    CNClathe["CNC LATHES TURNING"]
    FiveAxis["5 AXIS MACHINING TOOL ORIENTATION"]

    Cartesian -->|Default Reference| CNCmill
    Cylindrical -->|Rotation Symmetric Workpiece| CNClathe
    Spherical -->|Tool Vector and Orientation| FiveAxis
    Cartesian -->|Trig Conversion| Polar
    Polar -->|Add Z Axis| Cylindrical
    Cartesian -->|Polar Extension| Spherical
```

## 4.4 Work Coordinate System (WCS) Programming Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> In CNC programming, multiple coordinate systems coexist on the same machine. The hierarchy is critical for KTU examination questions on **work offsets** (G54–G59 in milling, G50 in turning).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MCS["MACHINE COORDINATE SYSTEM MCS Home Position Set by Manufacturer"]
    G92["G92 Temporary Programmable Origin Shift"]
    G54["G54 Work Offset 1"]
    G55["G55 Work Offset 2"]
    G56["G56 Work Offset 3"]
    G57["G57 Work Offset 4"]
    G58["G58 Work Offset 5"]
    G59["G59 Work Offset 6"]
    ToolOffset["TOOL LENGTH OFFSET H Register"]
    ToolComp["TOOL RADIUS COMPENSATION G41 G42"]

    MCS --> G92
    G92 --> G54
    G92 --> G55
    G92 --> G56
    G92 --> G57
    G92 --> G58
    G92 --> G59
    G54 --> ToolOffset
    ToolOffset --> ToolComp
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a coordinate system. List the four major types of coordinate systems used in advanced manufacturing with one industrial application each.**

**Model Answer:**

A coordinate system is a mathematical framework used to uniquely specify the position of a point in space relative to a defined origin and set of reference axes.

The four major types of coordinate systems used in advanced manufacturing are:

| S.No. | Coordinate System | Industrial Application |
|-------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Cartesian (X, Y, Z)** | 3-axis CNC milling machines, 3D printers |
| 2 | **Polar (r, θ)** — 2D | Tool radius compensation entry/exit arcs |
| 3 | **Cylindrical (r, θ, z)** | CNC turning centers (lathes) for rotational workpieces |
| 4 | **Spherical (r, θ, φ)** | 5-axis CNC machining for tool vector orientation |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State and explain the right-hand rule for a 3-axis Cartesian coordinate system. Why is this convention universally adopted in CNC machining?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Right-Hand Rule (RHR)** is the standardized convention for orienting a 3-axis Cartesian coordinate system. To apply it:
- Point the **right index finger** along the positive **+X axis**.
- Point the **right middle finger** along the positive **+Y axis**.
- The **right thumb** then naturally points along the positive **+Z axis**.

This convention is universally adopted in CNC machining because:
1. It provides an **unambiguous, standardized orientation** of the X, Y, Z axes.
2. It complies with **ISO 841:2001** and **EIA RS-267-B** standards.
3. It enables **portability of NC code** between different CNC machine manufacturers.
4. It eliminates confusion in **multi-axis programming**, especially for 5-axis machines using A, B, C rotational axes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the conversion equations between **Cartesian and Cylindrical coordinate systems**. A point on a CNC lathe is at cylindrical coordinates **(r = 50 mm, θ = 120°, z = 25 mm)**. Convert this point to Cartesian coordinates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **degrees of freedom** concept in the context of CNC machine tools. With a neat sketch, classify machine tools based on the number of axes (3-axis, 4-axis, 5-axis, and 6-axis) and state one industrial application of each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

Let a point P have cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ where:
- $r$ = radial distance from Z-axis
- $\theta$ = angle from +X axis (counterclockwise positive) in XY-plane
- $z$ = vertical height

**Projection onto XY-plane:**
When projected onto the XY-plane, point P forms a vector of magnitude $r$ making angle $\theta$ with the +X axis. Using standard trigonometry:

$$x = r \cos\theta$$
$$y = r \sin\theta$$

The Z-coordinate remains unchanged: $z = z$.

**Inverse Conversion:**

Squaring and adding the X and Y equations:

$$x^2 + y^2 = r^2\cos^2\theta + r^2\sin^2\theta = r^2$$

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

Dividing Y by X:

$$\frac{y}{x} = \tan\theta \implies \theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $r = 50$ mm, $\theta = 120°$, $z = 25$ mm.

$$x = 50 \cos(120°) = 50 \times (-0.5) = -25.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$y = 50 \sin(120°) = 50 \times 0.866 = +43.30 \text{ mm}$$

$$z = 25.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** $P(-25.00, +43.30, 25.00)$ mm in Cartesian coordinates.

**Verification:** $r = \sqrt{(-25)^2 + (43.30)^2} = \sqrt{625 + 1874.89} = \sqrt{2499.89} \approx 50$ mm ✓

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

A rigid body in 3D space possesses a maximum of **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: 3 translational motions along X, Y, Z, and 3 rotational motions about X, Y, Z axes (denoted A, B, C per ISO 841).

**Classification of CNC Machine Tools:**

| Machine Type | Axes Available | DOF | Industrial Application |
|-------------|----------------|-----|------------------------|
| **3-axis** | X, Y, Z | 3 | Prismatic parts, mold cavities, 2.5D profiles |
| **4-axis** | X, Y, Z, + A (or B) | 4 | Cylindrical engraving, blade machining, index tables |
| **5-axis** | X, Y, Z, + A and C (or B and C) | 5 | Aerospace impellers, turbine blades, complex sculptured surfaces |
| **6-axis** | X, Y, Z, A, B, C (all rotations) | 6 | Advanced robotic machining, multi-directional welding |

**Key Points:**
- A **3-axis mill** can only move the tool linearly — the cutting tool always remains perpendicular to the table.
- A **5-axis machine** can simultaneously tilt the tool/spindle, enabling access to undercuts and reducing setups.
- **6-axis systems** are rare in conventional machining; they are mostly used in robotic machining cells.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** What is a **spherical coordinate system**? Derive the conversion equations from **spherical to Cartesian** form. A cutting tool tip is positioned at spherical coordinates **(r = 100 mm, θ = 60°, φ = 30°)**. Find its Cartesian coordinates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **work coordinate systems (WCS)** in CNC machining. With suitable diagrams, differentiate between **Machine Coordinate System (MCS)** and **Work Coordinate System (WCS)**. List the G-codes used to invoke work offsets in a CNC mill. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

A **spherical coordinate system** specifies a point P in 3D space using:
- $r$ = radial distance from origin
- $\theta$ = azimuthal angle (in XY-plane from +X axis)
- $\phi$ = polar angle (measured from +Z axis downward)

**Derivation:**

The point P has a vertical (Z-axis) projection:

$$z = r \cos\phi$$

The radial projection onto the XY-plane has magnitude:

$$\rho = r \sin\phi$$

Decomposing this in-plane projection using azimuthal angle $\theta$:

$$x = \rho \cos\theta = r \sin\phi \cos\theta$$

$$y = \rho \sin\theta = r \sin\phi \sin\theta$$

**Inverse Conversion:**

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$$

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{z}{r}\right)$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $r = 100$ mm, $\theta = 60°$, $\phi = 30°$.

$$x = 100 \sin(30°)\cos(60°) = 100 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 = 25.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$y = 100 \sin(30°)\sin(60°) = 100 \times 0.5 \times 0.866 = 43.30 \text{ mm}$$

$$z = 100 \cos(30°) = 100 \times 0.866 = 86.60 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** $P(25.00, 43.30, 86.60)$ mm in Cartesian coordinates.

**Verification:** $r = \sqrt{25^2 + 43.30^2 + 86.60^2} = \sqrt{625 + 1874.89 + 7499.56} = \sqrt{9999.45} \approx 100$ mm ✓

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

A **Work Coordinate System (WCS)** is a **localized coordinate system** set up by the programmer at a convenient reference point on the workpiece, rather than at the machine's fixed home position. It allows the NC programmer to write part programs using **workpiece-based dimensions**, which are far more intuitive than machine-based coordinates.

**Difference between MCS and WCS:**

| Feature | Machine Coordinate System (MCS) | Work Coordinate System (WCS) |
|---------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Origin Location** | Fixed at machine home position (set by manufacturer) | Set by programmer at workpiece datum |
| **Determined By** | Machine tool builder | NC programmer / setup person |
| **Flexibility** | Cannot be changed | Can be shifted freely |
| **Programming** | Requires knowing machine zero | Uses part zero for easier programming |
| **Reset** | Only at machine power-on / reference return | Adjustable via G10 command or probe |

**G-codes for Work Offsets in CNC Mills:**

| G-Code | Function |
|--------|----------|
| **G54** | Work Offset 1 (most commonly used) |
| **G55** | Work Offset 2 |
| **G56** | Work Offset 3 |
| **G57** | Work Offset 4 |
| **G58** | Work Offset 5 |
| **G59** | Work Offset 6 |
| **G10** | Programmable data input (set work offsets dynamically) |
| **G92** | Temporary coordinate system shift (legacy) |

For **CNC lathes**, the equivalent system uses **G50** (for setting the coordinate system on Fanuc controllers) and tool offset registers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Angle Units Confusion**: Forgetting to convert degrees to radians (or vice versa) in trigonometric functions. The KTU answer key demands unit consistency — if the input is in degrees, the output of $\tan^{-1}$ must be re-converted.
>
> 2. **Sign of Coordinates**: Failing to apply negative signs correctly when angles exceed 90° (e.g., $\cos(120°) = -0.5$, not $+0.5$). Always visualize the quadrant first.
>
> 3. **Quadrant Ambiguity in ATAN**: Using $\tan^{-1}(y/x)$ instead of **ATAN2(y, x)**. The simple inverse tangent cannot distinguish between Quadrant I and Quadrant III, leading to incorrect angles.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Units**: Writing "5" instead of "5 mm" or "5 cm". KTU examiners award marks for proper SI unit notation.
>
> 5. **Direction Cosines Constraint**: Writing $(\cos\alpha + \cos\beta + \cos\gamma = 1)$ instead of the correct **squared sum** identity: $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$.
>
> 6. **Confusing A, B, C Axes**: Mixing up which rotational axis corresponds to which translation axis. Memorize: **A-about-X, B-about-Y, C-about-Z** (alphabetical order matches spatial order).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [x] **Coordinate System** = mathematical framework to define position in space
- [x] **Four Major Types** = Cartesian, Polar, Cylindrical, Spherical
- [x] **Cartesian** uses (x, y, z) — default for 3-axis CNC
- [x] **Cylindrical** uses (r, θ, z) — default for CNC lathes
- [x] **Spherical** uses (r, θ, φ) — used in 5-axis tool orientation
- [x] **Right-Hand Rule** governs axis orientation — universally applied
- [x] **6 DOF** = 3 translations (X,Y,Z) + 3 rotations (A,B,C)
- [x] **ISO 841** is the standard for CNC axis nomenclature
- [x] **A = rotation about X**, **B = rotation about Y**, **C = rotation about Z**
- [x] **Direction cosines satisfy**: $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$
- [x] **Cylindrical to Cartesian**: $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$
- [x] **Spherical to Cartesian**: $x = r\sin\phi\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\phi\sin\theta$, $z = r\cos\phi$
- [x] **MCS** is fixed; **WCS** is programmable (G54–G59 in milling)
- [x] **Distance formula**: $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$
- [x] **Midpoint formula**: $M = \left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}, \frac{z_1+z_2}{2}\right)$
- [x] **Always include units** (mm or inch) in numerical answers
- [x] **Always specify the quadrant** when calculating inverse trigonometric functions
- [x] **5-axis machines** offer simultaneous tool tilt — used for aerospace turbine blades and impellers
- [x] **Polar coordinate** (2D, r, θ) is foundational for understanding cylindrical and spherical systems

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of CNC Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of CNC Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Classification of CNC Systems** refers to the systematic categorization of Computer Numerical Control (CNC) machine tools based on distinct functional, structural, and operational characteristics. A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) system** is a microprocessor-based automation system in which the operations of a machine tool are directly controlled by a sequence of coded instructions (the part program) processed by a dedicated computer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Classification of CNC machines can be done based on **(a) Motion Control Type**, **(b) Loop Control System (Open/Closed)**, **(c) Number of Axes**, **(d) Type of Drive System**, and **(e) Application/Machine Type**.

The classification framework is critical for the selection, programming, and operational planning of CNC machines in modern manufacturing industries (Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Think of classifying CNC systems the way you classify **vehicles**:
- A car can be classified **by fuel** (petrol, diesel, electric, hybrid),
- **by purpose** (passenger, cargo, racing, off-road),
- **by transmission** (manual, automatic, CVT).

Similarly, a CNC machine can be classified by **how it moves** (motion control), **how accurately it positions** (loop control), **how many directions it can move** (axes), and **what powers its motion** (drive system).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** No single classification is "complete" — they are all **parallel and complementary**. A single CNC machine belongs to **multiple categories simultaneously**. For example, a 5-axis CNC milling machine is also a closed-loop, contouring, servo-driven system.

### 1.3 Primary Classification Criteria (KTU High-Yield)

The five principal classification bases are:

1. **Motion Control** — Point-to-Point, Straight-line, Contouring
2. **Loop Control** — Open Loop, Closed Loop, Semi-Closed Loop
3. **Number of Axes** — 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5 axes
4. **Drive System** — Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electric, Hybrid
5. **Application** — Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding, EDM, etc.

These classifications are non-overlapping in the **architectural sense** but coexist in a **real-world CNC machine**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Based on Motion Control

The motion control classification defines **how the tool moves relative to the workpiece** during machining.

#### (a) Point-to-Point (PTP) / Positioning Systems
- The tool moves to a defined **discrete coordinate position** without any controlled path between points.
- Machining (cutting) happens **only at the destination**; the path between points is irrelevant.
- Example: **Drilling, Tapping, Boring, Spot facing**.
- Control complexity: **Lowest** among all motion types.

#### (b) Straight-Cut (Line) Control Systems
- The tool moves in **straight lines along one axis at a time** (or parallel to an axis).
- Can perform both PTP operations and **straight-line cutting**.
- Example: **Simple milling of rectangular pockets**, face milling of flat surfaces.

#### (c) Contouring (Continuous Path) Systems
- The tool moves along a **continuously controlled path** in 2D or 3D space.
- Requires **simultaneous multi-axis interpolation** (linear, circular, helical, parabolic).
- Example: **Complex profile milling, die-making, sculptured surface machining, 5-axis aerospace component machining**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpolation** is the process of computing intermediate tool-position points between the start and end of a desired path. Two primary types: **Linear Interpolation (G01)** and **Circular Interpolation (G02/G03)**.

### 2.2 Classification Based on Loop Control

#### (a) Open Loop System
- No feedback sensor to verify actual tool position.
- Uses **stepper motors** — each pulse = a known angular step.
- Advantages: Simple, cheap, low maintenance.
- Disadvantages: **No error correction**, lost steps cause positional errors.

#### (b) Closed Loop System
- Uses **feedback transducers** (linear scales, encoders) mounted directly on the moving slide.
- Compares **commanded position vs. actual position** and corrects errors in real-time.
- High accuracy, high cost, complex tuning.

#### (c) Semi-Closed Loop System
- Feedback sensor is on the **motor shaft** (rotary encoder), not on the final slide.
- Compensates for **motor-side errors only**; mechanical errors (ballscrew wear, backlash) are NOT corrected.
- Most common industrial compromise — used in **majority of CNC machining centers**.

### 2.3 Classification Based on Number of Axes

| Axis Configuration | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **2-Axis** | Only 2 linear axes controlled (typically X, Z) | CNC Lathes, simple turning |
| **2.5-Axis** | 2 axes simultaneous + 1 axis in stepped positions | Engraving, simple milling |
| **3-Axis** | X, Y, Z simultaneous interpolation | Standard CNC milling |
| **4-Axis** | 3 linear + 1 rotary (A or B axis) | Indexed rotary table milling |
| **5-Axis** | 3 linear + 2 rotary (A+B or A+C) | Aerospace blisks, impellers, dies |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> The following formulas are the most commonly tested numerical/numerical-conceptual formulas in KTU Module-1 of PCMET502.

| Formula | Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_r = n \times z \times f_z$ | Feed rate calculation | mm/min | Spindle-feed relationship |
| $v_c = \pi \times D \times n$ | Cutting speed | m/min | Lathe turning calculations |
| $P_{step} = \theta_{step} \times N$ | Open-loop position | steps | Stepper motor positioning |
| $e_{pos} = x_{cmd} - x_{actual}$ | Position error | mm | Closed-loop correction |
| $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta x_{output}}{\Delta e_{input}}$ | Proportional gain | — | Servo loop tuning |
| $L = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$ | Linear interpolation length | mm | Tool path length |
| $\Delta u = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \times (u_2-u_1)$ | Parametric interpolation (any axis u) | mm | Contouring motion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Motion-control classification directly determines the **CNC controller capability** (number of interpolator channels), the **part program complexity** (G-code level), and the **machine price tier** (PTP < Line < Contouring < 5-axis Contouring).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive sector** — 3-axis contouring CNC mills for engine blocks, gearboxes.
- **Aerospace sector** — 5-axis contouring CNC mills for turbine blades (blisks) and structural components.
- **Die & Mold** — Closed-loop, 3 to 5-axis contouring systems with linear scales.
- **Mass production** — Open-loop stepper systems for low-cost PCB drilling and pick-and-place.
- **Heavy industry** — Hydraulic-drive CNC for large workpieces (e.g., turbine shafts).


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Interpolation Feed Distribution (Parametric Interpolation)

In a **contouring CNC system**, when the tool moves from point $P_1(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ to point $P_2(x_2, y_2, z_2)$ under a commanded feed rate $F$ (mm/min), the controller must distribute the feed velocity along each axis proportionally.

**Step 1 — Total displacement vector length:**

$$
L = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}
$$

This is the **3D Euclidean distance** between the two endpoints. The tool path length equals this displacement for a perfectly linear path.

**Step 2 — Total travel time at commanded feed:**

$$
t = \frac{L}{F} \quad \text{[minutes]}
$$

This is simply the kinematic relation: time = distance / feed. The CNC interpolator must complete the move in exactly this duration.

**Step 3 — Parametric position variable:**

$$
u \in [0, 1]
$$

where $u = 0$ corresponds to the start point $P_1$ and $u = 1$ corresponds to the end point $P_2$. This is the **normalized parameter** used internally by the interpolator.

**Step 4 — Position of any axis at parameter $u$:**

$$
x(u) = x_1 + u(x_2 - x_1)
$$

$$
y(u) = y_1 + u(y_2 - y_1)
$$

$$
z(u) = z_1 + u(z_2 - z_1)
$$

This is the **parametric linear equation** of a straight line in 3D space. The CNC interpolator generates thousands of these intermediate points per second (typically 1 ms to 4 ms block cycle).

**Step 5 — Velocity component along any axis:**

$$
v_x = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{F(x_2-x_1)}{L}
$$

$$
v_y = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{F(y_2-y_1)}{L}
$$

$$
v_z = \frac{dz}{dt} = \frac{F(z_2-z_1)}{L}
$$

Differentiating the position equations with respect to time and substituting $u = t/t$ gives the **velocity vector** distributed among the three axes.

**Step 6 — Verification of resultant velocity magnitude:**

$$
\sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2} = F \cdot \frac{\sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}}{L} = F
$$

The square root collapses to $F$ exactly, confirming that the **vector sum of the axis velocities equals the commanded feed rate**. This is the fundamental check that the interpolator is functioning correctly.

### 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Position Error and Correction

**Step 1 — Define commanded position and actual position:**

Let $x_c(t)$ be the commanded position and $x_a(t)$ be the actual position of the slide at time $t$. The **instantaneous position error** is:

$$
e(t) = x_c(t) - x_a(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity command (after P-gain in servo):**

$$
v_{cmd}(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)
$$

The servo amplifier multiplies the error by the proportional gain $K_p$ and outputs a velocity command to the motor.

**Step 3 — Steady-state error in a Type-1 closed-loop system:**

For a CNC servo with **integrator action** (PI or PID controller), the steady-state position error under a constant velocity command is theoretically:

$$
e_{ss} = 0
$$

This is the key advantage of an **integral action** — it eliminates steady-state position lag, which is critical for circular interpolation accuracy.

**Step 4 — Following error in a proportional-only (P) loop:**

If only a proportional controller is used (no integrator), the steady-state error during constant-velocity motion is:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{v_{cmd}}{K_p}
$$

This non-zero lag is acceptable for low-speed operations but causes **contouring errors** at high feed rates, especially during circular interpolation.

**Step 5 — Design implication:**

To minimize $e_{ss}$ in a P-only loop, the gain $K_p$ must be **increased**, but excessive $K_p$ leads to **instability, overshoot, and chatter**. This trade-off is the core of CNC servo tuning.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Open Loop | Semi-Closed | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedback sensor | None | Rotary encoder on motor | Linear scale on slide |
| Typical motor | Stepper | Servo (AC/DC) | Servo (AC/DC) |
| Compensates for mechanical errors (backlash, ballscrew pitch error) | No | No | **Yes** |
| Accuracy | Low (0.01 mm) | Medium (0.005 mm) | High (0.001 mm or better) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical application | Hobby, low-cost PCB drills, small engravers | General CNC machining | Precision dies, aerospace |
| Lost-step problem | Yes (critical) | No (encoder feedback) | No (direct feedback) |
| KTU Exam Frequency | High | High | High |


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Hierarchy (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CNC SYSTEM CLASSIFICATION]:::root
    A --> B1[Motion Control Type]:::branch
    A --> B2[Loop Control Type]:::branch
    A --> B3[Number of Axes]:::branch
    A --> B4[Drive System Type]:::branch
    A --> B5[Application Type]:::branch

    B1 --> C1A[Point to Point]:::leaf
    B1 --> C1B[Straight Line]:::leaf
    B1 --> C1C[Contouring Continuous Path]:::leaf

    B2 --> C2A[Open Loop]:::leaf
    B2 --> C2B[Semi Closed Loop]:::leaf
    B2 --> C2C[Closed Loop]:::leaf

    B3 --> C3A[2 Axis]:::leaf
    B3 --> C3B[2.5 Axis]:::leaf
    B3 --> C3C[3 Axis]:::leaf
    B3 --> C3D[4 Axis]:::leaf
    B3 --> C3E[5 Axis]:::leaf

    B4 --> C4A[Hydraulic Drive]:::leaf
    B4 --> C4B[Electric Servo Drive]:::leaf
    B4 --> C4C[Pneumatic Drive]:::leaf
    B4 --> C4D[Hybrid Drive]:::leaf

    B5 --> C5A[CNC Lathe Turning]:::leaf
    B5 --> C5B[CNC Milling]:::leaf
    B5 --> C5C[CNC Drilling]:::leaf
    B5 --> C5D[CNC Grinding]:::leaf
    B5 --> C5E[CNC EDM]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#dbeafe,color:#0f172a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop vs Open-Loop Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Part Program G Code]:::input --> B[CNC Controller Interpolator]:::control
    B --> C[Servo Amplifier]:::control
    C --> D[Servo Motor]:::control
    D --> E[Ballscrew and Slide]:::mechanical
    E --> F[Tool and Workpiece]:::mechanical

    F -.-> G[Actual Machined Path]:::feedback
    E -.-> H[Linear Scale Position Sensor]:::feedback
    H -.->|Actual Position Signal| B
    G -.->|Dimensional Verification| I[Quality Check]:::output
    D -.->|Rotary Encoder Signal| B

    classDef input fill:#fef3c7,color:#0f172a,stroke:#b45309
    classDef control fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e40af
    classDef mechanical fill:#9ca3af,color:#ffffff,stroke:#374151
    classDef feedback fill:#fb923c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#9a3412
    classDef output fill:#86efac,color:#0f172a,stroke:#166534
```

### 4.3 Axis Configuration Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Part Program]:::input --> X[X Axis Linear]:::axis
    P --> Y[Y Axis Linear]:::axis
    P --> Z[Z Axis Linear]:::axis
    P --> R1[A Axis Rotary]:::axis
    P --> R2[B Axis Rotary]:::axis

    X --> CON[2 Axis CNC Lathe]:::config
    X --> Y --> CON2[3 Axis CNC Mill]:::config
```
