    classDef input fill:#fef3c7,color:#0f172a,stroke:#b45309
    classDef axis fill:#c7d2fe,color:#0f172a,stroke:#3730a3
    classDef config fill:#86efac,color:#0f172a,stroke:#166534
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Classify CNC systems based on motion control. Briefly explain the three categories with one example application for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

CNC systems are classified into three categories based on motion control:

1. **Point-to-Point (PTP) Systems** *(1 mark)*: The tool moves to a specified coordinate position, and machining (cutting) occurs only at the destination. The path between points is uncontrolled. Example: **Drilling holes** on a workpiece.

2. **Straight-Line Control Systems** *(1 mark)*: The tool can perform both PTP operations and straight-line cutting along a single axis at a time. Example: **Milling a rectangular slot**.

3. **Contouring (Continuous Path) Systems** *(1 mark)*: The tool follows a continuously controlled path in 2D or 3D using simultaneous multi-axis interpolation. Example: **Machining a circular profile or sculptured die surface**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop CNC systems with respect to feedback, accuracy, and cost.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback sensor** *(1 mark)* | None — uses stepper motor | Linear scale or encoder on the slide |
| **Accuracy** *(1 mark)* | Low (~0.01 mm) | High (~0.001 mm) |
| **Cost** *(1 mark)* | Low | High |

In an open-loop system, there is no verification of actual tool position, so lost steps cause errors. A closed-loop system continuously compares commanded vs. actual position and corrects in real-time, providing superior contouring accuracy.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the classification of CNC systems based on **loop control**. Compare open-loop, semi-closed loop, and closed-loop systems with neat block diagrams. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNC milling machine uses a **semi-closed loop** system. The rotary encoder on the motor shaft gives **2000 pulses/rev**. The motor is coupled to a ballscrew with **pitch = 5 mm/rev**. The commanded position is $x_c = 50.000$ mm, and the actual slide position (from a separate linear scale verification) is $x_a = 49.985$ mm. Calculate the **position error** and comment on the **limitation of semi-closed loop** in detecting this error. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The classification based on loop control determines **how the CNC system monitors and corrects positioning errors**.

**Open Loop System** *(2 marks)*:
- **No feedback** of actual tool/slide position.
- Uses a **stepper motor**, where each electrical pulse produces a known angular rotation.
- The controller assumes that the commanded pulse train produces the desired mechanical motion.
- **Limitations**: Lost steps due to mechanical overload or resonance cause cumulative positioning errors that are **never detected or corrected**.
- **Application**: Low-cost engraving machines, small PCB drilling, 3D printers (early designs).

**Semi-Closed Loop System** *(2 marks)*:
- Feedback sensor (rotary encoder) is mounted on the **motor shaft**, not on the final slide.
- The controller measures **motor rotation** and infers slide position assuming a rigid mechanical transmission.
- **Limitation**: Errors caused by **ballscrew pitch error, backlash, thermal expansion, or mechanical flexure** between the motor and the slide are **NOT detected** because the sensor cannot "see" the slide.
- **Application**: Most standard CNC machining centers (cost-effective compromise).

**Closed Loop System** *(2 marks)*:
- Feedback sensor (linear scale, Heidenhain glass scale, or laser interferometer) is mounted **directly on the moving slide**.
- The controller compares **commanded position vs. measured slide position** at every servo cycle (typically 1–4 ms).
- Errors from **all sources** (motor, ballscrew, backlash, thermal, mechanical) are detected and corrected.
- **Application**: Ultra-precision machining, optical molds, aerospace structural components, large gantry machines.

**Block diagram comparison** *(1 mark)* — Students should draw three simple block diagrams:
- Open loop: `[Controller] → [Stepper] → [Slide]` (no feedback arrow).
- Semi-closed: `[Controller] → [Servo] → [Ballscrew] → [Slide]` with feedback arrow from the servo motor back to controller.
- Closed loop: Same as above but with feedback arrow taken from the **slide (linear scale)** back to controller.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Given data** *(1 mark)*:
- Commanded position: $x_c = 50.000$ mm
- Actual slide position: $x_a = 49.985$ mm
- Encoder resolution: 2000 pulses/rev
- Ballscrew pitch: $p = 5$ mm/rev

**Step 2 — Calculate the position error** *(2 marks)*:

$$
e = x_c - x_a = 50.000 - 49.985 = 0.015 \text{ mm}
$$

So the actual slide is **15 µm short** of the commanded position.

**Step 3 — Calculate the encoder resolution in linear terms** *(2 marks)*:

$$
\text{Encoder linear resolution} = \frac{p}{N} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{2000 \text{ pulses}} = 0.0025 \text{ mm/pulse}
$$

This is the **smallest linear displacement the semi-closed loop can detect** via the motor-mounted encoder.

**Step 4 — Verify the semi-closed loop limitation** *(2 marks)*:

The semi-closed loop system would only detect an error if the motor's actual rotation **diverged** from the commanded rotation. In this case, the motor may have rotated **exactly as commanded** (encoder reading matches the expected count), but the slide is still **15 µm short** because of:

- Ballscrew **pitch error** (manufacturing tolerance),
- Mechanical **backlash** reversal error,
- **Thermal expansion** of the ballscrew,
- Coupling **slippage** or flexure.

The semi-closed loop **cannot detect this 15 µm discrepancy** because its sensor is on the motor shaft, not on the slide. This is the fundamental limitation of semi-closed loop systems.

**Conclusion** *(Bonus mark for clear statement)*: For applications requiring < 5 µm accuracy, a **closed-loop (linear-scale feedback)** system is mandatory.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Classify CNC machines based on **number of axes**. Explain 3-axis, 4-axis, and 5-axis CNC machines with suitable applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A contouring CNC machine performs a **linear interpolation** from $P_1(0, 0, 0)$ to $P_2(60, 80, 30)$ mm at a programmed feed rate of $F = 1000$ mm/min. Calculate:
- (i) Total path length $L$
- (ii) Travel time $t$
- (iii) Velocity components $v_x, v_y, v_z$ in mm/min *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**3-Axis CNC Machine** *(2 marks)*:
- Has three linear axes: $X$, $Y$, $Z$.
- All three axes can move **simultaneously** under linear or circular interpolation.
- Suitable for **prismatic parts**, pockets, slots, 3D mold cavities.
- Limitation: cannot produce undercuts or complex sculptured surfaces in a single setup.

**4-Axis CNC Machine** *(2 marks)*:
- Adds one **rotary axis** (A axis — rotation about X, or B axis — rotation about Y) to the 3 linear axes.
- The rotary axis is often an **indexing table** (4th axis positioning) or a **continuous rotary table**.
- Applications: **Impeller machining, helical cutting, multi-sided machining** without re-fixturing.

**5-Axis CNC Machine** *(3 marks)*:
- Adds **two rotary axes** (typically A + B, or A + C) to the 3 linear axes.
- Allows the tool to approach the workpiece from **any direction** in a single setup.
- Types: **Trunnion-table** style and **Swivel-head** style.
- Applications: **Aerospace turbine blades (blisks), impellers, medical implants, complex die cavities, marine propellers**.
- Advantages: reduced setups, better surface finish (shorter tool, higher spindle speeds), improved accuracy.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Given data** *(1 mark)*:
- Start point: $P_1(0, 0, 0)$
- End point: $P_2(60, 80, 30)$ mm
- Feed rate: $F = 1000$ mm/min

**Step 2 — Calculate total path length $L$** *(2 marks)*:

$$
L = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}
$$

$$
L = \sqrt{(60-0)^2 + (80-0)^2 + (30-0)^2}
$$

$$
L = \sqrt{3600 + 6400 + 900} = \sqrt{10900}
$$

$$
L \approx 104.403 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate travel time $t$** *(2 marks)*:

$$
t = \frac{L}{F} = \frac{104.403}{1000} = 0.1044 \text{ min} \approx 6.265 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate velocity components** *(2 marks)*:

$$
v_x = \frac{F \cdot (x_2-x_1)}{L} = \frac{1000 \times 60}{104.403} \approx 574.66 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
v_y = \frac{F \cdot (y_2-y_1)}{L} = \frac{1000 \times 80}{104.403} \approx 766.21 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
v_z = \frac{F \cdot (z_2-z_1)}{L} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{104.403} \approx 287.33 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Verification of resultant feed** *(Bonus / sanity check)*:

$$
\sqrt{574.66^2 + 766.21^2 + 287.33^2} \approx 1000 \text{ mm/min} \ \checkmark
$$

The vector sum of the three axis velocities equals the commanded feed rate, confirming correct interpolator operation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse semi-closed loop with closed loop** in diagrams — the feedback arrow location is the *defining* feature. Many students lose 1–2 marks by drawing the feedback from the slide in a "semi-closed loop" diagram. The **rotary encoder must be on the motor** in semi-closed loop.
> - In linear interpolation numericals, **do not forget to verify** $\sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2} = F$ at the end. KTU examiners often give a **bonus mark** for the verification step.
> - For 5-axis classification, students often forget to mention **two simultaneous rotary axes** — just having "a rotary table" is not enough; the simultaneous interpolation is the key.
> - In Part A, avoid writing vague one-liners. Use the **3-mark structure**: Definition (1 mark) + Example (1 mark) + Distinguishing feature (1 mark).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- CNC classification has **five parallel dimensions**: Motion Control, Loop Control, Number of Axes, Drive System, and Application.
- **Motion control** has three tiers — **PTP < Straight-line < Contouring**, with complexity and cost increasing in that order.
- **Contouring systems** are the most capable and the most expensive; they are required for any curved or sculptured surface.
- **Open loop** = stepper, no feedback, cheap but inaccurate. **Semi-closed** = encoder on motor (most common). **Closed loop** = linear scale on slide (most accurate).
- The fundamental **limitation of semi-closed loop** is that it cannot detect **mechanical errors** (backlash, ballscrew pitch error) between the motor and the slide.
- **Axis classification** ranges from 2 (lathe) to 5 (aerospace). Each additional axis **multiplies machine cost by ~1.5–2×**.
- The **5-axis** machine is defined by the presence of **3 linear + 2 rotary axes with simultaneous interpolation**.
- Linear interpolation velocity components are computed using the **parametric projection** $v_i = F \cdot \Delta x_i / L$.
- For KTU exams, always draw **block diagrams** in loop-control questions — they carry **dedicated 1–2 marks**.
- Memorize the **encoder-linear-resolution formula** $r = p/N$ — it is a high-yield numerical.
- The **vector sum check** $\sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2} = F$ is a guaranteed bonus mark in numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Point-to-Point and Contouring Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Point-to-Point and Contouring Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Numerical Control (NC) / Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** systems, the classification of machine tool motion is fundamentally divided into two categories based on the **trajectory** that the cutting tool (or workpiece) traces between successive programmed positions.

**Point-to-Point (PTP) System** (also called *Positioning System*):
> A motion control system in which the primary objective is to move the tool from one programmed point to another with **no requirement for controlled path** between the start and end coordinates. The path taken is irrelevant; only the **final position** and the **feed rate** along the path matter.

**Contouring System** (also called *Continuous Path System*):
> A motion control system in which the tool is required to traverse a **continuously controlled path** (straight line, circular arc, or complex curve) between programmed points. The path itself is the **product feature** (e.g., a contoured mold cavity or a turbine blade profile), so motion along all axes must be **coordinated in real time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A 2-axis simultaneous contouring system is the *minimum* requirement for true contouring. Pure PTP machines typically operate with **independent single-axis drives** (one moves at a time) or rapid positioning, while contouring machines need **continuous interpolation** across multiple axes.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogies

| System | Real-World Analogy | What it Means |
|---|---|---|
| **Point-to-Point** | A **dot-matrix printer** – the print head jumps from letter to letter, but the path between letters doesn't matter. | The drill on a drilling machine punching holes at (10,20), (45,60), (90,30) – the drill goes "up, over, down" for each hole. |
| **Contouring** | A **calligraphy pen drawing the letter "S"** – the entire curve of the S is the product. | The milling cutter cutting a circular pocket or a sculpted aerodynamic surface – the curve between points IS the part. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** The physical machine is identical (a CNC mill is *capable* of both). What changes is the **control algorithm** (G-code commands like `G00` rapid vs. `G01` linear interpolation vs. `G02/G03` circular interpolation) and the **drive coordination logic**.

### 1.3 Sub-Categories (KTU Mandatory Distinction)

**PTP is further divided into:**
- **Type I (Incremental PTP):** Only one axis moves at a time (sequential); the next axis starts only after the previous reaches the target.
- **Type II (Concurrent PTP):** All axes begin moving simultaneously but each axis moves at its **maximum (rapid) rate**; therefore one axis may finish before another. Path is still uncontrolled.

**Contouring is sub-divided by axis count:**
- **2-axis contouring:** X–Y (flat profile milling, PCB routing).
- **2.5-axis contouring:** X–Y with one of the other axes moving discretely (e.g., drilling multiple depths).
- **3-axis contouring:** X–Y–Z simultaneous (3D mold machining).
- **4/5-axis contouring:** Includes rotational axes A, B, or C (turbine blades, impellers).

---

**Standards & Typical Metrics in KTU Questions:**

- **Positional Accuracy:** typically **± 0.005 mm to ± 0.025 mm** for modern CNC.
- **Repeatability:** usually **± 0.0025 mm** for precision contouring.
- **Resolution:** **0.001 mm** (1 µm) for ball-screw driven axes.
- **Least Count / BLU (Basic Length Unit):** the smallest programmable increment (often **0.001 mm**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each System

**Why PTP Exists (Engineering Logic):**
Operations like drilling, tapping, reaming, and boring only require the tool to be **located accurately at a coordinate** – the cutting action happens in the **Z-axis plunge** at a fixed X–Y location. Spending control complexity on the in-between path is wasteful. Hence PTP systems use simpler controllers, often with **open-loop stepping motors** for low-cost applications (PCB drilling, small benchtop drills).

**Why Contouring Exists (Engineering Logic):**
Milling external profiles, engraving, gear cutting, and mold-making require the **tool center to follow a precise curve**. Any deviation in path becomes a **dimensional/geometric error** on the part. Hence contouring requires:
1. **Closed-loop servo control** on every axis.
2. **Interpolator hardware/software** (linear, circular, or spline).
3. **Coordinated velocity commands** (the heart of CNC).

### 2.2 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step

**Point-to-Point Operational Flow:**
1. Controller reads next target coordinate (X, Y) from part program.
2. For Type I: Axis X is commanded to move to X-target; axis Y is commanded to move to Y-target *only after* X finishes (one-at-a-time).
3. For Type II: Both axes are commanded simultaneously; each axis accelerates at its **maximum rate**, so the path is a non-linear "staircase" but reaches target **fastest possible time**.
4. On arrival, **position feedback** (encoder or linear scale) confirms arrival within tolerance window.
5. Spindle is activated → drilling/boring cycle begins.
6. Tool retracts → next coordinate loaded.

**Contouring Operational Flow:**
1. Interpolator receives start point, end point, feed rate F, and path type (line, arc, spline).
2. Interpolator breaks the path into a sequence of **Basic Length Units (BLUs)** along the path length.
3. For each BLU increment (ΔL = 1 BLU), the interpolator computes the **corresponding increments in each axis**:
   - For a straight line: $\Delta X = \Delta L \cdot \cos\theta$, $\Delta Y = \Delta L \cdot \sin\theta$.
   - For a circular arc: $\Delta X$ and $\Delta Y$ depend on the parametric angle $\phi(t)$.
4. Commands are sent to each axis's servo drive **simultaneously and continuously**.
5. The result is a **smooth, controlled trajectory** that exactly matches the programmed geometry.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **every** formula the KTU board has historically asked on this topic. Memorize these.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tangential Feed Rate (combined) | $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$ | $F_x, F_y$ = axis feed components | mm/min |
| 2 | Axis Feed Component (linear path) | $F_x = F \cos\theta,\;\; F_y = F \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle of path w.r.t. X-axis | degrees/rad |
| 3 | Time to traverse one BLU | $t_{BLU} = \dfrac{BLU}{F}$ | BLU = Basic Length Unit (least count) | min |
| 4 | Axis velocity component (circular) | $V_x = -\omega R \sin\phi,\;\; V_y = \omega R \cos\phi$ | $\omega$ = angular vel., $R$ = radius | mm/s, rad/s |
| 5 | Resultant feed in circular | $F = \omega R$ | tangential feed | mm/min |
| 6 | Positioning time (Type I PTP) | $T_{PTP} = t_x + t_y + t_z$ | sum of individual axis traverse times | s |
| 7 | Positioning time (Type II PTP) | $T_{PTP} = \max(t_x, t_y, t_z)$ | longest axis dominates (parallel motion) | s |
| 8 | Straight-line path distance | $L = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ | straight-line distance | mm |
| 9 | Circular arc length | $L_{arc} = R \cdot \theta_{arc}$ | $\theta_{arc}$ in radians | mm |
| 10 | Number of BLUs per move | $N = \dfrac{L}{BLU}$ | total interpolation steps | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Questions 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 appear most often. Circular interpolation (Q4, Q5, Q9) is **favourite** for 14-mark problems.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PTP applications:** PCB drilling, shoe-sole punching, automatic riveting, EDM hole-popping, coordinate-measuring machines (CMMs) for **inspection** (not cutting).
- **Contouring applications:** Die/mold making, aerospace structural parts, turbine blades, orthopedic implants, gear tooth profiles, sculpture/relief engraving, woodworking CNC routers.
- **Economic impact:** The shift from PTP-only machining (~1960s) to multi-axis contouring (~1980s onwards) enabled the modern aerospace, automotive, and medical-implant industries, where **free-form surfaces** are mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Combined Feed Rate from Axis Components

**Problem Statement:** A 2-axis contouring system has axis feed rates $F_x$ and $F_y$. Derive the resultant feed rate $F$ along the path.

**Step 1 — Velocity Vector Setup**

Each axis moves with its own velocity. The total velocity of the tool tip is the **vector sum**:

$$
\vec{V} = V_x \hat{i} + V_y \hat{j}
$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of Resultant**

The magnitude (which equals the feed rate) is given by the Pythagorean theorem:

$$
F = \vert \vec{V} \vert = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Relating to Path Angle**

For a straight-line path at angle $\theta$ to the X-axis, the components are:

$$
V_x = V \cos\theta, \quad V_y = V \sin\theta
$$

Substituting:

$$
F = \sqrt{(F\cos\theta)^2 + (F\sin\theta)^2} = F\sqrt{\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta} = F \cdot 1
$$

This confirms the **identity**, validating that the magnitude decomposition is consistent.

> **Engineering insight:** The inverse problem (given F and θ, find axis components) is what the CNC interpolator computes **thousands of times per second**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Contouring a Circular Arc (Parametric Form)

**Problem Statement:** A CNC mill must cut a circular arc of radius $R$, swept by the tool tip at tangential feed $F$. Derive the axis velocities $V_x$ and $V_y$ at angular position $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Parametric Position Equations**

The tool position at angle $\phi$ measured from +X axis is:

$$
x(\phi) = R \cos\phi
$$

$$
y(\phi) = R \sin\phi
$$

**Step 2 — Time-Derivative for Velocity**

Differentiate with respect to time $t$ (using chain rule with $\phi = \phi(t)$):

$$
V_x = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = -R \sin\phi \cdot \dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = -R \omega \sin\phi
$$

$$
V_y = \dfrac{dy}{dt} = R \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = R \omega \cos\phi
$$

where $\omega = d\phi/dt$ is the angular velocity (rad/s).

**Step 3 — Relate to Tangential Feed**

The tangential speed along the arc is:

$$
F = R \omega
$$

This follows from the arc-length differential: $ds = R\, d\phi \Rightarrow ds/dt = R\, d\phi/dt = R\omega$.

**Step 4 — Verification of Velocity Magnitude**

$$
\sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2} = \sqrt{R^2\omega^2\sin^2\phi + R^2\omega^2\cos^2\phi} = R\omega = F \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Exam-relevant conclusion:** At any point on a circular contour, the magnitude of the resultant axis-velocity vector is **constant** (equal to F), but its **direction rotates** with the tool.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Time Savings of Type II PTP over Type I PTP

**Problem Statement:** A drill must move from (0,0) to (X=80 mm, Y=60 mm). Rapid traverse rate is $R_a = 1000$ mm/min for both axes. Compare Type I (sequential) vs. Type II (simultaneous) positioning time.

**Step 1 — Distances**

$$
d_x = 80 \text{ mm}, \quad d_y = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Individual Axis Times (at full rapid)**

$$
t_x = \dfrac{d_x}{R_a} = \dfrac{80}{1000} = 0.080 \text{ min}
$$

$$
t_y = \dfrac{d_y}{R_a} = \dfrac{60}{1000} = 0.060 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Type I Time (Sequential)**

$$
T_I = t_x + t_y = 0.080 + 0.060 = 0.140 \text{ min} = 8.4 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Type II Time (Simultaneous)**

$$
T_{II} = \max(t_x, t_y) = \max(0.080, 0.060) = 0.080 \text{ min} = 4.8 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Time Saving**

$$
\Delta T = T_I - T_{II} = 0.140 - 0.080 = 0.060 \text{ min} = 3.6 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_I} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.060}{0.140} \times 100 \approx 42.86\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** Type II (concurrent) PTP is significantly faster, but **neither achieves Type I/II's controlled contour**. The actual path traced in Type II is uncontrolled and could collide with fixturing — so it is only safe in **rapid positioning above the workpiece** (G00 in G-code).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked)

| Parameter | PTP (Type I) | PTP (Type II) | Contouring (2-axis) | Contouring (3-axis) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Path control | None | None (open) | Full (line/arc) | Full (free-form) |
| Axis coordination | One at a time | Parallel, independent rates | Real-time coordinated | Real-time + Z coupling |
| Typical controller | Stepper motor (open loop) | Stepper / DC servo | DC/AC servo + interpolator | Multi-axis CNC |
| Accuracy requirement | Low (position only) | Low–Medium | High | Very High |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | High |
| Example machine | Bench drill, EDM hole | Small CNC drill | 2-axis lathe, router | 3-axis VMC, mold CNC |
| Program command | G00 (rapid) | G00 (rapid) | G01/G02/G03 | G01/G02/G03 + Z moves |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Part Program G-code] --> B{Controller Mode}
    B -->|G00 Rapid| C[PTP System]
    B -->|G01 Linear| D[Contouring Linear]
    B -->|G02 G03 Circular| E[Contouring Circular]
    B -->|G06 Spline| F[Contouring Spline]

    C --> C1[Single Axis Servo]
    C --> C2[Parallel Axis Drive]

    D --> D1[Linear Interpolator]
    D --> D2[Velocity Vector Generator]

    F --> F2[NURBS Engine]

    C1 --> X1[Tool Jumps to Point]
    D1 --> X2[Tool Cuts Straight Line]
    E1 --> X3[Tool Cuts Arc Path]
    F1 --> X4[Tool Cuts Free Form Curve]

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Contouring CNC

    R --> I[Interpolator Block]

    A2 --> A3[Move Y to 60]

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading:** In **Type I**, you see an "L-shape" path. In **Type II**, you see a **staircase** (uncontrolled). In **Contouring**, you see a **clean straight line** (or perfect arc). This is the *single most important* diagram for visualizing the difference in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Distinguish clearly between Point-to-Point (PTP) and Contouring systems. Give two examples of machines for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Contouring |
|---|---|---|
| **Path control** | No control over the path between start and end points | Continuous, accurate control of the path |
| **Objective** | Achieve correct *position* | Achieve correct *geometry/curve* |
| **Axis motion** | One axis at a time (Type I) or parallel at max rate (Type II) | All axes move simultaneously with coordinated velocities |
| **Control complexity** | Simple (often open-loop stepper) | Complex (closed-loop servo with interpolator) |

**Examples:**
- **PTP machines:** Drilling machine, EDM, PCB drill, punching machine.
- **Contouring machines:** 2-axis lathe (turning), CNC milling machine (profiling), engraving machine.

> **Award 1 mark each** for definition, distinction table, and examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is an interpolator? Why is it essential in a contouring system but unnecessary in a pure PTP system?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **interpolator** is a sub-system of the CNC controller that converts a programmed path (defined by start point, end point, and path type such as line, arc, or spline) into a stream of small incremental commands (BLUs) sent to each axis's servo drive in real time.

It is **essential in contouring** because the tool must follow a *specific* trajectory; the interpolator computes, for every BLU, the correct **Δx** and **Δy** increments so that the tool tip traces the desired curve to within one BLU of accuracy.

It is **unnecessary in pure PTP** because the tool only needs to *reach* a coordinate; any uncontrolled path is acceptable, so the controller can simply command each axis to its target without calculating intermediate points.

> **Award 1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for contouring justification, **1 mark** for PTP justification.

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q.A (a)** Explain the working principle of a **2-axis linear interpolation** system with a neat block diagram. A CNC milling machine is programmed to cut a straight line from point P1 (10, 20) to point P2 (70, 50) at a feed rate of **100 mm/min**. If the BLU = 0.01 mm, determine:
   (i) The path length L
   (ii) The angle θ of the path
   (iii) The axis feed components $F_x$ and $F_y$
   (iv) The total number of BLUs required

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Path length (1 mark):**
$$L = \sqrt{(70-10)^2 + (50-20)^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500}$$
$$L = 67.082 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Path angle (1 mark):**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{30}{60}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.5)$$
$$\theta = 26.565^\circ$$

**(iii) Axis feed components (3 marks):**
$$F_x = F \cos\theta = 100 \times \cos(26.565^\circ) = 100 \times 0.8944 = 89.44 \text{ mm/min}$$
$$F_y = F \sin\theta = 100 \times \sin(26.565^\circ) = 100 \times 0.4472 = 44.72 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Verification (1 mark):**
$$F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{89.44^2 + 44.72^2} = \sqrt{8000 + 2000} = \sqrt{10000} = 100 \text{ mm/min} \quad \checkmark$$

**(iv) Number of BLUs (1 mark):**
$$N = \dfrac{L}{BLU} = \dfrac{67.082}{0.01} = 6708 \text{ BLUs}$$

---

**Q.A (b)** A CNC vertical machining centre must drill four holes at coordinates (0,0), (50,30), (90,30), and (140,0) using **Type I PTP** positioning, then perform a circular contour from (140,0) back to (0,0) along a **semicircle of radius 70 mm** at 120 mm/min. The rapid traverse is 2000 mm/min.
   (i) Calculate total PTP positioning time (Type I).
   (ii) Calculate circular contouring time and total axis feed components at angle 30° from start.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Distances between hole positions:**
- Hole 1 → 2: $\sqrt{50^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{3400} = 58.31$ mm
- Hole 2 → 3: $\sqrt{40^2 + 0^2} = 40$ mm
- Hole 3 → 4: $\sqrt{50^2 + 30^2} = 58.31$ mm
- Hole 4 → 1 (linear, NOT contouring for this segment of question): $\sqrt{140^2 + 0^2} = 140$ mm

**(i) Type I PTP Total Time (3 marks):**

For Type I PTP, axis X and Y move sequentially. Time = sum of individual axis times at rapid rate $R_a = 2000$ mm/min.

- **Move 1 (0,0)→(50,30):** $t_{x1}=50/2000=0.025$ min, $t_{y1}=30/2000=0.015$ min → Sum = 0.040 min
- **Move 2 (50,30)→(90,30):** $t_{x2}=40/2000=0.020$ min, $t_{y2}=0/2000=0$ min → Sum = 0.020 min
- **Move 3 (90,30)→(140,0):** $t_{x3}=50/2000=0.025$ min, $t_{y3}=30/2000=0.015$ min → Sum = 0.040 min
- **Move 4 (140,0)→(0,0):** $t_{x4}=140/2000=0.070$ min, $t_{y4}=0$ min → Sum = 0.070 min

$$T_{PTP,\text{Total}} = 0.040 + 0.020 + 0.040 + 0.070 = 0.170 \text{ min} = 10.2 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Circular Contouring Time and Components (4 marks):**

Semicircle arc length: $L_{arc} = \pi R = \pi \times 70 = 219.91$ mm

$$\omega = \dfrac{F}{R} = \dfrac{120}{70} = 1.7143 \text{ rad/min} = 0.02857 \text{ rad/s}$$

Contouring time:
$$T_{arc} = \dfrac{L_{arc}}{F} = \dfrac{219.91}{120} = 1.833 \text{ min} = 110 \text{ s}$$

At angle $\phi = 30° = \pi/6$ rad from the start of the arc (assuming the arc starts at the +X axis):

$$V_x = -R\omega \sin\phi = -70 \times 1.7143 \times \sin(30^\circ) = -70 \times 1.7143 \times 0.5 = -60.0 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$V_y = R\omega \cos\phi = 70 \times 1.7143 \times \cos(30^\circ) = 70 \times 1.7143 \times 0.866 = 103.92 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Verification:** $\sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 103.92^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 10800} = \sqrt{14400} = 120$ mm/min ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**Q.B (a)** With a clear block diagram, explain the **working of a closed-loop CNC contouring system**. List the major sub-systems and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks) — describe in words since drawing is textual:**

```
[Part Program] → [Data Input / Tape Reader] → [Decoder] → [Interpolator]
   → [Velocity Command Generator] → [Servo Amplifier] → [Servo Motor]
   → [Ball Screw] → [Machine Slide / Spindle / Tool]
                                         ↑                    ↓
                                  [Encoder] ←[Comparator] ←[Feedback]
```

**Sub-systems and functions (4 marks — 1 mark each for four key blocks):**

1. **Data Input Device / Reader:** Reads the part program from punched tape, USB, or network. Converts physical/Digital data into machine-readable electrical signals.
2. **Decoder & Interpolator:** Decodes G/M-codes, identifies the type of motion (rapid, linear, circular), and computes intermediate points (BLU-level) along the desired path.
3. **Servo Drive (Amplifier + Motor + Ball Screw):** Converts low-power command signals into high-power mechanical motion of the slide; the ball screw converts rotary motion to linear motion of the table.
4. **Feedback Element (Encoder / Linear Scale) + Comparator:** Continuously measures the *actual* position of the slide, compares it to the *commanded* position, and generates an **error signal**. The error drives the servo to reduce the discrepancy — this is what makes the system **closed-loop** and self-correcting.
5. **Controller / MCU:** Supervises sequencing, tool changes, spindle on/off, and coolant control.

> The **key difference from PTP** is the **interpolator + closed-loop feedback on every axis** operating in real time.

---

**Q.B (b)** A CNC lathe must turn a **circular arc profile** of radius 80 mm on a workpiece. The programmed feed rate is **200 mm/min** along the arc. Calculate:
   (i) The angular velocity $\omega$ of the tool along the arc.
   (ii) The components of feed rate $F_x$ and $F_y$ at an angular position $\phi = 45°$ from the +X axis.
   (iii) The time to cut a 120° arc segment.
   (iv) The number of BLUs if BLU = 0.005 mm.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Angular velocity (1 mark):**

$$F = R\omega \Rightarrow \omega = \dfrac{F}{R} = \dfrac{200}{80} = 2.5 \text{ rad/min} = 0.04167 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Feed components at φ = 45° (3 marks):**

$$F_x = -R\omega \sin\phi = -80 \times 2.5 \times \sin(45^\circ) = -80 \times 2.5 \times 0.7071 = -141.42 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$F_y = R\omega \cos\phi = 80 \times 2.5 \times \cos(45^\circ) = 80 \times 2.5 \times 0.7071 = 141.42 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Verification (1 mark within above):** $\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{141.42^2 + 141.42^2} = 141.42 \times \sqrt{2} \approx 200$ mm/min ✓

**(iii) Time for 120° arc (1 mark):**

$$\theta_{arc} = 120° = \dfrac{2\pi}{3} \text{ rad} = 2.0944 \text{ rad}$$

$$L_{arc} = R\theta_{arc} = 80 \times 2.0944 = 167.55 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_{arc} = \dfrac{L_{arc}}{F} = \dfrac{167.55}{200} = 0.8378 \text{ min} = 50.27 \text{ s}$$

**(iv) Number of BLUs (1 mark):**

$$N = \dfrac{L_{arc}}{BLU} = \dfrac{167.55}{0.005} = 33{,}510 \text{ BLUs}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Reasons:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing mm/min and mm/s without conversion (ω was 2.5 rad/min in part (i); converting to rad/s requires dividing by 60).
> 2. **Sign error:** Forgetting the **negative sign** in $F_x = -R\omega \sin\phi$ for counter-clockwise arcs (most students lose 1 full mark here).
> 3. **Angle in degrees vs. radians:** $\sin(45°)\neq \sin(45 \text{ rad})$. The arc-length formula $L = R\theta$ requires **radians**.
> 4. **Confusing PTP Type I with Type II time formulas:** $T_I = t_x + t_y$ but $T_{II} = \max(t_x, t_y)$ — using the wrong one in a comparison question loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** ($\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = F$): Examiners award a "consistency" mark if you verify — never skip it.
> 6. **Forgetting the interpolator in block diagrams:** This is the single most-skipped block in contouring block diagrams.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

| # | Key Take-Away |
|---|---|
| ✅ | **PTP** = correct **position**; **Contouring** = correct **path**. |
| ✅ | PTP Type I = axes move **sequentially** (one at a time). |
| ✅ | PTP Type II = axes move **simultaneously** at max rate, but path is **uncontrolled**. |
| ✅ | Contouring requires **interpolation** (linear, circular, or spline). |
| ✅ | Combined feed formula: $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$. |
| ✅ | Linear path: $F_x = F\cos\theta$, $F_y = F\sin\theta$. |
| ✅ | Circular path: $F_x = -R\omega\sin\phi$, $F_y = R\omega\cos\phi$, and $F = R\omega$. |
| ✅ | BLU = smallest programmable increment; $N = L/\text{BLU}$. |
| ✅ | Time to traverse 1 BLU: $t_{BLU} = \text{BLU}/F$. |
| ✅ | Arc length: $L_{arc} = R\theta$ (θ in **radians**). |
| ✅ | Straight-line length: $L = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$. |
| ✅ | PTP Type I time = $\sum t_{axis}$; Type II time = $\max t_{axis}$. |
| ✅ | 2-axis contouring = minimum for true contouring; 2.5-axis = 2 contouring + 1 discrete. |
| ✅ | Closed-loop feedback (encoder) is **mandatory** for high-accuracy contouring. |
| ✅ | PTP example machines: drill, EDM, punch. Contouring example: CNC mill, lathe, router. |
| ✅ | G-code: G00 = PTP rapid; G01 = linear contour; G02/G03 = circular contour. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** When you see a question, **first identify** whether it is **PTP** (path doesn't matter, just position) or **Contouring** (path matters). Then decide if it is **linear** (use $\cos\theta$, $\sin\theta$) or **circular** (use $R\omega\sin\phi$, $R\omega\cos\phi$). This single decision will guide every subsequent calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incremental and Absolute Programming Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Incremental and Absolute Programming Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In CNC (Computer Numerical Control) part programming, every tool movement instruction must be expressed as a set of numerical coordinates. The manner in which these coordinates are interpreted by the machine controller defines the **programming method**. The two canonical methods prescribed by ISO 6983 and adopted by KTU 2024 scheme are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Programming (G90):** A coordinate system in which every tool position (target point) is specified with respect to a **fixed datum / origin (zero point)** of the workpiece. The numerical values of X, Y, Z (and auxiliary axes) directly represent the tool position measured from this origin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incremental Programming (G91):** A coordinate system in which every tool position is specified as a **signed distance (delta) from the immediately preceding tool position**. Each successive block expresses only the *change* in position rather than the absolute location.

The active programming mode is governed by preparatory (G) codes. The modal G-code **G90** activates absolute programming, while **G91** activates incremental programming. Once set, the mode remains active until another G-code of the same group is encountered.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are giving directions to a friend standing at the **front gate of a college campus**.

- **Absolute Programming (Address-style directions):** You tell your friend, *"Go to the Mechanical Block — it is exactly 120 metres East and 80 metres North of the main gate."* Every instruction is referenced to a **fixed landmark (the gate)**. If your friend forgets the previous instruction, they can still locate any new point using just the gate as reference.

- **Incremental Programming (Step-by-step directions):** You tell your friend, *"From where you are, walk 30 metres forward, then turn and walk 20 metres right, then 15 metres back..."* Every instruction is a **displacement from the previous spot**. The friend must remember each step; losing any one step means losing the path.

In CNC terms, the **gate is the workpiece origin (X0, Y0, Z0)**, and your friend is the **cutting tool**. Both methods reach the same destination, but the numerical bookkeeping is fundamentally different.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric in KTU 2024 Syllabus:** All coordinate values in NC code are assumed to be in **millimetres (mm)** for linear axes and **degrees (°)** for rotary axes unless the active G-code (G20/G21) specifies otherwise. Modal G-code **G21 (Metric)** is the KTU board-exam default.

## 1.3 Why Two Methods Exist — The Engineering Rationale

| Aspect | Absolute (G90) | Incremental (G91) |
|---|---|---|
| Reference Datum | Fixed workpiece origin | Previous tool position |
| Error Propagation | Does not propagate between blocks | **Compounds** with each block |
| Code Readability | Easier to verify against engineering drawing | Requires running tabulation |
| Typical Use | Complex contours, subprograms, mirror imaging | Repetitive patterns, equal divisions |
| Datum Shift Handling | Coordinates change directly | Delta values remain unchanged if pattern repeats |

Both methods are mathematically equivalent; the controller simply performs an internal integration (for incremental) or direct assignment (for absolute). The choice is a **programmer's convenience and risk-management decision**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Mathematics of Tool Position

Let the workpiece origin be denoted as $\mathbf{O} = (X_0, Y_0, Z_0)$. For a sequence of $n$ successive tool positions, define:

- $\mathbf{P}_i = (X_i, Y_i, Z_i)$ → the **absolute coordinates** of the $i^{th}$ point.
- $\mathbf{D}_i = (\Delta X_i, \Delta Y_i, \Delta Z_i)$ → the **incremental displacement** required to move from $\mathbf{P}_{i-1}$ to $\mathbf{P}_i$.

### 2.1.1 Absolute Mode (G90) — Direct Coordinate Specification

In absolute mode, the programmed word $X$ in any block directly equals the absolute coordinate of the target point:

$$
X_{\text{programmed}} = X_i, \quad Y_{\text{programmed}} = Y_i, \quad Z_{\text{programmed}} = Z_i
$$

The CNC controller interprets each block as: *"Move the tool such that its position equals the programmed value, measured from origin O."*

### 2.1.2 Incremental Mode (G91) — Displacement Vector Specification

In incremental mode, the programmed word represents the **change** in coordinate from the previous block:

$$
\Delta X_i = X_i - X_{i-1}, \quad \Delta Y_i = Y_i - Y_{i-1}, \quad \Delta Z_i = Z_i - Z_{i-1}
$$

The controller performs an internal cumulative update of the form:

$$
\mathbf{P}_i = \mathbf{P}_{i-1} + \mathbf{D}_i
$$

## 2.2 Conversion Logic — From Absolute to Incremental and Vice-Versa

Given any two successive absolute points $\mathbf{P}_{i-1}$ and $\mathbf{P}_i$, the incremental displacements are obtained by **simple subtraction** along each axis:

$$
\Delta X_i = X_i - X_{i-1}
$$

$$
\Delta Y_i = Y_i - Y_{i-1}
$$

$$
\Delta Z_i = Z_i - Z_{i-1}
$$

Conversely, to reconstruct the absolute position from a sequence of incremental displacements, we apply **cumulative summation**:

$$
X_i = X_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{i} \Delta X_k
$$

$$
Y_i = Y_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{i} \Delta Y_k
$$

$$
Z_i = Z_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{i} \Delta Z_k
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Board Convention):** A **positive sign** denotes motion in the **+X, +Y, or +Z** direction (right, away, up — based on the right-hand rule). A **negative sign** denotes motion in the opposite sense. The sign must be explicitly written in incremental programming; absence of sign defaults to **positive** in most controllers, but KTU examiners require explicit signage for full marks.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important cheat sheet** for solving any coordinate-conversion problem in the KTU ESE. Memorize the sign conventions and the summation identity.

| Parameter | Absolute Mode (G90) | Incremental Mode (G91) |
|---|---|---|
| Programmed Word Meaning | $X = X_i$ (target position from origin) | $X = X_i - X_{i-1}$ (displacement from previous) |
| Forward Conversion (Abs → Inc) | Not applicable | $\Delta X_i = X_i - X_{i-1}$ |
| Inverse Conversion (Inc → Abs) | Not applicable | $X_i = X_{i-1} + \Delta X_i$ |
| Cumulative Position Formula | Direct read | $X_i = X_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{i} \Delta X_k$ |
| Sign Convention for +X motion | Positive value | Positive value with `+` prefix |
| Sign Convention for −X motion | Negative value | Negative value with `−` prefix |
| Typical Use Case | Irregular contours, drawing-based coding | Repetitive equal-spacing patterns (bolt circles, pocket milling) |
| Datum Shift (G92) Behaviour | Coordinates shift directly | Only the **starting origin** shifts; deltas unchanged |
| Subprogram Compatibility | Excellent (parametric via variables) | Limited (delta values are sequence-dependent) |
| Error Magnification | **None** — each block independent | **Linear growth** with $i$ due to summation |
| Standard Unit | mm (with G21 active) | mm (with G21 active) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace structural milling:** Absolute programming is preferred because the part drawing is dimensioned from a single datum (typically the tooling hole or clamping face), making verification straightforward.
- **Pocket milling with bolt-hole patterns:** Incremental programming excels because the **angular step** between holes is constant; one delta block can be repeated via subprogram call.
- **Lathe turning (G-code G90/G91 in Fanuc):** Incremental is commonly used for **chamfer, taper, and radius transitions** where the displacement from the current diameter is more intuitive than the absolute diameter.
- **Wire-EDM and laser profiling:** Absolute is dominant because the entire contour is dimensioned from the workpiece corner.
- **Robotic pick-and-place:** Incremental displacement vectors mirror the **kinematic joint-angle deltas** used in robot Jacobian calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — The Standard KTU Pattern

This is the **single most frequent question type** in KTU Module 1 university examinations. A contour of four points is given in absolute coordinates; the student is asked to convert the program into incremental form, and vice-versa.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A milling cutter must traverse the following four vertices sequentially to machine a closed pocket. The workpiece origin is at the lower-left corner. The absolute coordinates of the tool path (in mm) are:

| Point | X (mm) | Y (mm) | Z (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| P0 (Start) | 10 | 10 | 0 |
| P1 | 40 | 10 | 0 |
| P2 | 40 | 30 | 0 |
| P3 | 10 | 30 | 0 |
| P4 (Return) | 10 | 10 | 0 |

> **Required:** 
> (a) Write the NC blocks in **Absolute mode (G90)**.
> (b) Convert the same tool path into **Incremental mode (G91)** block-by-block, showing the delta calculation explicitly.

---

### 3.1.2 Part (a) — Absolute Program (Direct)

In absolute mode, the programmed words are simply the coordinates of each point. The resulting NC program segment is:

```
N10  G90 G21 G17        ; Absolute mode, metric, XY plane
N20  G00 X10  Y10  Z0   ; Rapid to P0
N30  G01 X40  Y10  Z0   ; Linear cut to P1
N40  G01 X40  Y30  Z0   ; Linear cut to P2
N50  G01 X10  Y30  Z0   ; Linear cut to P3
N60  G01 X10  Y10  Z0   ; Linear cut to P4 (closure)
```

**Verification logic for each block:**
- N20: $X = 10$ (read directly from P0 row, column X). $Y = 10$ (read directly).
- N30: $X = 40$, $Y = 10$. No computation required; values are transcribed from the table.

---

### 3.1.3 Part (b) — Incremental Program (Derived)

In incremental mode, every block after the first expresses the **displacement** from the *immediately preceding* point. The derivation is now shown step-by-step for each transition.

**Step 1 — From P0 to P1:**

$$
\Delta X_1 = X_1 - X_0 = 40 - 10 = +30 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta Y_1 = Y_1 - Y_0 = 10 - 10 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta Z_1 = Z_1 - Z_0 = 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — From P1 to P2:**

$$
\Delta X_2 = X_2 - X_1 = 40 - 40 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta Y_2 = Y_2 - Y_1 = 30 - 10 = +20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta Z_2 = Z_2 - Z_1 = 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — From P2 to P3:**

$$
\Delta X_3 = X_3 - X_2 = 10 - 40 = -30 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta Y_3 = Y_3 - Y_2 = 30 - 30 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta Z_3 = Z_3 - Z_2 = 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — From P3 to P4:**

$$
\Delta X_4 = X_4 - X_3 = 10 - 10 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta Y_4 = Y_4 - Y_3 = 10 - 30 = -20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta Z_4 = Z_4 - Z_3 = 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Resulting Incremental Program (G91):**

```
N10  G91 G21 G17        ; Incremental mode activated
N20  G00 X+30  Y0   Z0  ; P0 → P1 (move +30 in X, 0 in Y)
N30  G01 X0    Y+20 Z0  ; P1 → P2 (move 0 in X, +20 in Y)
N40  G01 X-30  Y0   Z0  ; P2 → P3 (move −30 in X, 0 in Y)
N50  G01 X0    Y-20 Z0  ; P3 → P4 (move 0 in X, −20 in Y)
```

---

### 3.1.4 Reverse Verification — Incremental to Absolute (Closure Check)

To mathematically confirm correctness, we reconstruct the absolute positions from the deltas using the cumulative summation identity:

$$
X_i = X_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{i} \Delta X_k, \quad Y_i = Y_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{i} \Delta Y_k
$$

$$
X_1 = 10 + (+30) = 40 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
X_2 = 10 + (+30 + 0) = 40 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
X_3 = 10 + (+30 + 0 - 30) = 10 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
X_4 = 10 + (+30 + 0 - 30 + 0) = 10 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
Y_1 = 10 + (0) = 10 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
Y_2 = 10 + (0 + 20) = 30 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
Y_3 = 10 + (0 + 20 + 0) = 30 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
Y_4 = 10 + (0 + 20 + 0 - 20) = 10 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

The reconstructed coordinates **exactly match the input table**, confirming algebraic closure of the contour. This closure check is the gold-standard method for KTU full-mark scoring.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mixed Mode Block (KTU Favourite)

A subtle but high-value KTU question is the **mixed-mode block**, where a programmer starts in one mode, switches mid-program, and the student must track the coordinates correctly. Consider:

> *"Block N10 sets G90 and positions the tool at (20, 15). Block N20 switches to G91 and programs X30 Y-15. What is the tool position at the end of N20?"*

**Solution Trace:**

**Step 1:** In N10, G90 is active. The controller sets the **machine coordinate register** as:

$$
X_{\text{reg}} = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad Y_{\text{reg}} = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** In N20, the modal switch to G91 occurs. The controller now interprets X30 Y-15 as a **displacement** from the current register value:

$$
X_{\text{reg, new}} = X_{\text{reg, old}} + 30 = 20 + 30 = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
Y_{\text{reg, new}} = Y_{\text{reg, old}} + (-15) = 15 - 15 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Final tool position is $(50, 0)$ in absolute coordinates, regardless of the current G-code. **The machine position is mode-independent**; only the **interpretation** of the next programmed word changes.

$$
\boxed{\text{Tool Position} = (50 \text{ mm}, \ 0 \text{ mm})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The modal G-code (G90/G91) governs only **how the next block is interpreted**, not the internal tool position register. The internal position is always maintained in absolute coordinates inside the controller's memory.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Circular Bolt-Hole Pattern (Why Incremental Wins)

A circular flange has **6 equally spaced bolt holes** on a **bolt circle of diameter 100 mm** (radius 50 mm), starting at the 3-o'clock position. The holes lie in the XY plane.

**Step 1 — Angular Step:** Each hole is separated by:

$$
\theta_{\text{step}} = \frac{360°}{6} = 60°
$$

**Step 2 — Cartesian Coordinates of Each Hole (Absolute Form):**

For hole $k$ (indexed from 0 to 5), the position is:

$$
X_k = 50 \cos(60° \cdot k), \quad Y_k = 50 \sin(60° \cdot k)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0 &: (50, \ 0) \\
H_1 &: (25, \ 43.301) \\
H_2 &: (-25, \ 43.301) \\
H_3 &: (-50, \ 0) \\
H_4 &: (-25, \ -43.301) \\
H_5 &: (25, \ -43.301)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Incremental Displacement Between Holes:** The Euclidean distance between any two adjacent holes is:

$$
\Delta s = 2 R \sin\!\left(\frac{\theta_{\text{step}}}{2}\right) = 2 \times 50 \times \sin(30°) = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

However, the **per-axis incremental moves** vary because the chord is not axis-aligned. The actual deltas must be computed from the absolute coordinates:

$$
\Delta X_k = X_{k+1} - X_k, \quad \Delta Y_k = Y_{k+1} - Y_k
$$

For the first transition (H0 → H1):

$$
\Delta X_0 = 25 - 50 = -25 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta Y_0 = 43.301 - 0 = +43.301 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Even for repetitive patterns, the **per-axis deltas are not constant** (only the chord length and angular step are). Therefore, a subprogram with parametric polar-to-Cartesian conversion is typically used instead of pure incremental. However, for **purely linear repetitive patterns** (e.g., a row of equally spaced holes along the X-axis), incremental programming with a constant `X+10 Y0` block is dramatically more concise than absolute.

## 3.4 Summary Table — When to Use Which Mode

| Scenario | Recommended Mode | KTU Board Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Complex multi-contour part with irregular geometry | Absolute (G90) | Each coordinate is verifiable from drawing |
| Linear bolt-hole row, equal pitch | Incremental (G91) | One delta block suffices for all holes |
| Datum shift required (G92) mid-program | Absolute (G90) | Direct recomputation; no error compounding |
| Mirror image machining (G51.1) | Absolute (G90) | Sign reversal is per-coordinate, not per-delta |
| Subprogram with parametric variables | Absolute (G90) | Macro variables are absolute-friendly |
| Manual data input (MDI) on shop floor | Absolute (G90) | Operator can read and verify from drawing |
| Chamfer / taper on lathe | Incremental (G91) | Displacement from current diameter is natural |
| Polar coordinate mode (G15/G16) | Absolute (G90) | Radius and angle are referenced to origin |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — G90 vs G91 Interpretation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the CNC controller routes a programmed word (e.g., `X30`) through the active modal G-code logic to update the internal tool position register.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NC Block Input: X30] --> B{Active G-Code Group 3}
    B -->|G90 Detected| C[Interpretation: Absolute]
    B -->|G91 Detected| D[Interpretation: Incremental]
    C --> E[Set Register: Xreg = 30 mm]
    D --> F[Compute Delta: dX = 30 mm]
    F --> G[Update Register: Xreg_new = Xreg_old + 30]
    E --> H[Internal Machine Position Store]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Servo Drive Command]
    I --> J[Axis Motor Motion]
    H --> K[Display to Operator: Absolute X]
    K --> L[Verification on CNC Screen]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **NC Block Input** is the raw text word read from the part program.
- The **G-Code Group 3** is the modal interpreter; G90 and G91 are mutually exclusive members of this group (only one can be active at any time).
- The **Internal Machine Position Store** is always maintained in **absolute coordinates** internally — the modal code only affects **how new inputs are interpreted**, not the storage format.
- The **Display to Operator** always shows the **absolute** position, even when the program is running in G91 incremental mode.

## 4.2 Tool Path Trace — Absolute vs Incremental Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph abs["ABSOLUTE MODE (G90)"]
        O1[(Origin X0 Y0)] --> P1a[Block N20: X10 Y10]
        P1a --> P2a[Block N30: X40 Y10]
        P2a --> P3a[Block N40: X40 Y30]
        P3a --> P4a[Block N50: X10 Y30]
        P4a --> P5a[Block N60: X10 Y10]
    end
    subgraph inc["INCREMENTAL MODE (G91)"]
        O2[(Origin X0 Y0)] --> Q1b[Block N20: X+10 Y+10]
        Q1b --> Q2b[Block N30: X+30 Y0]
        Q2b --> Q3b[Block N40: X0 Y+20]
        Q3b --> Q4b[Block N50: X-30 Y0]
        Q4b --> Q5b[Block N60: X0 Y-20]
    end
    P5a -.Equals.-> Q5b
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **left subgraph (G90)** shows the tool moving in absolute jumps: each node's coordinates are measured from the origin O1.
- The **right subgraph (G91)** shows the tool moving in deltas: each node's coordinates represent the **displacement from the previous node**.
- The **dotted line** at the bottom shows that both modes terminate at the **same final absolute position** — they are mathematically equivalent paths.

## 4.3 Modal Group Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph grp3["Modal Group 03: Absolute / Incremental"]
        G90[G90 Absolute Mode]
        G91[G91 Incremental Mode]
    end
    subgraph grp6["Modal Group 06: Units"]
        G20[G20 Imperial inch]
        G21[G21 Metric mm]
    end
    subgraph grp17["Modal Group 17: Plane Selection"]
        G17[G17 XY Plane]
        G18[G18 XZ Plane]
        G19[G19 YZ Plane]
    end
    G90 --> NC1[NC Block Interpretation Logic]
    G91 --> NC1
    G20 --> NC1
    G21 --> NC1
    G17 --> NC1
    G18 --> NC1
    G19 --> NC1
    NC1 --> OUT[Servo Command Stream]
```

**Key Takeaway:** G90 and G91 belong to **Modal Group 03** as defined by ISO 6983. The active code in this group controls the interpretation of all subsequent X, Y, Z, A, B, C words in the part program. Other modal groups (Units, Plane Selection) operate independently and concurrently.

## 4.4 Error Propagation Behaviour Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer Inputs dX1, dX2, dX3] --> B[Incremental Controller]
    B --> C[Register: X1 = X0 + dX1]
    C --> D[Register: X2 = X1 + dX2]
    D --> E[Register: X3 = X2 + dX3]
    E --> F[Total Error = e1 + e2 + e3]
    F --> G[Cumulative Drift]

    A2[Programmer Inputs X1, X2, X3] --> B2[Absolute Controller]
    B2 --> C2[Register: X1 directly]
    C2 --> D2[Register: X2 directly]
    D2 --> E2[Register: X3 directly]
    E2 --> F2[Total Error = e3 only]
    F2 --> G2[No Drift Independent Block Errors]
```

**Engineering Implication:** In incremental programming, a small rounding error in $\Delta X_1$ propagates into $\Delta X_2$, then into $\Delta X_3$, accumulating linearly. In absolute programming, each block is independently written, so an error in one block does not affect the others. This is why **absolute programming is the KTU-preferred method for high-precision parts** (aerospace, medical implants).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

> *"Differentiate between absolute and incremental programming methods in CNC part programming. State the relevant G-codes."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Absolute Programming (G90) | Incremental Programming (G91) |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Coordinates measured from a **fixed workpiece origin** | Coordinates measured from **previous tool position** |
| Programmed word meaning | The **target position** directly | The **displacement** to be moved |
| Error handling | Each block is independent; errors do not propagate | Errors **accumulate** across blocks |
| Datum shift (G92) | Coordinates must be **recalculated** | Only the **starting origin** shifts; deltas remain valid |
| Typical application | Complex irregular contours | Repetitive equal-pitch patterns |

**(1 mark each for: definition with G-code, key difference in reference, error behaviour / application).**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

> *"A tool is at position (25, 40) in absolute coordinates. The next block is `G91 G01 X15 Y-20`. Determine the tool position at the end of this block."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $\mathbf{P}_{i-1} = (25, 40)$ and the active mode is **incremental (G91)**.

The controller interprets X15 and Y-20 as **displacements** from the current position:

$$
X_i = X_{i-1} + \Delta X = 25 + 15 = 40 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
Y_i = Y_{i-1} + \Delta Y = 40 + (-20) = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Final Tool Position} = (40 \text{ mm}, \ 20 \text{ mm})}
$$

**(1 mark for identifying the mode, 1 mark for the formula application, 1 mark for the final answer).**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Option A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Full 14-Mark Pattern]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** A milling cutter is required to machine a rectangular pocket with vertices at the following absolute coordinates (in mm):
> - P0: (15, 15)
> - P1: (65, 15)
> - P2: (65, 45)
> - P3: (15, 45)
> - P4: (15, 15) — return to start
>
> Write the complete NC program in:
> (i) Absolute mode (G90)
> (ii) Incremental mode (G91)
> Show all delta calculations explicitly.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A part program contains the following sequence:
> ```
> N10  G90 G00 X20  Y30
> N20  G01 X50  Y30
> N30  G91 G01 X20  Y-30
> N40  G01 X-10 Y20
> N50  G90 G00 X0   Y0
> ```
> For each block, determine the **absolute tool position** at the end of the block. State the active programming mode for each block.

---

#### Model Solution for B1(a) — Pocket Milling Program [7 Marks]

**(i) Absolute Program (G90):**

The coordinates of each vertex are transcribed directly into the NC blocks:

```
N10  G90 G21 G17              ; Absolute mode, metric, XY plane
N20  G00 X15  Y15             ; Rapid to P0
N30  G01 X65  Y15  F100       ; Linear cut P0 → P1
N40  G01 X65  Y45             ; Linear cut P1 → P2
N50  G01 X15  Y45             ; Linear cut P2 → P3
N60  G01 X15  Y15             ; Linear cut P3 → P4 (closure)
```

**(ii) Incremental Program (G91) — Step-by-Step Deltas:**

**P0 → P1:** $\Delta X = 65-15 = +50$, $\Delta Y = 15-15 = 0$
**P1 → P2:** $\Delta X = 65-65 = 0$, $\Delta Y = 45-15 = +30$
**P2 → P3:** $\Delta X = 15-65 = -50$, $\Delta Y = 45-45 = 0$
**P3 → P4:** $\Delta X = 15-15 = 0$, $\Delta Y = 15-45 = -30$

```
N10  G91 G21 G17              ; Incremental mode, metric, XY plane
N20  G00 X+50 Y0              ; P0 → P1
N30  G01 X0   Y+30 F100       ; P1 → P2
N40  G01 X-50 Y0              ; P2 → P3
N50  G01 X0   Y-30            ; P3 → P4
```

**(Mark split: 1 mark for delta formulas, 2 marks for arithmetic, 2 marks for G90 program, 2 marks for G91 program.)**

---

#### Model Solution for B1(b) — Mixed-Mode Position Trace [7 Marks]

We maintain a running register $(X_{\text{reg}}, Y_{\text{reg}})$ and the **active mode** after each block. The register is **always in absolute coordinates** internally.

| Block | Programmed | Active Mode | Calculation | Final Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| N10 | G90 G00 X20 Y30 | **G90** | Direct assignment: $X = 20$, $Y = 30$ | **(20, 30)** |
| N20 | G01 X50 Y30 | **G90** (modal) | Direct assignment | **(50, 30)** |
| N30 | G91 G01 X20 Y-30 | **G91** (switched) | $\Delta X = 20$, $\Delta Y = -30$ | **(70, 0)** |
| N40 | G01 X-10 Y20 | **G91** (modal) | $\Delta X = -10$, $\Delta Y = 20$ | **(60, 20)** |
| N50 | G90 G00 X0 Y0 | **G90** (switched) | Direct assignment | **(0, 0)** |

**Detailed arithmetic for N30 (mode switch block):**

$$
X_{\text{reg, new}} = X_{\text{reg, old}} + 20 = 50 + 20 = 70 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
Y_{\text{reg, new}} = Y_{\text{reg, old}} + (-30) = 30 - 30 = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Detailed arithmetic for N40:**

$$
X_{\text{reg, new}} = 70 + (-10) = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
Y_{\text{reg, new}} = 0 + 20 = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result table:**

| Block | Absolute Position (X, Y) mm |
|---|---|
| End of N10 | (20, 30) |
| End of N20 | (50, 30) |
| End of N30 | (70, 0) |
| End of N40 | (60, 20) |
| End of N50 | (0, 0) |

**(Mark split: 2 marks for state tracking, 3 marks for arithmetic, 2 marks for final table.)**

---

### Question B1 (Option B — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Alternative Pattern]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the difference between absolute and incremental coordinate systems in CNC milling. State the conditions under which each is preferred, with two real-world examples for each method.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A drilling operation requires 5 holes on a straight line, starting at (10, 20) and ending at (90, 20), with equal pitch. Write the program in:
> (i) Absolute mode (G90)
> (ii) Incremental mode (G91)
> Compute and tabulate all intermediate hole coordinates. Which program is shorter, and why?

---

#### Model Solution for B1(a) [7 Marks]

**Absolute Coordinate System:** In the absolute system, the origin (X0, Y0, Z0) is established at a fixed datum on the workpiece — typically the corner or centre. Every tool position is specified as a direct distance from this origin. The Cartesian position of point P is uniquely given as $(X_P, Y_P, Z_P)$. This corresponds to **drawing-based dimensioning**, where all dimensions on an engineering drawing originate from a single datum.

**Incremental Coordinate System:** In the incremental system, there is no single fixed reference. Every tool position is given as a displacement $(\Delta X, \Delta Y, \Delta Z)$ from the **immediately preceding** tool position. The "origin" effectively moves with the tool.

**Sketch description (textual):**

```
ABSOLUTE SYSTEM
Y axis
|
|  •P1 (40, 50)         ← All coords from fixed origin O
|  •P2 (30, 30)
|  •P3 (20, 10)
|  •_____________ X axis
       O (0,0)

INCREMENTAL SYSTEM
Y axis
|
|  P1 — (+40, +50) from O
|  P2 — (−10, −20) from P1
|  P3 — (−10, −20) from P2
|  •_____________ X axis
       O (0,0) starting reference
```

**When Absolute is Preferred:**
1. **Aerospace structural components** — complex contours with one master datum on the engineering drawing.
2. **Mould and die cavities** — coordinates dimensioned from the die centreline; verification is direct.

**When Incremental is Preferred:**
1. **Row of bolt holes with equal pitch** — one `X+10` block repeated.
2. **Lathe chamfer / taper turning** — the radial and axial *reductions* from the current diameter are more intuitive than the absolute diameter.

**(Mark split: 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for sketch / explanation, 1 mark for conditions, 2 marks for examples.)**

---

#### Model Solution for B1(b) — Drilling a Row of 5 Holes [7 Marks]

**Pitch Calculation:**

$$
\text{Number of intervals} = 5 - 1 = 4
$$

$$
\text{Pitch} = \frac{90 - 10}{4} = \frac{80}{4} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Hole Coordinates (Absolute):**

| Hole | X (mm) | Y (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| H1 | 10 | 20 |
| H2 | 30 | 20 |
| H3 | 50 | 20 |
| H4 | 70 | 20 |
| H5 | 90 | 20 |

**Program (i) — Absolute (G90):**

```
N10  G90 G21 G17
N20  G00 X10  Y20           ; Rapid to H1
N30  M03 S1200              ; Spindle on
N40  G81 X10  Y20 Z-5 R2    ; Drill H1
N50  G81 X30  Y20 Z-5 R2    ; Drill H2
N60  G81 X50  Y20 Z-5 R2    ; Drill H3
N70  G81 X70  Y20 Z-5 R2    ; Drill H4
N80  G81 X90  Y20 Z-5 R2    ; Drill H5
N90  G80                    ; Cancel drilling cycle
N100 M05                    ; Spindle off
```

**Program (ii) — Incremental (G91):**

The displacement from H1 to H2 is $\Delta X = +20$, $\Delta Y = 0$. The same delta applies for all successive holes.

```
N10  G91 G21 G17
N20  G00 X10  Y20           ; Rapid to H1 (absolute since first block)
N30  M03 S1200
N40  G81 X+20 Y0  Z-5 R2    ; Drill H2
N50  G81 X+20 Y0            ; Drill H3
N60  G81 X+20 Y0            ; Drill H4
N70  G81 X+20 Y0            ; Drill H5
N80  G80
N90  M05
```

**Compactness via Subprogram (G91 with M98 call):**

```
N10  G91 G21 G17
N20  G00 X10  Y20
N30  M03 S1200
N40  M98 P4000              ; Call subprogram O4000 four times
N50  G80
N60  M05
N70  M30
O4000                       ; Subprogram: drill one hole at +20
G81 X+20 Y0 Z-5 R2
M99
```

**Conclusion on Program Length:** The **incremental program is shorter and more elegant** for this repetitive pattern. Only one delta value (`X+20 Y0`) is required regardless of the number of holes, while the absolute program must specify the full X-coordinate of every hole. The advantage of incremental programming grows **linearly with the number of holes**.

**(Mark split: 2 marks for pitch calc, 2 marks for G90 program, 2 marks for G91 program, 1 mark for comparison.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Incremental/Absolute Programming Questions (KTU ESE):**
>
> 1. **Sign Omission (Loses 1–2 marks per block):** In incremental programming, students often write `X30` instead of `X+30` for a positive move. KTU board examiners require **explicit signage** for every displacement. Always write `+` and `−` explicitly.
>
> 2. **Modal Mode Persistence (Loses 1–2 marks):** Once G90 or G91 is set, it remains active until another G-code of the same group (Group 03) appears. Students often forget this and assume G90 is the default. In a problem where the mode is switched mid-program, track it block-by-block.
>
> 3. **Subtraction Direction Error (Loses 2–3 marks):** When computing $\Delta X_i = X_i - X_{i-1}$, students sometimes compute the reverse ($X_{i-1} - X_i$), flipping the sign of every delta. This compounds into a completely wrong tool path. **Always subtract the previous coordinate from the new target coordinate**, not vice versa.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Z-coordinate (Loses 1 mark):** In milling, the Z motion is also incremental/absolute. A common mistake is to set only X and Y in incremental blocks, assuming Z defaults to zero. Z must be **explicitly programmed** or it will use the modal Z value.
>
> 5. **Mixing Units Without G20/G21 (Loses 1–2 marks):** Writing `X25.4` (an inch-equivalent) in a metric-active program is a fatal error. Always confirm the unit system with **G21 (metric)** or **G20 (imperial)** at the start of the program.
>
> 6. **Datum Shift Confusion (Loses 2–3 marks):** When `G92` (or G50 on lathes) is used to shift the workpiece origin, absolute coordinates change directly, but incremental deltas do **not** change. Students frequently recalculate deltas after a G92 shift — this is incorrect.
>
> 7. **Block Numbering Gaps (Minor, loses 0.5 mark):** Always use sequential block numbers (N10, N20, N30...) or a consistent increment. Random block numbers (N10, N35, N77) confuse the examiner and may suggest a copy-paste error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- **G90 = Absolute mode** (coordinates from a fixed origin). **G91 = Incremental mode** (coordinates as displacement from the previous position). These two are **mutually exclusive members of Modal Group 03** (ISO 6983).

- The **internal machine position register** is *always* maintained in **absolute coordinates** — the G-code only affects **how the next programmed word is interpreted**, not the storage format.

- The **forward conversion formula** is:
  $$\Delta X_i = X_i - X_{i-1}, \quad \Delta Y_i = Y_i - Y_{i-1}, \quad \Delta Z_i = Z_i - Z_{i-1}$$

- The **inverse conversion formula** (cumulative summation) is:
  $$X_i = X_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{i} \Delta X_k$$

- **Default mode at machine power-on** is typically **G90 (absolute)** on Fanuc, Siemens, and Heidenhain controllers — but **always declare the mode explicitly** in the first block of the program (good NC-code practice, and KTU-exam requirement).

- **Incremental programming suffers from linear error accumulation** with the number of blocks; absolute programming does not. Choose absolute for high-precision work and for parts with a single master datum.

- **Incremental programming shines** for repetitive equal-pitch patterns (rows of holes, milling slots at fixed intervals) because the same delta block can be reused or subprogrammed.

- **Sign convention:** `+` denotes motion in the positive axis direction; `−` denotes motion in the negative axis direction. **Always write the sign explicitly** in incremental mode — KTU examiners will deduct marks otherwise.

- **Datum shift (G92 / G10) interaction:** A datum shift **modifies absolute coordinates directly**; it does **not** alter incremental delta values. If you shift the origin, your G91 program continues to work correctly with the same deltas.

- **Mixed-mode programs** are valid and common: a block can switch from G90 to G91 (or vice versa), and the controller will interpret only the *next* block in the new mode. Track the mode in a state table during exams.

- **Verification strategy for KTU exams:** After every incremental calculation, **reconstruct the absolute positions** using the cumulative summation formula to confirm the contour closes properly. This is the single most reliable way to catch arithmetic errors.

- **Units:** Use `G21` for metric (mm) — this is the KTU default. Use `G20` for imperial (inches) only when explicitly required.

- **Plane selection matters for circular interpolation** (G02/G03) but **does not affect the G90/G91 interpretation** — these are independent modal groups.

- **In lathe programming (Turning centres):** G90 is used for **diameter programming** (X = diameter value), while G91 is used for **displacement in radius** (X = radial delta, which is half the diameter change). This is a **favourite KTU trick question**.

- **One-line memory aid:** *"G90 = Where am I? (absolute position). G91 = How far do I move from here? (incremental displacement)."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Control System? (Foundational Primer)

In the context of **Advanced Manufacturing Engineering (PCMET502)**, a *control system* is a collection of interconnected physical components and electronic modules that work synergistically to regulate, command, direct, or manage the behaviour of a manufacturing process, machine tool, or robotic manipulator. The primary objective of a control system is to achieve a desired output (such as a specific spindle speed, feed rate, position, or temperature) by manipulating the inputs based on a predefined logic or feedback mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> A control system is defined as an arrangement of physical components connected or related in such a manner as to command, direct, or regulate itself or another system to achieve a precise desired output.

Every manufacturing process—from the simple turning operation on a centre lathe to a complex 5-axis CNC machining centre—relies fundamentally on the principles of control systems. The two foundational architectures that form the backbone of all modern automation are **Open Loop Systems** and **Closed Loop Systems**.

---

## 1.2 Open Loop System — Formal Academic Definition

An **Open Loop System** (also known as a *non-feedback system* or *feedforward control system*) is a control architecture in which the control action from the controller is completely independent of the desired output. In other words, the system does not measure its actual output and does not use that measurement to correct any deviation from the intended target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Open Loop Control:**
> The output has absolutely **no influence** on the input control action. The system operates purely on a *time-based* or *pre-programmed* basis, with no self-correcting mechanism.

**Mathematical Representation (Transfer Function Form):**

$$G(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)}$$

where:
- $G(s)$ = Open Loop Transfer Function
- $C(s)$ = Controlled Output (Laplace Domain)
- $R(s)$ = Reference Input (Laplace Domain)

---

## 1.3 Closed Loop System — Formal Academic Definition

A **Closed Loop System** (also referred to as a *feedback control system* or *regenerative control system*) is an architecture in which the actual output is continuously monitored, fed back to a comparator, and compared against the desired reference input. Any discrepancy (error signal) is then used to generate a corrective control action, driving the output toward the target with high precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Closed Loop Control:**
> The output is continuously sensed and returned to the input side via a feedback element. The system is *self-correcting* and *error-driven*, making it suitable for high-precision manufacturing tasks.

**Mathematical Representation (Transfer Function Form):**

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

where:
- $G(s)$ = Forward Path Transfer Function
- $H(s)$ = Feedback Path Transfer Function
- $G(s)H(s)$ = Loop Transfer Function
- $1 + G(s)H(s)$ = Characteristic Equation Denominator

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ironing vs. Washing Machine" Example

Let us make this distinction vivid using real-world analogies that any KTU student can immediately visualize.

### Analogy 1: Open Loop — The "Manual Iron" 🧺
Imagine you are pressing a shirt with a traditional flat iron. You set the thermostat dial to a specific value (say, Level 3 for cotton). The iron heats up to roughly that temperature and stays there. **However**, if the room is cold, the fabric is damp, or you press for too long, the iron cannot sense the actual fabric temperature. It simply continues to deliver the same heat. If the shirt gets scorched, the iron has no idea. This is a textbook **Open Loop System** — the output (shirt temperature / condition) does not feed back to alter the input (heater power).

### Analogy 2: Closed Loop — The "Modern Washing Machine" 🌀
Now consider a fully automatic washing machine with a water level sensor. You set the desired water level (say, 30 litres). The machine starts the inlet valve, and the sensor continuously measures the actual water level. The moment the water reaches 30 litres, the sensor signals the controller, which cuts off the inlet valve. If water leaks or pressure drops, the sensor detects it and signals the valve to open again. This is a classic **Closed Loop System** — output measurement is fed back to adjust input action.

### Manufacturing Mapping

| Manufacturing Component | Open Loop Analogue | Closed Loop Analogue |
|------------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Spindle Speed Control | AC motor with fixed VFD setting | Servo motor with encoder feedback |
| Position Control | Stepper motor (open loop stepping) | Servo motor with resolver |
| Temperature Furnace | Bimetallic strip heater | PID controller with thermocouple |
| Robotic Arm | Pre-programmed pneumatic actuator | Closed-loop servo with force sensors |

---

## 1.5 Real-World Manufacturing Examples

### Open Loop Examples in Manufacturing
- **Stepper motor-driven table** in a low-cost CNC machine or 3D printer (open loop position control).
- **Traffic light controller** based purely on a timer circuit.
- **Automatic washing machine** in the older timer-only models.
- **Pneumatic cylinder actuation** in a fixed-sequence assembly line.
- **Drilling machine** with a cam-operated automatic feed.

### Closed Loop Examples in Manufacturing
- **CNC Machining Centre** with linear scales and servo motor encoders providing real-time position feedback.
