The cumulative error from rounding is recycled into a **remainder register** so that no drift accumulates over many steps — this is the heart of the DDA integrator.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — End-to-End Solution

**Problem Statement (KTU-style, 14 marks):**
> A CNC milling cutter must trace a CCW circular arc from P1(40, 0) mm to P2(0, 40) mm in the XY plane. The center of the arc is at the origin C(0, 0), and the radius is R = 40 mm. The programmed feed is F = 2400 mm/min and the basic length unit is BLU = 0.01 mm. The controller uses a DDA interpolator with a sampling time of T_s = 8 ms.
> Using the DDA method, determine:
> (a) The total subtended angle, the number of segments, and the step angle.
> (b) The X and Y axis pulse counts for the **first three** interpolation steps.

**Step 1: Compute start and end angles.**

$$\theta_{start} = \text{atan2}(0 - 0,\ 40 - 0) = \text{atan2}(0,\ 40) = 0 \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta_{end} = \text{atan2}(40 - 0,\ 0 - 0) = \text{atan2}(40,\ 0) = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad} = 1.5708 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2: Total subtended angle (CCW).**

$$\theta_{total} = \theta_{end} - \theta_{start} = 1.5708 \text{ rad} = 90°$$

**Step 3: Feed per servo tick.**

$$v = \frac{2400}{60} = 40 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$\Delta s = v \cdot T_s = 40 \times 0.008 = 0.32 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Step angle.**

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s}{R} = \frac{0.32}{40} = 0.008 \text{ rad} = 0.4584°$$

**Step 5: Number of segments.**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{1.5708}{0.008} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 196.35 \right\rceil = 197 \text{ steps}$$

**Step 6: Per-step Δx and Δy formulas (CCW, first octant).**

$$\Delta x_i = -R \cdot \sin\theta_i \cdot \Delta\theta = -40 \cdot \sin\theta_i \cdot 0.008 = -0.32 \sin\theta_i$$

$$\Delta y_i = R \cdot \cos\theta_i \cdot \Delta\theta = 0.32 \cos\theta_i$$

**Step 7: First three steps (analytical table).**

| Step i | $\theta_i$ (rad) | $\sin\theta_i$ | $\cos\theta_i$ | $\Delta x_i$ (mm) | $\Delta y_i$ (mm) | $P_{x,i}$ (pulses) | $P_{y,i}$ (pulses) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.3200 | 0 | 32 |
| 1 | 0.0080 | 0.0080 | 1.0000 | −0.00256 | 0.31998 | 0 | 32 |
| 2 | 0.0160 | 0.0160 | 0.9999 | −0.00512 | 0.31997 | −1 | 32 |

> Negative $P_x$ indicates motion in the −X direction; positive $P_y$ indicates motion in the +Y direction.

**Step 8: Verify with cumulative position check (after 3 steps).**

$$\theta_3 = 3 \times 0.008 = 0.024 \text{ rad}$$
$$x_3 = 40\cos(0.024) = 40 \times 0.99971 = 39.9884 \text{ mm}$$
$$y_3 = 40\sin(0.024) = 40 \times 0.023999 = 0.95995 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 9: Linear distance from origin (radius check).**

$$R_{computed} = \sqrt{39.9884^2 + 0.95995^2} = \sqrt{1599.072 + 0.9215} = \sqrt{1599.994} \approx 39.9999 \text{ mm} \approx R = 40 \text{ mm} \;\;\checkmark$$

This confirms that the DDA algorithm maintains radial integrity within the BLU tolerance.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Helical Interpolation (Extension to Complete Interpolator)

A **helical interpolator** adds a linear Z-axis component to the circular XY motion. The total number of steps remains N (from the circular part), and the Z increment per step is:

$$\Delta z = \frac{z_{end} - z_{start}}{N}$$

Each axis command in the unified interpolation loop becomes:

$$P_{x,i} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{\Delta x_i^{circ}}{\text{BLU}}\right)$$
$$P_{y,i} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{\Delta y_i^{circ}}{\text{BLU}}\right)$$
$$P_{z,i} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{\Delta z}{\text{BLU}}\right)$$

All three pulse streams are **time-multiplexed** by the feedrate distributor at the servo rate, producing a true 3-D helical tool path — the defining capability of a **complete interpolator**.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Interpolator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    gcode[G Code Program Block] --> decode[Block Decoder and Parser]
    decode --> param[(Interpolator Parameters: Start End Center Radius Feed Plane)]
    param --> planner[Trajectory Planner]
    planner --> angcalc[Angle and Segment Calculator]
    angcalc --> core[Interpolator Core DDA Engine]
    core --> linear[Linear Interpolation Module]
    core --> circ[Circular Interpolation Module]
    core --> hel[Helical Interpolation Module]
    linear --> feedgen[Feedrate Generator and Acc Dec]
    circ --> feedgen
    hel --> feedgen
    feedgen --> dist[Pulse Distributor Time Multiplexer]
    dist --> xdrv[X Axis Servo Drive]
    dist --> ydrv[Y Axis Servo Drive]
    dist --> zdrv[Z Axis Servo Drive]
    xdrv --> machine[CNC Machine Tool]
    ydrv --> machine
    zdrv --> machine
    machine --> fb[Position Feedback Encoders]
    fb --> xdrv
    fb --> ydrv
    fb --> zdrv
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Circular Interpolation Algorithm (DDA)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Start of Block G02 or G03]) --> read[Read Start P1 End P2 Radius R Direction]
    read --> validate{R is greater than or equal to half chord}
    validate -- No --> alarm[Raise Alarm and Halt]
    validate -- Yes --> compcenter[Compute Center Coordinates xc yc]
    compcenter --> compang[Compute theta start and theta end using atan2]
    compang --> stepang[Compute step angle Delta theta equals BLU over R]
    stepang --> nseg[Compute N equals ceiling of theta total over Delta theta]
    nseg --> loop[Initialize i equals 0 and theta i equals theta start]
    loop --> test{i less than N}
    test -- Yes --> inc[Compute Delta x and Delta y at theta i]
    inc --> pulse[Convert to integer pulse counts and output to servo]
    pulse --> uptheta[theta i plus 1 equals theta i plus Delta theta]
    uptheta --> inc2[i equals i plus 1]
    inc2 --> test
    test -- No --> end([Block Complete and Decelerate])
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Interpolator Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cn[CNC Controller] --> ci[Complete Interpolator]
    ci --> lp[Linear Primitive]
    ci --> cp[Circular Primitive]
    ci --> hp[Helical Primitive]
    cp --> q1[Quadrant 1: 0 to 90 deg]
    cp --> q2[Quadrant 2: 90 to 180 deg]
    cp --> q3[Quadrant 3: 180 to 270 deg]
    cp --> q4[Quadrant 4: 270 to 360 deg]
    q1 --> o1[Octant 1: 0 to 45 deg]
    q1 --> o2[Octant 2: 45 to 90 deg]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why octants?** Industrial DDA controllers reduce computation by **8-fold symmetry** — the algorithm only computes one octant (0°–45°) and then maps the result to the other seven by simple sign flips on Δx, Δy, and the axis directions. This is a classic KTU short-answer / 3-mark topic.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a circular interpolator. List the four essential input parameters it requires.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **circular interpolator** is the computational module in a CNC controller that generates intermediate axis commands to make the cutting tool traverse a circular arc between a programmed start and end point. The four essential inputs are:
1. **Start point** P1(x₁, y₁) — current tool position.
2. **End point** P2(x₂, y₂) — target position.
3. **Arc center** C(x_c, y_c) **or** **Radius R** — defines arc geometry.
4. **Direction** — **CW (G02)** or **CCW (G03)**, plus the **machining plane** (G17/G18/G19).

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for the four inputs + 1 mark for G02/G03/Plane mention.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by a "complete interpolator"? How does it differ from a circular-only interpolator?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **complete interpolator** is a unified trajectory generator capable of producing **linear, circular, and helical** (and in modern controllers, spline) motion within a single servo loop, across multiple axes simultaneously. A **circular-only interpolator** can only trace arcs in one plane. The complete interpolator differs in three ways:
1. It combines **all primitive contour types** (linear + circular + helical) in one computation kernel.
2. It performs **simultaneous multi-axis pulse distribution** honouring programmed feed on the *true 3-D path length*, not the projected 2-D length.
3. It is the basis of **contouring CNC** machines (5-axis mills, lathes with C-axis), whereas circular-only interpolators are restricted to 2.5-D milling.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the three distinguishing points.)*

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Module-1 distribution (per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** Part B questions on Module 1 typically carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. A choice is offered between two questions on the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**
**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a **complete interpolator** in a modern CNC controller. Clearly identify the roles of the block parser, trajectory planner, DDA core, and feedrate/Acc-Dec block.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The tool in a CNC milling machine is to move along a **CCW circular arc** in the XY plane from point **P1(30, 0) mm** to **P2(0, 30) mm** with center at the **origin (0, 0)**. The programmed feed is **F = 1500 mm/min**, the basic length unit is **BLU = 0.005 mm**, and the servo sampling time is **T_s = 4 ms**. Using the **DDA method**, calculate:
- (i) the step angle $\Delta\theta$,
- (ii) the total number of interpolation segments N,
- (iii) the X and Y axis pulse counts for the first two steps.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Block Diagram & Architecture (7 marks):**

The complete interpolator of a CNC controller consists of **five major blocks** operating in real time at the servo sampling rate:

| Block | Role |
|---|---|
| 1. **Block Parser / Decoder** | Reads one G-code block, extracts modal G-codes (G00/G01/G02/G03/G17), coordinates (X, Y, Z), and feed (F). Outputs structured command to trajectory planner. |
| 2. **Trajectory Planner** | Computes total 3-D path length, target velocity, and Acc/Dec profile. Determines whether block needs linear, circular, or helical sub-routine. |
| 3. **Interpolator Core (DDA Engine)** | Runs at servo rate T_s. Emits incremental ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ each tick using parametric equations of the active primitive (line, arc, helix). |
| 4. **Feedrate Generator / Pulse Distributor** | Converts ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ (in mm) into integer axis pulses and time-multiplexes them so that the *true path velocity* equals F. |
| 5. **Acc/Dec Pre-processor** | Applies trapezoidal or S-curve ramping at block boundaries so that axis velocity does not jump — eliminates mechanical shock. |

The block diagram (refer to **Section 4.1** above) shows the data flow: G-code → Decoder → Planner → DDA Core → Pulse Distributor → Servo Drives → Machine → Encoder Feedback.

*(1 mark for naming each block + 2 marks for explaining the role of three blocks + 1 mark for the closed-loop encoder feedback + 1 mark for the feedrate/Acc-Dec role + 2 marks for clarity and diagram.)*

**(b) Numerical DDA Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute start and end angles:**

$$\theta_{start} = \text{atan2}(0 - 0,\ 30 - 0) = 0 \text{ rad}$$
$$\theta_{end} = \text{atan2}(30 - 0,\ 0 - 0) = \frac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708 \text{ rad}$$
$$\theta_{total} = 1.5708 \text{ rad} \;(90°)$$

**Step 2 — Compute feed per servo tick:**

$$v = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ mm/s}$$
$$\Delta s = v \cdot T_s = 25 \times 0.004 = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Step angle:**

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s}{R} = \frac{0.10}{30} = 0.00333 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Number of segments:**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{1.5708}{0.00333} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 471.24 \right\rceil = 472 \text{ steps}$$

**Step 5 — Per-step formulas (CCW, first octant):**

$$\Delta x_i = -R \sin\theta_i \cdot \Delta\theta = -0.10 \sin\theta_i$$
$$\Delta y_i = R \cos\theta_i \cdot \Delta\theta = 0.10 \cos\theta_i$$

**Step 6 — First two steps:**

| Step | $\theta_i$ (rad) | $\sin\theta_i$ | $\cos\theta_i$ | $\Delta x_i$ (mm) | $\Delta y_i$ (mm) | $P_{x,i}$ (pulses) | $P_{y,i}$ (pulses) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.1000 | 0 | 20 |
| 1 | 0.00333 | 0.00333 | 1.0000 | −0.000333 | 0.1000 | 0 | 20 |

> *Note:* $P = \Delta / \text{BLU} = 0.10 / 0.005 = 20$ pulses per step on Y-axis for the first octant; X pulses remain effectively 0 until the angle grows beyond the integer-rounding threshold.

**Step 7 — Final verification (cumulative after 2 steps):**

$$\theta_2 = 0.00667 \text{ rad}$$
$$x_2 = 30\cos(0.00667) = 29.9993 \text{ mm}$$
$$y_2 = 30\sin(0.00667) = 0.2001 \text{ mm}$$
$$R_{check} = \sqrt{29.9993^2 + 0.2001^2} = 30.0000 \text{ mm} \;\;\checkmark$$

*(1 mark for angle setup + 1 mark for step angle + 1 mark for N + 2 marks for first two step computations + 1 mark for pulse conversion + 1 mark for verification.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**(a)** Explain the **G02 and G03** G-codes used for circular interpolation. Discuss the role of the **G17, G18, G19** plane-selection codes and the difference between **I, J, K** (incremental center) and **R** (radius) programming formats with suitable examples.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular arc is to be cut in a workpiece from start point **P1(50, 25) mm** to end point **P2(75, 50) mm** with a radius **R = 30 mm** in the **CW direction (G02)**. Compute the coordinates of the **arc center** and verify the validity of the arc.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) G02, G03 and Plane Selection (7 marks):**

- **G02 — Clockwise Circular Interpolation (CW):** Used to command a CW arc as viewed from the **positive direction of the axis perpendicular to the active plane**. In G17 (XY plane), CW is viewed from +Z.
- **G03 — Counter-Clockwise Circular Interpolation (CCW):** Mirror of G02.

**Plane Selection:**

| G-Code | Active Plane | Perpendicular Axis | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| G17 | XY | Z | Milling (default) |
| G18 | XZ | Y | Turning (default) |
| G19 | YZ | X | Special milling / grinding |

**Programming formats:**

1. **Radius format (R-format):** `G02 X_ Y_ R_ ;`
   - Centre is computed internally; sign of R selects minor (R+) vs major (R−) arc.
2. **Centre-vector format (I, J, K format):** `G02 X_ Y_ I_ J_ ;` (G17 plane)
   - I, J are *incremental* vectors from start point to centre: $I = x_c - x_1$, $J = y_c - y_1$.
   - More flexible for full circles (R-format cannot program a full 360°).

**Example (G17, XY plane, CW):**
```
G17 G02 X50 Y25 I10 J0 F100 ;
```
This cuts a CW arc ending at (50, 25) with centre 10 mm to the right of the start.

*(1 mark G02/G03 + 1 mark G17/18/19 + 2 marks I/J/K vs R + 2 marks example + 1 mark application table.)*

**(b) Numerical — Centre Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** P1(50, 25), P2(75, 50), R = 30 mm, G02 (CW).

**Step 1 — Chord vector and length:**

$$\Delta x = 75 - 50 = 25, \qquad \Delta y = 50 - 25 = 25$$
$$L = \sqrt{25^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{1250} = 35.355 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Half-chord and validity check:**

$$D = \frac{L}{2} = 17.678 \text{ mm}$$
$$2R = 60 \text{ mm} > L = 35.355 \text{ mm} \;\;\checkmark \quad \text{(arc is valid)}$$

**Step 3 — Sagitta h:**

$$h = \sqrt{R^2 - D^2} = \sqrt{30^2 - 17.678^2} = \sqrt{900 - 312.5} = \sqrt{587.5} = 24.238 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Centre coordinates (G02 → CW, centre to the right of travel):**

Using the formulas from Section 3.1 with the sign convention for CW:

$$x_c = \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2} - h \cdot \frac{\Delta y}{L} = \frac{50 + 75}{2} - 24.238 \cdot \frac{25}{35.355}$$

$$x_c = 62.5 - 24.238 \times 0.7071 = 62.5 - 17.142 = 45.358 \text{ mm}$$

$$y_c = \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2} + h \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{L} = \frac{25 + 50}{2} + 24.238 \cdot \frac{25}{35.355}$$

$$y_c = 37.5 + 17.142 = 54.642 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Radius verification:**

$$R_{check} = \sqrt{(50 - 45.358)^2 + (25 - 54.642)^2} = \sqrt{21.55 + 879.16} = \sqrt{900.71} \approx 30.01 \text{ mm} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 6 — G-code output (G17, R-format, minor arc, CW):**

```
N10 G17 G02 X75 Y50 R30 F150 ;
```

*(1 mark for chord + 1 mark for validity + 1 mark for sagitta + 2 marks for centre computation + 1 mark for verification + 1 mark for G-code line.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the atan2 quadrant correction.** Students commonly write $\tan^{-1}(\Delta y / \Delta x)$ and lose a mark for arc-end angle in Q2/Q3/Q4.
> 2. **Sign error on h in the centre formula.** Mixing the ±/∓ for CW vs CCW is the single biggest mark-loss cause in numerical questions. *Always* draw the chord and pick the side visually.
> 3. **Confusing feed units.** F1500 in mm/min must be divided by 60 before multiplying by T_s. Forgetting this gives a step angle off by 60×.
> 4. **Skipping the BLU rounding step.** The pulses must be integers; the DDA remainder register is part of the algorithm — sketch it briefly to earn full marks.
> 5. **Plane mismatch.** Writing G02 in a G18 block while using X, Y in the program (instead of X, Z) is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Full circle in R-format.** A full 360° arc *cannot* be programmed with R (degenerate chord). Use I, J, K. Many students miss this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] An **interpolator** is the *real-time trajectory generator* in a CNC controller; it converts high-level motion commands into per-servo-tick axis pulses.
- [x] **Circular interpolator** handles only **G02 / G03** arcs in **G17 / G18 / G19** planes.
- [x] **Complete interpolator** = **Linear + Circular + Helical** (and modern: Spline) integrated into one kernel.
- [x] The four **essential inputs** to a circular interpolation are: **Start, End, Center (or R), Direction**.
- [x] **Chord validity check:** $2R \ge L = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$. Violation → controller alarm.
- [x] **Sagitta formula:** $h = \sqrt{R^2 - D^2}$ where $D = L/2$.
- [x] **DDA step angle:** $\Delta\theta = v T_s / R = \text{BLU}/R$ (rad).
- [x] **Segment count:** $N = \lceil \theta_{total} / \Delta\theta \rceil$.
- [x] **Per-step displacements (CCW, XY plane):** $\Delta x = -R\sin\theta\,\Delta\theta$, $\Delta y = R\cos\theta\,\Delta\theta$.
- [x] **Pulse conversion:** $P = \text{round}(\Delta / \text{BLU})$; remainder carried forward.
- [x] **Octant symmetry** is exploited to reduce DDA computation 8-fold — only one octant (0°–45°) is computed natively.
- [x] **Plane selection:** G17 (XY, milling default), G18 (XZ, turning default), G19 (YZ).
- [x] **I, J, K** are *incremental vectors* from start to centre (I in X, J in Y, K in Z). R is a signed scalar (R+ → minor arc, R− → major arc).
- [x] **Helical interpolation** = circular XY + linear Z with $N$ circular steps; $\Delta z = (z_2 - z_1)/N$.
- [x] **Acc/Dec pre-processing** is mandatory at block boundaries — never omit it in a 14-mark answer.
- [x] **Closed-loop feedback** from encoders closes the position loop; interpolator commands are *references*, not absolute positions.
- [x] Industrial CNC controllers run the DDA core at **1–4 ms** servo period with **BLU of 1–10 μm**.
- [x] KTU 2024 frequently tests: (i) centre formula signs, (ii) DDA step-angle derivation, (iii) full-circle programming limitations, (iv) plane selection, (v) helical pitch.
- [x] Always **draw the arc and label the centre side** before writing any sign-based formula — this single habit eliminates 70% of numerical errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manual Part Programming; Computer Aided Part Programming - APT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manual Part Programming; Computer Aided Part Programming - APT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Manual Part Programming (MPP)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Manual Part Programming is the direct, line-by-line writing of Numerical Control (NC) instructions by a human programmer using a standardized **Word Address Format** (also called the *Tape Format* or *Block Format*) on a coding sheet. The programmer manually computes all tool path coordinates, including straight-line and circular interpolation points, and writes the alphanumeric codes (N, G, X, Y, Z, F, S, T, M) for every block of the program.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** Manual Part Programming is "the process of preparing an NC program in which the programmer writes each command in a prescribed sequence on a coding sheet, computing all coordinates and tool path data by hand, without the aid of a computer language or CAM software."

## 1.2 Computer Aided Part Programming (CAPP) – APT

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
**APT (Automatically Programmed Tool)** is a high-level, **English-like symbolic programming language** developed at MIT in the 1950s. The programmer describes the **part geometry** (using statements like `POINT`, `LINE`, `CIRCLE`) and the **cutter motion** (using statements like `GOTO`, `GOLFT`, `GORGT`, `GOFWD`). A **preprocessor** (APT compiler) translates the APT statements into a generic cutter-location (CL) file, which is then converted by a **postprocessor** into the specific G-code/M-code dialect of the target CNC machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** APT is the first and most influential **part-programming language** in which the programmer specifies the workpiece geometry and the tool motion in symbolic, English-like terms, and a computer (preprocessor + postprocessor) generates the final NC code automatically.

## 1.3 Real-World Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you need to send an instruction letter from Kottayam to a workshop in Kochi.

| Method | Analogy | Manufacturing Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Part Programming** | You handwrite every letter on a coding sheet in a rigid format — you must compute every coordinate yourself, like a postman walking house-to-house. | Programmer writes G/M codes block by block. |
| **Computer Aided Part Programming (APT)** | You type a story in **Microsoft Word** ("Dear workshop, make a 50 mm circle, mill 5 mm deep, then drill 3 holes"), and the software automatically formats and prints it as a formal letter. | APT statement → Preprocessor → CL file → Postprocessor → NC code. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In **Manual Programming**, the *programmer is the postprocessor*. In **APT**, the *computer is the postprocessor*. This distinction is a **favourite KTU question** and fetches 3–5 marks almost every semester.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Glossary

- **Block (Sequence):** A single complete line of NC code, terminated by an end-of-block (EOB) character (typically `LF` or `;`).
- **Word:** A single letter followed by a numerical value within a block (e.g., `X25.000`).
- **Word Address Format:** The standardized structure where each word is identified by its address letter (X, Y, Z, F, etc.).
- **Preparatory Function (G-code):** Tells the machine *what type of motion* is to be performed (linear, circular, rapid).
- **Miscellaneous Function (M-code):** Tells the machine *what auxiliary action* to perform (spindle on/off, coolant, tool change).
- **Cutter Location (CL) Data:** The intermediate, machine-independent X, Y, Z, I, J, K coordinates of the cutter tip in APT.
- **Postprocessor:** A software module that converts CL data to a specific CNC machine's NC code.
- **Canned Cycle:** A pre-programmed, repeating machining cycle (drilling, tapping, boring) called by a single G-code.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Word Address Format (Block Format)

Every NC block follows a strict logical sequence of letters. The order of letters within a block is **fixed by convention** (not by syntax rule), and any letter can be **omitted** if its function is inactive in that block.

**Standard Letter Order within a Block:**

$$
\text{N} \rightarrow \text{G} \rightarrow \text{X, Y, Z (or U, V, W)} \rightarrow \text{I, J, K} \rightarrow \text{R} \rightarrow \text{F} \rightarrow \text{S} \rightarrow \text{T} \rightarrow \text{M}
$$

### 2.1.1 Detailed Letter-Code Reference Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Address Letter | Meaning | Function Category | Example Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N** | Sequence (Block) Number | Block identification | `N0040` |
| **G** | Preparatory Function | Motion type | `G01` (Linear) |
| **X** | X-axis coordinate | Dimension word | `X+25.400` |
| **Y** | Y-axis coordinate | Dimension word | `Y-12.500` |
| **Z** | Z-axis coordinate | Dimension word | `Z-05.000` |
| **U, V, W** | Secondary axes (parallel to X,Y,Z) | Used on lathes | `U+02.000` |
| **I, J, K** | Arc center (or thread pitch) | Circular interpolation | `I+10.000` |
| **R** | Arc radius (or canned-cycle retract) | Circular / Canned | `R+15.000` |
| **F** | Feed rate | Cutting parameter | `F150.0` (mm/min) |
| **S** | Spindle speed | Cutting parameter | `S1200` (rpm) |
| **T** | Tool selection | Tool change | `T03` |
| **M** | Miscellaneous function | Aux. action | `M08` (Coolant ON) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize the **letter-to-meaning** mapping. A 3-mark question in KTU frequently asks: *"List the various letter codes used in an NC block and state their functions."*

## 2.2 Standard G-Code & M-Code Library (High-Yield Table)

### 2.2.1 Common G-Codes (Preparatory Functions)

| G-Code | Group | Function Description | Modal / Non-Modal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **G00** | 01 | Rapid positioning (no cutting) | Modal |
| **G01** | 01 | Linear interpolation at feed rate | Modal |
| **G02** | 01 | Circular interpolation – Clockwise (CW) | Modal |
| **G03** | 01 | Circular interpolation – Counter-Clockwise (CCW) | Modal |
| **G04** | 00 | Dwell (programmed pause, P=milliseconds) | Non-Modal |
| **G17** | 02 | XY plane selection (default for milling) | Modal |
| **G18** | 02 | XZ plane selection (default for turning) | Modal |
| **G19** | 02 | YZ plane selection | Modal |
| **G20** | 06 | Inch input mode | Modal |
| **G21** | 06 | Metric input mode | Modal |
| **G28** | 00 | Return to machine zero (home) via intermediate point | Non-Modal |
| **G40** | 07 | Cutter compensation – CANCEL | Modal |
| **G41** | 07 | Cutter compensation – LEFT of part | Modal |
| **G42** | 07 | Cutter compensation – RIGHT of part | Modal |
| **G43** | 08 | Tool length compensation – POSITIVE | Modal |
| **G49** | 08 | Tool length compensation – CANCEL | Modal |
| **G80** | 09 | Canned cycle – CANCEL | Modal |
| **G81** | 09 | Drilling canned cycle | Modal |
| **G83** | 09 | Deep-hole peck drilling cycle | Modal |
| **G90** | 03 | Absolute coordinate programming | Modal |
| **G91** | 03 | Incremental coordinate programming | Modal |
| **G94** | 05 | Feed rate in mm/min (or inch/min) | Modal |
| **G95** | 05 | Feed rate in mm/rev (feed per revolution) | Modal |
| **G98** | 10 | Canned cycle – return to initial level | Modal |
| **G99** | 10 | Canned cycle – return to R-point level | Modal |

### 2.2.2 Common M-Codes (Miscellaneous Functions)

| M-Code | Function Description |
|---|---|
| **M00** | Program Stop (unconditional) |
| **M01** | Optional Stop (operator-selected) |
| **M02** | End of Program |
| **M03** | Spindle ON – Clockwise |
| **M04** | Spindle ON – Counter-Clockwise |
| **M05** | Spindle STOP |
| **M06** | Automatic Tool Change (ATC) |
| **M07** | Coolant Mist ON |
| **M08** | Coolant Flood ON |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF |
| **M19** | Spindle Orient (for ATC) |
| **M30** | End of Program and Rewind |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked to "write a manual part program," students **must** show: Program number header, safety block (G90/G40/G80), tool change (M06 Txx), spindle/coolant ON, actual motion blocks (G00/G01 with X, Y, Z, F, S), and a proper end (M05, M09, M30). Missing the safety block loses 1–2 marks routinely.

## 2.3 Sequential Steps in Manual Part Programming

The procedural framework (used as a 7-mark answer):

1. **Analyze the engineering drawing** → identify geometry, datums, tolerances.
2. **Select the work-holding device** (vice, chuck, fixture).
3. **Decide the machining process** (milling, turning, drilling, etc.).
4. **Select cutting tools** and assign tool numbers (T01, T02, …).
5. **Determine the work coordinate system (WCS)** origin position.
6. **Compute the tool path coordinates** for every point (manually).
7. **Write the NC program** in Word Address Format on the coding sheet.
8. **Verify** by dry-run, single-block execution, or by simulation software.

## 2.4 The APT Programming Framework

APT has a strict, four-section logical structure. A complete APT program must contain all four.

### 2.4.1 The Four Logical Sections of an APT Program

$$
\text{Identification} \rightarrow \text{Initialization} \rightarrow \text{Geometry} \rightarrow \text{Motion + Auxiliaries + Termination}
$$

### 2.4.2 APT Geometry-Statement Reference Table

| Statement | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `POINT` | `P1 = POINT/10.0, 20.0, 0.0` | Defines a point in 3D space. |
| `LINE` | `L1 = LINE/P1, P2` | Line through two points. |
| `LINE` | `L1 = LINE/P1, LEFT, TANTO, C1` | Line tangent to a circle. |
| `CIRCLE` | `C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P0, RADIUS, 15.0` | Circle by center + radius. |
| `CIRCLE` | `C1 = CIRCLE/P1, P2, P3` | Circle through 3 points. |
| `CIRCLE` | `C1 = CIRCLE/YLARGE, L1, L2, RADIUS, 20.0` | Tangent to two lines. |
| `PLANE` | `PL1 = PLANE/P1, P2, P3` | Plane through 3 points. |

### 2.4.3 APT Motion-Statement Reference Table

| Statement | Syntax | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `GOTO` | `GOTO/P5` | Move cutter in straight line TO point P5. |
| `GODLTA` | `GODLTA/10.0, 0.0, -5.0` | Move by an incremental **delta** (Δx, Δy, Δz). |
| `GOLFT` | `GOLFT/L1` | Move along left side of line L1 (past it). |
| `GORGT` | `GORGT/L1` | Move along right side of line L1. |
| `GOFWD` | `GOFWD/L1` | Move forward (toward) the line/point. |
| `GOBACK` | `GOBACK/L1` | Move backward (away) from line/point. |
| `GOUP` | `GOUP/PL1` | Move up to (away from) plane PL1. |
| `GODOWN` | `GODOWN/PL1` | Move down to (into) plane PL1. |
| `TLLFT`, `TLRGT`, `TLON`, `TLOFF` | — | Tool control: Tool Left/Right/On/Off. |

### 2.4.4 APT Postprocessor / Auxiliary Statements

| Statement | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `PARTNO` | Identification label of the part. |
| `MACHIN/`, `OP` | Specify the machine tool and operation. |
| `CLPRNT` | Print the CL data file (debugging). |
| `COOLNT/ON` or `COOLNT/OFF` | Coolant command. |
| `SPINDL/1200, CLW` | Spindle speed (rpm) and direction. |
| `FEDRAT/150.0, IPM` | Feed rate + units (IPM or IPR). |
| `TURRET/T03` or `LOADTL/T03` | Tool change command. |
| `END` | Marks end of geometry definition. |
| `FINI` | Marks physical end of APT program. |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Process-Planning Computations)

These formulas are mandatory for the "process planning" sub-question often tied to programming.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Derivation Hint |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Spindle speed (rpm); $v$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D$ = tool $\varnothing$ (mm). |
| 2 | $f_z = f_{total} / (N \cdot z)$ | Feed per tooth; $f_{total}$ = table feed (mm/min), $z$ = no. of teeth. |
| 3 | $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | Table feed rate (mm/min). |
| 4 | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{v_f}$ | Machining time (min); $L$ = tool travel length. |
| 5 | $\text{MRR} = v \cdot a_p \cdot f$ | Material removal rate (mm³/min); $a_p$ = depth of cut. |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{C}{v^{m}}$ | Taylor's tool life equation. |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{D}{2} - d$ | Cutter center offset (for milling along arc); $d$ = finished radius. |
| 8 | $I = X_2 - X_c$ | Arc center I-offset (signed). |
| 9 | $J = Y_2 - Y_c$ | Arc center J-offset (signed). |
| 10 | $K = Z_2 - Z_c$ | Arc center K-offset (signed, for 3D arcs). |

> [!NOTE]
> For circular interpolation in **G02/G03**, the I, J, K values are **not** the absolute center coordinates — they are the **signed incremental distances** from the start point of the arc to the arc center. This is a **classic KTU trap** that costs 2 marks.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Example 1: Manual Part Program for a Pocket-Milling Operation

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

A **rectangular pocket** of size **80 mm × 60 mm**, **5 mm deep** is to be machined on the top of a mild-steel plate (200 mm × 200 mm × 20 mm). The pocket has rounded corners of radius 10 mm. The pocket is positioned with its lower-left corner at **X=20, Y=20** (in the WCS). 

**Given:** Cutter = Ø10 mm end mill; cutting speed $v = 30$ m/min; feed per tooth $f_z = 0.05$ mm; number of teeth $z = 2$. Use absolute programming, metric mode, XY plane.

**Required:** Write the complete **manual NC part program** using Word Address Format, showing all process-parameter calculations.

### 3.1.2 Process-Parameter Calculations (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed $N$:**
Apply the formula $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 10} \\
  &= \frac{30000}{31.4159} \\
  &= 954.93 \text{ rpm} \\
  &\approx 955 \text{ rpm (rounded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Table Feed $v_f$:**
Apply $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_f &= 0.05 \times 2 \times 955 \\
    &= 95.5 \text{ mm/min} \\
    &\approx 95 \text{ mm/min (rounded down for safety)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Tool-Path Waypoints:**

| Point | X (mm) | Y (mm) | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P0 (Start)** | 20.000 | 20.000 | Lower-left corner of pocket (entry) |
| **P1** | 100.000 | 20.000 | Move along bottom edge (80 mm) |
| **P2 (Arc)** | 100.000 | 50.000 | After 10 mm of arc start |
| **P3 (Arc End)** | 90.000 | 60.000 | End of right-side arc (radius 10) |
| **P4** | 30.000 | 60.000 | Top edge (60 mm width minus 2×R) |
| **P5 (Arc End)** | 20.000 | 50.000 | End of left-side arc |
| **P6 (Arc End)** | 20.000 | 30.000 | Start of bottom-left arc (return to entry) |

(Note: We use cutter-center path; the start point for cutting is offset by 5 mm cutter radius from the actual pocket edge — this calculation is shown in 3.1.3.)

**Step 4 — Calculate Cutter-Center Coordinates (Cutter Radius Comp OFF — Manual Path):**
The cutter is Ø10, so its **center** travels 5 mm outside the pocket. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{center}} &= X_{\text{edge}} \pm 5 \text{ (sign depends on side)}\\
Y_{\text{center}} &= Y_{\text{edge}} \pm 5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P0 (start)} &= (20-5,\ 20-5) = (15,\ 15) \\
\text{P1} &= (100+5,\ 20-5) = (105,\ 15) \\
\text{P2} &= (100+5,\ 30) = (105,\ 30) \quad [\text{arc start, CW}] \\
\text{P3} &= (100,\ 35) \quad [\text{arc end, 90° CW, center}=(100, 35), R=5] \\
\text{P4} &= (30,\ 60+5) = (30,\ 65) \\
\text{P5} &= (20,\ 35) \quad [\text{arc end of upper-left corner}] \\
\text{P6} &= (15,\ 30) \quad [\text{arc end of lower-left corner — return to start}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate I, J, K Offsets for Each Arc:**
Recall: $I = X_{\text{center}} - X_{\text{start}}$; $J = Y_{\text{center}} - Y_{\text{start}}$.

**Arc 1 (P2 → P3, CW):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 100 - 105 = -5 \\
J &= 35 - 30 = +5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Arc 2 (P5 → P6, CW):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 20 - 20 = 0 \\
J &= 35 - 30 = +5
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Final Manual NC Program (Word Address Format)

```
O0001 (RECTANGULAR POCKET 80x60, 5 DEEP) ;
N0010 G21 G90 G17 G40 G80 G49 (SAFETY BLOCK: METRIC, ABSOLUTE, XY-PLANE) ;
N0020 T01 M06 (LOAD TOOL 1: Ø10 END MILL) ;
N0030 M03 S955 (SPINDLE CW AT 955 RPM) ;
N0040 G00 X15.000 Y15.000 (RAPID TO P0 AT 5 MM ABOVE) ;
N0050 Z2.000 (RAPID DOWN TO 2 MM ABOVE STOCK) ;
N0060 G01 Z-5.000 F95.0 (PLUNGE TO DEPTH 5 MM AT 95 MM/MIN) ;
N0070 G01 X105.000 Y15.000 F95.0 (LINEAR CUT BOTTOM EDGE) ;
N0080 G01 X105.000 Y30.000 (LINEAR UP TO ARC START P2) ;
N0090 G02 X100.000 Y35.000 I-5.000 J5.000 (CW ARC: ARC-1) ;
N0100 G01 X30.000 Y65.000 (LINEAR CUT TOP EDGE) ;
N0110 G02 X20.000 Y35.000 I0.000 J5.000 (CW ARC: ARC-2) ;
N0120 G01 X15.000 Y30.000 (LINEAR BACK DOWN) ;
N0130 G02 X15.000 Y15.000 I-5.000 J0.000 (CW ARC: ARC-3, CLOSING) ;
N0140 G00 Z50.000 (RAPID RETRACT) ;
N0150 X0.000 Y0.000 (RAPID TO HOME) ;
N0160 M05 (SPINDLE STOP) ;
N0170 M09 (COOLANT OFF) ;
N0180 M30 (END OF PROGRAM, REWIND) ;
%
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block 0090 — deep analysis:** G02 denotes **Clockwise** circular interpolation in the XY plane (G17 is active). The end point of the arc is `X100.000 Y35.000`. The I and J values are **signed** and refer to the **arc center** measured **from the arc start point**, not from the WCS origin. This is exactly the trap mentioned in 2.5.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example 2: APT Program for the Same Pocket

### 3.2.1 Same Part, APT-Translated

Now we will describe the **same rectangular pocket** in APT. We use the same dimensions: pocket from X=20 to X=100, Y=20 to Y=60, R=10 corners, depth=5 mm, cutter Ø10 mm.

### 3.2.2 APT Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1 — Define Part Geometry (Points, Lines, Circles):**

```
P0 = POINT/20.0, 20.0, 0.0          (Lower-left corner of pocket)
P1 = POINT/100.0, 20.0, 0.0         (Lower-right corner)
P2 = POINT/100.0, 60.0, 0.0         (Upper-right corner)
P3 = POINT/20.0, 60.0, 0.0          (Upper-left corner)

L1 = LINE/P0, P1, ATANGL, 0         (Bottom edge)
L2 = LINE/P1, P2, ATANGL, 90        (Right edge)
L3 = LINE/P2, P3, ATANGL, 180       (Top edge)
L4 = LINE/P3, P0, ATANGL, 270       (Left edge)

C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P0, RADIUS, 10.0  (Lower-left fillet)
C2 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, RADIUS, 10.0  (Lower-right fillet)
C3 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P2, RADIUS, 10.0  (Upper-right fillet)
C4 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P3, RADIUS, 10.0  (Upper-left fillet)

PL_TOP = PLANE/P0, PARLEL, XYPLAN, Z=20.0   (Top of stock)
PL_BOT = PLANE/P0, PARLEL, XYPLAN, Z=15.0   (Pocket floor, 5 mm below)
```

**Step 2 — Set Cutter:**

```
CUTTER/10.0
```

(Defines an end mill of Ø10 mm; APT internally offsets the cutter by 5 mm.)

**Step 3 — Define Tool Path Using Motion Statements:**

The programmer specifies that the cutter must remain **to the left** of the contour (i.e., inside the pocket when viewed from the direction of travel). APT automatically performs cutter-radius offset using `TLLFT`.

```
FROM/SETPT                          (Start from a safe pre-set point)
GO/TO, P0                           (Rapid to lower-left corner)
GO/DOWN, PL_TOP, L0                 (Plunge to clearance plane L0)
GO/DOWN, PL_BOT, L0                 (Plunge to floor of pocket)
TLLFT, GOFWD, C1, L1, GORGT, C1, GOFWD, L2
TLLFT, GORGT, C2, GOFWD, L2, GORGT, C2, GOFWD, L3
TLLFT, GORGT, C3, GOFWD, L3, GORGT, C3, GOFWD, L4
TLLFT, GORGT, C4, GOFWD, L4, GORGT, C4, GOFWD, L1
GO/UP, PL_TOP, L0
GOTO/SETPT
```

**Step 4 — Auxiliary (Postprocessor) Commands:**

```
COOLNT/ON
SPINDL/955, CLW
FEDRAT/95.0, IPM
```

**Step 5 — Termination:**

```
END
FINI
```

### 3.2.3 Complete APT Program (Compiled Form)

```
PARTNO  RECTANGULAR POCKET 80x60x5
MACHIN/ MILL, 3AXIS
CLPRNT
OUTTOL/ 0.05
INTOL/ 0.05
CUTTER/ 10.0
SPINDL/ 955, CLW
FEDRAT/ 95.0, IPM
COOLNT/ ON

P0 = POINT/20.0, 20.0, 0.0
P1 = POINT/100.0, 20.0, 0.0
P2 = POINT/100.0, 60.0, 0.0
P3 = POINT/20.0, 60.0, 0.0

L1 = LINE/P0, ATANGL, 0
L2 = LINE/P1, ATANGL, 90
L3 = LINE/P2, ATANGL, 180
L4 = LINE/P3, ATANGL, 270

C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P0, RADIUS, 10.0
C2 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, RADIUS, 10.0
C3 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P2, RADIUS, 10.0
C4 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P3, RADIUS, 10.0

PL_TOP = PLANE/P0, PARLEL, XYPLAN, Z20.0
PL_BOT = PLANE/P0, PARLEL, XYPLAN, Z15.0
PL_FLO = PLANE/P0, PARLEL, XYPLAN, Z 0.0

SETPT  = POINT/0.0, 0.0, 50.0
L0     = LINE/SETPT, ATANGL, 0

FROM/SETPT
GO/TO, P0
GO/DOWN, PL_TOP, L0
GO/DOWN, PL_BOT, L0
TLLFT, GOFWD, C1, GORGT, C1, GOFWD, L1
TLLFT, GOFWD, C2, GORGT, C2, GOFWD, L2
TLLFT, GOFWD, C3, GORGT, C3, GOFWD, L3
TLLFT, GOFWD, C4, GORGT, C4, GOFWD, L4
GO/UP, PL_TOP, L0
GOTO/SETPT

COOLNT/OFF
SPINDL/OFF
END
FINI
```

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Manual vs. APT Programming

| Attribute | Manual Part Programming | Computer Aided (APT) Programming |
|---|---|---|
| **Coordinate computation** | Done by hand, line by line | Done automatically by the preprocessor |
| **Cutter compensation** | Programmer must compute center-path geometry | `TLLFT/TLRGT` enables automatic offset |
| **Tool path complexity** | Best for simple 2½-D contours; poor for 3-D sculptured surfaces | Excellent for 3-D and sculptured surfaces |
| **Skill level required** | High mathematical skill; lower computer skill | Lower math; higher APT language skill |
| **Error rate** | High (arithmetic and sign errors common) | Low (compiler checks syntax and geometry) |
| **Time required** | Very long for complex parts | Short even for complex parts |
| **Machine independence** | Tied to one machine's code dialect | CL file is **machine-independent**; postprocessor is machine-specific |
| **Postprocessor need** | No postprocessor needed | **Mandatory** — every CNC machine has its own postprocessor |
| **Best suited for** | 2-axis turning, simple drilling, simple milling | 5-axis machining, free-form surfaces, mold & die |
| **Cost** | Low software cost, high labor cost | High software cost, low labor cost |
| **Documentation** | Coding sheet only | Self-documenting (English-like statements) |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End NC/CNC Programming Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete industrial flow — from a 2D/3D engineering drawing all the way to the final machined component — showing where Manual and APT programming branch out.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Engineering Drawing] --> A1{Part Complexity?}
    A1 -- Simple 2.5D --> A2[Manual Part Programming]
    A1 -- Complex 3D Surface --> A3[APT or CAM Software]

    A2 --> B1[Compute Coordinates by Hand]
    A3 --> B2[Define Geometry: POINT LINE CIRCLE PLANE]
    B2 --> B3[Define Motion: GOTO GOLFT GORGT GOFWD]

    B1 --> C1[Write NC Code in Word Address Format]
    B3 --> C2[APT Preprocessor generates CL File]
    C2 --> C3{CL File is Machine Independent}
    C3 --> C4[Postprocessor converts CL to specific NC Code]
    C4 --> C5[Machine Specific NC Code Ready]

    C1 --> C5
    C5 --> D1[Transfer to CNC Machine DNC or Tape]
    D1 --> D2[Machine executes Program]
    D2 --> D3[Verify First Article Inspection]
    D3 --> A4{Conforms to Drawing?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Edit Program or Re Machine]
    A5 --> C5
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[STOP: Bulk Production]
```

## 4.2 Manual Part Programming — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This diagram shows the **internal logic of a typical block** and the **system-level block structure** of a manual program.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BLOCK_A[Single NC Block]
        S0[Block Number N] --> S1[Preparatory G]
        S1 --> S2[Coordinate Words X Y Z]
        S2 --> S3[Arc Center Words I J K]
        S3 --> S4[Feed F and Spindle S]
        S4 --> S5[Tool T and Misc M]
    end

    subgraph PROG_B[Complete NC Program]
        P0[Header Block O0001] --> P1[Safety Block G90 G21 G40]
        P1 --> P2[Tool Change M06 Txx]
        P2 --> P3[Spindle and Coolant ON M03 M08]
        P3 --> P4[Rapid to Start Point G00]
        P4 --> P5[Plunge and Cutting G01 G02 G03]
        P5 --> P6[Return to Home G28 or G00]
        P6 --> P7[Spindle Coolant OFF M05 M09]
        P7 --> P8[End of Program M30]
    end
```

## 4.3 APT System Block Architecture (Preprocessor + Postprocessor)

The schematic below clarifies the role of the preprocessor (geometry/motion → CL data) and the postprocessor (CL → machine-specific NC code).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[APT Source Program English like] --> R1[Preprocessor APT Compiler]
    R1 --> R2[Geometry and Motion Computed]
    R2 --> R3[CL Data File X Y Z I J K per block]
    R3 --> R4{Choose Machine}
    R4 --> R5[Postprocessor for Fanuc]
    R4 --> R6[Postprocessor for Siemens]
    R4 --> R7[Postprocessor for Heidenhain]
    R4 --> R8[Postprocessor for Haas]
    R5 --> R9[Machine Specific NC Code]
    R6 --> R9
    R7 --> R9
    R8 --> R9
    R9 --> R10[Send to CNC Machine via DNC]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — When to Use Manual vs. APT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K0[New Part to be Programmed] --> K1{Is the part 2D or 3D?}
    K1 -- 2D with simple prismatic features --> K2{Number of holes and slots greater than 20?}
    K1 -- 3D freeform or sculptured --> K4[Use APT or modern CAM]

    K2 -- No --> K3[Manual Part Programming is adequate]
    K2 -- Yes --> K4
    K4 --> K5{Does the part have 5 axis features?}
    K5 -- Yes --> K6[APT with full 5 axis postprocessor]
    K5 -- No --> K7[APT with 3 axis postprocessor]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Word Address Format" as used in NC programming. List any four commonly used address letters and state their functions.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Word Address Format** (also called Block Format or Tab Sequential Format) is the standardized structure of an NC instruction line in which each piece of information is identified by an *address letter* (a single alphabetic character) followed by a numerical value (the *word*). This letter-based addressing makes the code self-explanatory, easy to read, and easy to edit.

Four commonly used address letters:

| Address | Function |
|---|---|
| **N** | Sequence (block) number for identification and branching. |
| **G** | Preparatory function — selects the type of motion (e.g., G01 = linear). |
| **F** | Feed rate — sets the cutting feed in mm/min or mm/rev. |
| **S** | Spindle speed — sets the rotational speed in rpm. |

*(Any four valid address letters and their meanings fetch full 3 marks.)*

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is APT? Mention any two advantages of APT over manual part programming.*

**Model Answer:**

**APT (Automatically Programmed Tool)** is a high-level, English-like symbolic language used to write part programs for numerically controlled machine tools. In APT, the programmer describes the **part geometry** (using statements like `POINT`, `LINE`, `CIRCLE`) and the **cutter motion** (using statements like `GOTO`, `GOLFT`, `GORGT`). A computer (preprocessor + postprocessor) then automatically converts the APT source code into machine-specific NC code.

**Two advantages of APT over manual part programming:**

1. **Reduction of computation effort** — In manual programming, the programmer must hand-calculate every coordinate and cutter-center offset. In APT, the preprocessor performs all geometry and tool-path computations automatically.
2. **Machine independence** — The APT source program is a single file; only the postprocessor must be changed to generate NC code for different CNC machines, making the program portable across a workshop's machine pool.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (typically a theory question + a numerical/programming question). Students must answer **one full question (both sub-parts)**.

---

### **Question B1 (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Explain with a neat sketch the **Word Address Format** of an NC block. List the common G-codes and M-codes used in a CNC milling program with their functions.*

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to Section 2.1 and 2.2 of these notes for the full table.)*

The Word Address Format is the **structured sequence of words** in an NC block. The standard order is:

$$
N \rightarrow G \rightarrow (X, Y, Z \text{ or } U, V, W) \rightarrow (I, J, K) \rightarrow R \rightarrow F \rightarrow S \rightarrow T \rightarrow M
$$

**Block diagram of a Word Address Format line:**

```
+----------------+----------+-----------+-----------+----+------+------+------+------+
| Sequence No.   | Prep. Fn.|  Coord.   | Arc Cent. | R  | Feed | Spin.| Tool | Misc.|
|     N          |    G     |  X Y Z    |  I J K    |    |  F   |  S   |  T   |  M   |
+----------------+----------+-----------+-----------+----+------+------+------+------+
       |              |          |            |        |      |      |      |      |
       v              v          v            v        v      v      v      v      v
   Identifies    Motion     End-point   Signed    Arc   Cutting  Tool  Tool  Auxiliary
   the block      type       data     offsets   radius  param.  speed  no.  action
```

**Common G-codes for milling** (G00, G01, G02, G03, G17, G20/G21, G40/G41/G42, G43, G80, G81, G90/G91) — *see Section 2.2.1 table for full description.*

**Common M-codes for milling** (M00, M01, M03, M05, M06, M08, M09, M30) — *see Section 2.2.2 table for full description.*

> (7 marks awarded for: correct format order 2, sketch/table of G-codes 3, M-codes 2.)

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*A circular pocket of Ø60 mm and depth 4 mm is to be machined on a steel plate using a Ø10 mm end mill. Given: cutting speed $v = 25$ m/min, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.04$ mm/tooth, number of teeth $z = 2$. The pocket center is at X=0, Y=0 in the WCS. Write the complete **manual NC part program** for this operation.*

**Model Answer — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Compute spindle speed.**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 10} = \frac{25000}{31.4159} = 795.77 \text{ rpm} \approx 796 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2: Compute feed rate.**

$$
v_f = f_z \times z \times N = 0.04 \times 2 \times 796 = 63.68 \text{ mm/min} \approx 64 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3: Compute cutter center coordinates (Ø10 cutter, so 5 mm offset).**

| Point | X | Y | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| P0 | 5.000 | 0.000 | Entry point, right side of center |
| P1 | 5.000 | 0.000 | Start of helical/spiral entry (use linear plunge) |
| P2 | -5.000 | 0.000 | Left edge (diameter 10) |
| P3 | 0.000 | 5.000 | Top edge |

**For a full circular pocket of Ø60, the cutter path is a circle of Ø55 (60 − 2×2.5? No — 60 − 10 = 50; let us use 5 mm offset).** 

Cutter center path radius = 30 − 5 = **25 mm**.

**Step 4: Final manual NC program.**

```
O0010 (CIRCULAR POCKET DIAM 60 DEPTH 4) ;
N0010 G21 G90 G17 G40 G80 G49 (SAFETY BLOCK) ;
N0020 T01 M06 (LOAD Ø10 END MILL) ;
N0030 M03 S796 (SPINDLE CW 796 RPM) ;
N0040 G00 X0.000 Y0.000 (RAPID TO CENTER) ;
N0050 Z2.000 (RAPID TO 2 MM ABOVE STOCK) ;
N0060 G01 Z-4.000 F64.0 (PLUNGE TO DEPTH) ;
N0070 G01 X25.000 Y0.000 F64.0 (MOVE TO ENTRY OF CIRCLE) ;
N0080 G03 X25.000 Y0.000 I-25.000 J0.000 F64.0 (FULL CIRCLE CW) ;
N0090 G00 Z50.000 (RAPID RETRACT) ;
N0100 X0.000 Y0.000 (RAPID TO HOME) ;
N0110 M05 (SPINDLE STOP) ;
N0120 M09 (COOLANT OFF) ;
N0130 M30 (END OF PROGRAM) ;
%
```

**Block 0080 deep analysis:** The end point equals the start point (`X25 Y0`), forming a complete circle. I = −25, J = 0 means the arc center is 25 mm to the left of the start point, i.e., at `(0, 0)`. G03 selects counter-clockwise direction (from outside the pocket looking down).

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Explain the **APT language** in detail. Discuss its **geometry statements** and **motion statements** with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to Section 2.4 of these notes for the complete statement tables.)*

APT is structured in **four logical sections**:

**1. Identification / Initialization Section:**
Contains housekeeping statements like `PARTNO`, `MACHIN/`, `CLPRNT`, `OUTTOL`, `INTOL`. These identify the part, declare the machine, and set tolerance levels.

**2. Geometry Section:**
The programmer defines the workpiece as a set of points, lines, circles, and planes.

- `POINT/x, y, z` — defines a point.
  - Example: `P1 = POINT/15.0, 25.0, 0.0`
- `LINE/` — defines a line by two points, by a point and an angle, or tangent to a curve.
  - Example: `L1 = LINE/P1, P2`
- `CIRCLE/` — defines a circle by center + radius, by three points, or by tangents.
  - Example: `C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P0, RADIUS, 20.0`
- `PLANE/` — defines a plane by three points.
  - Example: `PL1 = PLANE/P1, P2, P3`

**3. Motion + Tool Control Section:**
Commands the cutter motion relative to the defined geometry.

- `GOTO/P5` — move TO a specific point.
- `GODLTA/dx, dy, dz` — incremental move.
- `GOLFT/L1` — move along the left side of line L1 (past it).
- `GORGT/L1` — move along the right side.
- `GOFWD/L1` or `GOBACK/L1` — approach or retreat from a feature.
- `GOUP/PL1` or `GODOWN/PL1` — move away from / into a plane.
- `TLLFT`, `TLRGT`, `TLON`, `TLOFF` — tool control (cutter compensation).

**4. Auxiliary / Termination Section:**
Contains postprocessor commands like `SPINDL`, `FEDRAT`, `COOLNT`, `TURRET`, `END`, and `FINI`.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Write a complete **APT program** to machine the **outside profile** of a triangular plate whose vertices are at: A(0,0), B(80,0), C(40,60). Use a Ø10 mm end mill, cutting depth 3 mm, spindle speed 800 rpm, feed 80 mm/min.*

**Model Answer:**

```
PARTNO  TRIANGULAR PLATE PROFILE
MACHIN/ MILL, 3AXIS
CLPRNT
OUTTOL/ 0.05
INTOL/ 0.05
CUTTER/ 10.0

P1 = POINT/0.0, 0.0, 0.0
P2 = POINT/80.0, 0.0, 0.0
P3 = POINT/40.0, 60.0, 0.0

L1 = LINE/P1, P2                    (Base edge AB)
L2 = LINE/P2, P3                    (Right edge BC)
L3 = LINE/P3, P1                    (Left edge CA)

PL_TOP = PLANE/P1, PARLEL, XYPLAN, Z 10.0
PL_BOT = PLANE/P1, PARLEL, XYPLAN, Z  3.0

SETPT  = POINT/-25.0, -25.0, 25.0
L0     = LINE/SETPT, ATANGL, 0

SPINDL/ 800, CLW
FEDRAT/ 80.0, IPM
COOLNT/ ON

FROM/SETPT
GO/TO, P1
GO/DOWN, PL_TOP, L0
GO/DOWN, PL_BOT, L0
TLRGT, GOFWD, L1
TLRGT, GORGT, L1, GOFWD, L2
TLRGT, GORGT, L2, GOFWD, L3
TLRGT, GORGT, L3, GOFWD, L1
GO/UP, PL_TOP, L0
GOTO/SETPT

COOLNT/OFF
SPINDL/OFF
END
FINI
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in I, J, K** — Always compute I, J, K as the **arc center MINUS the start point** (not the WCS origin). A 2-mark penalty is common.
> 2. **Wrong G02/G03 direction** — For milling, viewed from **above (Z+), CW = G02 and CCW = G03**. For turning, viewed from **+X (towards chuck)**, the convention flips. Wrong direction = 1–2 marks lost.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety block** (G90 G21 G40 G80) — 1 mark is reserved for the safety block in KTU's official answer key.
> 4. **Mixing G90 (absolute) and G91 (incremental) in the same program** without resetting — causes silent coordinate errors. Always make G90/G91 explicit at the start.
> 5. **APT: Forgetting `FINI`** — the program will not terminate, leading to a "missing termination" error. Examiners explicitly check for `END` and `FINI`.
> 6. **APT: Spelling `GOTO` as `GOTO/` with inconsistent punctuation** — APT syntax is case-insensitive but the **slash** `/` is mandatory. Forgetting it costs marks.
> 7. **Manual program: omitting M30** — the program will not rewind. Always end with M30, not M02 (M02 is older standard).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**

- [ ] Manual Part Programming uses **Word Address Format**: each *word* = one address letter + numerical value.
- [ ] Block order: **N → G → X,Y,Z → I,J,K → R → F → S → T → M**.
- [ ] **G00** = rapid (no cutting); **G01** = linear feed; **G02** = CW arc; **G03** = CCW arc.
- [ ] **M03** = spindle CW; **M05** = spindle stop; **M06** = tool change; **M08** = coolant ON; **M30** = end + rewind.
- [ ] **I, J, K** = **signed** incremental arc-center offsets from the arc *start point*, NOT the WCS origin.
- [ ] **G17** = XY plane (milling); **G18** = XZ plane (turning); **G19** = YZ plane.
- [ ] **G90** = absolute coordinates; **G91** = incremental coordinates.
- [ ] **G41** = cutter LEFT of part; **G42** = cutter RIGHT of part; **G40** = cancel.
- [ ] Spindle speed formula: $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$.
- [ ] Feed rate formula: $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$.
- [ ] **APT** = English-like symbolic language. **Preprocessor** → CL file. **Postprocessor** → machine NC code.
- [ ] APT geometry: `POINT`, `LINE`, `CIRCLE`, `PLANE`. APT motion: `GOTO`, `GODLTA`, `GOLFT`, `GORGT`, `GOFWD`, `GOBACK`, `GOUP`, `GODOWN`.
- [ ] Tool side control: `TLLFT`, `TLRGT`, `TLON`, `TLOFF`.
- [ ] Every APT program must end with `END` (end of geometry) and `FINI` (end of program).
- [ ] **Advantage of APT over manual**: reduced computation effort, machine-independent source code, automatic cutter compensation, suitability for complex 3-D and 5-axis contours.
- [ ] **Disadvantage of APT**: requires a postprocessor for each CNC machine; requires APT-trained programmer.
- [ ] Always include a **safety block** in any manual NC program: `G21 G90 G17 G40 G80 G49`.
- [ ] Always include a **proper end** in any manual NC program: `M05 M09 M30`.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Exercises in Turning, Drilling and Milling Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Programming Exercises in Turning, Drilling and Milling Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CNC Part Programming** is the systematic process of preparing a sequence of alphanumeric instructions (known as **G-codes**, **M-codes**, and address words) that command a Computer Numerically Controlled (CNC) machine tool to perform a specific manufacturing operation—*turning, drilling, or milling*—on a workpiece with the required geometry, tolerance, and surface finish.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, *Programming Exercises in Turning, Drilling, and Milling Operations* is a **practical-application module** where the student writes, simulates, and dry-runs (or executes) **Manual Part Programs (MPP)** for:
- **Turning Centers (2-axis lathes)** using Fanuc / Siemens / Hass controller codes.
- **Drilling Machines (CNC mill or drilling center)** with canned cycles.
- **Milling Centers (3-axis VMC)** with linear and circular interpolation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "CNC as a Recipe for a Robot Chef"** 🍳
> Imagine a robotic chef that can only cook if you write down *every single step* in its native language. You cannot say "make pasta"; you must say "move hand 10 cm right → lower hand 2 cm → grasp object → lift → release after 3 seconds." A **CNC program is that exact recipe** — every tool movement, spindle rotation, and coolant switch is dictated in a strict, line-by-line code that the machine's interpreter (controller) reads one block at a time.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Block** | A single line of NC code (one command sentence). |
| **Word** | An address letter + numeric value (e.g., `X25.0`, `M08`). |
| **Word Address Format** | Modern programming where each parameter has a letter prefix. |
| **Modal Code** | A G-code that remains active until another code in the same group overrides it. |
| **Modal vs. Non-Modal** | G00/G01 are *modal*; G04 (dwell) is *non-modal*. |
| **Canned Cycle** | A pre-defined subprogram (e.g., G81 for drilling) that simplifies repetitive operations. |
| **Dry Run** | A controller mode that runs the program at high feed *without* the tool touching the workpiece, to verify path. |
| **Tool Offset** | The (ΔX, ΔZ) compensation that the controller adds to account for the physical tool tip geometry. |
| **Work Offset (G54–G59)** | The zero-point (origin) of the workpiece coordinate system on the machine table. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 practical record, you will be expected to write **at least one manual part program each** for turning, drilling (as part of a turning/milling cycle), and milling — using **absolute (G90)** and **incremental (G91)** modes, and canned cycles where applicable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Every Program

1. **Preparation Codes (G-codes)** — Define *how* the tool moves (linear, circular, rapid, feed).
2. **Miscellaneous Codes (M-codes)** — Define *auxiliary* actions (spindle on/off, coolant on/off, tool change).
3. **Axis Words (X, Y, Z, A, B, C, U, V, W)** — Define *where* the tool moves, in the active coordinate system.

### 2.1.1 Standard G-Code Reference (Fanuc-compatible, KTU Lab Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> The codes below are the **canonical set** tested in KTU lab examinations. Memorize the *Group* numbers because the controller cancels a modal code in the same group automatically.

| G-Code | Group | Function | Modal? |
|---|---|---|---|
| G00 | 01 | Rapid traverse (positioning) | Modal |
| G01 | 01 | Linear interpolation (cutting feed) | Modal |
| G02 | 01 | Circular interpolation CW | Modal |
| G03 | 01 | Circular interpolation CCW | Modal |
| G04 | 00 | Dwell (pause) | Non-modal |
| G20 | 06 | Inch input | Modal |
| G21 | 06 | Metric input | Modal |
| G28 | 00 | Return to reference point | Non-modal |
| G40 | 07 | Cutter compensation cancel | Modal |
| G41/G42 | 07 | Cutter comp left/right | Modal |
| G54–G59 | 12 | Work coordinate selection 1–6 | Modal |
| G70 | 00 | Finishing cycle (lathe) | Non-modal |
| G71 | 00 | Stock removal cycle (lathe) | Non-modal |
| G80 | 09 | Canned cycle cancel | Modal |
| G81 | 09 | Drilling canned cycle | Modal |
| G82 | 09 | Drilling with dwell | Modal |
| G83 | 09 | Peck drilling cycle | Modal |
| G90 | 03 | Absolute programming | Modal |
| G91 | 03 | Incremental programming | Modal |
| G94 | 05 | Feed per minute | Modal |
| G95 | 05 | Feed per revolution | Modal |
| G96 | 02 | Constant surface speed (CSS) | Modal |
| G97 | 02 | Constant RPM | Modal |

### 2.1.2 Standard M-Code Reference

| M-Code | Function |
|---|---|
| M00 | Program stop |
| M01 | Optional stop |
| M02 / M30 | Program end / rewind |
| M03 | Spindle on CW |
| M04 | Spindle on CCW |
| M05 | Spindle stop |
| M06 | Tool change |
| M08 | Coolant ON |
| M09 | Coolant OFF |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Machining Calculations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The numerical problems in KTU 2024 ESE frequently ask you to compute spindle speed, feed rate, or machining time. Memorize the formulas below. **Note: I use `\vert` instead of `|` to keep the markdown table intact.**

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spindle Speed (RPM) | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rev/min | $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D$ = tool/workpiece dia. (mm) |
| Feed Rate (mm/min) | $f_r = N \cdot f$ | mm/min | $f$ = feed per rev (mm/rev) |
| Feed Rate (mm/rev) from mm/min | $f = \dfrac{f_r}{N}$ | mm/rev | Inverse of above |
| Material Removal Rate | $MRR = a_p \cdot f \cdot V_c$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ = depth of cut (mm) |
| Machining Time (Turning) | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ | min | $L$ = total tool travel in feed direction |
| Spindle Speed for CSS (Lathe) | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D_{current}}$ | rev/min | $D_{current}$ varies along the cut; controller updates live |
| Effective Feed (Milling) | $f_{eff} = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f_z$ = feed/tooth, $z$ = number of teeth |
| Drilling Thrust | $F_z \approx 0.6 \cdot D \cdot f \cdot k_c$ | N | $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| Power Required | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60\,000}$ | kW | $F_c$ = cutting force (N) |

## 2.3 Coordinate System Logic (Why It Matters)

In **Turning**, the programmer uses **two axes only: X (radial) and Z (axial/longitudinal)**. The X-axis is on the diameter — so to reduce a Ø50 mm shaft to Ø40 mm, you command `X40.0`, not `X20.0`.

In **Milling**, three linear axes **X, Y, Z** plus optional rotary axes are used. The programmer specifies *tool tip positions* (centerline) for milling, but **must apply cutter radius compensation (G41/G42)** when the part profile is critical.

## 2.4 The Three Programming Modes

1. **Absolute (G90)** — Every coordinate is referenced to the *fixed workpiece origin*. Safer, easier to debug, and **mandatory in KTU lab** unless incremental is explicitly asked.
2. **Incremental (G91)** — Coordinates are referenced to the *current tool position*. Useful for repeated patterns (e.g., bolt-circle drilling).
3. **Polar / Mixed** — Some controllers allow polar coordinates (G16) for milling arcs using radius + angle.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Why does industry care about manual programming when CAM software exists?
> - **Debugging:** A shop-floor CNC operator *must* read a program line-by-line to find a crash cause. CAM-generated code is opaque.
> - **Prototype & repair work:** Manual programming is faster for one-off jobs.
> - **Cost:** CAM seats and post-processors are expensive; small job shops rely on manual code.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Programs & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are **three full, exam-ready manual part programs** — one each for Turning, Drilling (lathe), and Milling. Each program is preceded by a *Workpiece Drawing Interpretation*, followed by a *Line-by-Line Explanation*, and concludes with a *Verification Calculation* using the formulas from Section 2.

---

## 3.1 PROGRAM 1 — Turning Operation

### 3.1.1 Workpiece Specification

A mild-steel (EN8) bar of **Ø50 mm × 80 mm length** is chucked in a 2-axis CNC lathe. The final profile required is shown below:

- Step 1: Face the front face.
- Step 2: Turn Ø50 → Ø40 over a length of 30 mm (starting at Z = 0 to Z = -30).
- Step 3: Turn Ø40 → Ø30 over a length of 20 mm (Z = -30 to Z = -50).
- Step 4: Chamfer 2 × 45° at the shoulder.
- Step 5: Cut a groove at Z = -50, width 4 mm, depth 5 mm.
- Step 6: Part off at Z = -70 (leave 10 mm stock).

**Tooling:** T01 = CNMG 120408 (rough/finish turning), T03 = grooving tool (width 4 mm), T05 = parting tool (width 3 mm).
**Cutting parameters:** $V_c$ = 180 m/min, $f$ = 0.25 mm/rev (turning), $f$ = 0.10 mm/rev (grooving/parting).

### 3.1.2 Program Listing (Fanuc G-code)

```
O0001 (TURNING — STEPPED SHAFT)
N10  G21 G97 G99 G90          ; Metric, constant RPM, feed/rev, absolute
N20  G54                      ; Work offset 1 (set at front face center)
N30  M08                      ; Coolant ON
N40  T01 M06                  ; Tool change → Turning tool
N50  G97 S1500 M03            ; Spindle CW at 1500 RPM
N60  G00 X52.0 Z2.0           ; Rapid to safe start point
N70  G01 Z0 F0.25             ; Touch front face
N80  G00 X52.0 Z2.0           ; Retract
N90  G00 X48.0 Z2.0           ; Position for facing
N100 G01 Z-2.0 F0.15          ; Face across front (leave 2 mm face width)
N110 G00 X52.0 Z2.0           ; Retract
N120 G00 X40.5 Z2.0           ; Position for first turn pass
N130 G01 Z-30.0 F0.25         ; Turn Ø50 → Ø40 (first pass, leave 0.5 mm)
N140 G00 X52.0                ; Retract in X
N150 G00 Z2.0                 ; Retract in Z
N160 G00 X40.0 Z2.0           ; Position for finish pass
N170 G01 Z-30.0 F0.10         ; Finish turn to Ø40
N180 G01 X30.0 F0.15          ; Chamfer/shoulder approach
N190 G00 X52.0 Z2.0           ; Retract
N200 G00 X30.5 Z-28.0         ; Position for second step
N210 G01 Z-50.0 F0.25         ; Turn Ø40 → Ø30 (rough)
N220 G00 X52.0                ; Retract
N230 G00 Z2.0
N240 G00 X30.0 Z-28.0
N250 G01 Z-50.0 F0.10         ; Finish turn
N260 G01 X26.0 F0.15          ; Chamfer at second shoulder
N270 G00 X100.0 Z100.0        ; Go to tool-change position
N280  T03 M06                 ; Tool change → Grooving tool
N290 G97 S800 M03             ; Lower RPM for grooving
N300 G00 X32.0 Z-48.0         ; Approach groove
N310 G01 X24.0 F0.08          ; Plunge to depth 5 mm (Ø32 → Ø24, depth 4 radial)
N320 G04 X1.0                 ; Dwell 1 s for clean groove bottom
N330 G01 X32.0 F0.15          ; Retract from groove
N340 G00 X100.0 Z100.0
N350  T05 M06                 ; Tool change → Parting tool
N360 G97 S600 M03
N370 G00 X32.0 Z-69.0
N380 G01 X0.0 F0.06           ; Part off
N390 G04 X2.0
N400 G00 X100.0 Z100.0
N410 M09                      ; Coolant OFF
N420 M05                      ; Spindle stop
N430 M30                      ; Program end & rewind
```

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic

- **Line N10** establishes the *modal environment*: metric, constant RPM, feed per revolution, absolute coordinates. These stay active for the rest of the program unless changed.
- **Lines N20–N30** set the work zero (front face, centerline) and turn on coolant.
- **Line N50** computes the spindle speed: with Ø50 mm and $V_c = 180$ m/min, $N = 1000 \cdot 180 / (\pi \cdot 50) \approx 1146$ RPM. We round to **1500 RPM** to be safe (a common practice in labs).
- **Line N100** is the *facing pass* — the tool moves along Z from `+2.0` to `-2.0`, cutting the front face flat.
- **Lines N130–N170** are the *rough* and *finish* turning passes for the first step. We leave 0.5 mm radially for the finish pass (N170) so the surface is clean.
- **N180** produces the **2 × 45° chamfer** by feeding from X30.0 to X26.0 over Z = -30 to Z = -32 (the chamfer angle arises from the 2:2 slope).
- **N280–N330** switch to the grooving tool. **G04** in N320 creates a 1-second dwell to give a clean groove bottom (important for O-ring grooves).
- **N380** parts off the workpiece. We feed to X0 (the spindle centerline) at a slow feed (0.06 mm/rev) because parting generates high tool stress.

### 3.1.4 Verification Calculation

For the rough turning pass (N130, Ø50 → Ø40):
- Depth of cut: $a_p = (50 - 40) / 2 = 5 \text{ mm}$ (radial).
- Cutting length: $L = 30 \text{ mm}$.
- Feed: $f = 0.25 \text{ mm/rev}$, RPM = 1500.
- Feed rate: $f_r = 1500 \times 0.25 = 375 \text{ mm/min}$.
- Machining time:
$$
T_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{30}{375} = 0.08 \text{ min} \approx 4.8 \text{ s}
$$

---

## 3.2 PROGRAM 2 — Drilling Operation (on a CNC Lathe)

> [!NOTE]
> In a **CNC turning center**, drilling is performed with a **drill tool held in the tailstock turret or a powered cross-drilling attachment**. The canned cycles used are G81 (simple drill), G82 (drill + dwell), and G83 (peck drill). We illustrate **G81 + G80** and **G83** for a deep hole.

### 3.2.1 Workpiece Specification

Same Ø50 mm mild-steel bar. A **Ø10 mm through-hole** must be drilled at the centerline, **50 mm deep** from the front face. The part is to be drilled *before* parting off.

### 3.2.2 Program Listing (Fanuc G-code)

```
O0002 (DRILLING — DEEP HOLE PROGRAM)
N10  G21 G90 G98             ; Metric, absolute, feed/min (G98 = per min, retract to initial)
N20  G54
N30  T07 M06                 ; Tool change → Ø10 mm drill
N40  M08
N50  G97 S1200 M03           ; Spindle CW at 1200 RPM
N60  G00 X0.0 Z5.0           ; Rapid to hole center, 5 mm above face
N70  G99 G81 R2.0 Z-55.0 F80 ; Drilling cycle: R-point = 2 mm above face, Z = -55, F = 80 mm/min
N80  G80                     ; Cancel canned cycle
N90  G00 Z50.0               ; Retract drill
N100 M09
N110 M05
N120 M30
```

> [!WARNING]
> **For a 50 mm deep hole in mild steel with a Ø10 mm drill, G81 alone is risky** — chip evacuation will fail and the drill may break. Use **G83 (peck drilling)**:

```
N10  G21 G90 G99
N20  G54
N30  T07 M06
N40  M08
N50  G97 S1200 M03
N60  G00 X0.0 Z5.0
N70  G83 R2.0 Z-55.0 Q5.0 F80   ; Q5.0 = peck depth 5 mm (drill 5, retract fully, repeat)
N80  G80
N90  G00 Z50.0
N100 M09
N110 M05
N120 M30
```

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Logic

- **G99 (Feed per revolution)** is set so the drill feed is expressed in mm/rev consistently. (When using G98/G99 inside a canned cycle, the F value interpretation changes — verify with your controller.)
- **G81** is a *single-peck* cycle: rapid to R-point, feed to Z, rapid retract. Suitable only for shallow holes.
- **G83 Q5.0** breaks the 50 mm depth into **ten 5-mm pecks**, retracting fully after each peck to evacuate chips. Critical for hole depth > 3 × drill diameter.
- **G80** must be coded immediately after the cycle to **cancel** the modal canned cycle — otherwise the next motion block will trigger an unintended drill.

### 3.2.4 Verification

- Spindle speed: $N = 1000 \cdot 60 / (\pi \cdot 10) \approx 1910$ RPM (we conservatively use 1200 RPM).
- Feed per rev: $f = 80 / 1200 = 0.067 \text{ mm/rev}$ — within recommended range (0.05–0.15 mm/rev for Ø10 mm in mild steel).
- Number of pecks: $\lceil 55 / 5 \rceil = 11$ pecks (the last peck is partial).
- Total drilling time (ignoring retracts):
$$
T_m = \frac{55 \text{ mm}}{80 \text{ mm/min}} = 0.6875 \text{ min} \approx 41.3 \text{ s}
$$

---

## 3.3 PROGRAM 3 — Milling Operation (3-Axis VMC)

### 3.3.1 Workpiece Specification

A **rectangular pocket** is to be milled on a 120 mm × 80 mm × 20 mm mild-steel plate.
- Pocket dimensions: **60 mm (X) × 40 mm (Y) × 5 mm deep**.
- Center of pocket: **X60.0, Y40.0** (with G54 zero at the lower-left corner of the plate, top face).
- Use a **Ø10 mm flat-end mill, 4-flute**.
- Cutting parameters: $V_c$ = 60 m/min, $f_z$ = 0.04 mm/tooth, $a_e$ = 5 mm (radial), $a_p$ = 5 mm (axial).
- The pocket is to be **roughed out** with linear passes (zig-zag) — a common KTU lab exercise.

### 3.3.2 Parameter Calculation

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot 10} \approx 1910 \text{ RPM (use 1800 RPM)}
$$

$$
f_{eff} = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.04 \cdot 4 \cdot 1800 = 288 \text{ mm/min (use 280 mm/min)}
$$

### 3.3.3 Program Listing (Fanuc G-code)

```
O0003 (MILLING — RECTANGULAR POCKET)
N10  G21 G90 G94 G17 G54      ; Metric, absolute, feed/min, XY plane, work offset
N20  M08
N30  T01 M06                  ; Tool change → Ø10 mm end mill
N40  M03 S1800                ; Spindle CW at 1800 RPM
N50  G00 X10.0 Y10.0 Z50.0    ; Rapid to safe position above pocket corner
N60  Z5.0                     ; Rapid to 5 mm above top face
N70  G01 Z-5.0 F60            ; Plunge to depth 5 mm at 60 mm/min
N80  G01 X110.0 Y10.0 F280    ; Pass 1: along X+
N90  G01 Y20.0                ; Step in Y+ by 10 mm (= 0.8 × D, proper step-over)
N100 G01 X10.0                ; Pass 2: along X-
N110 G01 Y30.0                ; Step in Y+
N120 G01 X110.0               ; Pass 3: along X+
N130 G01 Y40.0                ; Step in Y+
N140 G01 X10.0                ; Pass 4: along X-
N150 G01 Z50.0 F300           ; Retract in Z
N160 G00 X0.0 Y0.0            ; Return to home
N170 M09
N180 M05
N190 M30
```

### 3.3.4 Step-by-Step Logic

- **N10** sets the *global modal environment*: G17 selects the **XY plane** (essential for circular interpolation later, if added).
- **N60** positions the tool 5 mm above the plate, in *rapid* mode.
- **N70** is the *plunge move* — a *vertical* feed into the material at a *reduced* feed (60 mm/min, typically ~30% of the cutting feed) to avoid end-mill breakage.
- **N80 → N140** are the **zig-zag milling passes**. The step-over is 10 mm, which is **1 × D** (100% of tool diameter). For roughing, 50–100% step-over is acceptable; for finishing, 10–25% is standard.
- **N150** retracts in Z first, *then* in XY, to avoid dragging the end mill across the finished surface.
- **N190 (M30)** ends the program and rewinds to the start, ready for the next part.

### 3.3.5 Verification Calculation

- Total cut length (X passes): $4 \times 100 \text{ mm} = 400 \text{ mm}$.
- Total cut length (Y steps): $4 \times 10 \text{ mm} = 40 \text{ mm}$.
- Total cut length: $L = 440 \text{ mm}$.
- Cutting feed: $f_r = 280 \text{ mm/min}$.
- Machining time (cutting only, no plunge/retract):
$$
T_m = \frac{440}{280} = 1.57 \text{ min} \approx 94.5 \text{ s}
$$

---

## 3.4 Summary Matrix — Program Comparison

| Aspect | Turning | Drilling | Milling |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of axes used | 2 (X, Z) | 2 (X, Z) for lathe; 3 (X, Y, Z) for VMC | 3 (X, Y, Z) |
| Modal G-codes | G21, G90, G99, G97 | G21, G90, G99 | G21, G90, G94, G17 |
| Canned cycle used | G70/G71 (not shown above) | G81 / G83 | G81 (if drilling on mill) |
| Tool change command | T__ M06 | T__ M06 | T__ M06 |
| Typical feed unit | mm/rev (G99) | mm/rev (G99) | mm/min (G94) |
| Coolant strategy | Flood (M08) | Flood (M08) | Flood or MQL |
| Most common KTU mistake | Forgetting diameter vs. radius in X | Not using G83 for deep holes | Forgetting G17 for arc plane |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNC Program Execution Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Part Drawing] --> B[Identify Machining Operations]
    B --> C[Select Workpiece Zero G54]
    C --> D[Select Tools T-codes]
    D --> E[Compute Spindle Speed N from Vc and D]
    E --> F[Compute Feed Rate fr from f and N]
    F --> G[Write Program Block by Block]
    G --> H[Verify Syntax and Modal States]
    H --> I[Simulate via Dry Run]
    I --> J{Any Collision / Error?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Edit and Re-Simulate]
    K --> I
    J -- No --> L[Run on Machine with Reduced Feed]
    L --> M[Measure and Verify Dimensions]
    M --> N{Within Tolerance?}
    N -- No --> O[Apply Tool Offset Compensation]
    O --> L
    N -- Yes --> P[End: Program Complete]
```

## 4.2 Turning Center — Tool Path Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WP[Workpiece: Initial Dia 50mm, Length 80mm]
    direction LR
    A0[Start X52 Z2] --> A1[Face Z-2]
    A1 --> A2[Turn to X40 Z-30]
    A2 --> A3[Turn to X30 Z-50]
    A3 --> A4[Chamfer to X26 Z-52]
    A4 --> A5[Groove at Z-50 to X24]
    A5 --> A6[Part off to X0 Z-70]
    end
    style WP fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Milling Center — Pocket Tool Path (Zig-Zag)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Pocket[Rectangular Pocket: 60mm x 40mm]
    direction LR
    P0[Safe Z 50mm] --> P1[Plunge Z-5mm at X10 Y10]
    P1 --> P2[Pass1: X110 Y10]
    P2 --> P3[Step Y+10 to Y20]
    P3 --> P4[Pass2: X10 Y20]
    P4 --> P5[Step Y+10 to Y30]
    P5 --> P6[Pass3: X110 Y30]
    P6 --> P7[Step Y+10 to Y40]
    P7 --> P8[Pass4: X10 Y40]
    P8 --> P9[Retract Z50]
    P9 --> P10[Return Home X0 Y0]
    end
    style Pocket fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
