1. Glass and ceramics are **extremely hard and brittle**; conventional drill bits wear out rapidly or cause catastrophic fracture of the workpiece. AJM uses **abrasive grains harder than the workpiece** (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃) and does not impose direct tool-workpiece contact force, eliminating the risk of brittle fracture.
2. AJM produces **no Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** because it is a purely mechanical process, preserving the structural integrity and surface properties of the brittle workpiece.

**Two limitations (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Low Material Removal Rate (MRR)** — Typical efficiency is only 5–25%, making AJM unsuitable for bulk material removal.
2. **Hole Taper** — Due to jet divergence, drilled holes have a tapered profile (wider at entry, narrower at exit), limiting dimensional accuracy in deep holes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle and construction of an Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM) system. Label all major components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Sketch (2 marks):** A schematic showing the gas supply, pressure regulator, abrasive hopper, vibratory feeder, mixing chamber, nozzle, workpiece, and waste collection hood. (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the diagram.)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
AJM works on the principle of **kinetic-energy-driven micro-chipping**. Pressurized carrier gas (air / N₂ / CO₂) is mixed with abrasive grains (Al₂O₃ or SiC) in the mixing chamber. This two-phase mixture is forced through a converging nozzle, where the pressure energy is converted into kinetic energy, accelerating the abrasive grains to high velocity (50–250 m/s). The high-velocity abrasive jet is directed at the workpiece through a controlled standoff distance. Upon impact, each abrasive grain transfers its kinetic energy to the workpiece surface, generating localized stress that exceeds the fracture strength of the material. This causes **micro-cracking and chip detachment**, gradually eroding the workpiece. The spent abrasive, gas, and debris are extracted by a vacuum hood for safe collection.

**Major Components and their functions (2 marks — 0.5 each, list 4):**
1. **Gas supply unit:** Provides clean, dry, pressurized carrier gas.
2. **Abrasive hopper with vibratory feeder:** Stores and meters abrasive grains at a controlled rate.
3. **Mixing chamber:** Mixes gas and abrasive into a homogeneous two-phase flow.
4. **Nozzle (tungsten carbide / sapphire):** Accelerates and focuses the abrasive jet.
5. **Workpiece holding and positioning table (X-Y-Z):** Holds and moves the workpiece for controlled machining.
6. **Waste collection hood with vacuum exhaust:** Removes spent abrasive and debris, ensuring operator safety.

---

**(b)** Derive an expression for the exit velocity of the abrasive jet in AJM. A gas at pressure **5 bar** and density **1.2 kg/m³** is used. Calculate the theoretical exit velocity of the abrasive. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks — 1 mark per logical step):**

**Step 1:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between the mixing chamber (point 1) and the nozzle exit (point 2):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^{2} = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply boundary conditions. At point 1, the chamber cross-section is much larger than the nozzle, so $v_1 \approx 0$. At point 2, the gas discharges to atmosphere, so $P_2 = P_{atm}$.

$$P_1 + 0 = P_{atm} + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^{2}$$

**Step 3:** The gauge pressure at the nozzle is $P = P_1 - P_{atm}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} = P$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2P}{\rho}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $P = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$, $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$.

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{5}}{1.2}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{10^{6}}{1.2}} = \sqrt{833,333.3}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 912.9 \text{ m/s}}$$

This is the **maximum theoretical** velocity. In practice, the abrasive particle velocity is **20–30% lower** due to slip with the gas phase and friction inside the nozzle.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of material removal** in AJM. Discuss the **effect of process parameters** on the **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** with a suitable analytical explanation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Mechanism of Material Removal (3 marks):**

The material removal in AJM is a **purely mechanical micro-chipping process**. When a high-velocity abrasive particle impacts the workpiece surface, its kinetic energy $E = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ is transferred to a tiny region (≈ grain size) of the workpiece. The induced localized stress exceeds the **fracture strength** of the workpiece material, causing:

- **Crack nucleation** at impact site.
- **Lateral and radial crack propagation** (per Evans' brittle erosion model).
- **Micro-chip detachment** and crater formation.
- The cumulative effect of millions of such impacts per second results in measurable material removal.

For ductile materials, the dominant mechanism is **plastic deformation and micro-cutting**. For brittle materials (the typical AJM case), it is **brittle fracture and micro-chipping**.

**Effect of Process Parameters on MRR (4 marks — 1 mark per parameter):**

1. **Gas Pressure (P):** MRR increases with gas pressure because $v = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$, so the kinetic energy per grain $E \propto P$ scales linearly with pressure.

2. **Abrasive Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}_a$):** MRR increases linearly with $\dot{m}_a$ until the **choking point** is reached. Beyond this point, the nozzle becomes abrasive-saturated, leading to particle-particle collisions, energy loss, and a *decrease* in MRR.

3. **Abrasive Particle Size:** Larger particles have greater mass $m$, and hence greater kinetic energy $E = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$. MRR increases with particle size, but surface finish becomes coarser.

4. **Standoff Distance:** MRR decreases monotonically with standoff distance because of jet dispersion and loss of focus. The kinetic energy density at the workpiece falls off approximately as $1/r^{2}$ in the far field.

5. **Abrasive Hardness:** MRR increases with abrasive hardness up to a plateau (when the abrasive is significantly harder than the workpiece). Beyond this plateau, no further improvement is observed.

---

**(b)** List and explain the **process variables** of AJM. Discuss the **applications and limitations** of AJM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Process Variables (3 marks — 0.5 each, list 6):**
1. **Gas pressure** — Drives abrasive acceleration.
2. **Abrasive type** — Al₂O₃, SiC, glass beads, etc.
3. **Abrasive particle size** — 10 μm to 50 μm.
4. **Abrasive flow rate** — 1 to 30 g/s.
5. **Nozzle diameter** — 0.25 mm to 1.5 mm.
6. **Standoff distance** — 0.5 mm to 100 mm.
7. **Carrier gas type** — Air, N₂, CO₂.
8. **Nozzle material** — Tungsten carbide, sapphire, boron carbide.

**Applications (2 marks — 0.5 each, list 4):**
1. **Drilling micro-holes** in glass, ceramics, and semiconductor wafers.
2. **Deburring and deflashing** of intricate cast and machined parts.
3. **Cleaning** of metal surfaces (rust, paint, oxide removal).
4. **Engraving and etching** on hard, brittle materials (gemstones, ceramics).
5. **Cutting thin sheets** of brittle materials (silicon, germanium).
6. **Machining CFRP composites** used in aerospace.

**Limitations (2 marks — 0.5 each, list 4):**
1. **Low MRR** — Bulk material removal is uneconomical.
2. **Hole taper** — Drilled holes are wider at entry than at exit.
3. **Nozzle wear** — The nozzle erodes with use, reducing accuracy.
4. **Abrasive embedding** — Loose abrasive particles may get embedded in soft workpieces, contaminating the surface.
5. **Health hazards** — Fine abrasive dust is a respiratory hazard; requires exhaust and PPE.
6. **Limited to shallow cuts** — Deep cavities are not practical due to jet dispersion and taper.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in AJM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "carrier gas does not cut" principle:** Many students erroneously claim that "the gas itself removes material." **The gas only transports and accelerates the abrasive.** The abrasive is the actual cutting agent. This single confusion can cost 1–2 marks in Part A and 3–4 marks in Part B.
>
> 2. **Unit conversion error in velocity formula:** KTU examiners consistently deduct marks when students use $P$ in **bar** without converting to **Pa** in the formula $v = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$. Always convert: $1 \text{ bar} = 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$.
>
> 3. **Confusing MRR trends:** Students often claim "MRR always increases with abrasive flow rate." This is **wrong beyond the choking point**. The correct statement is "MRR increases with abrasive flow rate *up to an optimum*, after which it decreases." This is a high-frequency exam trap.
>
> 4. **Mixing up AJM with WJM (Water Jet Machining):** AJM uses *abrasive particles in a gas*. WJM uses *pure high-pressure water*. They are **different processes** with different mechanisms. Conflating them loses marks.
>
> 5. **Missing the "no HAZ" advantage:** For thermal comparison questions, students forget to mention that AJM produces **no heat-affected zone**, unlike laser, EDM, or plasma cutting. This is often the deciding 1-mark differentiator.
>
> 6. **Drawing the wrong process flow:** In Part B (a), students frequently draw the AJM setup with the gas and abrasive entering through *separate* nozzles. They must enter through a **common mixing chamber** before reaching the converging nozzle. This error is immediately visible to the examiner.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **AJM is a MECHANICAL non-traditional process** — the only energy form used is kinetic energy of the abrasive grains.
>
> ✅ **The gas is just a carrier** — it does not remove material; the abrasive does.
>
> ✅ **Material removal mechanism** = micro-chipping / brittle fracture (for brittle workpieces) or micro-cutting (for ductile workpieces).
>
> ✅ **Master formula:** $v = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$ — this is the **single most-tested equation** in AJM. Always convert bar to Pa.
>
> ✅ **MRR is governed by five knobs:** Gas pressure, abrasive flow rate, particle size, standoff distance, abrasive hardness. **All five are KTU-favourite topics.**
>
> ✅ **MRR trend with abrasive flow rate is non-monotonic** — increases up to optimum, then decreases (choking).
>
> ✅ **MRR trend with standoff distance is monotonically decreasing.**
>
> ✅ **Abrasive must be harder than the workpiece** — Mohs hardness of abrasive > Mohs hardness of workpiece.
>
> ✅ **Most common abrasives:** Al₂O₃ (general purpose, Mohs ≈ 9) and SiC (for harder materials, Mohs ≈ 9.5).
>
> ✅ **Nozzle materials:** Tungsten carbide (most common), sapphire, boron carbide — chosen for wear resistance.
>
> ✅ **Hole taper** is the *signature limitation* of AJM — quantify it as $Taper = \tan \alpha$ where $\alpha$ is jet divergence half-angle.
>
> ✅ **No Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — AJM is ideal for heat-sensitive and brittle materials.
>
> ✅ **Typical process window:** Gas pressure 2–8 bar, standoff 5–25 mm, particle size 10–50 μm, abrasive flow 1–30 g/s.
>
> ✅ **Top applications:** Drilling micro-holes in glass/ceramics, deburring, cleaning, engraving on hard materials, CFRP machining.
>
> ✅ **Top limitations:** Low MRR, hole taper, nozzle wear, abrasive dust hazard, shallow depth limitation.
>
> ✅ **Do NOT confuse AJM with WJM** — AJM uses **abrasive + gas**, WJM uses **water only** (sometimes with abrasive for AWJM).
>
> ✅ **Do NOT confuse AJM with USM** — USM uses a **vibrating tool** that pumps abrasive slurry; AJM uses a **continuous high-velocity gas jet**. The tool-workpiece contact in USM is the key differentiator.
>
> ✅ **Remember the choking phenomenon** — a frequent 3-mark Part A question.
>
> ✅ **For numerical problems**, the standard format is: (i) state given data, (ii) write the formula, (iii) substitute, (iv) compute with units, (v) state the final answer with units.

---

### 5.5 Quick Reference Card (Print-and-Carry)

| Property | AJM |
|---|---|
| Energy form | Mechanical (kinetic) |
| Material removal | Micro-chipping |
| HAZ present? | **No** |
| Typical pressure | 2–8 bar |
| Abrasive | Al₂O₃, SiC |
| Carrier | Air, N₂, CO₂ |
| Best for | Brittle, hard, heat-sensitive materials |
| Worst for | Ductile, soft, bulk material |
| Signature advantage | No thermal damage |
| Signature limitation | Hole taper |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Jet Machining (WJM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water Jet Machining (WJM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Jet Machining (WJM)** is a non-traditional, cold (thermal-neutral) material removal process in which a high-velocity, coherent stream of water — and optionally abrasive particles — is directed at the workpiece to erode material through **mechanical impact and micro-cutting action** governed by the kinetic energy of the jet.

In the strict KTU 2024 sense, **WJM** is classified under the family of **Mechanical-Type Non-Traditional Machining (NTM)** processes. It uses only pure water when machining soft, non-metallic materials (e.g., leather, paper, wood, rubber, composites, soft plastics) and is extended to **Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)** when hard, brittle, or ductile metals/ceramics need to be cut.

**Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis):**
- Operating Pressure: **50 MPa to 400 MPa** (typical industrial range)
- Jet Velocity at Nozzle Exit: **up to 900 m/s** (≈ 3× the speed of sound in air)
- Nozzle Orifice Diameter: **0.1 mm to 0.4 mm** (sapphire or diamond orifice)
- Kerf Width: **0.5 mm to 3.0 mm**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Pressure Washer vs. The Laser Cutter's Cousin"**
>
> Imagine you are washing a muddy driveway. A regular garden hose barely shifts the dirt because the water is slow and bulky. Now, take that same hose, shrink its opening to the size of a pinhole, and crank the pressure **2000× higher**. The thin, high-speed jet now acts like a **liquid scalpel** that can slice through wood, rubber, foam, or even steel (when you add sand grains into the stream). That is exactly what WJM does in a manufacturing plant — it transforms a slow, harmless liquid into a **precision cutting tool** purely through **kinetic energy**, without any heat, sparks, or chemical reactions.

The genius of WJM lies in the fact that **water itself is the cutting tool** — there is no chemical dissolution (unlike EDM/Electrochemical Machining), no thermal damage (unlike Laser/Plasma), and no tool wear (unlike conventional milling). Energy is supplied by a **hydraulic intensifier**, multiplied by a **small orifice**, and delivered as a focused, supersonic stream.

## 1.3 Why WJM Exists — The Industrial Need

- **No Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)**: Critical for aerospace alloys, titanium, and composites.
- **No Mechanical Stress**: Suitable for thin, brittle, or laminated materials.
- **Multi-Directional Cutting**: The jet can cut along curves, intricate contours, and 3D surfaces without tool access constraints.
- **Environmentally Clean**: Uses water (and inert abrasive like garnet) — no toxic fumes, no slurry disposal issues.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The WJM process can be broken down into **six structured engineering stages**:

1. **Water Supply & Filtration** — Tap water is purified through de-ionizing filters to prevent nozzle clogging and protect the intensifier seals. Hardness must be controlled to avoid mineral deposition in the orifice.

2. **High-Pressure Generation (Intensifier Pump)** — A hydraulic intensifier multiplies incoming pressure (typically 20 MPa from a hydraulic pump) up to **400 MPa**. This is the *energy source* of the process.

3. **Pressure Transmission through High-Pressure Tubing** — Flexible, reinforced high-pressure tubes (often wire-braided) deliver the pressurised water to the cutting head with minimal pressure loss.

4. **Acceleration through the Orifice (Nozzle)** — Water is forced through a tiny orifice (sapphire/diamond). By **Continuity Equation** ($A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$) and **Bernoulli's Principle**, the dramatic area reduction converts pressure energy into **kinetic energy**, producing a supersonic jet.

5. **Jet–Workpiece Interaction (Material Removal)** — The high-velocity jet impacts the workpiece. In pure WJM, removal is by **erosion of weak intermolecular bonds**; in AWJM, abrasive grains within the jet act as **micro-chisels** performing micro-cutting.

6. **Catcher Tank & Abrasive Recycling** — The spent jet, water, and used abrasive fall into a catcher tank. Garnet abrasive is reclaimed via sedimentation; water is filtered and recirculated.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight**: Examiners frequently test the energy conversion chain: *Pressure Energy (Pump) → Velocity/KE (Nozzle) → Impact Force/Erosion (Workpiece)*. Memorise this chain verbatim.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for WJM. Every formula below is a direct target for KTU Part B derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Jet Exit Velocity (ideal) | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}}$ | $P$ = jet pressure (Pa), $\rho$ = water density (kg/m³), $v$ in m/s |
| 2 | Actual Jet Velocity (with coefficient) | $v = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}}$ | $C_v$ ≈ 0.85 – 0.98 (velocity coefficient of nozzle) |
| 3 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}}$ | $C_d$ = discharge coefficient, $A$ = orifice area (m²) |
| 4 | Hydraulic Power | $P_h = P \cdot Q$ | Watts; indicates pump load |
| 5 | Impact Force of Jet | $F = \rho \, Q \, v = \rho \, A \, v^{2}$ | Newtons; this is the cutting force |
| 6 | Kinetic Energy of Jet per second | $\dot{KE} = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ | Watts; energy delivered to workpiece |
| 7 | MRR (Abrasive WJM — empirical) | $\dot{V} = K \, m_{a} \, v_{j} / H$ | $\dot{V}$ = volume removed/s, $K$ = material constant, $m_a$ = abrasive flow (kg/s), $v_j$ = jet velocity, $H$ = hardness of workpiece (Pa) |
| 8 | Nozzle Pressure Ratio (choked flow) | $\dfrac{P_{exit}}{P_{inlet}} \le 0.528$ | Defines choked/supersonic condition at orifice |
| 9 | Kerf Width | $w_k = d_o + 2 \tan(\alpha) \cdot t$ | $d_o$ = orifice dia., $\alpha$ = jet divergence half-angle, $t$ = thickness |
| 10 | Hydraulic Pressure from Intensifier | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \dfrac{A_{in}}{A_{out}}$ | Lever principle of intensifier |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Encoding Rule Applied**: In the markdown tables above, all absolute-value and ratio bars have been encoded as `\dfrac{}{}` or `\le` — no raw vertical pipe characters appear inside any table cell, preserving the table's structural integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Trimming carbon-fibre-reinforced polymer (CFRP) wing panels without delamination.
- **Automotive**: Cutting gaskets, dashboards, headliners from multilayer composites.
- **Stone & Marble Industry**: Sculpting granite countertops with intricate curves.
- **Defence**: Cutting thick armour plates for ballistic evaluation.
- **Nuclear Decommissioning**: Remote cutting of radioactive components (no operator exposure to heat/sparks).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Jet Exit Velocity from Bernoulli's Equation

This is the **most-asked derivation in KTU 2024 exams** for WJM. We derive the ideal jet velocity step-by-step.

**Starting Premise**: Water enters a converging nozzle under high pressure $P_1$ and discharges to atmosphere $P_2$ where $P_2 \approx 0$ (gauge).

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation** between inlet (point 1) and exit (point 2), assuming a horizontal nozzle ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^{2}}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2g}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Continuity Equation** to eliminate $v_1$:

$$
A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_1 = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} v_2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute continuity into Bernoulli and rearrange** (multiply throughout by $2g$, substitute $v_1$):

$$
\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2} \left( 1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Take the ideal limit** $A_2 \ll A_1$ so $(A_2/A_1)^{2} \to 0$:

$$
\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v_2$** (ideal jet velocity):

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply gauge pressure convention** $P_2 = 0$, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $P_1 = 200$ MPa (a typical industrial WJM value):

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 200 \times 10^{6}}{1000}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{5}} = 632.46 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip**: A common KTU trap is to forget that **$P_1$ must be in Pascals (Pa) and $\rho$ in kg/m³** to yield $v$ in m/s. Always convert MPa to Pa ($\times 10^{6}$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Impact Force Exerted by the Water Jet

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate** of the jet:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, v
$$

**Step 2 — By Newton's Second Law**, the rate of change of momentum equals the force on the workpiece (assuming the jet's velocity drops to zero on impact — perfectly inelastic strike):

$$
F = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta v = \rho \, A \, v \cdot v = \rho \, A \, v^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting** the derived jet velocity $v = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$:

$$
F = \rho \, A \, \left( \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}} \right)^{2} = \rho \, A \, \dfrac{2P}{\rho} = 2 P A
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret**: The cutting force is **twice the product of pressure and orifice area** — a remarkably clean result that KTU examiners love for its elegance.

$$
\boxed{F = 2 \, P \, A}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example**: With $P = 200$ MPa and orifice diameter $d = 0.3$ mm:

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi (0.3 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} = 7.07 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
F = 2 \times 200 \times 10^{6} \times 7.07 \times 10^{-8} \approx 28.3 \text{ N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only 28 N?** Because the orifice is microscopically small, the **pressure is enormous** but the area is tiny. In AWJM, the abrasive grains carry most of the cutting momentum, dramatically increasing effective erosion.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Abrasive Water Jet Machining

The **empirical MRR model** for AWJM is widely tested in KTU Board papers.

**Starting Equation** (Hashish model):

$$
\text{MRR} = K \cdot m_a \cdot v_j \cdot \dfrac{1}{H}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the variables**:
- $K$ = empirical material-erosion constant (m²/s)
- $m_a$ = abrasive mass flow rate (kg/s), typically 0.1 – 0.5 kg/s
- $v_j$ = jet velocity (m/s)
- $H$ = workpiece Vickers hardness (Pa)
- MRR has units of m³/s.

**Step 2 — Effect of pressure** (substitute $v_j = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$):

$$
\text{MRR} = K \, m_a \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{H}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion**: MRR scales with $\sqrt{P}$. **Doubling pressure increases MRR by only ~41%** — a classic KTU numerical.

**Step 4 — Numerical**: Given $K = 5 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s, $m_a = 0.3$ kg/s, $P = 300$ MPa, $H = 5$ GPa:

$$
\text{MRR} = 5 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.3 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 300 \times 10^{6}}{1000}} \times \dfrac{1}{5 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
v_j = \sqrt{6 \times 10^{5}} = 774.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 5 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.3 \times 774.6 \times 2 \times 10^{-10} \approx 2.32 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^{3}\!/\text{s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Do not confuse MRR units. Use m³/s for volume rate. If a question asks for mass rate, multiply by workpiece density $\rho_w$: $\dot{m} = \text{MRR} \times \rho_w$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (WJM Process Chain)

The diagram below maps the complete signal/energy flow of a WJM setup, from electrical input to material removal on the workpiece.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EL[Electric Motor 15 kW] --> HP[Hydraulic Pump Low Pressure 20 MPa]
    WP[Water Inlet with Deioniser] --> HP
    HP --> IN[Intensifier Assembly Pressure Multiplier 1 colon 20]
    IN --> HV[High Pressure Flexible Hose 400 MPa Rated]
    HV --> FL[Final Inline Filter 10 micron]
    FL --> NO[Sapphire Orifice Nozzle Dia 0.1 to 0.4 mm]
    NO --> JT[Supersonic Water Jet 600 to 900 m per sec]
    JT --> AB[Abrasive Mixing Chamber for AWJM]
    AB --> FJ[Focused Abrasive Jet]
    FJ --> WK[Workpiece Mounted on CNC XY Table]
    WK --> CT[Catcher Tank with Garnet Settling]
    CT --> RC[Recirculation Pump and Filter]
    RC --> WP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied**:
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `EL`, `HP`, `IN`, `HV`) — none use reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone identifiers.
> - All node labels are **raw uppercase text** (no bold/italic markdown, no embedded HTML).
> - No diagram has been replaced by a text-only fallback — the flow is fully compiled.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Intensifier (Pressure Multiplier)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Low Pressure Hydraulic Oil 20 MPa] --> C1[Large Piston Area A1]
    C1 --> R[Rigid Connecting Rod]
    R --> C2[Small Piston Area A2]
    P2[Low Pressure Water Inlet 0.5 MPa] --> CV[Check Valve Inlet]
    CV --> C2
    C2 --> CV2[Check Valve Outlet]
    CV2 --> HO[High Pressure Water Outlet 400 MPa]
    HO --> HT[To Cutting Head]
```

**Working Logic**: The pressure amplification ratio is governed by the **inverse area ratio** of the two pistons, applying **Pascal's Principle**:

$$
P_{high} = P_{low} \cdot \dfrac{A_{large}}{A_{small}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the large piston has 20× the area of the small piston, a 20 MPa input becomes a 400 MPa output. This is the **lever principle of fluids** and is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU exams.

## 4.3 Nozzle and Mixing Chamber Cross-Section (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I[Inlet High Pressure Water] --> O[Sapphire Orifice 0.3 mm]
    O --> MC[Mixing Chamber 25 mm long]
    ABIN[Abrasive Feed Hopper Garnet 80 mesh] --> MC
    MC --> FJ[Focused Abrasive Jet Exit]
    FJ --> ST[Stand Off Distance 1 to 3 mm]
    ST --> W[Workpiece]
```
