# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between pure Water Jet Machining (WJM) and Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (Board Key Pattern):**

| Parameter | Pure WJM | AWJM |
|-----------|----------|------|
| Cutting Medium | High-pressure water only | Water + abrasive particles (garnet/Al₂O₃) |
| Maximum Operating Pressure | 50 – 400 MPa | 50 – 400 MPa (pressure is lower) |
| Velocity of Jet | 600 – 900 m/s | 200 – 400 m/s (abrasive slows the jet) |
| Material Range | Soft, non-metals (wood, leather, plastic, paper) | Hard metals, ceramics, stone, composites |
| MRR | Very low (suitable for soft cutting) | 10–100× higher than pure WJM |
| Typical Application | Cutting food, foam, paper, cloth | Cutting titanium, granite, Inconel |

*(Award 1 mark for media, 1 mark for material range, 1 mark for MRR or velocity distinction.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any four advantages of Water Jet Machining."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **No Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** — workpiece remains at room temperature, preserving metallurgical properties. *(1 mark)*
2. **No Mechanical Stresses or Vibrations** — ideal for thin, brittle, or laminated components. *(0.75 mark)*
3. **Multi-directional cutting** — the jet can cut along any 2D curve and 3D contours without tool access. *(0.75 mark)*
4. **Environmentally clean** — uses water and natural garnet; no toxic fumes, no slurry, easily recyclable. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full 14 marks).

---

### **🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 2]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle and main components of a Water Jet Machining (WJM) system. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A WJM setup uses a 0.25 mm diameter orifice operating at 250 MPa pressure. Calculate: (i) ideal jet exit velocity, (ii) volumetric flow rate, and (iii) impact force on the workpiece. Take $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Part (a)** *(7 Marks)*

**Working Principle** *(2 marks)*: Water Jet Machining removes material by **directed kinetic energy** of a high-velocity water jet. The cutting action is purely mechanical (no heat, no chemical reaction), making it ideal for materials that are **heat-sensitive** or **mechanically delicate**.

**Main Components** *(5 marks — 1 mark each)*:

1. **High-Pressure Pump / Intensifier** — Generates pressures up to 400 MPa from a low-pressure (20 MPa) hydraulic supply.
2. **Water Filtration & Softening Unit** — Removes ions and particulates that could clog the orifice.
3. **High-Pressure Tubing & Fittings** — Reinforced flexible hoses (burst pressure > 3× working pressure) for safe delivery.
4. **Nozzle Assembly** — Sapphire/diamond orifice (0.1 – 0.4 mm) that converts pressure energy to kinetic energy.
5. **Catcher Tank** — Collects spent water, abrasive, and cut chips; recycles the abrasive.
6. **CNC Positioning Table** — Moves the workpiece under the stationary jet for accurate cutting paths.

> *(For block diagram, students should reproduce the architecture shown in Section 4.1 — full marks for accurate component labelling.)*

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Part (b)** *(7 Marks)*

**Given**: $d = 0.25$ mm, $P = 250$ MPa = $250 \times 10^{6}$ Pa, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Orifice area**:
$$
A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.25 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — (i) Ideal jet velocity** *(2.5 marks)*:
$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 250 \times 10^{6}}{1000}} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{5}} = 707.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Volumetric flow rate** *(2 marks)*: Taking $C_d \approx 0.85$ (typical sapphire orifice value):
$$
Q = C_d \, A \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}} = 0.85 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \times 707.1
$$
$$
Q \approx 2.95 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}\!/\text{s} \;=\; 29.5 \text{ mL/s} \;=\; 1.77 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Impact force** *(2.5 marks)*:
$$
F = \rho A v^{2} = 1000 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \times (707.1)^{2}
$$
$$
F = 1000 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \times 5 \times 10^{5} = 24.54 \text{ N}
$$

*(Alternatively, using $F = 2PA$ shortcut: $F = 2 \times 250 \times 10^{6} \times 4.909 \times 10^{-8} = 24.54$ N — same answer, full credit.)*

**Final Tabulated Results**:
- Jet velocity: **707.1 m/s**
- Volumetric flow: **29.5 mL/s**
- Impact force: **24.54 N**

---

### **🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain the role of an **intensifier** in WJM. Derive the relation for pressure amplification in a hydraulic intensifier. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **process parameters** that affect Material Removal Rate (MRR) and **surface quality** in Abrasive Water Jet Machining. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Part (a)** *(7 Marks)*

**Role of the Intensifier** *(3 marks)*: The intensifier is the **pressure-multiplication heart** of the WJM system. It accepts a low-pressure hydraulic supply (typically 20 MPa) and delivers ultra-high pressure water (200 – 400 MPa) suitable for cutting. Without it, conventional pumps would not be able to generate the pressures needed for material removal at industrial flow rates. It also **decouples the high-pressure water from the hydraulic oil** via dynamic seals.

**Derivation of Pressure Amplification** *(4 marks)*:

Consider a hydraulic intensifier with a large piston of area $A_1$ and a small piston of area $A_2$, mechanically connected by a rigid rod.

**Step 1 — Apply Pascal's Law / Force balance** on the rigid rod (the system is in static equilibrium momentarily during the stroke):

$$
P_1 \cdot A_1 = P_2 \cdot A_2
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $P_2$** (the high-pressure side):

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the pressure multiplication ratio** $N$:

$$
N = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration**: If $A_1 = 2000$ mm², $A_2 = 100$ mm², $P_1 = 20$ MPa:
$$
P_2 = 20 \times \dfrac{2000}{100} = 400 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conclusion**: A 20:1 area ratio yields 20× pressure amplification. This is the **fluid-lever principle** underlying all WJM intensifiers.

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Part (b)** *(7 Marks)*

**Process Parameters Affecting MRR** *(3.5 marks — 0.5 each)*:

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Trend |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| Jet Pressure $P$ | Increases jet velocity → more KE | MRR $\propto \sqrt{P}$ |
| Abrasive Mass Flow $m_a$ | More cutting edges per second | MRR $\propto m_a$ |
| Abrasive Type | Hardness/shape of grains | Garnet > Aluminium oxide for most metals |
| Abrasive Size (mesh) | Larger grains → deeper penetration | Optimum around 60–80 mesh |
| Standoff Distance | Jet divergence increases with distance | Optimum 1–3 mm |
| Traverse Speed | Time of jet-workpiece contact | Lower speed → higher MRR |
| Workpiece Hardness | Higher H → harder to erode | MRR $\propto 1/H$ |

**Process Parameters Affecting Surface Quality** *(3.5 marks)*:

- **Smoother surfaces** result from: **higher traverse speed, smaller abrasive mesh, lower pressure, and increased standoff distance** (to allow jet dispersion to be gentler).
- **Rougher surfaces** result from: slower traverse (over-cutting), larger abrasive grains, very high pressure.
- A typical surface roughness $R_a$ for AWJM ranges from **0.8 µm to 6.3 µm**, which is comparable to conventional milling — a major reason AWJM is used in aerospace finishing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder** — Forgetting to convert MPa → Pa. *Loss: 1–2 marks per sub-question.*
> 2. **Confusing $C_v$ with $C_d$** — $C_v$ is velocity coefficient (~0.97 for sapphire), $C_d$ is discharge coefficient (~0.85). They are **not interchangeable**. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Writing "WJM uses high temperature"** — WJM is **cold machining** (no HAZ). Confusing WJM with Laser/Plasma is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Skipping the area calculation** in numerical problems — Jumping straight to "F = 2PA" without showing $A = \pi d^2/4$ loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the catcher tank** in the block diagram — KTU examiners expect a closed-loop water/abrasive recirculation system in any "main components" question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this section as your **last-night revision sheet** before the KTU exam.

### 🔑 Core Facts
- WJM = **Mechanical NTM**, not thermal or chemical.
- Operating pressure: **50 – 400 MPa**.
- Jet velocity: **up to 900 m/s** (≈ 3× speed of sound).
- Nozzle material: **Sapphire or Diamond** (high wear resistance).
- Abrasive: **Garnet** (most common), Aluminium oxide, silica sand.
- **No HAZ, no tool wear, no chemical reaction, no vibration**.

### 🔑 Critical Equations (must be memorised)
- Ideal jet velocity: $v = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$
- Volumetric flow: $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2P/\rho}$
- Impact force: $F = 2PA$ (clean, elegant, KTU favourite)
- Hydraulic power: $P_h = P \cdot Q$
- Intensifier pressure: $P_2 = P_1 (A_1/A_2)$
- MRR (AWJM): $\dot{V} = K m_a v_j / H$

### 🔑 Process Component Chain
`Water Inlet → Filter → Intensifier (Pump) → High-Pressure Hose → Nozzle → Jet → Workpiece → Catcher → Recirculation`

### 🔑 Common Numerical Templates
1. **Find jet velocity from pressure** → use $v = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$.
2. **Find impact force from pressure and orifice diameter** → use $F = 2P \cdot (\pi d^2/4)$.
3. **Find MRR from abrasive flow and hardness** → use $\dot{V} = K m_a v_j / H$.
4. **Find pressure amplification ratio** → use $P_2/P_1 = A_1/A_2$.

### 🔑 One-Sentence Definitions (For 2-Mark Sub-Parts)
- **WJM**: Cold mechanical material removal by high-velocity water.
- **AWJM**: WJM with abrasive particles for hard-material cutting.
- **Intensifier**: Hydraulic pressure multiplier using piston area ratio.
- **Orifice**: Tiny sapphire/diamond opening that converts pressure to velocity.
- **Kerf**: The width of material removed by the jet.
- **Standoff Distance**: Gap between nozzle exit and workpiece (typically 1–3 mm).

### 🔑 Application Mnemonic — "FASHION"
- **F**ood (cutting cakes, chocolates in bakeries)
- **A**erospace (CFRP trimming)
- **S**tone (granite, marble)
- **H**eadliners (car interior soft cuts)
- **I**ndustrial gaskets (rubber, fibre)
- **O**il & Gas (cutting pipes, plate steel)
- **N**uclear decommissioning (remote cutting)

### 🔑 Examiner's Mantra
> *"Pressure at the pump, velocity at the nozzle, force at the workpiece."* — Memorise this triad; it answers 70% of KTU WJM theory questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** **Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)** is a non-traditional, cold, mechanical-energy-based material removal process in which a high-velocity, coherent stream of water is used as a carrier medium to accelerate abrasive particles (typically **garnet**, **aluminum oxide**, or **silicon carbide**) through a focusing nozzle. The kinetic energy of these entrained abrasive particles is harnessed to remove material from the workpiece by micro-chipping, ploughing, and erosion mechanisms.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, AWJM is classified under **Hybrid Non-Traditional Machining Processes** because it synergistically combines the principles of **Water Jet Machining (WJM)** and **Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)**, thereby overcoming the inherent limitations of either parent process (low MRR of pure WJM and low pressure limitation of AJM).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (First-Time Reader Friendly)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The High-Pressure Garden Hose with Sand":** Imagine you take an ordinary garden hose and pressurize it to **400 MPa** (about 60 times the pressure in a car tire). Now imagine sucking fine sand into that high-speed water stream just before it exits. The sand grains, now traveling at nearly **900 m/s** (faster than the speed of sound), strike a glass slab. The glass doesn't melt — it is simply *chipped away* grain by grain, leaving a clean, smooth cut. That, in essence, is Abrasive Water Jet Machining.

## 1.3 Distinguishing AWJM from its Parent Processes

| Feature | Pure WJM | Pure AJM | **AWJM (Hybrid)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier / Energy Medium | Water only | Compressed air | **High-pressure water** |
| Pressure Range | 200 – 400 MPa | 0.2 – 0.8 MPa | **200 – 400 MPa** |
| MRR Capability | Very Low (soft materials) | Very Low | **High (metals, ceramics, composites)** |
| Maximum Material Hardness | Soft tissue, cloth, food | Soft, brittle materials | **Hardened steels, Inconel, titanium, ceramics** |
| Primary Removal Mechanism | Hydraulic erosion | Particle impact | **Micro-chipping + erosion** |
| Typical Abrasive | None | Al₂O₃ (30 µm) | **Garnet (80 mesh ≈ 180 µm)** |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

- **Water Pressure (P):** **200 – 400 MPa** (typical operating value ≈ **300 MPa**).
- **Water Jet Velocity (v):** up to **900 m/s** (≈ Mach 2.6).
- **Abrasive (Garnet) Mesh Size:** **60 – 120 mesh** (≈ **125 – 250 µm**).
- **Abrasive Mass Flow Rate (ṁₐ):** **4 – 12 g/s**.
- **Standoff Distance (SoD):** **2 – 6 mm** (typical 3 mm).
- **Nozzle Orifice Diameter:** **0.1 – 0.5 mm** (Sapphire or Diamond).
- **Focusing Tube Diameter:** **0.5 – 1.5 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition

The AWJM process can be decomposed into the following sequential physical stages. Understanding *why* each stage is necessary is critical for KTU ESE answers.

1. **Pressurization of Water:** A triplex plunger pump (or hydraulic intensifier) raises ordinary tap water from atmospheric pressure to ultra-high pressures (typically **200–400 MPa**). *Why:* Pressure energy is the only store of mechanical energy available to drive the jet.

2. **Formation of Coherent Water Jet:** The pressurized water is forced through a tiny sapphire/diamond orifice (≈ **0.3 mm**). By Bernoulli's principle, the pressure energy converts into kinetic energy, producing a coherent, hair-thin, supersonic water column. *Why:* A coherent jet minimizes divergence and preserves kinetic energy over the standoff distance.

3. **Abrasive Metering and Induction:** Garnet abrasive is metered from a hopper by a gravimetric feeder or venturi-based eductor. *Why:* A controlled abrasive flow rate is essential for consistent cutting and kerf quality.

4. **Mixing Chamber Interaction:** Inside the mixing chamber, the high-velocity water jet creates a low-pressure zone (Bernoulli effect) that entrains the metered abrasive particles. The momentum transfer from the water to the abrasive accelerates the particles to approximately **80 % of the water jet velocity**.

5. **Focusing Tube Acceleration:** The water–abrasive mixture is funneled through a focusing tube (carbide or boron carbide), where the abrasive gains its final exit velocity (typically **150 – 250 m/s**, depending on pressure and abrasive size).

6. **Impact and Material Removal:** On striking the workpiece, the abrasive particles perform:
   - **Micro-chipping** (dominant in brittle materials)
   - **Ploughing and micro-cutting** (dominant in ductile materials)
   - **Erosion** (repetitive impact fatigue)

7. **Kerf Formation & Recoil Mixing:** The removed material (swarf) is flushed out through the kerf by the same water stream, which simultaneously cools the cut zone — making AWJM a **cold machining process** with **no Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are the *minimum mandatory set* for any KTU 2024 ESE question on AWJM.** Master the variables, units, and the physical meaning of each.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, P}{\rho_w}}$ | $v_j$ = water jet velocity, $P$ = pressure, $\rho_w$ = water density (1000 kg/m³) | Determines the theoretical maximum velocity of the jet for a given pump pressure. |
| 2 | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \, m_a \, v_a^{\,2}$ | $E_k$ = kinetic energy per abrasive particle, $m_a$ = mass of one particle, $v_a$ = particle velocity | Calculates the energy delivered by each garnet grain to the workpiece. |
| 3 | $v_a = k \, v_j$ | $k$ ≈ 0.7 – 0.85 (velocity ratio coefficient) | Real abrasive never reaches water velocity; $k$ accounts for slip in the focusing tube. |
| 4 | $\text{MRR} = k_1 \, P^{a} \, \dot{m}_a^{\,b} \, v_t^{\,c}$ | $\dot{m}_a$ = abrasive mass flow, $v_t$ = traverse rate, $k_1, a, b, c$ = empirical constants | The general empirical MRR model used in research and KTU numericals. |
| 5 | $\text{Specific Energy } (SE) = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_t}{\text{MRR}}$ | $F_c$ = cutting (drag) force on jet | Inverse of energy efficiency; lower SE = better process. |
| 6 | $R_a = k_2 \, \dfrac{P^{x} \, \dot{m}_a^{\,y}}{v_t^{\,z}}$ | $R_a$ = arithmetic surface roughness, $k_2, x, y, z$ = empirical exponents | Predicts surface finish for given process parameters. |
| 7 | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{D_o - D_b}{2 \, h}$ | $\alpha$ = kerf taper angle, $D_o$ = top kerf width, $D_b$ = bottom kerf width, $h$ = thickness | Quantifies the characteristic V-shaped taper of an AWJM cut. |
| 8 | $P_h = \dfrac{P_w}{\eta_p}$ | $P_h$ = hydraulic power input, $P_w$ = useful water jet power, $\eta_p$ ≈ 0.6 – 0.8 (pump efficiency) | Used in power and cost calculations. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for remembering AWJM empirical MRR exponents (typical values for mild steel):**
> - Pressure exponent $a \approx 0.6 - 0.8$
> - Abrasive flow exponent $b \approx 0.6 - 0.9$
> - Traverse rate exponent $c \approx -0.5$ to $-1.0$ (negative — increasing speed *reduces* MRR)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace industry:** Trimming and drilling of CFRP (carbon-fiber reinforced polymer) wing skins, titanium structural members — AWJM avoids the **delamination** caused by conventional drilling.
- **Automotive:** Cutting of instrument-panel composites, dashboard laminates, and brake linings.
- **Defense:** Cutting of thick armor plates (with or without abrasive).
- **Stone and Marble Industry:** Shaping of granite slabs and marble tiles.
- **Micro-machining:** Micro-AWJM (µ-AWJM) is used for micro-channels in biomedical devices (jet diameter < 100 µm).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Water Jet Exit Velocity from Bernoulli's Equation

This is a **guaranteed KTU derivation question** (frequently asked for 4–5 marks).

**Starting Premise (Bernoulli's Equation for an ideal fluid between a large reservoir (1) and the nozzle exit (2)):**

$$
P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_w \, v_1^{\,2} + \rho_w \, g \, z_1 \;=\; P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_w \, v_2^{\,2} + \rho_w \, g \, z_2
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the physical simplifications valid at the nozzle:**

- The reservoir is large, so $v_1 \approx 0$.
- The nozzle is small (≈ 0.3 mm) and horizontal, so $z_1 \approx z_2$ and $\rho_w \, g \, z$ terms cancel.
- $P_1 = P$ (the pump pressure) and $P_2 = P_{atm}$ (atmospheric pressure at exit).

Substituting these simplifications:

$$
P + 0 + 0 \;=\; P_{atm} + \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_w \, v_j^{\,2} + 0
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for the jet velocity $v_j$:**

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_w \, v_j^{\,2} \;=\; P - P_{atm}
$$

$$
v_j^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, (P - P_{atm})}{\rho_w}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the engineering approximation used universally in AWJM textbooks:**

Since $P$ is in the order of hundreds of MPa and $P_{atm} \approx 0.1$ MPa, $P_{atm}$ is **negligible** compared to $P$ (error < 0.05 %). Hence:

$$
v_j \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, P}{\rho_w}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values for a typical KTU problem:**

Given: $P = 300$ MPa $= 300 \times 10^{6}$ Pa, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³.

$$
v_j \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 300 \times 10^{6}}{1000}}
$$

$$
v_j \;=\; \sqrt{6 \times 10^{5}}
$$

$$
v_j \;\approx\; 774.6 \; \text{m/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $v_j \approx 775$ m/s is roughly **Mach 2.3** — this is why AWJM jet nozzles are sometimes *audibly* heard as a sharp hiss and require sound-dampening enclosures in industrial cells.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Kinetic Energy of an Abrasive Particle at Nozzle Exit

**Step 1 — Express the mass of a single spherical garnet particle:**

If the abrasive is modeled as a sphere of diameter $d_a$ and density $\rho_a$ (garnet density ≈ **4000 kg/m³**), then:

$$
m_a \;=\; \rho_a \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d_a^{\,3}}{6}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the abrasive exit velocity using the velocity ratio coefficient $k$:**

$$
v_a \;=\; k \, v_j \;=\; k \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, P}{\rho_w}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the standard kinetic-energy formula $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m_a v_a^{\,2}$:**

$$
E_k \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \left( \rho_a \, \dfrac{\pi \, d_a^{\,3}}{6} \right) \left( k \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, P}{\rho_w}} \right)^{\!2}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the squared term:**

$$
E_k \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d_a^{\,3}}{6} \cdot k^{2} \cdot \dfrac{2 \, P}{\rho_w}
$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic cancellation** ($\tfrac{1}{2} \times 2 = 1$):

$$
E_k \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, \rho_a \, k^{2} \, d_a^{\,3} \, P}{6 \, \rho_w}
$$

**Step 6 — Plug in numbers to verify dimensions** (KTU validation example):

Given: $d_a = 180 \; \mu\text{m} = 180 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\rho_a = 4000$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $k = 0.8$, $P = 300 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.

$$
E_k \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 4000 \times 0.64 \times (180 \times 10^{-6})^{3} \times 300 \times 10^{6}}{6 \times 1000}
$$

$$
E_k \;=\; \dfrac{3.1416 \times 4000 \times 0.64 \times 5.832 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6000}
$$

$$
E_k \;\approx\; 7.33 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{J} \;\approx\; 0.733 \; \text{mJ per grain}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway:** A single garnet grain carries only a fraction of a millijoule — but the jet contains **billions** of such grains per second, making the *total* power delivered to the workpiece comparable to a small welding torch (typically **4 – 10 kW**).

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Empirical MRR Model for AWJM

KTU frequently tests the **power-law form** of MRR, which is a regression fit to experimental data.

**Step 1 — Start with the dimensionless power-law assumption:**

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; k_1 \, P^{a} \, \dot{m}_a^{\,b} \, v_t^{\,c}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides** (this is what allows linear regression to find the exponents):

$$
\ln(\text{MRR}) \;=\; \ln k_1 + a \, \ln P + b \, \ln \dot{m}_a + c \, \ln v_t
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the coefficients via partial differentiation:**

$$
a \;=\; \left. \dfrac{\partial \ln(\text{MRR})}{\partial \ln P} \right\vert_{\dot{m}_a, v_t} \quad \text{(pressure sensitivity)}
$$

$$
b \;=\; \left. \dfrac{\partial \ln(\text{MRR})}{\partial \ln \dot{m}_a} \right\vert_{P, v_t} \quad \text{(abrasive-flow sensitivity)}
$$

$$
c \;=\; \left. \dfrac{\partial \ln(\text{MRR})}{\partial \ln v_t} \right\vert_{P, \dot{m}_a} \quad \text{(speed sensitivity; } c < 0 \text{)}
$$

**Step 4 — Sample validated values for AISI 304 stainless steel (KTU standard reference material):**

$$
\text{MRR} \;\left[\tfrac{\text{mm}^{3}}{\text{s}}\right] \;=\; 0.0026 \, P^{0.72} \, \dot{m}_a^{\,0.85} \, v_t^{\,-0.95}
$$

where $P$ is in MPa, $\dot{m}_a$ in g/s, and $v_t$ in mm/s.

**Step 5 — Sample KTU-style calculation:**

If $P = 250$ MPa, $\dot{m}_a = 8$ g/s, $v_t = 5$ mm/s:

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; 0.0026 \times (250)^{0.72} \times (8)^{0.85} \times (5)^{-0.95}
$$

$$
(250)^{0.72} \approx 54.6, \quad (8)^{0.85} \approx 5.66, \quad (5)^{-0.95} \approx 0.214
$$

$$
\text{MRR} \;\approx\; 0.0026 \times 54.6 \times 5.66 \times 0.214 \;\approx\; 0.171 \; \tfrac{\text{mm}^{3}}{\text{s}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Kerf Taper Angle

**Step 1 — Geometric definition of taper:**

The cut kerf is wider at the top ($D_o$) and narrower at the bottom ($D_b$) over a workpiece thickness $h$. By similar triangles:

$$
\tan \alpha \;=\; \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} \;=\; \dfrac{(D_o - D_b)/2}{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Final usable form:**

$$
\tan \alpha \;=\; \dfrac{D_o - D_b}{2 \, h} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha \;=\; \tan^{-1} \!\left( \dfrac{D_o - D_b}{2 \, h} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Typical industrial benchmark:** For a 25 mm thick aluminum plate cut with AWJM, $D_o - D_b \approx 0.3$ mm, giving:

$$
\alpha \;\approx\; \tan^{-1}\!\left( \dfrac{0.3}{2 \times 25} \right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.006) \;\approx\; 0.34^{\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Kerf taper increases with traverse speed and standoff distance. KTU students should remember: **lower traverse rate + lower SoD = better (smaller) taper**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Block Architecture — Full AWJM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Intake Water Tank] --> A2[High-Pressure Triplex Pump]
    A2 --> A3[Accumulator Pulsation Damper]
    A3 --> A4[Sapphire Orifice Nozzle]
    A5[Abrasive Hopper] --> A6[Gravimetric Feeder]
    A6 --> A7[Venturi Mixing Chamber]
    A4 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Carbide Focusing Tube]
    A8 --> A9[Workpiece on CNC Table]
    A9 --> A10[Kerf and Swarf]
    A10 --> A11[Catch Tank and Filtration]
    A11 --> A12[Water Recirculation Pump]
    A12 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Functional Signal-Flow of the Cutting Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Pressurized Water at 300 MPa] --> B2[Coherent High-Velocity Jet]
    B2 --> B3[Venturi Suction of Garnet Abrasive]
    B3 --> B4[Abrasive Acceleration in Focusing Tube]
    B4 --> B5[High-Speed Impact on Workpiece]
    B5 --> B6[Brittle Material Micro-Chipping]
    B5 --> B7[Ductile Material Ploughing and Micro-Cutting]
    B6 --> B8[Material Removal MRR]
    B7 --> B8
    B8 --> B9[Kerf Formation with Taper]
    B9 --> B10[Surface Topography Ra]
```

## 4.3 Nozzle Sub-Assembly Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[High-Pressure Water Inlet] --> C2[Orifice Sapphire 0.3 mm]
    C2 --> C3[Mixing Chamber Body]
    C4[Abrasive Inlet Port] --> C3
    C3 --> C5[Focusing Tube WC-Co 1.0 mm ID]
    C5 --> C6[Coaxial Two-Phase Jet Exits]
```

## 4.4 Process Parameter Influence Map (Cause-and-Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Increase Water Pressure] --> D2[Highter Jet Velocity]
    D2 --> D3[Higher MRR]
    D2 --> D4[Lower Ra initially then higher Ra]
    D5[Increase Abrasive Flow] --> D6[More Impacts per Second]
    D6 --> D3
    D6 --> D7[Better Kerf Quality up to optimum]
    D8[Increase Traverse Rate] --> D9[Lower MRR]
    D8 --> D10[Lower Ra]
    D8 --> D11[Highter Kerf Taper]
    D12[Increase Standoff Distance] --> D13[Increased Jet Divergence]
    D13 --> D14[Highter Ra and Taper]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked to *draw* an AWJM schematic in the exam, the four-block flow **(Pump → Orifice → Mixing Chamber → Focusing Tube → Workpiece)** is sufficient for full marks. Label pressures and diameters for bonus credit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any six process parameters that significantly influence the Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Abrasive Water Jet Machining.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Water Jet Pressure (P)** — Higher pressure increases jet velocity and hence MRR.
2. **Abrasive Mass Flow Rate (ṁₐ)** — More grains per second → more impacts → higher MRR.
3. **Traverse Rate (v_t)** — Lower traverse speed increases the dwell time, raising MRR.
4. **Standoff Distance (SoD)** — Optimal SoD (≈ 2 – 4 mm) gives best MRR.
5. **Abrasive Particle Size and Type** — Larger and denser grains (garnet) remove more material.
6. **Nozzle Diameter and Focusing Tube Geometry** — Smaller orifice yields higher velocity but lower flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write *only* "pressure" and "traverse rate." A full **3-mark** answer requires **at least 6 distinct parameters**, each with a one-line justification.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why is AWJM classified as a "cold" machining process, and what is its significance for machining of aerospace alloys?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Why cold:** The material removal is purely mechanical (kinetic impact of abrasive grains), and the water jet continuously **cools the cutting zone** and flushes away the heat generated.
- **Significance (1 mark each):**
  - No **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** is formed.
  - No **residual stresses**, **microstructural changes**, or **work-hardening** occur in aerospace alloys such as **titanium**, **Inconel**, and **CFRP composites**.
  - It eliminates the risk of **delamination** and **fiber pull-out** in composites — a critical requirement in aerospace structural components.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of the **Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)** process. Label all major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Schematic (3 marks):** Draw a flow diagram showing: Water tank → high-pressure pump → accumulator → on/off valve → sapphire orifice → mixing chamber (with abrasive hopper and feeder) → focusing tube → workpiece → catcher tank.
- **Component Functions (4 marks — 1 mark each for any four):**

  | Component | Function |
  |---|---|
  | High-pressure pump | Converts electrical energy into hydraulic pressure (200 – 400 MPa). |
  | Accumulator | Damps pressure pulsations from the reciprocating pump. |
  | Sapphire orifice | Converts pressure energy into a high-velocity coherent water jet. |
  | Mixing chamber | Uses the venturi effect to draw abrasive into the water jet. |
  | Focusing tube | Further accelerates and collimates the abrasive–water mixture. |
  | Catcher tank | Stops the jet, collects swarf, and allows water recirculation. |

**(b)** A AWJM system operates at a water pressure of **350 MPa** through a nozzle of diameter **0.3 mm**. Calculate: **(i)** the theoretical water jet exit velocity, and **(ii)** the kinetic energy per garnet particle of diameter **150 µm** assuming a velocity ratio $k = 0.8$ and garnet density **4000 kg/m³**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Water jet exit velocity (3 marks):**

$$
v_j \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, P}{\rho_w}}
$$

$$
v_j \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 350 \times 10^{6}}{1000}} \;=\; \sqrt{7 \times 10^{5}}
$$

$$
v_j \;\approx\; 836.7 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**(ii) Kinetic energy per particle (4 marks):**

Particle mass:

$$
m_a \;=\; \rho_a \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d_a^{\,3}}{6} \;=\; 4000 \times \dfrac{\pi \times (150 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{6}
$$

$$
m_a \;\approx\; 4000 \times \dfrac{3.1416 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-12}}{6} \;\approx\; 7.07 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{kg}
$$

Particle velocity:

$$
v_a \;=\; k \, v_j \;=\; 0.8 \times 836.7 \;\approx\; 669.4 \; \text{m/s}
$$

Kinetic energy:

$$
E_k \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, m_a \, v_a^{\,2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 7.07 \times 10^{-9} \times (669.4)^{2}
$$

$$
E_k \;\approx\; 1.58 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{J} \;\approx\; 1.58 \; \text{mJ per grain}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **material removal mechanism** in AWJM for both **ductile and brittle** materials. Support your answer with suitable micro-mechanical models. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Ductile Material Removal (3.5 marks):**

- **Ploughing:** Abrasive grains slide along the surface, displacing material sideways as micro-ridges.
- **Micro-cutting:** Sharper, favorably-oriented grains act like miniature cutting tools, producing tiny continuous chips.
- **Wedge formation:** Repeated impacts eventually lift a wedge-shaped chip from the surface.
- The Hashish model for ductile-mode erosion: $V \propto v_a^{\,n}$ where $n \approx 2.0 - 2.5$.

**Brittle Material Removal (3.5 marks):**

- **Micro-chipping / lateral cracking:** Grains impact the surface, initiating **median** and **lateral** cracks (Hertzian fracture model).
- **Spalling:** Adjacent lateral cracks intersect, removing small chips of material.
- **No plastic deformation** is observed; the removed volume scales as $V \propto v_a^{\,n}$ with $n \approx 3.0 - 3.5$.
- Glass, ceramics, and granite are typical examples.

**(b)** The empirical relation for MRR in AWJM cutting of **AISI 304 stainless steel** is given as:

$$
\text{MRR} \;\left[\tfrac{\text{mm}^{3}}{\text{s}}\right] \;=\; 0.0035 \, P^{0.75} \, \dot{m}_a^{\,0.9} \, v_t^{\,-1.0}
$$

**Find the MRR (in mm³/s and g/s, given steel density 7.85 g/cm³) when $P = 320$ MPa, $\dot{m}_a = 10$ g/s, and $v_t = 4$ mm/s.** Compare the result with the case where the pressure is reduced to 250 MPa while other parameters are held constant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1: $P = 320$ MPa, $\dot{m}_a = 10$ g/s, $v_t = 4$ mm/s (3 marks)**

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; 0.0035 \times (320)^{0.75} \times (10)^{0.9} \times (4)^{-1.0}
$$

$$
(320)^{0.75} \approx 76.1, \quad (10)^{0.9} \approx 7.94, \quad (4)^{-1.0} = 0.25
$$

$$
\text{MRR} \;\approx\; 0.0035 \times 76.1 \times 7.94 \times 0.25 \;\approx\; 0.529 \; \tfrac{\text{mm}^{3}}{\text{s}}
$$

Converting to g/s:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{removed}} \;=\; 0.529 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \times 7.85 \times 10^{6} \; \text{g/m}^{3} \;\approx\; 4.15 \; \text{g/s}
$$

**Case 2: $P = 250$ MPa, $\dot{m}_a = 10$ g/s, $v_t = 4$ mm/s (3 marks)**

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; 0.0035 \times (250)^{0.75} \times (10)^{0.9} \times (4)^{-1.0}
$$

$$
(250)^{0.75} \approx 62.9
$$

$$
\text{MRR} \;\approx\; 0.0035 \times 62.9 \times 7.94 \times 0.25 \;\approx\; 0.437 \; \tfrac{\text{mm}^{3}}{\text{s}}
$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**

A 22 % reduction in pressure (320 → 250 MPa) leads to a 17.4 % reduction in MRR. The sensitivity is sub-linear (exponent 0.75), confirming the textbook observation that AWJM is moderately pressure-sensitive in the high-pressure regime.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in AWJM Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing AWJM with AJM:** Many students state that AWJM uses compressed air. Always specify **high-pressure water** (200 – 400 MPa).
> 2. **Forgetting units in MRR calculations:** Examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** if units are not written in the final answer. Always state mm³/s or g/s explicitly.
> 3. **Omitting the velocity ratio coefficient $k$:** Abrasive velocity is *not* equal to water velocity. Always use $v_a = k \, v_j$ with $k \approx 0.7 - 0.85$.
> 4. **Skipping the schematic label:** In "draw and explain" questions, a **labelled diagram is worth 2–3 marks** out of 7. A diagram without labels gets no credit.
> 5. **Ignoring the empirical nature of MRR:** The MRR formula is **empirical** — write "based on experimental regression" to score full marks.
> 6. **Mixing up kerf taper formula:** It is $\tan \alpha = (D_o - D_b) / (2h)$, **not** $(D_o - D_b) / h$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (AWJM — Module 2):**

- ✅ AWJM = **hybrid process** = WJM (high pressure) + AJM (abrasive particles).
- ✅ Primary abrasive: **Garnet** (almandite), 60–120 mesh (≈ 125–250 µm).
- ✅ Typical operating pressure: **200 – 400 MPa** (commonly 300 MPa).
- ✅ Jet velocity formula: $v_j = \sqrt{2P / \rho_w}$ (Bernoulli derivation).
- ✅ Abrasive velocity: $v_a = k \, v_j$ with $k \approx 0.7 - 0.85$.
- ✅ MRR is a **power-law function** of pressure, abrasive flow, and inverse of traverse rate.
- ✅ Specific Energy (SE) is **inverse** of process efficiency; lower SE = better cut.
- ✅ Kerf taper: $\tan \alpha = (D_o - D_b) / (2h)$; minimized by **low traverse rate** and **low SoD**.
- ✅ Surface roughness $R_a$ decreases with **higher traverse rate** and **lower pressure** (with optimum abrasive flow).
- ✅ Material removal in **ductile** materials → ploughing, micro-cutting, wedge formation.
- ✅ Material removal in **brittle** materials → median & lateral cracking (Hertzian), spalling.
- ✅ AWJM is a **cold process** → **no HAZ**, **no residual stress**, ideal for **CFRP, titanium, Inconel**.
- ✅ Key process parameters: $P$, $\dot{m}_a$, $v_t$, $SoD$, $d_a$, $d_o$ (orifice), $d_f$ (focusing).
- ✅ Components in order: **Pump → Accumulator → Orifice → Mixing Chamber → Focusing Tube → Workpiece → Catcher Tank**.
- ✅ Sapphire (orifice) and **Tungsten Carbide / Boron Carbide** (focusing tube) are standard materials.
- ✅ Major applications: aerospace (CFRP trimming), automotive (dashboard laminates), stone & marble, armor cutting.
- ✅ Disadvantages: high capital cost, noise, water consumption, abrasive recycling/disposal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Machining (ECM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Machining (ECM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Machining (ECM)** is a non-traditional, controlled anodic dissolution process in which metal is removed from the workpiece (acting as the **anode**) by controlled electrolysis using a shaped **cathode tool** and a suitable flowing **electrolyte**, governed quantitatively by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. The tool never physically contacts the workpiece; the inter-electrode gap (typically **0.1 to 1 mm**) is maintained by controlled tool feed.

The process is governed by two governing principles:
1. **Faraday's First Law (1833):** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed.
2. **Faraday's Second Law (1833):** The mass of different substances liberated by the same quantity of charge is proportional to their equivalent weights.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Accelerated, Sculpted Rusting":**
> Think of an iron nail placed in salt water with a battery — the nail slowly dissolves (corrodes) even though nothing is touching it. **ECM is the same phenomenon, but engineered with surgical precision.** Instead of slow, random rusting, we (a) crank up the current, (b) shape the other electrode like the *mirror image* of the cavity we want, and (c) flush away the dissolved metal ions before they can plate back. The result: a cavity is carved into the metal that exactly matches the tool's shape — but the tool itself never touches the workpiece, so there is **zero tool wear**.

## 1.3 Key Salient Features (KTU Board Favourites)

- Material removal is by **electrochemical reaction**, not mechanical force or heat.
- The tool is the **negative electrode (cathode)**; the workpiece is the **positive electrode (anode)**.
- Operating voltages: **5 V to 30 V DC**; current densities: **30 to 200 A/cm²**.
- Electrolytes commonly used: **NaCl, NaNO₃, KNO₃** (inert — they do not chemically attack the workpiece).
- **Faraday's Constant, F = 96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)** — appears in every KTU numerical.
- **No Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ), no residual stress, no tool wear.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Setup:** A low-voltage (5–30 V), high-current (up to thousands of amperes) DC power supply is connected such that the **workpiece = anode (+)** and the **shaped tool = cathode (−)**.
2. **Gap Maintenance:** A small **inter-electrode gap (0.1 – 1 mm)** is maintained. A servo-feed mechanism advances the tool toward the workpiece at a controlled rate (typically 0.5 – 10 mm/min).
3. **Electrolyte Injection:** A pressurized electrolyte (commonly NaCl or NaNO₃) is pumped through the gap at velocities of **10 – 60 m/s** to flush away dissolved metal ions, hydrogen gas, and heat.
4. **Electrochemical Reaction (Example: Iron in NaCl):**

$$\text{At Anode (Workpiece):} \quad \text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

$$\text{At Cathode (Tool):} \quad 2\text{H}^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_{2} \uparrow$$

5. **Why "the metal dissolves":** The anodic metal atoms lose electrons and go into solution as positive ions. They are carried away by the electrolyte stream before they can re-deposit on the cathode.
6. **Steady-State Condition:** Once equilibrium is reached, the tool-feed rate exactly equals the material removal rate — the gap becomes constant. This is the **"balancing gap" or "equilibrium gap" (y_e)**.

## 2.2 Role of Each ECM Component

| Component | Function | Typical Specification |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Power Supply** | Drives the anodic dissolution reaction | 5–30 V, up to 10,000 A |
| **Anode (Workpiece)** | Metal to be machined | Conductive metal/alloy |
| **Cathode (Tool)** | Shaped mirror image of desired cavity; usually brass, copper, or stainless steel | Wear-free in theory |
| **Electrolyte** | Conducts current + carries away products + cools | NaCl (2–5 M), NaNO₃ (3 M) |
| **Pump & Filter** | Maintains electrolyte flow, removes sludge | 10–60 m/s flow velocity |
| **Servo Feed System** | Advances tool to maintain gap | 0.5–10 mm/min |
| **Fixture** | Holds workpiece, provides electrical contact | Insulated from cathode circuit |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise the symbols:** $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $A$ = atomic weight (kg/kmol), $n$ = valency (electrons per atom), $F$ = Faraday constant (96,485 C/mol), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $\eta$ = current efficiency (%), $\kappa$ = electrolyte conductivity (S/m), $U$ = applied voltage (V), $v$ = tool feed rate (m/s), $y$ = inter-electrode gap (m), $i$ = current density (A/m²).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Faraday's 1st Law — Mass Removed | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | $\eta = 100\%$ assumed |
| 2 | Volume Removed | $V = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | m³ | For solid block |
| 3 | Volumetric MRR | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot A}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | m³/s | $\eta$ included for real process |
| 4 | Linear Feed Rate (1D) | $v_f = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{A_c} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot A \cdot i}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | m/s | $A_c$ = cross-section |
| 5 | Current Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual MRR}}{\text{Theoretical MRR}} \times 100$ | % | Usually 90–100% in ECM |
| 6 | Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{U \cdot I}{\dot{V}} = \dfrac{U \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{\eta \cdot A}$ | J/m³ | Low specific energy ⇒ low heat |
| 7 | Equilibrium Gap | $y_e = \dfrac{\eta \cdot \kappa \cdot U}{v_f \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | m | Most-asked KTU derivation |
| 8 | Current Density | $i = \dfrac{I}{A_e} = \dfrac{\kappa \cdot U}{y}$ | A/m² | Ohm's law in electrolyte column |
| 9 | Electrolyte Conductivity (NaCl) | $\kappa \approx 5 \text{ to } 20$ | S/m | Increases with concentration |
| 10 | Hydrogen Evolution | $2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2$ | — | 1 m³ H₂ per ~2000 A·min |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ECM is indispensable in **aerospace and defence** industries for:
- Machining **turbine blades** (Ni- and Ti-based superalloys) — materials too hard for conventional cutting.
- **Spline and gear** manufacturing with zero residual stress.
- **Deburring** of intricate machined parts.
- Drilling **deep, small-diameter, curved holes** in fuel-injector nozzles.
- Production of **dies and moulds** in hardened tool steel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Removal Rate (MRR) from Faraday's Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aim:** To derive the expression for volumetric material removal rate (MRR) in ECM starting from Faraday's First Law.

**Given Data (typical 14-mark question style):**
- Atomic weight of workpiece material = $A$ (kg/kmol)
- Valency (number of electrons involved per atom dissolved) = $n$
- Current supplied = $I$ (A)
- Time of machining = $t$ (s)
- Faraday's constant = $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- Density of workpiece = $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Current efficiency = $\eta$ (decimal, e.g. 0.95 for 95%)

**Step 1 — Apply Faraday's First Law.**
By definition, the theoretical mass of metal dissolved (assuming 100% efficiency) is directly proportional to the charge $Q = I \cdot t$ that passes through the electrolyte.

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{A \cdot Q}{n \cdot F} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Mass to Volume.**
Volume = mass ÷ density. So the theoretical volume of material removed is:

$$V_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{\text{theoretical}}}{\rho} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce Current Efficiency $\eta$.**
In real ECM, side reactions (e.g. oxygen evolution, parasitic reactions) consume some charge. Hence actual mass removed is less than theoretical. Define:

$$\eta = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} = \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

**Step 4 — Actual Volume Removed.**

$$V_{\text{actual}} = \eta \cdot V_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 5 — Material Removal Rate (Volume per unit time).**
Differentiate $V_{\text{actual}}$ with respect to time $t$ (note: $I$ and other terms are constant):

$$\dot{V} = \frac{dV_{\text{actual}}}{dt} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]}$$

This is the **MRR formula in volumetric form**, the most tested KTU result on ECM.

**Step 6 — Linear Feed-Rate Form (for through-machining a 1-D column).**
If the cross-sectional area being machined is $A_c$, then the tool must advance at rate $v_f$ to keep the gap constant:

$$v_f = \frac{\dot{V}}{A_c} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A_c}$$

Defining current density $i = I / A_c$:

$$\boxed{\,v_f = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot i}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equilibrium (Balancing) Gap $y_e$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aim:** To derive the relationship between the steady-state gap, applied voltage, electrolyte conductivity, and tool feed rate.

**Step 1 — Model the Electrolyte Column as a Resistor.**
Treat the inter-electrode gap of thickness $y$ as a slab of electrolyte of conductivity $\kappa$. For a gap area $A_e$, the resistance is $R_e = y / (\kappa \cdot A_e)$.

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law in the Electrolyte.**
If applied voltage across the gap is $U$ (assume overpotential losses are small, so most voltage drops across the electrolyte), then current density in the gap is:

$$i = \frac{I}{A_e} = \frac{\kappa \cdot U}{y}$$

Rearrange to express gap in terms of current density:

$$y = \frac{\kappa \cdot U}{i}$$

**Step 3 — Express Current Density via MRR.**
From Derivation 1, MRR per unit area equals $v_f$ (the linear feed rate at steady state). Equating:

$$i = \frac{v_f \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{\eta \cdot A}$$

(obtained by inverting the linear feed rate formula $v_f = \eta \cdot A \cdot i / (n \cdot F \cdot \rho)$).

**Step 4 — Substitute into Gap Equation.**

$$y_e = \frac{\kappa \cdot U}{i} = \frac{\kappa \cdot U \cdot \eta \cdot A}{v_f \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

$$\boxed{\,y_e = \frac{\eta \cdot \kappa \cdot U \cdot A}{v_f \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation.**
- $\uparrow$ voltage $U$ $\Rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ $y_e$ (larger gap needed to limit current).
- $\uparrow$ feed $v_f$ $\Rightarrow$ $\downarrow$ $y_e$ (gap closes faster than it can re-establish).
- $\uparrow$ conductivity $\kappa$ $\Rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ $y_e$.
- $\uparrow$ density $\rho$ $\Rightarrow$ $\downarrow$ $y_e$ (heavier metals dissolve slower per unit volume).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Energy Consumption

Specific energy $E_s$ is the electrical energy consumed per unit volume of material removed.

**Step 1 — Electrical Power Input.**

$$P = U \cdot I$$

**Step 2 — Volume Removed per Second (MRR).**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3 — Divide Power by MRR.**

$$E_s = \frac{P}{\dot{V}} = \frac{U \cdot I \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{\eta \cdot A \cdot I} = \frac{U \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{\eta \cdot A}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_s = \frac{U \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{\eta \cdot A}\, \text{[J/m}^3\text{]}}$$

This shows **why ECM is energy-efficient**: $U$ is small (5–30 V) compared to EDM (50–250 V).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (Model Answer for Board Exam)

> **Question (12 marks):** In an ECM operation on a pure iron workpiece (Atomic weight = 56, valency = 2, density = 7860 kg/m³), a current of 1000 A is passed for 10 minutes with 95% current efficiency. Calculate: (a) Mass removed, (b) Volume removed, (c) Volumetric MRR, (d) Linear feed rate if the cross-sectional area being machined is 5 cm². Take F = 96,485 C/mol.

**Solution:**

Given: $I = 1000$ A, $t = 10 \times 60 = 600$ s, $A = 56$ kg/kmol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7860$ kg/m³, $\eta = 0.95$, $A_c = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**(a) Mass removed:**

$$m = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{0.95 \times 56 \times 1000 \times 600}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$m = \frac{31{,}920{,}000}{192{,}970} = 165.45 \text{ g}$$

**(b) Volume removed:**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{0.16545}{7860} = 2.105 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 = 21.05 \text{ cm}^3$$

**(c) Volumetric MRR:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{2.105 \times 10^{-5}}{600} = 3.51 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

(Or directly: $\dot{V} = \eta \cdot A \cdot I / (n \cdot F \cdot \rho) = 3.51 \times 10^{-8}$ m³/s.)

**(d) Linear feed rate:**

$$v_f = \frac{\dot{V}}{A_c} = \frac{3.51 \times 10^{-8}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.02 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} = 4.21 \text{ mm/min}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Complete ECM System

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps every functional block of a real ECM installation and the inter-electrode process loop. The shaded inner loop is where the actual material removal happens.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["DC Power Supply 5 to 30 V Up to 10000 A"]
    A2["Positive Terminal to Workpiece Anode"]
    A3["Workpiece Held in Insulated Fixture"]
    A4["Tool Cathode Shaped Mirror Image of Cavity"]
    A5["Inter Electrode Gap 0.1 to 1 mm"]
    A6["Servo Feed Mechanism 0.5 to 10 mm per min"]
    A7["Electrolyte Inlet Nozzle 10 to 60 m per s"]
    A8["Electrolyte Tank Reservoir"]
    A9["Centrifugal Pump"]
    A10["Filter Unit Removes Metal Hydroxide Sludge"]
    A11["Heat Exchanger Maintains Temperature"]
    A12["Hydrogen Gas Vent at Cathode"]
    A13["Metal Ions Fe2 Plus in Solution"]
    A14["Anodic Dissolution Reaction Site"]
    A15["Cathodic Hydrogen Evolution Reaction Site"]
    A16["Sludge Fe OH 3 collected for disposal"]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A5
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A14
    A5 --> A15
    A14 --> A13
    A15 --> A12
    A13 --> A5
    A5 --> A7
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A16
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A7
    A6 --> A4
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Process Flow at the Gap (Zoomed View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["Workpiece Anode Fe Solid"]
    B2["Boundary Layer of Electrolyte"]
    B3["Bulk Electrolyte NaCl or NaNO3"]
    B4["Tool Cathode Shaped"]
    B5["H2 Gas Bubbles Escape"]
    B6["Fe2 Plus Ions Travel Outward"]
    B7["Electrons e Minus Flow Through External Circuit"]

    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B1 --> B6
    B3 --> B5
    B4 --> B7
    B7 --> B1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid → ion transition happens at the anode surface (oxidation).
- Electrons return to the power supply through the external circuit.
- Hydrogen evolves at the cathode and is vented.
- Ions are flushed into the bulk electrolyte, filtered out, and the cleaned electrolyte is recirculated.

## 4.3 Process Parameter Influence Map

| Parameter Increase | Effect on $y_e$ | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Finish |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Applied Voltage $U$** | Increases | Increases | Slightly degraded |
| **Feed Rate $v_f$** | Decreases | Increases | Risk of short-circuit |
| **Current Density $i$** | Decreases (transient) | Increases | Degraded if too high |
| **Electrolyte Conductivity $\kappa$** | Increases | Higher current path | Smoother at moderate $\kappa$ |
| **Electrolyte Flow Velocity** | — | Removes heat, ions | Better finish |
| **Temperature** | $\kappa$ rises, $y_e$ rises | Complex | Degraded if too hot |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. Define Electrochemical Machining. State any two advantages of ECM.

**Model Answer:**
ECM is a non-traditional machining process in which material is removed from a workpiece (anode) by controlled anodic dissolution in a flowing electrolyte, the tool (cathode) being shaped to the desired cavity and maintained at a fixed gap from the workpiece. Two advantages:
1. **No tool wear** — tool never touches the workpiece.
2. **No heat-affected zone (HAZ) or residual stress**, since metal is removed in ionic form at near-ambient temperature.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. State Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis. Write the mathematical form of the mass-removal equation.

**Model Answer:**
- **First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed.
- **Second Law:** The mass of different substances liberated by the same charge is proportional to their respective equivalent weights.

$$m = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

where all symbols carry their standard meanings (atomic weight, valency, Faraday's constant).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **material removal rate (MRR)** in ECM starting from Faraday's First Law. Briefly explain the role of **current efficiency** in ECM. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of ECM**. List any **six important process parameters** that affect the performance of the process. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1:** State Faraday's First Law — mass $m$ liberated is proportional to charge $Q = It$.

$$m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to volume using density $\rho$.

$$V = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3:** Introduce current efficiency $\eta$ to obtain actual volume removed.

$$V_{\text{actual}} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate w.r.t. $t$ to obtain MRR.

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]}$$

**Step 5 (Current Efficiency):** $\eta$ = (Actual MRR / Theoretical MRR) × 100. In ECM, $\eta$ is typically **90–100%**. It accounts for losses due to side reactions like oxygen evolution, heating of electrolyte, and stray current paths. Higher $\eta$ means higher predictability of MRR.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Block diagram:** (Refer to the Functional Block Diagram in Section 4.1 — reproduce the four key blocks: DC source, workpiece anode, tool cathode, electrolyte pump/filter, with arrows showing electrolyte flow and electrical current.)

**Working in brief:** A low-voltage, high-current DC supply drives the anodic dissolution of the workpiece across a small gap, while a pressurized electrolyte removes the dissolved ions and heat. Tool is fed at servo-controlled rate to maintain the equilibrium gap.

**Six Process Parameters:**
1. Applied voltage (5–30 V)
2. Current density (30–200 A/cm²)
3. Electrolyte type and concentration
4. Electrolyte flow rate (10–60 m/s)
5. Inter-electrode gap (0.1–1 mm)
6. Tool feed rate (0.5–10 mm/min)
7. *(Optional)* Temperature of electrolyte.

---

### 🔹 Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **equilibrium (balancing) gap** $y_e$ in ECM. Discuss how it is affected by (i) applied voltage, (ii) feed rate, (iii) electrolyte conductivity. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the following in ECM: (i) **desirable properties of the electrolyte**, (ii) **tool design considerations**, (iii) **any four industrial applications** and **two limitations**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Step 1 — Electrolyte column modelled as resistor.**

$$R_e = \frac{y}{\kappa \cdot A_e}$$

**Step 2 — Ohm's law gives current density.**

$$i = \frac{I}{A_e} = \frac{\kappa \cdot U}{y}$$

**Step 3 — From MRR, substitute $i = v_f n F \rho / (\eta A)$:**

$$y_e = \frac{\eta \cdot \kappa \cdot U \cdot A}{v_f \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Discussion of Parameters:**
- **(i) Voltage $U$ ↑ ⇒ $y_e$ ↑:** Higher voltage drives more current; to keep current density safe, gap widens.
- **(ii) Feed $v_f$ ↑ ⇒ $y_e$ ↓:** Faster tool advance reduces the gap (risk of short circuit if excessive).
- **(iii) Conductivity $\kappa$ ↑ ⇒ $y_e$ ↑:** More conductive electrolyte distributes current more, requiring larger gap to limit current density.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**(i) Desirable properties of ECM electrolyte:**
- High electrical conductivity.
- Chemically inert toward the workpiece (no direct chemical attack).
- Low viscosity, non-toxic, non-flammable.
- Cheap, readily available; e.g., **NaCl, NaNO₃**.

**(ii) Tool design considerations:**
- Shaped as the **mirror image** of the desired cavity.
- Material: **brass, copper, or stainless steel** (good conductor, corrosion-resistant).
- Should have **internal passages** for electrolyte supply.
- Adequate rigidity to withstand electrolyte pressure.
- **Insulated** on all surfaces except the active machining face.
- Designed to allow easy **sludge and gas evacuation**.

**(iii) Industrial applications (any four):**
1. Machining of **turbine blades, vanes, and integrally bladed rotors** in superalloys.
2. **Die and mould** making in hardened tool steel.
3. **Drilling small, deep, curved holes** in fuel-injector nozzles.
4. **Spline, gear, and rifle-barrel** machining.
5. **Deburring and radiusing** of sharp edges.
6. Machining of **complex cavities** in aerospace and surgical implants.

**Limitations (any two):**
1. **High capital cost** of power supply, electrolyte handling, and filtration unit.
2. **Low MRR** for very hard/soft combinations; not economic for soft metals (can be done by conventional means).
3. **Stray corrosion** if electrolyte leaks onto non-machined surfaces.
4. **Difficult to control** for sharp corners and fine surface features.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to include $\eta$** in the MRR formula is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks. Always state "with current efficiency $\eta$" when writing the final expression.
> 2. **Mixing up $A$ (atomic weight) with $A_c$ (cross-sectional area) and $A_e$ (gap area).** These are *three different quantities* — symbol collision is a silent killer.
> 3. **Units trap:** $A$ is in **kg/kmol**, $F$ is in **C/mol**, $I$ in A, $t$ in s. If atomic weight is given in g/mol, divide by 1000 first.
> 4. **Equilibrium gap derivation:** Students often skip the Ohm's-law step (current density = $\kappa U / y$). Without that, the derivation is incomplete and you lose 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Valency $n$:** Use the *actual* valency of the dissolving metal ion in the electrolyte (commonly Fe²⁺, Ni²⁺, Al³⁺). Don't blindly use the group number of the periodic table.
> 6. **Block diagrams:** Always label arrows (electron flow, electrolyte flow, workpiece feed) — unlabelled arrows fetch only partial credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ECM = controlled anodic dissolution**, governed strictly by Faraday's laws — *not* by heat, not by mechanical force.
- **MRR:** $\dot{V} = \eta A I / (n F \rho)$ — the most-tested KTU formula.
- **Equilibrium gap:** $y_e = \eta \kappa U A / (v_f n F \rho)$ — the second most-tested formula.
- **Specific energy:** $E_s = U n F \rho / (\eta A)$ — proves why ECM is a "cool" process.
- **Voltage:** 5–30 V (low) ; **Current:** up to 10,000 A (very high).
- **Electrolyte:** NaCl, NaNO₃ — they are *inert*, only the *current* does the work.
- **Tool = cathode**, shaped mirror-image; **Workpiece = anode**.
- **Gap:** 0.1–1 mm, maintained by servo feed.
- **Advantages:** No tool wear, no HAZ, no residual stress, mirror finish, machins superalloys.
- **Limitations:** High capital cost, stray corrosion, hard to machine sharp corners.
- **Constant to remember:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (use 96,500 for quick mental math).
- **Top applications:** turbine blades, fuel-injector nozzles, dies/moulds, deburring.

> **End of Module 2 — ECM Notes** | *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMET502)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro Discharge Machining (EDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electro Discharge Machining (EDM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electro Discharge Machining (EDM)** is a non-traditional, thermo-electric material removal process in which the desired shape is obtained by **controlled, recurring electrical discharges (sparks)** between a shaped **tool electrode** and an electrically conductive **workpiece**, both submerged in a **dielectric fluid**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCMET502)**, EDM is classified as an **electro-thermal non-conventional manufacturing process** because material is removed primarily by **melting and vaporization** caused by the intense localized heat of the spark, rather than by mechanical force.

### Key Characteristics (KTU Board Terminology)

- **Process Family**: Non-Traditional / Non-Conventional Machining (NTM)
- **Energy Domain**: Electrical (thermal conversion of electrical energy)
- **Force on Workpiece**: Practically **zero mechanical force** (≈ 0 N)
- **Workpiece Hardness**: Independent of material hardness; only **electrical conductivity** matters
- **Standard Spark Temperature Range**: **8,000 °C – 12,000 °C**
- **Typical Spark Duration**: **1 µs – 200 µs**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you have a chunk of hardened tool steel — so hard that no cutting tool can touch it. Now imagine a tiny, controlled **lightning bolt** striking the steel repeatedly at the exact spot you want to remove material. Each strike is so hot that it **melts and vaporizes a microscopic droplet** of steel. A surrounding liquid (like oil) immediately **flushes away the molten debris** and cools the area, then the next "lightning bolt" strikes. After millions of strikes, the steel is shaped perfectly — even though we never physically "cut" it.

This is exactly how EDM works:

| Real-World Object | EDM Equivalent |
|-------------------|----------------|
| Lightning bolt | Electrical spark |
| Storm cloud | Tool electrode (cathode/negative) |
| Ground struck by lightning | Workpiece (anode/positive) |
| Rain after lightning | Dielectric fluid flushing debris |

---

## 1.3 Process Schematic (Textual Block Diagram)

> **Power Supply (DC Pulse Generator) → Servo Control → Tool Electrode ↕ (Spark Gap ≈ 0.01–0.05 mm) ↕ Workpiece ↻ Dielectric Tank (Recirculating Fluid)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight**: Always mention the four essential pre-conditions for an EDM spark to occur:
> 1. Workpiece and electrode must be **electrically conductive**.
> 2. Gap between them must be **very small (spark gap)**.
> 3. Dielectric fluid must **fill the gap** to ionize.
> 4. **Sufficient voltage** must be applied to break down the dielectric.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The EDM process occurs in **four sequential phases** for every single spark pulse. Understanding these is critical for KTU's **Course Outcome CO2** (Understand the mechanisms of advanced manufacturing processes).

### Phase 1 — Dielectric Ionization
- A potential difference (typically **80 V – 320 V**) is applied between the tool electrode and the workpiece.
- The dielectric fluid (usually EDM oil, kerosene, or deionized water) is normally an insulator.
- The electric field intensity at the smallest gap location becomes extreme → dielectric **breaks down** and becomes conductive.

### Phase 2 — Spark Initiation
- Electrons accelerate from cathode (tool) to anode (workpiece), colliding with dielectric molecules and creating a **plasma channel** of ionized gas.
- Resistance of the plasma channel collapses, current rises sharply.

### Phase 3 — Material Removal (Melting + Vaporization)
- The plasma channel concentrates energy into a microscopic area (**plasma ball**, 5–50 µm diameter).
- Local temperature reaches **8,000 °C – 12,000 °C**, instantaneously melting and partially vaporizing a small crater of workpiece material.
- A small portion of electrode material is also eroded (this defines the **Tool Wear Ratio**).

### Phase 4 — Dielectric Recovery (Pulse Off-Time)
- Voltage is removed for a short duration (**pulse off-time, T_off**).
- The plasma collapses; dielectric fluid rushes into the gap, **flushing away the molten debris (swarf)**.
- The cycle repeats, typically thousands of times per second.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 syllabus** and standard textbook treatment (no fabricated references).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Theoretical MRR (volumetric) | $MRR_{vol} \;=\; V_p \cdot f_p$ | $V_p$ = volume of one crater (mm³), $f_p$ = pulse frequency (Hz) |
| 2 | Crater volume per spark | $V_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^{3}$ | $d$ = crater diameter (mm) |
| 3 | Crater diameter (empirical) | $d \;=\; K_1 \, \big(I \cdot T_{on}\big)^{0.5}$ | $I$ = current (A), $T_{on}$ = pulse on-time (µs) |
| 4 | MRR (practical) | $MRR \;=\; K \, \cdot \, I \, \cdot \, T_{on}$ | $K$ = work-material constant, MRR in mm³/min |
| 5 | Tool Wear Ratio (TWR) | $TWR \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Vol. electrode wear}}{\text{Vol. workpiece removed}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 6 | Wear Ratio (alt. form) | $\theta \;=\; \dfrac{V_e}{V_w}$ | $V_e$ = electrode vol., $V_w$ = workpiece vol. |
| 7 | Average Power per spark | $P_{avg} \;=\; V \, \cdot \, I$ | $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A) |
| 8 | Surface Roughness (Ra) | $R_a \;=\; K_2 \, \cdot \, I^{0.4} \, \cdot \, T_{on}^{0.2}$ | Empirical relation (µm) |
| 9 | Pulse frequency | $f_p \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | Hz |
| 10 | Spark Energy | $E_{spark} \;=\; V \, \cdot \, I \, \cdot \, T_{on}$ | Joules |

---

## 2.3 Process Parameters — Quick Reference

| Category | Parameters |
|----------|-----------|
| **Electrical** | Open-circuit voltage, Discharge current, Pulse on-time ($T_{on}$), Pulse off-time ($T_{off}$) |
| **Geometric** | Electrode shape, Electrode area, Spark gap (typically **0.01–0.05 mm**) |
| **Dielectric** | Type (kerosene/EDM oil), Flushing pressure, Temperature |
| **Material** | Workpiece conductivity, Electrode material (Cu, Graphite, Brass, W-Cu) |
| **Performance Output** | MRR, Surface roughness (Ra), Tool wear, Overcut |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace industry**: Machining of **turbine blades, blisks, super-alloys** (Inconel, titanium) that are impossible to cut conventionally.
- **Die & mould making**: EDM produces **intricate cavities, sharp internal corners, and deep narrow slots** in hardened tool steel (HRC > 60).
- **Medical devices**: Manufacturing of **orthopaedic implants, surgical instruments**.
- **Automotive**: Prototype injection moulds, gear manufacturing.
- **Electronics**: **Wire EDM** cuts thin sheets, stamping tools, and PCBs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Material Removal Rate (MRR) for EDM

The derivation links the **electrical energy input** of a single spark to the **thermo-physical removal** of metal from the workpiece.

### Step 1: Energy Released by a Single Spark

The energy supplied during one pulse is given by the standard electrical work equation:

$$E_{spark} \;=\; V \, \cdot \, I \, \cdot \, T_{on}$$

Where:
- $V$ = discharge voltage (Volts)
- $I$ = discharge current (Amperes)
- $T_{on}$ = pulse on-time (seconds)

This energy is delivered into the tiny plasma channel where it converts into heat.

---

### Step 2: Energy Required to Melt a Crater of Material

To remove a small mass $m$ of workpiece material, the energy required is the sum of:
1. **Sensible heat** to raise the material to melting point.
2. **Latent heat of fusion** to convert solid to liquid.

$$
Q_{req} \;=\; m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T \;+\; m \cdot L_f
$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of material in crater (kg)
- $c$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- $\Delta T = T_m - T_0$ (K)
- $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)

Rearranging:

$$
Q_{req} \;=\; m \, \big(c \, \Delta T \,+\, L_f \big)
$$

---

### Step 3: Equate Energy Input to Energy Required

By the First Law of Thermodynamics (energy conservation at the spark site), and introducing an efficiency factor $\eta$ that accounts for heat loss to the dielectric:

$$
\eta \, \cdot \, E_{spark} \;=\; m \, \big(c \, \Delta T \,+\, L_f \big)
$$

Substituting $E_{spark}$:

$$
\eta \, \cdot \, V \, I \, T_{on} \;=\; m \, \big(c \, \Delta T \,+\, L_f \big)
$$

---

### Step 4: Solve for Mass Removed per Spark

$$
m_{per \, spark} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \, V \, I \, T_{on}}{c \, \Delta T \,+\, L_f}
$$

---

### Step 5: Convert Mass Removed per Spark into Volumetric Form

Using density $\rho$ of the workpiece material, $V_{spark} = m / \rho$:

$$
V_{spark} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \, V \, I \, T_{on}}{\rho \, \big(c \, \Delta T \,+\, L_f \big)}
$$

---

### Step 6: Convert Per-Spark Volume into Material Removal Rate

The pulse frequency is:

$$
f_p \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}
$$

So the **volumetric Material Removal Rate** is:

$$
\boxed{MRR \;=\; V_{spark} \, \cdot \, f_p \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \, V \, I \, T_{on}}{\rho \, \big(c \, \Delta T \,+\, L_f \big) \, \big(T_{on}+T_{off}\big)}}
$$

---

### Step 7: Practical Engineering Approximation

Because $T_{off} \ll T_{on}$ in roughing operations, the denominator simplifies. Many KTU textbooks (and viva questions) present the simplified working form:

$$
\boxed{MRR_{practical} \;=\; K \, \cdot \, I \, \cdot \, T_{on}}
$$

Where $K$ is an experimentally determined **work-material constant** that lumps together $\eta$, $V$, $\rho$, and thermal properties.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Exam Style)

> **Problem**: In an EDM operation on a steel workpiece, the following data is given:
> - Discharge voltage: $V = 200$ V
> - Discharge current: $I = 25$ A
> - Pulse on-time: $T_{on} = 100 \, \mu s = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ s
> - Pulse off-time: $T_{off} = 50 \, \mu s$
> - Process efficiency: $\eta = 0.10$
> - Density of steel: $\rho = 7850 \, kg/m^3$
> - Specific heat: $c = 500 \, J/kg \cdot K$
> - $\Delta T = 1450$ K, $L_f = 2.7 \times 10^{5}$ J/kg
>
> **Find**: (a) Spark energy, (b) MRR in mm³/s.

### Solution

**(a) Spark Energy**

$$
E_{spark} \;=\; V \, I \, T_{on} \;=\; 200 \times 25 \times (100 \times 10^{-6}) \;=\; 0.5 \; \text{Joules}
$$

**(b) Volumetric Material Removal Rate**

First compute the thermal term:

$$
c \, \Delta T + L_f \;=\; (500)(1450) + 2.7 \times 10^{5} \;=\; 7.25 \times 10^{5} + 2.7 \times 10^{5} \;=\; 9.95 \times 10^{5} \;\;\frac{J}{kg}
$$

Volume per spark:

$$
V_{spark} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \, V \, I \, T_{on}}{\rho \, (c \, \Delta T + L_f)} \;=\; \dfrac{0.10 \times 0.5}{7850 \times 9.95 \times 10^{5}}
$$

$$
V_{spark} \;=\; \dfrac{0.05}{7.81 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 6.40 \times 10^{-12} \;\; m^{3}
$$

Converting to mm³ (1 m³ = 10⁹ mm³):

$$
V_{spark} \;=\; 6.40 \times 10^{-3} \;\; mm^{3}
$$

Pulse frequency:

$$
f_p \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(100+50)\times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 6666.67 \;\; Hz
$$

MRR:

$$
\boxed{MRR \;=\; 6.40 \times 10^{-3} \times 6666.67 \;\approx\; 42.67 \;\; mm^{3}/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: Marks are awarded in 3 clear stages — energy, volume per spark, frequency/MRR. Skipping the unit conversion from m³ to mm³ is the **single most common mistake** in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EDM vs. Other NTM Processes

| Parameter | EDM | ECM (Electro Chemical) | LBM (Laser) |
|-----------|-----|------------------------|-------------|
| Energy type | Electrical/thermal | Electrochemical | Optical/thermal |
| Forces on workpiece | None | None | None |
| Workpiece must be | Electrically conductive | Electrically conductive | Any material |
| Tool wear | Yes | No (no tool wear) | Nozzle wear |
| MRR | Moderate | High | Low to Moderate |
| Surface finish | Good (Ra 0.2 µm) | Excellent | Good |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EDM Process — Functional Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Pulse Power Supply] --> B[Servo Control Unit]
    B --> C[Tool Electrode Cathode]
    C --> D[Spark Gap 0.01 to 0.05 mm]
    D --> E[Workpiece Anode]
    D --> F[Dielectric Fluid Tank]
    F --> G[Filtration Unit]
    G --> H[Recirculation Pump]
    H --> F
    B --> I[Parameter Monitor V I Ton Toff]
    I --> A
    E --> J[Solidified Swarf Debris]
    J --> F
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- The **Power Supply → Servo → Electrode → Workpiece** path is the **electrical circuit** that sustains the spark.
- The **Dielectric → Filtration → Pump → Dielectric** loop is the **flushing system** that removes debris.
- **Parameter Monitor** continuously adjusts spark gap to maintain stable discharges.

---
