> **KTU Diagram-based question tip**: In 2024 scheme, **Module 2 questions often ask students to label a schematic of an EDM machine**. Memorize these four blocks — *Power Supply, Dielectric System, Servo Control, and Spark Gap Zone* — as they are the standard answer structure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State any three essential conditions for the EDM process to take place.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, CO1 — Remember):**
1. The workpiece and tool electrode must be **electrically conductive** to sustain current flow across the spark gap. *(1 mark)*
2. The inter-electrode gap must be very small (typically **0.01–0.05 mm**) so that the dielectric fluid can be ionized by the applied voltage. *(1 mark)*
3. The gap must be filled with a suitable **dielectric fluid** (e.g., EDM oil, kerosene, deionized water) which acts as an insulator until voltage breakdown, then as a coolant and debris-flushing medium. *(1 mark)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the four sequential phases of an EDM spark cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, CO1 — Remember):**
1. **Dielectric Ionization** — voltage applied across the gap ionizes the fluid.
2. **Spark Initiation / Discharge** — plasma channel forms, current surges.
3. **Material Removal** — localized heating (8,000–12,000 °C) melts and vaporizes a micro-crater.
4. **Dielectric Recovery** — voltage drops, plasma collapses, fluid flushes debris. *(3 marks, 0.75 each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — EDM Working Principle, Mechanism & Process Parameters** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of the **Electro Discharge Machining (EDM)** process. Describe the role of the dielectric fluid. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss any **four important process parameters** of EDM and their effect on Material Removal Rate (MRR) and surface roughness. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### (a) Working Principle and Dielectric Role (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Setup**: A shaped **tool electrode** (cathode, –ve) and the **workpiece** (anode, +ve) are submerged in a dielectric tank. A pulsed DC supply (typically **80–320 V**) is connected across them with a small spark gap of **0.01–0.05 mm**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Dielectric Ionization**: When the electric field intensity $E = V/d$ exceeds the dielectric strength of the fluid (~10 kV/mm), electrons are stripped from fluid molecules creating a plasma channel of ions and free electrons. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Spark Discharge & Material Removal**: The plasma channel acts as a near-short circuit. Current surges to several hundred amperes in microseconds, concentrating energy in a microscopic area. Local temperatures reach **8,000–12,000 °C**, melting and vaporizing a small volume of workpiece — the **Material Removal Rate (MRR)**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Role of Dielectric Fluid** *(1 mark)*:
- Acts as an **insulator** until breakdown.
- Acts as a **coolant** after the spark.
- **Flushes away** vaporized debris (swarf) preventing short circuits.
- **Re-establishes insulation** between pulses so that the next discharge can occur at a fresh location.

#### (b) Four Process Parameters (7 marks)

| # | Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Roughness |
|---|-----------|---------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Discharge Current (I)** | Increases MRR **strongly** (MRR ∝ I·T_on) | Higher I → Larger craters → **Higher Ra** (rougher) |
| 2 | **Pulse On-Time (T_on)** | Longer T_on → More energy per spark → **Higher MRR** | Longer T_on → Deeper craters → **Higher Ra** |
| 3 | **Pulse Off-Time (T_off)** | Longer T_off → Better flushing → Stable process → Slight increase in MRR | Slight improvement in surface finish due to better debris removal |
| 4 | **Open-circuit Voltage (V)** | Higher V → Larger spark gap, easier initiation → **Slightly higher MRR** | Higher V → **Larger overcut** → poorer dimensional accuracy |

*(2 marks for parameter identification, 2 marks for MRR effects, 2 marks for Ra effects, 1 mark for concluding remarks)*

---

### **Question B — EDM Process Parameters, MRR Derivation and Applications** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail any **four characteristics of the EDM process**. Compare EDM with **conventional machining** on the basis of (i) force on the workpiece, (ii) workpiece material limitation, and (iii) tool wear. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An EDM operation is performed on a hardened die steel with the following data:  
$V = 200$ V, $I = 30$ A, $T_{on} = 80 \, \mu s$, $T_{off} = 40 \, \mu s$, $\eta = 0.08$, $\rho = 7800 \, kg/m^3$, $c = 500$ J/kg·K, $L_f = 2.7 \times 10^5$ J/kg, $\Delta T = 1500$ K.  
Calculate the **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** in mm³/s. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### (a) Four Characteristics & Comparison (7 marks)

**Four Characteristics of EDM** *(4 marks)*:
1. **No mechanical force** on the workpiece — ideal for thin, delicate, or low-rigidity parts.
2. **Independent of material hardness** — only electrical conductivity matters; can machine fully hardened steel (HRC 65+), carbides, and super-alloys.
3. **High temperature localized removal** — 8,000–12,000 °C melts/vaporizes the material.
4. **Tool wear** — though small, electrode wear is non-zero; copper, graphite, brass, and tungsten-copper are common electrode materials.

**Comparison with Conventional Machining** *(3 marks)*:

| Criterion | EDM | Conventional |
|-----------|-----|---------------|
| (i) Force on workpiece | **≈ 0 N** (electro-thermal) | High mechanical cutting force |
| (ii) Material limitation | Only conductive materials | Hardness-limited |
| (iii) Tool wear | Present (TWR = 0.1–25%) | Sharpness-dependent wear |

#### (b) MRR Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Spark Energy** *(1 mark)*:

$$
E_{spark} \;=\; V \, I \, T_{on} \;=\; 200 \times 30 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 0.48 \;\; J
$$

**Step 2 — Thermal Term** *(2 marks)*:

$$
c \, \Delta T + L_f \;=\; (500)(1500) + 2.7 \times 10^{5} \;=\; 7.5 \times 10^{5} + 2.7 \times 10^{5} \;=\; 1.02 \times 10^{6} \;\; J/kg
$$

**Step 3 — Mass Removed per Spark** *(1 mark)*:

$$
m \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \, E_{spark}}{c \, \Delta T + L_f} \;=\; \dfrac{0.08 \times 0.48}{1.02 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 3.765 \times 10^{-8} \;\; kg
$$

**Step 4 — Volume per Spark** *(1 mark)*:

$$
V_{spark} \;=\; \dfrac{m}{\rho} \;=\; \dfrac{3.765 \times 10^{-8}}{7800} \;=\; 4.83 \times 10^{-12} \;\; m^{3} \;=\; 4.83 \times 10^{-3} \;\; mm^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Pulse Frequency** *(1 mark)*:

$$
f_p \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(80+40) \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 8333.33 \;\; Hz
$$

**Step 6 — MRR** *(1 mark)*:

$$
\boxed{MRR \;=\; 4.83 \times 10^{-3} \times 8333.33 \;\approx\; 40.25 \;\; mm^{3}/s}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in EDM Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $\mu s$ to seconds** in $T_{on}$ — silent killer that makes MRR answers wrong by a factor of $10^{6}$.
> 2. **Ignoring the m³ to mm³ unit conversion** — a $10^{9}$ factor error.
> 3. **Omitting $\eta$** in derivations — KTU examiners specifically deduct **1 mark** if efficiency is not mentioned.
> 4. **Confusing TWR with Wear Ratio** — TWR is a *percentage*, WR is a *dimensionless ratio*.
> 5. **Not drawing the dielectric flow direction** in schematics — directional arrows in EDM diagrams carry **easy 1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Mixing up WEDM and Die-sinking EDM** in applications — board examiners flag this as a CO-level misunderstanding.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EDM — Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - EDM = **Electro-thermal**, **non-contact**, **non-traditional** process.
> - Spark temperature: **8,000–12,000 °C**; spark gap: **0.01–0.05 mm**.
> - Four phases: **Ionization → Spark → Melting/Vaporization → Dielectric Recovery**.
> - Workpiece must be **electrically conductive**; hardness is irrelevant.
> - Key parameters: **I, T_on, T_off, V, dielectric type, flushing**.
> - MRR ∝ **I · T_on** (practical formula); MRR ∝ **V · I · T_on · η / [ρ(c·ΔT + L_f) · (T_on + T_off)]** (theoretical formula).
> - TWR = (Volume of electrode wear / Volume of workpiece removed) × 100%.
> - Common electrode materials: **Copper, Graphite, Brass, Tungsten-Copper (W-Cu)**.
> - Common dielectrics: **EDM oil, Kerosene, Deionized water**.
> - **Die-sinking (Ram) EDM** uses a shaped electrode; **Wire EDM (WEDM)** uses a continuously travelling brass wire.
> - Zero mechanical force → ideal for **thin webs, delicate parts, low-rigidity components**.
> - Used heavily in **aerospace (turbine blades), die-mould industry, medical implants, automotive tooling**.
> - Surface finish achievable: **Ra 0.2 µm (finishing)**; typical accuracy: **± 0.005 mm**.
> - High MRR ⇔ High Ra (rough surface); High T_on ⇔ High MRR but poor surface — always quote the **trade-off** in viva.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Machining (EBM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Machining (EBM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electron Beam Machining (EBM)** is defined as a **non-contact, thermal-type, advanced non-traditional manufacturing process** in which a high-velocity, focused beam of **high-kinetic-energy electrons** is used to remove material from the workpiece by **thermal vaporization (sublimation)** of the workpiece material. The process is conducted under a **high vacuum (typically 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr)** to prevent electron scattering by air molecules and to suppress oxidation.

The electrons are emitted from a **thermionic emission cathode** (usually a tungsten filament), accelerated through a potential difference of **50 kV to 200 kV**, and focused by **electromagnetic lenses** onto a small spot on the workpiece. The **electron kinetic energy** is converted into intense heat upon impact, with power densities reaching **10⁹ W/mm²**, instantaneously vaporizing the workpiece material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** EBM is grouped under **Thermal Energy Based Non-Traditional Processes**, alongside **Laser Beam Machining (LBM)**, **Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)**, and **Electric Discharge Machining (EDM)**. Examiner's favourite topic due to its links with **modern physics (electron optics)** and **industrial micro-machining** relevance.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of EBM like a "tornado of tiny bullets" guided by magnets.**
> Imagine you have millions of super-small, super-fast bullets (electrons). You shoot them from a gun (electron gun). These bullets are so small and fast that when they hit metal, they don't bounce — they turn the metal instantly into vapor (like a magnifying glass burning paper, but **thousands of times more powerful and precise**). Because the bullets are charged particles (negative), you can steer them with magnets instead of mirrors. To stop them from getting lost in air, you shoot them inside an **airless chamber (vacuum chamber)**, similar to how space telescopes avoid atmospheric distortion.

---

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in EBM

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Accelerating Voltage | **50 kV – 200 kV** | Determines electron velocity & penetration depth |
| Beam Current | **10⁻⁹ A – 10⁻³ A** | Controls heat input rate |
| Power Density | **10⁹ W/mm²** | Far exceeds LBM (10⁶–10⁷ W/mm²) |
| Vacuum Pressure | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ torr** | Prevents electron-gas collisions |
| Beam Diameter | **0.01 mm – 0.1 mm** | Determines kerf width & precision |
| Material Removal Rate (MRR) | **0.1 – 50 mm³/min** | Depends on workpiece material |
| Electron Speed | **≈ 0.6 × Speed of Light** | At 100 kV accelerating voltage |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The EBM process is governed by **five sequential physical stages**, each essential for the conversion of electrical energy into material vaporization:

**Stage 1 — Thermionic Electron Emission**
- A **tungsten (W), tantalum (Ta), or lanthanum hexaboride (LaB₆) cathode** is heated to temperatures of **2500°C – 2800°C**.
- At this temperature, electrons acquire enough thermal energy to overcome the **work function (φ)** of the cathode material.
- Emission is governed by the **Richardson-Dushman Equation**:
  
$$J = A_R \, T^2 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{\phi}{k_B T}\right)$$

  where $J$ is emission current density (A/m²), $A_R$ is Richardson's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature (K), $\phi$ is work function (eV), and $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant.

**Stage 2 — Electron Acceleration**
- Emitted electrons are accelerated toward a positively biased anode.
- An electric field $E$ imparts kinetic energy to each electron:

$$eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

  where $e$ = electron charge (1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C), $V$ = accelerating voltage, $m_e$ = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg, $v$ = electron velocity.

- At 100 kV, electron velocity ≈ **1.6 × 10⁸ m/s** (≈ 0.53 c, where c = speed of light).

**Stage 3 — Electromagnetic Focusing**
- **Electromagnetic lenses (magnetic focusing coils)** act as convex lenses for electrons.
- Unlike optical lenses, their focal length can be **dynamically varied** by adjusting coil current.
- The beam is focused onto a spot diameter as small as **10 µm**.

**Stage 4 — Beam–Workpiece Interaction (Heat Generation)**
- Focused electrons strike the workpiece surface.
- Kinetic energy is converted to **heat** via inelastic collisions with workpiece atoms.
- Local temperature rises to **> 5000 K** within microseconds.
- Material is removed by:
  - **Vaporization** (primary mechanism)
  - **Melting and expulsion** (secondary)

**Stage 5 — Vapor Removal**
- Vaporized material is drawn out of the chamber by the vacuum system.
- **No mechanical force, no chemical reaction, no ionization of workpiece** (unlike EDM).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Terms | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$ | Kinetic energy of accelerated electron | $V$ in volts, $v$ in m/s |
| 2 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m_e}}$ | Electron velocity after acceleration | Valid for non-relativistic; relativistic correction needed > 50 kV |
| 3 | $\dfrac{v}{c} = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1 + \frac{eV}{m_e c^2})^2}}$ | Relativistic velocity ratio | Use when V > 50 kV |
| 4 | $J = A_R T^2 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi}{k_B T}\right)$ | Richardson-Dushman emission current density | T in Kelvin, φ in eV |
| 5 | $P = VI_b$ | Beam power delivered to workpiece | $I_b$ = beam current (A) |
| 6 | $MRR = \dfrac{P \cdot \eta}{H_v \cdot \rho}$ | Material removal rate | $\eta$ = efficiency factor (≈ 0.5–0.85), $H_v$ = latent heat of vaporization, $\rho$ = density |
| 7 | $Q = m \cdot H_v$ | Heat required to vaporize mass m | $H_v$ in J/kg |
| 8 | $\delta_{pen} = 2.1 \times 10^{-11} \cdot \dfrac{V^{1.7}}{\rho}$ | Electron penetration depth (Schröder's law) | $\rho$ = density in kg/m³, V in volts, $\delta$ in meters |
| 9 | $d_{spot} = \dfrac{d_0}{M}$ | Spot diameter after focusing | $d_0$ = crossover diameter, M = magnification of lens |
| 10 | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | Power density at workpiece | $A_{spot}$ in mm² |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** In tables and equations, never write `$\vert x \vert$` or `$|x|$` with vertical pipes. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. The above table uses LaTeX vertical bars only inside math mode ($...$), which is acceptable because they are NOT interpreted as markdown column separators when escaped.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Drilling of micro-holes in turbine blades, combustion chambers, and rocket engine nozzles (cooling holes).
- **Electronics:** Drilling of micro-vias in **multilayer PCBs** (printed circuit boards) — holes smaller than human hair.
- **Nuclear:** Fabrication of fuel element filters.
- **Medical:** Manufacturing of **microsurgical instruments** and micro-needles.
- **Watch & Precision Industry:** Drilling jewel bearings.
- **Semiconductor:** Maskless lithography and selective doping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of MRR (Material Removal Rate) in EBM

The MRR equation is a **KTU 2024 frequently asked 14-mark derivation**. We derive it from energy balance principles.

**Assumption:** All incident electron beam power is absorbed at the workpiece surface (negligible reflection). A fraction $\eta$ of this power is effectively used for material vaporization; the rest is lost through conduction, radiation, and reflection.

**Step 1 — Total beam power delivered to workpiece**

$$P = V \cdot I_b$$

where $V$ = accelerating voltage (V), $I_b$ = beam current (A).

**Step 2 — Effective heat used for vaporization**

$$Q_{eff} = \eta \cdot P = \eta \cdot V \cdot I_b$$

The factor $\eta$ (process efficiency) accounts for:
- **Back-scattered electrons** (energy lost, not deposited): 5–25%
- **Heat conduction** into bulk workpiece: ~30%
- **Heat radiation** from hot spot: ~5–10%
- **Vaporization efficiency**: remaining portion

Typical values: $\eta \approx 0.5$ to $0.85$.

**Step 3 — Heat required to vaporize mass $m$**

The latent heat of vaporization $H_v$ is the energy required per kilogram to vaporize the material.

$$Q_{vap} = m \cdot H_v$$

**Step 4 — Volume removed (MRR) is mass removed divided by density**

$$m = \rho \cdot \text{Volume}$$

$$Q_{eff} = \rho \cdot \text{Volume} \cdot H_v$$

**Step 5 — Solving for volume per unit time (MRR)**

The MRR is the volume of material removed per minute. Since $Q_{eff}$ is power (J/s), the MRR per second is:

$$\text{MRR}_{sec} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I_b}{\rho \cdot H_v}$$

Converting to **mm³/min** by multiplying by $6 \times 10^4$ (mm³/m³ conversion and seconds to minutes):

$$\text{MRR}_{min} = \frac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I_b}{\rho \cdot H_v} \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}$$

---

### 3.2 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An EBM machine uses an accelerating voltage of **150 kV** and a beam current of **5 mA** to drill holes in an aluminum sheet. The latent heat of vaporization of aluminum is **1.09 × 10⁷ J/kg** and density is **2700 kg/m³**. Assume process efficiency = 0.75. Calculate the MRR.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute beam power.

$$P = V \cdot I_b = (150 \times 10^3 \text{ V}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}) = 750 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Compute effective vaporization power.

$$Q_{eff} = \eta \cdot P = 0.75 \cdot 750 = 562.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Apply MRR formula per second.

$$\text{MRR}_{sec} = \frac{562.5}{2700 \cdot 1.09 \times 10^7}$$

$$\text{MRR}_{sec} = \frac{562.5}{2.943 \times 10^{10}} = 1.911 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to mm³/min.

$$1 \text{ m}^3 = 10^9 \text{ mm}^3, \quad 1 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{MRR}_{min} = 1.911 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 10^9 \cdot 60 = 1.147 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $\text{MRR} \approx 1.147 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$ — a realistic value for EBM machining of aluminum.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Electron Penetration Depth (Schröder-Badenhorst Equation)

Penetration depth governs the **maximum depth of cut per pass**.

**Step 1:** From the kinetic energy relation, the electron's momentum is:

$$p = m_e v = \sqrt{2 m_e e V}$$

**Step 2:** As the electron penetrates matter, it undergoes scattering and energy loss. Experimentally, the energy loss rate with depth $z$ is approximately:

$$\frac{dE}{dz} \propto -\frac{\rho}{V^{0.3}}$$

**Step 3:** Integrating gives the maximum penetration depth:

$$\delta_{pen} = K \cdot \frac{V^{1.7}}{\rho}$$

where $K \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-11}$ (when V is in volts, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $\delta$ in meters).

**Step 4:** For steel ($\rho \approx 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$) at $V = 100 \text{ kV}$:

$$\delta_{pen} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{-11} \cdot (10^5)^{1.7}}{7850}$$

$$\delta_{pen} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 5.012 \times 10^{8}}{7850}$$

$$\delta_{pen} \approx 1.34 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.134 \,\mu\text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** A single EBM pulse only vaporizes a depth of ≈ 100 nm. Multiple pulses (or workpiece motion) are required to drill deeper holes — hence the slow MRR but excellent precision.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: EBM vs LBM vs EDM

| Parameter | EBM | LBM | EDM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | High-velocity electrons | Photons (light) | Electrical discharge |
| Medium Required | Vacuum | Gas or air | Dielectric fluid |
| Power Density (W/mm²) | **10⁹** | 10⁶–10⁷ | 10⁵–10⁶ |
| MRR | Low | Medium | High |
| Accuracy (µm) | ± 0.02 | ± 0.025 | ± 5 |
| Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) | Very Small | Medium | Large |
| Workpiece Conductivity | Any material (conductors & insulators) | Any material | **Conductors only** |
| Energy Efficiency | Low (≈ 30%) | Medium (≈ 50%) | High (≈ 90%) |
| Surface Finish (Ra) | 0.5–1.5 µm | 0.5–2 µm | 0.3–1.5 µm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EBM Block Architecture (Functional Flow)

This block diagram represents the functional architecture of an EBM system, mapping the energy and signal flow from input power to material removal.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Power Supply Unit] -->|High Voltage DC 50 200 kV| A2[Step 2: Electron Gun Assembly]
    A2 -->|Thermionic Emission| A3[Step 3: Cathode Filament Tungsten LaB6]
    A3 -->|Accelerated Electrons| A4[Step 4: Magnetic Focusing Lens 1 Coarse]
    A4 -->|Partially Focused Beam| A5[Step 5: Magnetic Focusing Lens 2 Fine]
    A5 -->|Tightly Focused Beam| A6[Step 6: Beam Deflection Coils X Y Scan]
    A6 -->|Scanned Beam| A7[Step 7: Workpiece Surface]
    A8[Step 8: Vacuum Chamber 10 to power of minus 4 torr] -->|Provides Vacuum Environment| A4
    A8 -->|Provides Vacuum Environment| A5
    A8 -->|Provides Vacuum Environment| A6
    A9[Step 9: CNC Workpiece Table XY Movement] -->|Positions Workpiece| A7
    A10[Step 10: Vacuum Pump System] -->|Removes Vaporized Material| A8
    A11[Step 11: Operator Console HMI CNC] -->|Control Signals| A1
    A11 -->|Control Signals| A4
    A11 -->|Control Signals| A5
    A11 -->|Control Signals| A6
    A11 -->|Control Signals| A9
    A7 -->|Vaporized Material and Gases| A8
    A7 -->|Heat and Spatter| A12[Step 12: Heat Exchanger and Trap]
    A12 -->|Condensed Vapors| A10
```

**Reading the diagram:** Power flows from the **Power Supply Unit (A1)** → generates the high voltage that drives the **Electron Gun (A2)** → **Cathode (A3)** emits electrons → electrons are focused by **two magnetic lenses (A4 & A5)** → **Deflection coils (A6)** scan the beam across the **Workpiece (A7)**. The entire chamber is held under **vacuum (A8)** to prevent electron scattering. The **CNC table (A9)** provides macroscale motion. Vapor is extracted by the **vacuum pump (A10)**. The **operator console (A11)** orchestrates everything. The **heat exchanger (A12)** recovers condensable vapors for environmental safety.

---

### 4.2 EBM Process Flow (Logical Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Start: Workpiece Mounting] --> B2[Evacuate Chamber to High Vacuum]
    B2 --> B3[Heat Cathode Filament to Emission Temp]
    B3 --> B4[Apply Accelerating Voltage]
    B4 --> B5[Focus Beam via Magnetic Lenses]
    B5 --> B6{Decision: Drilling or Cutting?}
    B6 -->|Drilling| B7[Position Beam at Hole Location]
    B6 -->|Cutting| B8[Trace CNC Path on Workpiece]
    B7 --> B9[Vaporize Material by Pulsed Beam]
    B8 --> B9
    B9 --> B10{Decision: Desired Depth Achieved?}
    B10 -->|No| B9
    B10 -->|Yes| B11[Stop Beam and Cool Down]
    B11 --> B12[Vent Chamber with Inert Gas]
    B12 --> B13[Remove Finished Workpiece]
    B13 --> B14[End of Process]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two stages of magnetic focusing?** Magnetic lens 1 performs **coarse focusing** to capture the wide electron distribution emerging from the gun. Magnetic lens 2 performs **fine focusing** to reduce the beam to a near-spot diameter of ~10 µm. This two-stage system is borrowed from **electron microscopy optics**.

---

### 4.3 Heat Generation Mechanism (Energy Conversion Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Electrical Energy from Power Supply] --> C2[Electron Kinetic Energy 1/2 m v squared]
    C2 --> C3[Beam Impact on Workpiece Surface]
    C3 --> C4[Inelastic Collisions with Workpiece Atoms]
    C4 --> C5[Localized Temperature Rise Above 5000 K]
    C5 --> C6[Phase Change: Solid to Liquid]
    C6 --> C7[Phase Change: Liquid to Vapor]
    C7 --> C8[Material Removal by Vaporization]
    C5 --> C9[Heat Loss by Conduction into Bulk]
    C5 --> C10[Heat Loss by Radiation]
    C5 --> C11[Heat Loss by Backscattered Electrons]
```

**Key insight:** Only **Path C5 → C6 → C7 → C8** is productive. All other branches (C9, C10, C11) are **energy losses**, which is why the **overall thermal efficiency of EBM is only ~30%**, much lower than EDM.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Electron Beam Machining (EBM). List any three advantages of EBM over conventional machining.**

**Model Answer:**
> **Definition:** EBM is a thermal, non-contact, non-traditional machining process that uses a focused beam of high-velocity electrons, accelerated under high vacuum (50–200 kV), to remove material by **thermal vaporization**.

**Three Advantages:**
1. **No mechanical force or contact** → zero tool wear, no vibration, no chatter.
2. **Extremely high power density (10⁹ W/mm²)** → enables machining of any material (metals, ceramics, composites, refractories).
3. **Excellent dimensional accuracy (≈ ± 0.02 mm)** → superior surface finish (Ra 0.5–1.5 µm).
4. **Very small heat-affected zone (HAZ)** → minimal thermal distortion of workpiece.

*(Any three valid advantages)* — **3 marks**

---

#### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is EBM conducted in a high vacuum chamber? What happens if vacuum is compromised?**

**Model Answer:**
> **Reasons for Vacuum:**
> 1. **Prevent electron scattering:** Air molecules would deflect the electron beam, ruining focus and accuracy.
> 2. **Avoid oxidation:** Hot vaporized metal would react with oxygen, contaminating the chamber.
> 3. **Maintain cathode life:** Oxidation destroys the tungsten filament quickly.
> 4. **Enable high voltage without arcing:** Air breakdown at 100 kV would arc across, damaging equipment.

> **Consequences of Vacuum Loss:**
> - Beam diverges (defocused) → poor accuracy.
> - Filament burns out within minutes.
> - Vaporized metal oxidizes → toxic white smoke (e.g., tungsten oxide for W, aluminum oxide for Al).
> - High-voltage arcing possible → equipment damage.

**— 3 marks**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question B (A) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **working principle and main components of an Electron Beam Machining (EBM) system**. Discuss the role of the electron gun, magnetic focusing lenses, and deflection coils in detail. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **Material Removal Rate (MRR) in EBM** in terms of beam power, process efficiency, density, and latent heat of vaporization. A 150 kV EBM unit operates with a beam current of 8 mA to drill aluminum (H_v = 1.09 × 10⁷ J/kg, ρ = 2700 kg/m³). If efficiency η = 0.70, find the MRR in mm³/min. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

##### Model Solution to (a)

**Working Principle:** When a tungsten filament is heated to ~2500°C, it emits electrons via thermionic emission. These electrons are accelerated by a high positive potential (50–200 kV) toward the anode, pass through the anode bore, and travel as a high-velocity beam inside a vacuum chamber. Two magnetic lenses focus the beam to a fine spot, and electromagnetic deflection coils scan the spot across the workpiece in a controlled pattern (CNC-driven). The kinetic energy of the electrons converts to heat upon impact, vaporizing the workpiece material.

**Main Components (described in tabular form):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Electron Gun | Emits and accelerates electrons; contains cathode (filament), Wehnelt cylinder (control grid), and anode |
| Magnetic Focusing Lens | Focuses diverging electrons into a tight beam using magnetic fields; acts as a "lens for electrons" |
| Deflection Coils | Steer the focused beam in X-Y plane for CNC-controlled machining |
| Vacuum Chamber | Maintains 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr to prevent scattering |
| Vacuum Pump System | Rotary + diffusion pumps to achieve high vacuum |
| Workpiece Table | Multi-axis CNC positioning system |
| Power Supply | Provides 50–200 kV DC accelerating voltage and filament heating current |
| Control Console | HMI + CNC for programming and monitoring |

**Role of Electron Gun:** Generates a high-current, narrow electron beam via thermionic emission; controls beam current via Wehnelt cylinder bias; accelerates electrons to high kinetic energy using high voltage.

**Role of Magnetic Focusing Lenses:** Create a converging magnetic field that bends electron trajectories inward, focusing the beam to a small spot (10–100 µm). Their focal length is varied by adjusting coil current.

**Role of Deflection Coils:** Two pairs of coils (X and Y) produce transverse magnetic fields that deflect the beam laterally, allowing it to trace any CNC-defined pattern over the workpiece without moving the workpiece.

**— 7 marks (Component 3 + Diagram 2 + Description 2)**

---

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Derivation:** (Already shown in Section 3.1 — reproduced concisely for exam purpose)

Energy delivered per second (beam power):

$$P = V \cdot I_b$$

Useful energy for vaporization:

$$Q_{eff} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I_b$$

Heat required to vaporize mass $m$:

$$Q_{vap} = m \cdot H_v = \rho \cdot \text{Vol} \cdot H_v$$

Equating:

$$\eta \cdot V \cdot I_b = \rho \cdot \text{Vol/s} \cdot H_v$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{sec} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I_b}{\rho \cdot H_v}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $V = 150 \text{ kV} = 150{,}000 \text{ V}$
- $I_b = 8 \text{ mA} = 0.008 \text{ A}$
- $H_v = 1.09 \times 10^7 \text{ J/kg}$
- $\rho = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\eta = 0.70$

**Step 1:** Compute power.

$$P = 150{,}000 \cdot 0.008 = 1200 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Effective vaporization power.

$$Q_{eff} = 0.70 \cdot 1200 = 840 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** MRR per second.

$$\text{MRR}_{sec} = \frac{840}{2700 \cdot 1.09 \times 10^7}$$

$$\text{MRR}_{sec} = \frac{840}{2.943 \times 10^{10}} = 2.854 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to mm³/min.

$$\text{MRR}_{min} = 2.854 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 10^9 \cdot 60 = 1.713 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 1.71 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**— 7 marks (Derivation 4 + Numerical 3)**

---

#### Question B (B) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Compare **Electron Beam Machining (EBM)** and **Laser Beam Machining (LBM)** on the basis of: energy source, medium of operation, power density, accuracy, HAZ, MRR, and material suitability. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the **process parameters affecting EBM performance**. Discuss the effect of accelerating voltage, beam current, and focal spot size on MRR and surface finish. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

##### Model Solution to (a)

**Comparison Table (Model Answer):**

| Parameter | EBM | LBM |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | High-velocity electrons (kinetic energy) | Coherent photons (light energy) |
| **Medium of Operation** | High vacuum (10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ torr) | Gas (O₂, N₂, Ar) or air |
| **Power Density** | 10⁹ W/mm² | 10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm² |
| **Accuracy** | ± 0.02 mm (superior) | ± 0.025 mm |
| **HAZ** | Very small | Moderate |
| **MRR** | Lower (0.1–50 mm³/min) | Higher (especially with CO₂ laser) |
| **Material Suitability** | All materials | All materials (but reflectivity matters) |
| **Reflection Loss** | Negligible | Significant (especially with metals & CO₂ laser) |
| **Equipment Cost** | Very High | High |
| **Operator Safety** | X-ray hazard; needs shielding | Laser eye hazard |

**— 7 marks**

---

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Key Process Parameters:**

**1. Accelerating Voltage (V):**
- Higher V → higher electron velocity → greater kinetic energy.
- **Effect on MRR:** Increases linearly with V (MRR ∝ V).
- **Effect on penetration depth:** Increases (∝ V^1.7).
- **Effect on surface finish:** Slight degradation if too high (more spatter).

**2. Beam Current (I_b):**
- Higher I_b → more electrons per second → more energy deposition.
- **Effect on MRR:** Increases linearly (MRR ∝ I_b).
- **Effect on surface finish:** Excessive I_b → melt zone increases → rougher surface.

**3. Focal Spot Size (d):**
- Smaller spot → higher power density ($P_d = \frac{P}{\pi d^2/4}$).
- **Effect on MRR:** Higher P_d → faster vaporization → higher MRR.
- **Effect on accuracy:** Smaller spot → finer kerf → better dimensional accuracy.
- **Effect on surface finish:** Smaller spot → smoother surface (Ra down to 0.5 µm).

**4. Pulse Duration & Frequency (in pulsed mode):**
- Short pulses → localized heating → less HAZ → better finish.
- High frequency → higher effective MRR.

**5. Workpiece Material Properties:**
- $H_v$ and $\rho$ inversely affect MRR: harder-to-vaporize materials → lower MRR.

**6. Vacuum Quality:**
- Poor vacuum → beam divergence → accuracy loss.

**7. Scanning Speed:**
- Higher speed → less time per spot → lower local heating → lower MRR but smoother surface.

**— 7 marks**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing thermionic emission with field emission:** Filament emission is **thermionic** (heat-driven). Don't call it photoelectric or field emission.
> 2. **Forgetting vacuum in MRR derivation:** Examiner's trick question — "Why is MRR of EBM lower in air?" Answer: Without vacuum, electrons scatter → energy not delivered to workpiece → effective MRR drops to ~0.
> 3. **Wrong units in MRR numerical:** Always convert mm³/s to mm³/min by multiplying by 60, and m³ to mm³ by multiplying by 10⁹. Missing this conversion = -2 marks.
> 4. **Saying "EBM uses light" — WRONG.** EBM uses electrons, NOT photons. This is a -3 mark blunder.
> 5. **Drawing diagrams without labels:** Unlabeled block diagrams fetch only 1/3 marks. Always label each block and use arrows for direction of flow.
> 6. **Forgetting the role of the Wehnelt cylinder:** This control grid regulates beam current — often skipped by students.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] EBM = **Electron** Beam Machining (NOT light/laser).
- [x] Operates under **high vacuum** (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr).
- [x] Accelerating voltage range: **50 – 200 kV**.
- [x] Power density: **10⁹ W/mm²** (highest among NTM processes).
- [x] Material removal mechanism: **thermal vaporization** (sublimation).
- [x] Can machine **any material** — conductors, insulators, ceramics, refractories.
- [x] **No tool wear** — non-contact process.
- [x] **No mechanical stress** — no residual stress on workpiece.
- [x] Key equation: $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I_b}{\rho \cdot H_v}$
- [x] Electron velocity at 100 kV ≈ **0.53c** (relativistic regime).
- [x] Main components: **Electron Gun, Magnetic Lenses, Deflection Coils, Vacuum Chamber, CNC Table, Power Supply**.
- [x] Cathode materials: **Tungsten (W), Tantalum (Ta), LaB₆**.
- [x] Electron emission governed by **Richardson-Dushman equation**.
- [x] Penetration depth $\delta \propto \dfrac{V^{1.7}}{\rho}$.
- [x] **No dielectric fluid** required (unlike EDM).
- [x] **X-ray hazard** — requires lead shielding.
- [x] Applications: **micro-drilling in turbine blades, PCBs, micro-holes, watch jewels**.
- [x] **Limitation:** Cannot machine on materials with magnetic properties easily; small work chamber.
- [x] Overall efficiency: ~30% (low compared to EDM's 90%).
- [x] MRR range: **0.1 – 50 mm³/min** (slow but precise).

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory trick for EBM:**
> *"**E**lectrons in **V**acuum, accelerated to **H**igh **V**elocity, focused by **M**agnets, **V**aporize the **M**etal."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Beam Machining (LBM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laser Beam Machining (LBM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Definition**
> **Laser Beam Machining (LBM)** is a *non-contact, thermal-energy-based, advanced material removal process* in which a highly coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of photons (generated by stimulating a lasing medium) is optically focused onto a tiny area of the workpiece. The intense localized energy density (typically **10⁶ to 10⁷ W/mm²**) raises the workpiece temperature instantly beyond its **melting point** and subsequently its **vaporization point**, causing material ejection in the form of molten droplets and vapor. Because the photon beam imparts no mechanical force, LBM belongs to the family of **Advanced/Non-Traditional Manufacturing Processes (NTMP)**.

**Three cardinal properties of a Laser (LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation):**

| Property | Meaning | Engineering Relevance |
|----------|---------|------------------------|
| **Coherence** | All photons are in phase (both spatial & temporal) | Enables tight focusing to micron spots |
| **Monochromaticity** | Single, narrow wavelength | Predictable material interaction |
| **Collimation** | Parallel beam with minimal divergence | Long working distances possible |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Surgical Sun-Glass"

Imagine you are a child holding a magnifying glass on a bright sunny day. The sun's rays are diffuse and harmless, but the moment the lens bends them to a single bright spot on a piece of paper, the paper instantly smokes, chars, and burns through. Now imagine this:

- The **Sun** = an ordinary light source (incoherent, spread out).
- The **Magnifying glass** = the laser's optical focusing lens.
- The **Sunlight** = the **pumping energy** exciting the lasing medium.
- The **Paper** = the **workpiece**.

The laser is essentially a *magnifying glass for electricity* — but instead of paper, it can slice through **titanium, ceramics, diamond, and super-alloys** with micron precision. The "burning" is so instantaneous that the surrounding metal does not even get hot — this is called **cold machining** (a misnomer, but industrially accepted because the Heat Affected Zone is microscopic).

> [!NOTE] **Why LBM is "Non-Contact"**
> The photon beam has **zero mass and zero mechanical momentum** transferred to the workpiece. Therefore, there is no tool wear, no vibration, and no mechanical stress — a major advantage over conventional milling or EDM.

---

## 1.3 Classification of LBM (KTU Module 2 Highlight)

> [!NOTE] **KTU Most-Frequently Tested Sub-Topic**
> LBM is broadly classified by the **physical state of the removed material**:

1. **Material Removal (Cutting / Drilling / Engraving)** — molten + vapor phase ejection.
2. **Material Joining (Welding / Brazing)** — only melting, no vaporization.
3. **Material Treatment (Surface hardening / Cladding / Annealing)** — sub-melt heat input.

For Module 2, the focus is **Material Removal Mode**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

The LBM process unfolds in a precise physical sequence:

**Step 1 — Lasing Action in Resonator Cavity**
The lasing medium (gas, solid, or fiber) is excited by a pumping source (electrical discharge, flash lamp, or diode). Spontaneous photons stimulate the emission chain reaction, amplifying light between two mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially transmissive).

**Step 2 — Beam Delivery & Focusing**
The emitted laser beam is delivered via mirrors, fiber optics, or articulated arms, then focused by a lens (typically ZnSe for CO₂, fused silica for Nd:YAG) to a **spot diameter (d)** on the order of **0.1 to 1 mm**.

**Step 3 — Energy Coupling with Workpiece**
When photons strike the workpiece, their energy is absorbed by free electrons (for metals) or molecular bonds (for non-metals). The absorbed energy converts to lattice heat within picoseconds.

**Step 4 — Phase Change Chain Reaction**
$$\text{Solid} \xrightarrow{\text{Absorb } E} \text{Melt Pool} \xrightarrow{+E} \text{Vapor Plasma} \xrightarrow{\text{Eject}} \text{Kerf}$$

**Step 5 — Material Ejection**
A coaxial assist gas (O₂, N₂, Ar, or He) blows the molten + vaporized debris out of the cut zone, preventing re-deposition and cooling the cut edges.

---

## 2.2 Common Industrial Laser Sources (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Laser Type | Active Medium | Wavelength | Typical Power | Best Application |
|------------|---------------|------------|---------------|------------------|
| **CO₂ Laser** | CO₂/N₂/He gas mixture | **10.6 µm** (far-IR) | 100 W – 50 kW | Cutting wood, acrylic, mild steel, ceramics |
| **Nd:YAG Laser** | Neodymium-doped YAG crystal | **1.06 µm** (near-IR) | 50 W – 6 kW | Cutting/drilling metals via fiber delivery |
| **Fiber Laser** | Yb-doped optical fiber | **1.07 µm** | 20 W – 50 kW | Sheet metal cutting, marking |
| **Excimer Laser** | Rare-gas halide (e.g., ArF) | **0.193 µm** (UV) | < 100 W (pulsed) | Micro-machining, semiconductor lithography |
| **Disk Laser** | Yb:YAG thin disk | **1.03 µm** | 1 kW – 16 kW | High-speed welding, thick plate cutting |

---

## 2.3 Process Parameters Influencing LBM

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Process |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------------------|
| Laser Power | $P$ | 100 W – 10 kW | ↑ P ⇒ ↑ MRR, ↑ HAZ |
| Pulse Duration | $t_p$ | µs – ms (pulsed) / CW | ↓ $t_p$ ⇒ ↓ HAZ, ↑ precision |
| Pulse Frequency | $f$ | 1 – 100 kHz | Controls overlap & heat input |
| Focal Spot Diameter | $d$ | 0.1 – 1.0 mm | ↓ d ⇒ ↑ Power Density |
| Cutting Speed | $v$ | 0.1 – 20 m/min | ↑ v ⇒ ↓ HAZ, ↓ kerf taper |
| Assist Gas Pressure | $P_g$ | 1 – 20 bar | ↑ $P_g$ ⇒ cleaner cut |
| Stand-off Distance | $S$ | 0.5 – 2.0 mm | Affects focal position |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master These Equations — They Appear in 90% of Numericals**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units |
|---|--------------|----------|-------|
| 1 | **Power Density** (Intensity) | $I = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$ | W/mm² |
| 2 | **Total Energy per Pulse** | $E = P \times t_{p}$ | Joules (J) |
| 3 | **Energy per Unit Length** | $E_{L} = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | J/mm |
| 4 | **Spot Area** | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | mm² |
| 5 | **Heat Input** | $Q = P \times t = E$ | J |
| 6 | **Volumetric Material Removal Rate (MMRR)** | $\text{MRR}_{v} = v \times w \times t$ | mm³/s |
| 7 | **Specific Energy** | $E_{s} = \dfrac{P}{v \times w \times t}$ | J/mm³ |
| 8 | **Kerf Width Approximation** | $w \approx d$ (for CW) ; $w \approx d + 2 \times \delta_{\text{HAZ}}$ | mm |
| 9 | **Spot Diameter (Lens Equation)** | $d = \dfrac{4 \lambda f}{\pi D}$ | mm |
| 10 | **Absorptivity (Metal, Drude Model)** | $A_{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{0.365}{\sqrt{\rho \lambda}}$ | dimensionless |

Where:
- $P$ = Laser power (W)
- $d$ = Focal spot diameter (mm)
- $v$ = Traverse/cutting speed (mm/s)
- $t$ or $t_p$ = Pulse / interaction time (s)
- $w$ = Kerf width (mm)
- $\lambda$ = Laser wavelength (µm)
- $\rho$ = Electrical resistivity of workpiece (Ω·m)
- $f$ = Focal length of lens (mm)
- $D$ = Raw beam diameter (mm)

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of LBM

- **Aerospace industry**: Drilling micro-holes (Ø 0.1–1 mm) in turbine blades and combustor liners, in arrays of thousands.
- **Electronics**: Singulation of silicon wafers, PCB depaneling, micro-via drilling.
- **Medical devices**: Stent cutting, surgical blade profiling from stainless steel / Nitinol.
- **Automotive**: Tailored blank welding, gear hardening, gasket cutting.
- **Tool & Die**: Engraving EDM electrodes, cutting hardened tool steels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Density at the Focal Spot

**Statement:** Show that the *average power intensity* (power per unit area) at the focal spot of a laser is given by $I = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$.

**Given:**
- Laser output power = $P$ (W)
- Focal spot diameter = $d$ (mm), assumed circular
- Spot area $A$ is circular

**Step 1 — Definition of Intensity.**
Intensity $I$ is defined as power delivered per unit cross-sectional area:
$$I = \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Area of a circular focal spot.**
The focal spot is approximated as a perfect circle of diameter $d$, so its area is:
$$A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution.**
Substituting the expression for $A$ into the intensity equation:
$$I = \frac{P}{\dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}} = P \cdot \frac{4}{\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result.**
$$\boxed{I = \frac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}}$$

**Interpretation:** Halving the spot diameter *quadruples* the intensity. This is why LBM systems use very short focal-length lenses — to squeeze the spot diameter down and push intensity above the vaporization threshold (~ $10^{6}$ W/mm² for steel).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy per Unit Length of Cut (Heat Input per mm)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for energy supplied per millimetre of cut length, used to assess HAZ.

**Step 1 — Total heat input over time $t$:**
$$Q = P \times t$$

**Step 2 — Length traversed during time $t$:**
A workpiece moving at traverse speed $v$ covers:
$$L = v \times t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{L}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Heat input per unit length:**
Substitute $t$ back:
$$E_{L} = \frac{Q}{L} = \frac{P \times (L/v)}{L}$$

$$\boxed{E_{L} = \frac{P}{v}}$$

**Engineering use:** KTU exam problems often ask: "If cutting speed is doubled, how does heat input per mm change?" — Answer: *Halved*. This directly reduces the HAZ and improves cut quality.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Laser Cutting

**Statement:** Prove that the volumetric MRR for a straight-line laser cut equals $v \times w \times t$, where $w$ is the kerf width and $t$ is the depth of cut.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the cut.**
The laser removes a long rectangular slot of:
- Width = kerf width = $w$
- Depth = thickness penetrated = $t$ (or workpiece thickness for through-cut)
- Length traversed per second = $v$

**Step 2 — Volume of material removed per unit time.**
Volume per unit length = $w \times t$. Since the laser moves $v$ mm in 1 second:
$$\text{MRR}_{v} = \text{Volume per second} = (w \times t) \times v$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_{v} = v \cdot w \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — Specific Energy (process efficiency check):**
$$E_{s} = \frac{P}{\text{MRR}_{v}} = \frac{P}{v \cdot w \cdot t}$$

Typical values of $E_s$ for laser cutting of mild steel are in the range **4 – 10 J/mm³**, much lower than conventional machining.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Drilling Depth per Pulse (Energy Balance Model)

This is the most analytically challenging derivation and a *favourite KTU question*.

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the maximum depth of penetration achievable by a single laser pulse of energy $E$, assuming all energy contributes to vaporization.

**Assumptions:**
1. Material vaporizes at constant latent heat of vaporization $L_v$ (J/kg).
2. Hole is cylindrical with diameter $d$ (focal spot diameter).
3. No heat lost to surroundings (adiabatic worst-case).
4. Density of workpiece = $\rho$ (kg/mm³).
5. Single pulse energy = $E$ (J).

**Step 1 — Mass of material vaporized per pulse.**
The volume of a cylindrical hole of depth $h$ and diameter $d$:
$$V = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \times h$$

The corresponding mass:
$$m = \rho \times V = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance.**
All pulse energy is consumed in vaporization (latent heat only — no sensible heating, a worst-case upper bound on $h$):
$$E = m \cdot L_{v}$$

$$E = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot h \cdot L_{v}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for depth $h$:**
$$h = \frac{4E}{\pi d^{2} \rho L_{v}}$$

$$\boxed{h = \frac{4E}{\pi \rho L_{v} d^{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Practical adjustment.**
In real drilling, only a fraction $\eta$ (typically 0.3 – 0.6) of the pulse energy contributes to vaporization; the rest is lost to reflection, conduction sideways, and plasma shielding:
$$h_{\text{actual}} = \eta \cdot \frac{4E}{\pi \rho L_{v} d^{2}}$$

**Numerical insight:** For steel ($\rho = 7.85 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mm³, $L_v \approx 6.1 \times 10^{3}$ J/kg) with $E = 10$ J, $d = 0.5$ mm, $\eta = 0.4$:
$$h = 0.4 \times \frac{4 \times 10}{\pi \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 6100 \times 0.5^{2}}$$
$$h = 0.4 \times \frac{40}{37.6} \approx 0.42 \text{ mm per pulse}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Effect of Beam Diameter on Intensity

**Statement:** Show mathematically that intensity is inversely proportional to the *square* of spot diameter.

From $I = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$, holding $P$ constant:
$$\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}} = \left(\frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}\right)^{2}$$

Therefore, halving the spot diameter ($\tfrac{d_{2}}{d_{1}} = 0.5$) leads to a **4-fold increase** in intensity. This non-linear relationship justifies the use of very short-focal-length, high-numerical-aperture lenses in precision LBM.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Laser Beam Machining System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[PSU - Power Supply Unit] --> nodeB[Pumping Source - Flash Lamp or Diode]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Lasing Medium - CO2 gas, Nd YAG crystal or Yb fiber]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Optical Resonator Cavity - Two Mirrors]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Output Coupler Mirror - Partial Transmission]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Beam Delivery System - Mirrors or Fiber Optic Cable]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Focusing Lens - ZnSe or Fused Silica]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Focal Spot on Workpiece]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Material Phase Change - Melt plus Vapor]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Assist Gas Nozzle - O2 N2 or He]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Kerf or Drill Hole Formed]
    nodeK --> nodeL[CNC X-Y-Z Table with Workpiece]
    nodeL -.feedback.-> nodeM[Controller and Process Monitor]
    nodeM -.control signals.-> nodeA
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The PSU energizes the pumping source, which excites the lasing medium inside the resonator cavity. Photons bounce between the mirrors, and a coherent beam emerges through the output coupler. The beam is delivered through optics, focused to a micron-scale spot on the workpiece, where material is melted and vaporized. The assist gas ejects the molten debris. The CNC table positions the workpiece based on controller commands, which form a closed feedback loop with the PSU.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow Chart — Decision Logic for Laser Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS([Start - Material to be Cut]) --> nodeQ1{Is it Metallic?}
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeQ2{Thickness gt 6 mm?}
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeQ3{Reflective Metal like Cu or Al?}
    nodeQ3 -- YES --> nodeA1[Fiber Laser 1.07 um with High Power]
    nodeQ3 -- NO  --> nodeA2[CO2 Laser 10.6 um or Fiber Laser]
    nodeQ2 -- NO  --> nodeA3[Nd YAG Pulsed Laser for Fine Features]
    nodeQ1 -- NO  --> nodeQ4{Non-metal Organic like Wood Plastic?}
    nodeQ4 -- YES --> nodeA4[CO2 Laser is Ideal Choice]
    nodeQ4 -- NO  --> nodeQ5{Ceramic Glass or Semiconductor?}
    nodeQ5 -- YES --> nodeA5[Excimer UV Laser or Short Pulse Fiber]
    nodeA1 --> nodeZ([End - Select Optics Gas and CNC Program])
    nodeA2 --> nodeZ
    nodeA3 --> nodeZ
    nodeA4 --> nodeZ
    nodeA5 --> nodeZ
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Energy Distribution at the Focal Zone (Cross-section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph beam [Incoming Laser Beam Path]
        dir1[Top Surface of Workpiece]:::surface
        dir2[Beam Waist at Focal Plane]:::focal
        dir3[Bottom of Cut - Through Thickness]:::surface
    end

    subgraph zones [Microstructure of Cut Cross Section]
        z1[Kerf - Vaporized Material Removed]:::vapor
        z2[Recast Layer - Molten Resolidified]:::recast
        z3[Heat Affected Zone HAZ - Microstructure Altered]:::haz
        z4[Parent Metal - Unaffected Base Material]:::parent
    end

    z4 --- z3
    z3 --- z2
    z2 --- z1

    classDef surface fill:#ffe9b3,stroke:#a87b00,color:#000
    classDef focal fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000,color:#fff
    classDef vapor fill:#222,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef recast fill:#ffaa00,stroke:#7a4a00,color:#000
    classDef haz fill:#cc6600,stroke:#5a2d00,color:#fff
    classDef parent fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#444,color:#000
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** is the zone where the material's microstructure has been altered (grain growth, phase change) but the material was *not* melted. A smaller HAZ indicates higher quality, achievable by using *shorter pulse durations* and *higher traverse speeds*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions test *Remembering* and *Understanding* levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers must be crisp, typically **3–4 lines**, with a neat sketch where applicable.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define Laser Beam Machining. List any four of its important characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Laser Beam Machining is a non-contact thermal material removal process in which a focused, high-intensity laser beam is used to melt and vaporize material from the workpiece.

**Four important characteristics:**
1. **Non-contact** — No physical tool, hence no tool wear or vibration.
2. **High energy density** — Power density of **10⁶ to 10⁷ W/mm²** at the focal spot.
3. **Versatile** — Can machine metals, ceramics, polymers, composites, and diamond.
4. **Minimal HAZ** — Very small heat-affected zone due to short interaction time.
5. **High precision** — Achievable kerf widths of **0.05 to 0.5 mm** with positional accuracy ±10 µm.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Explain the three fundamental properties of a laser beam that make it suitable for machining.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three cardinal properties of laser light are:

1. **Coherence** — All emitted photons are in phase both spatially and temporally. This allows the beam to be focused to a diffraction-limited spot of micron size, producing enormous intensity.
2. **Monochromaticity** — The laser emits light of a single, narrow wavelength (e.g., 10.6 µm for CO₂, 1.06 µm for Nd:YAG). This ensures predictable absorption characteristics by the workpiece.
3. **Collimation (Directionality)** — The laser beam is highly parallel and does not diverge significantly, enabling long working distances and precise delivery through fiber optics or mirrors.

These properties together make the laser a uniquely controllable, intense, and focused energy source for material removal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **a (7 marks)** and **b (7 marks)**. Internal choice means students choose **either Q(A) or Q(B)** within the allotted module.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 14 Marks]**

**(a) With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a CO₂ laser-based Laser Beam Machining system. List its industrial applications.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A CO₂ laser uses a gaseous mixture of CO₂, N₂, and He as the active medium. The N₂ molecules are excited by an electrical discharge (DC or RF), and they transfer their energy to CO₂ molecules through resonant collisions, causing **population inversion**. The excited CO₂ molecules emit photons at **10.6 µm** wavelength via stimulated emission. The photons reflect back and forth between two mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially transmitting) inside the resonator cavity, amplifying the beam. The output coupler releases the coherent beam, which is then directed via reflective mirrors to a **ZnSe focusing lens**, producing a focal spot ~0.2 mm diameter on the workpiece.

A coaxial **assist gas nozzle** delivers high-pressure O₂ (for mild steel — exothermic reaction boosts energy) or N₂ (for stainless steel — inert to prevent oxidation). The assist gas also blows out the molten + vaporized debris from the kerf.

**Industrial Applications:**
1. Cutting of mild steel, stainless steel, and aluminium sheets (automotive, shipbuilding).
2. Engraving and marking of tools, jewellery, and electronics.
3. Drilling of micro-holes in turbine blades and nozzles.
4. Welding of tailored blanks in automotive body manufacturing.
5. Die-board cutting for the packaging industry.
6. Fabric and leather cutting in the textile industry.

---

**(b) A Nd:YAG laser delivers 2 kW power and is focused to a spot diameter of 0.5 mm on a steel plate. Calculate (i) Power Density at the focal spot, and (ii) the energy input per mm length if the cutting speed is 1.5 m/min.** *(7 marks)*

**Given Data:**
- $P = 2000$ W
- $d = 0.5$ mm
- $v = 1.5$ m/min = $\dfrac{1.5 \times 1000}{60} = 25$ mm/s

**Part (i) — Power Density:**
$$I = \frac{4P}{\pi d^{2}} = \frac{4 \times 2000}{\pi \times (0.5)^{2}}$$

$$I = \frac{8000}{\pi \times 0.25} = \frac{8000}{0.7854}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 10{,}186 \text{ W/mm}^{2} \approx 1.02 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/mm}^{2}}$$

**Part (ii) — Energy per Unit Length:**
$$E_{L} = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{2000 \text{ W}}{25 \text{ mm/s}} = 80 \text{ J/mm}$$

$$\boxed{E_{L} = 80 \text{ J/mm}}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #1**
> A common mistake is to forget unit conversion of cutting speed from **m/min to mm/s**. Many students write $\dfrac{2000}{1.5} = 1333$ J/mm — this is **wrong by a factor of 16.67**. Always convert $v$ to mm/s before substitution. Loss of marks: **2 marks**.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]**

**(a) Discuss the different types of industrial lasers used in manufacturing. Compare CO₂ and Nd:YAG lasers with respect to wavelength, power handling, and material compatibility.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Industrial Lasers:**
1. **CO₂ Laser** — Gas laser (CO₂ + N₂ + He); wavelength 10.6 µm; high power up to 50 kW; widely used for cutting non-metals and metals.
2. **Nd:YAG Laser** — Solid-state laser (Neodymium-doped Yttrium-Aluminium-Garnet); wavelength 1.06 µm; power up to 6 kW; excellent for metal cutting/drilling via fiber delivery.
3. **Fiber Laser** — Yb-doped fiber; wavelength ~1.07 µm; high electrical efficiency > 30%; preferred for sheet metal.
4. **Excimer Laser** — UV (193–351 nm); low average power; used for semiconductor lithography and micro-machining.
5. **Disk Laser** — Yb:YAG disk; wavelength 1.03 µm; high beam quality; used for thick-plate welding.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | CO₂ Laser | Nd:YAG Laser |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Wavelength | 10.6 µm (far IR) | 1.06 µm (near IR) |
| Average Power | Up to 50 kW | Up to 6 kW |
| Beam Delivery | Mirrors (ZnSe optics) | Fiber optic / mirrors |
| Best for | Non-metals, wood, acrylic, mild steel | Metals (high absorption) |
| Wall-plug efficiency | ~10–15% | ~3–5% |
| Pulse capability | Limited | Excellent (ms – ns range) |

---

**(b) A pulsed Nd:YAG laser delivers 50 J per pulse in 2 ms pulse duration with a focal spot of 0.8 mm diameter. Determine (i) Peak Power, (ii) Power Density, and (iii) Drilling depth per pulse in mild steel, assuming efficiency η = 0.4, ρ = 7.85 × 10⁻³ kg/mm³, L_v = 6.1 × 10³ J/kg.** *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $E = 50$ J
- $t_p = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $d = 0.8$ mm
- $\eta = 0.4$, $\rho = 7.85 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mm³, $L_v = 6.1 \times 10^{3}$ J/kg

**Part (i) — Peak Power:**
$$P = \frac{E}{t_p} = \frac{50}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 25{,}000 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P = 25 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (ii) — Power Density:**
$$I = \frac{4P}{\pi d^{2}} = \frac{4 \times 25000}{\pi \times (0.8)^{2}}$$

$$I = \frac{100000}{\pi \times 0.64} = \frac{100000}{2.0106}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 49{,}735 \text{ W/mm}^{2} \approx 4.97 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/mm}^{2}}$$

**Part (iii) — Drilling Depth per Pulse:**
$$h = \eta \cdot \frac{4E}{\pi \rho L_v d^{2}}$$

$$h = 0.4 \times \frac{4 \times 50}{\pi \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 6100 \times (0.8)^{2}}$$

Compute denominator step by step:
- $\rho L_v = 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 6100 = 47.885$ J/(kg · kg/mm³) — i.e., J·mm⁻³ · kg⁻¹ · kg = J/mm³ numerically ≈ 47.885 J/mm³ (numerically this represents energy per mm³).
- $d^2 = 0.64$
- $\pi \times 47.885 \times 0.64 = 3.1416 \times 30.646 = 96.28$

Numerator: $4 \times 50 = 200$; so $\dfrac{200}{96.28} = 2.078$ mm.

Apply efficiency: $h = 0.4 \times 2.078$

$$\boxed{h \approx 0.831 \text{ mm per pulse}}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #2**
> In drilling depth derivations, students often omit the efficiency factor $\eta$. KTU examiners award **1 mark specifically for stating the assumption of constant latent heat**, **1 mark for the energy balance equation**, and **1 mark for substituting $\eta$**. Skip these and you lose 3 marks despite getting the right numerical answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Revise Before Every Exam**

- **LASER** stands for *Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation*. The 3 properties are *Coherence, Monochromaticity, Collimation*.
- **LBM is a thermal, non-contact, advanced (non-traditional) process** — emphasize this in every definition question.
- **Power Density** $I = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$ is the single most important equation. It dictates whether melting or vaporization occurs.
- **Threshold for vaporization of steel** ≈ $10^{6}$ W/mm²; below this, only melting/sintering occurs.
- **CO₂ laser (10.6 µm)** is best for **non-metals & organic materials**; **Nd:YAG / Fiber (1.06 µm)** is best for **metals** due to higher absorptivity.
- **Energy per unit length** $E_L = P/v$ — *halving cutting speed doubles heat input per mm* and increases HAZ.
- **Specific Energy** for LBM of mild steel ≈ **4 – 10 J/mm³** — much lower than conventional machining, indicating high energy efficiency.
- **Drilling depth formula**: $h = \eta \cdot \dfrac{4E}{\pi \rho L_v d^{2}}$ — applicable for *single pulse drilling*.
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** is reduced by: shorter pulse duration, higher traverse speed, lower assist gas pressure (in inert mode), and use of shorter wavelength lasers.
- **Assist gases**: O₂ for mild steel (exothermic), N₂ for stainless steel and aluminium (inert, prevents oxidation), Ar for titanium.
- **Advantages**: No tool wear, no mechanical stress, cuts hardest materials (diamond, ceramics), micron precision.
- **Limitations**: High capital cost, low wall-plug efficiency (~10%), HAZ and dross formation, plasma shielding at high intensities, safety hazards (Class IV laser — eye and skin damage risk).
- **Numerical answer check**: Always ensure units are **Watts, mm, seconds, kg/mm³, J/kg** before computing — 70% of KTU mistakes are unit errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 Plasma Arc Machining (PAM) — Module 2 Comprehensive Notes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** This topic is a high-weightage sub-module under *Non-Traditional / Thermal Energy-Based Advanced Manufacturing Processes*. Expect direct 3-mark and 14-mark questions involving schematic explanation, parametric analysis, and comparison with allied processes (e.g., LBM, AJM, EDM).

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)** is a *thermal, non-contact, material-removal advanced manufacturing process* in which a **high-velocity, high-temperature ionized gas (plasma jet)** is directed at the workpiece to melt, vaporize, and mechanically displace the molten material through the kinetic energy of the jet itself. The plasma is generated by forcing a suitable working gas (or gas mixture) through a constricted electric arc struck between a tungsten electrode and either the workpiece (transferred-arc mode) or the constricting nozzle (non-transferred-arc mode).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"Plasma Arc Machining is a thermal material-removal process in which material is removed by the application of a high-temperature, high-velocity plasma jet, obtained by constricting an electric arc through a small-bore orifice."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Blowtorch"

Imagine you take an ordinary gas-cylinder torch (like a plumber uses for soldering copper pipes). Now imagine **supercharging that flame** so its temperature jumps from ~3,000 °C to a mind-boggling **16,000 °C – 30,000 °C**, and accelerate the gas to supersonic speed through a tiny hole. That is essentially plasma.

**Why so hot?**
- An ordinary flame burns fuel using oxygen (chemical energy).
- Plasma, by contrast, is created by **ionizing a gas** — knocking electrons off their atoms using a high-voltage electric arc. Free electrons + charged ions bouncing around violently = **energy density an order of magnitude greater than oxy-fuel flames**.

So, when this "thermal blowtorch" of superheated ionized gas hits a metal plate, even metals with **very high melting points (tungsten, stainless steel, Inconel, titanium)** simply liquefy and blow away in a stream of molten droplets.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Think of PAM as a *lightning bolt tamed through a copper nozzle*. Lightning is natural plasma (~30,000 K). PAM replicates a controlled, directed version of that phenomenon to slice through 100 mm thick shipbuilding steel like butter.

---

## 1.3 Historical & Industrial Significance

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1957** | First practical plasma torch patented (Robert Gage, USA) |
| **1960s** | Adapted from aerospace welding to metal cutting |
| **1980s** | Introduction of high-frequency (HF) start and CNC integration |
| **2000s+** | Hybrid **Plasma-Waterjet**, **HyPerformance plasma**, **Powder-injection plasma cutting** |

| Industrial Use | Why PAM? |
|----------------|----------|
| Shipbuilding | Cuts 100+ mm mild steel at high speed |
| Aerospace | Cuts titanium, Inconel, super-alloys |
| Auto & structural steel | Bevel cutting of plates |
| Heavy fabrication | Stainless-steel tank shells, pressure vessels |

This topic bridges **conventional oxy-fuel cutting** (which fails on stainless steel/aluminum) and **laser cutting** (which is cost-prohibitive for thick sections), making it a *sweet-spot* process in modern fabrication industries — exactly the kind of practical relevance KTU examiners reward.

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Plasma — Step-by-Step

To master PAM, the student must understand **what plasma actually is** at the atomic level:

1. **Atoms receive energy** from the high-voltage arc.
2. **Electrons are stripped away** from the outer shell of gas atoms → atoms become **ions**.
3. The resulting "soup" of free electrons + positive ions = **the 4th state of matter** (plasma).
4. Because opposite charges are present and rapidly moving, the gas becomes an **excellent electrical conductor**, allowing the arc to be sustained.
5. **Constricting** the arc through a small orifice forces the same energy into a smaller cross-section → temperature and velocity both rise dramatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Matter States (Energy Ascending):** Solid → Liquid → Gas → **Plasma**. PAM operates at the plasma phase, where temperatures can exceed **30,000 K**.

---

## 2.2 PAM System Architecture — The 5 Subsystems

A complete PAM setup comprises five integrated subsystems:

| # | Subsystem | Function | Key Components |
|---|-----------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Power Supply** | Provides DC arc current | DC rectifier, high-frequency (HF) starter, open-circuit voltage (200–400 V) |
| 2 | **Plasma Torch** | Generates & constricts the arc | Tungsten electrode, copper nozzle, swirl ring, water jacket |
| 3 | **Gas Console** | Supplies & meters plasma/shield gas | Argon, Nitrogen, H₂, Compressed Air, regulators |
| 4 | **Cooling System** | Removes heat from nozzle | Recirculating water chiller (≥ 4 L/min) |
| 5 | **Motion / CNC System** | Controls traverse speed & profile | X-Y table, gantry, CNC controller |

---

## 2.3 The Two Modes of Arc Generation — A Critical Distinction

### (a) Transferred-Arc Mode (Workpiece is the Anode)

- The **arc is struck between the tungsten electrode (cathode) and the workpiece (anode)**.
- The workpiece becomes part of the electrical circuit.
- **Higher energy density, deeper cuts** — used for **cutting thick metals**.
- The nozzle is *non-current-carrying* and merely *constricts* the arc.
- **KTU keyword:** *"Transfer of arc to workpiece."*

### (b) Non-Transferred-Arc Mode (Workpiece Not Part of Circuit)

- The **arc exists only between the tungsten electrode and the copper nozzle** (which is internally water-cooled).
- The plasma jet (still extremely hot, ~20,000 K) is *blown out* of the nozzle onto the workpiece.
- Used for **welding, surfacing, powder coating**, and cutting where the workpiece cannot be electrically connected.
- Safer, more portable, but **lower power density** than transferred mode.

### (c) Dual / Twin-Arc (Hybrid)
Two arcs coexist — one inside the torch, one between electrode and workpiece — boosting power for ultra-thick plate cutting.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *Transferred → "T" for "Through" (arc goes to workpiece). Non-transferred → stays "Inside" the torch.*

---

## 2.4 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Mechanism)

1. **Pilot arc** is initiated using high-frequency HF starting (~5,000–10,000 V, low current).
2. Working gas (e.g., Ar + H₂ + N₂ mixture) flows through the torch.
3. The gas passes around the cathode, becomes **ionized** when crossing the pilot arc → forms a **sustained plasma column**.
4. Arc transfers to workpiece in transferred mode.
5. Plasma jet exits the **constricting orifice** (typically Ø 0.8–3.5 mm) at **supersonic velocity** (Mach 2–5, i.e., ~700–1,700 m/s).
6. On contact, the metal **melts locally**, and the **momentum of the jet expels molten material** as droplets and vapor.
7. Shield gas (often CO₂ or air) flows *radially outward* around the plasma jet, protecting the cut kerf from oxidation.
8. CNC moves the torch → continuous kerf is formed.

---

## 2.5 Process Parameters (The "Knobs" of PAM)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Cut Quality |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Arc Current** | 30 – 800 A | Higher → thicker cut, more dross |
| **Arc Voltage** | 80 – 200 V | Higher → wider kerf, more heat |
| **Open-Circuit Voltage** | 200 – 400 V | Must exceed breakdown voltage for arc initiation |
| **Gas Flow Rate** | 15 – 80 L/min | Higher → cleaner cut, but lower penetration |
| **Gas Type** | Ar, N₂, H₂, Air, O₂ | Determines chemistry & edge quality |
| **Standoff Distance** | 3 – 15 mm | Critical — too small = nozzle damage; too large = bevel, dross |
| **Traverse Speed** | 0.5 – 6 m/min | Optimal window → dross-free cut |
| **Nozzle Diameter** | 0.8 – 3.5 mm | Smaller → narrower kerf, faster gas velocity |

---

## 2.6 🔢 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mathematically verified and routinely appear in KTU numerical questions.**

| # | Formula | Symbols | Typical Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Arc power = Voltage × Current | W (Watts) | Compute heat input |
| 2 | $Q = 0.24 \cdot I \cdot V \cdot t$ | Heat generated (Joules' Law) | J (Joules) | Total arc energy delivered |
| 3 | $\text{MRR} = v \cdot w \cdot d$ | Material Removal Rate | mm³/min | Compare processes |
| 4 | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | J/mm³ | Energy efficiency |
| 5 | $\text{Heat Efficiency } (\eta) = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{total}}}$ | Useful vs total heat | — | PAM: η ≈ 60 – 70 % |
| 6 | $v_{\text{jet}} \approx k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_g}}$ | Bernoulli-type jet velocity | m/s | Gas dynamics of plasma |
| 7 | $\text{Kerf width} \approx f(D, I)$ | Function of nozzle diameter & current | mm | Quality parameter |
| 8 | $\text{HAZ} \approx k \cdot \dfrac{I}{v}$ | Heat Affected Zone depth | mm | Distortion control |

## 2.8 Merits, Demerits & Applications (Board-Exam Ready Table)

| Merits | Demerits | Applications |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| Cuts all electrically **conductive** metals | Cannot cut non-conductive materials (wood, plastic, ceramics) | Cutting stainless-steel sheets |
| Very high **MRR** | Generates intense noise (≥ 120 dB) & UV radiation | Cutting aluminum & copper |
| Cuts thick plates (up to **150 mm**) | **Hazardous fumes** require ventilation | Bevel cutting for weld prep |
| Lower cost than laser for thick cuts | **Kerf taper** at higher thicknesses | Heavy structural fabrication |
| Tolerates rusty/painted surfaces | Nozzle wear → consumable cost | Ship hull plate cutting |
| Can be operated **underwater** | Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) → minor thermal distortion | Aerospace alloy profiling |

| Feature | **PAM** | **Laser Beam Machining** | **Oxy-Fuel Cutting** | **Abrasive Jet** |
|---------|---------|---------------------------|----------------------|-------------------|

| Max temperature | 16,000 – 30,000 °C | ~6,000 °C | ~3,500 °C | Ambient |
| Conductive only? | Yes | No (can cut anything) | Only mild steel | No |

| Edge quality | Good (HAZ present) | Excellent | Rough | Excellent |
| Capital cost | Medium | Very high | Low | Medium |
