# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No shortcuts. Every algebraic and physical step is written explicitly so the student can reproduce the answer in the KTU answer script.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Plasma Arc Cutting

For a **straight-line plasma cut**, the volume of material removed per unit time equals:

$$\text{Volume removed per minute} = \text{Cut length per minute} \times \text{Kerf width} \times \text{Plate thickness}$$

Let us define each variable formally:

| Symbol | Meaning | SI / Practical Unit |
|--------|---------|----------------------|
| $v$ | Traverse speed of torch | mm/min |
| $w$ | Kerf width (slot width) | mm |
| $d$ | Plate thickness | mm |

Therefore, the instantaneous **MRR** is:

$$\text{MRR} = v \cdot w \cdot d \quad \left[ \frac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{min}} \right]$$

**Practical Numerical Example:**

*Given:* $v = 1200 \text{ mm/min}, \; w = 3.5 \text{ mm}, \; d = 20 \text{ mm}$

Step 1 — Multiply traverse speed by kerf width:

$$v \cdot w = 1200 \times 3.5 = 4200 \text{ mm}^2/\text{min}$$

Step 2 — Multiply result by plate thickness:

$$\text{MRR} = 4200 \times 20 = 84{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

Step 3 — Convert to more intuitive cm³/min:

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{84{,}000}{1000} = 84 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = 84{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \;\; (\approx 84 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min})}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

SEC is the **energy required to remove one cubic millimetre of material**, a key metric used by KTU to compare thermal processes.

**Step 1 — Arc Power:**

$$P = V \times I \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

**Step 2 — Useful Heat Delivered:**

$$Q_{\text{useful}} = \eta \times V \times I \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

where $\eta$ is the heat-utilization efficiency. For PAM, typical $\eta \approx 0.60$ to $0.70$.

**Step 3 — MRR (already derived above):**

$$\text{MRR} = v \cdot w \cdot d$$

**Step 4 — SEC Definition:**

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{\text{MRR}}$$

Therefore, combining all:

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot w \cdot d} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \right]$$

**Numerical Example:**

*Given:* $V = 160 \text{ V}, \; I = 400 \text{ A}, \; \eta = 0.65, \; v = 1500 \text{ mm/min}, \; w = 4 \text{ mm}, \; d = 15 \text{ mm}$

Step 1 — Compute arc power:

$$P = 160 \times 400 = 64{,}000 \text{ W}$$

Step 2 — Useful heat:

$$Q_{\text{useful}} = 0.65 \times 64{,}000 = 41{,}600 \text{ W}$$

Step 3 — MRR:

$$\text{MRR} = 1500 \times 4 \times 15 = 90{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

Step 4 — Convert MRR to mm³/s:

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{60} = 1500 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

Step 5 — SEC:

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{41{,}600}{1500} \approx 27.73 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\text{SEC} \approx 27.7 \text{ J/mm}^3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison Context:** LBM ≈ 5 J/mm³, EDM ≈ 50–100 J/mm³, PAM ≈ 25–35 J/mm³. Hence PAM is *much more energy-efficient than EDM* but *less so than laser*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) Estimation

HAZ is the region adjacent to the cut where the microstructure has been altered by heat but **no melting has occurred**.

Empirically, HAZ depth scales inversely with traverse speed and directly with current:

$$\text{HAZ depth} \;\approx\; k \cdot \dfrac{I}{v}$$

where $k$ is a process-dependent constant ($\sim 0.1$ – $0.5$ for steel).

**Example:** If $k = 0.3, \; I = 350 \text{ A}, \; v = 2000 \text{ mm/min}$

$$\text{HAZ} = 0.3 \times \dfrac{350}{2000} = 0.0525 \text{ mm} \approx 52 \; \mu\text{m}$$

For thicker plates / lower speeds, HAZ grows rapidly → controls distortion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter-Effect Table (Board-Exam Favourite)

| Parameter ↑ (Increase) | MRR | Kerf Width | HAZ | Edge Quality (Dross) |
|-------------------------|-----|------------|-----|----------------------|
| Arc current ($I$) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Widens | ↑ Increases | ↑ More dross |
| Arc voltage ($V$) | Slight ↑ | ↑ Widens | ↑ Increases | ↑ Bevel risk |
| Traverse speed ($v$) | ↑ Direct | ↓ Narrows | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Risk of dross at very high speed |
| Gas flow rate | ↑ till optimum, ↓ beyond | ↑ Widens | Slight ↑ | ↑ Cleaner (then turbulent) |
| Standoff distance | ↓ Initially improves | ↑ Bevels | ↑ Increases | ↓ Dross if too large |
| Nozzle diameter | ↑ Widely | ↑ Widens | ↑ Slightly | ↓ Less precision |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When the question asks *"How will increasing traverse speed affect cut quality?"* the ideal answer is: *MRR increases (good), kerf narrows (good), but if speed is excessive, dross forms because insufficient heat is delivered to fully eject molten metal.*

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU-Style 7-Mark Question)

**Problem:**
A 12 mm thick mild-steel plate is cut using PAM. The arc voltage is 140 V, current is 250 A, and the heat efficiency is 60 %. The traverse speed is 1.5 m/min and the kerf width measured is 3 mm. Calculate:
(a) Arc power
(b) Heat input rate (useful)
(c) Material removal rate
(d) Specific Energy Consumption

**Solution:**

**(a) Arc Power:**

$$P = V \times I = 140 \times 250 = 35{,}000 \text{ W} = 35 \text{ kW}$$

**(b) Useful Heat Input Rate:**

$$Q_{\text{useful}} = \eta \times P = 0.60 \times 35{,}000 = 21{,}000 \text{ W} = 21 \text{ kW}$$

**(c) Material Removal Rate:**

Convert traverse speed: $v = 1.5 \text{ m/min} = 1500 \text{ mm/min}$

$$\text{MRR} = v \cdot w \cdot d = 1500 \times 3 \times 12 = 54{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

Convert to mm³/s: $\dfrac{54{,}000}{60} = 900 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$

**(d) Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{21{,}000 \text{ J/s}}{900 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}} \approx 23.33 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{P = 35 \text{ kW}, \; Q_{\text{useful}} = 21 \text{ kW}, \; \text{MRR} = 54{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}, \; \text{SEC} \approx 23.3 \text{ J/mm}^3}$$

---

## 3.6 Trade-Off Triangle — *Quality vs Speed vs Cost*

```
                  ┌──────────────┐
                  │   QUALITY    │
                  │  (Low Dross) │
                  └──────┬───────┘
                         │
        High speed ──────┼────── Slow speed
        (Poor quality)   │      (Best quality,
                         │       but uneconomic)
                  ┌──────┴───────┐
                  │   COST /     │
                  │   MRR        │
                  └──────────────┘
```

> [!TIP]
> **Optimal Operating Window:** KTU expects students to recognise that **PAM optimisation is a balancing act** — increasing any one variable (current, speed, gas flow) usually degrades another (edge quality, kerf, HAZ). The role of the process engineer is to operate at the *Pareto-optimal* point.

# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Q: Define Plasma Arc Machining. Mention any two advantages of PAM.**

**Model Answer:**

Plasma Arc Machining (PAM) is a thermal material-removal process in which a high-velocity, high-temperature ionized gas (plasma jet) is used to melt and remove metal from the workpiece. The plasma is generated by constricting an electric arc through a small-bore nozzle, achieving temperatures up to **30,000 °C**.

**Two advantages:**
1. Can cut any electrically conductive metal (including stainless steel, aluminum, copper, titanium) which oxy-fuel cutting cannot.
2. Very high Material Removal Rate (MRR) — capable of cutting plates over **100 mm thick** at industrial speeds.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: Differentiate between transferred-arc and non-transferred-arc plasma processes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Transferred-Arc | Non-Transferred-Arc |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|
| Arc path | Electrode → Workpiece | Electrode → Nozzle (inside torch) |
| Workpiece role | Anode (part of circuit) | Not part of circuit |
| Energy density | Higher | Lower |
| Application | Cutting thick metal plates | Welding, surfacing, powder coating |
| Safety | Lower (workpiece electrically live) | Higher |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**, each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student is given an **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. **Both alternatives are provided below.**

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — *Theory + Schematic Emphasis* `[KTU ESE — May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of Plasma Arc Machining with a neat labelled schematic of the plasma torch. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** PAM removes metal by directing a high-velocity, high-temperature plasma jet onto the workpiece. Plasma is generated by ionizing a working gas (Ar / N₂ / H₂ mixture) through a constricted electric arc struck between a tungsten cathode and either the workpiece (transferred mode) or the water-cooled copper nozzle (non-transferred mode).

**Working Steps:**
1. The DC power supply is switched on, establishing open-circuit voltage (200–400 V) across the electrode and nozzle.
2. A high-frequency (HF) starter ionizes a small gap → pilot arc forms.
3. Working gas is injected tangentially through the swirl ring, becoming ionized.
4. Arc transfers to the workpiece in transferred mode (or remains inside the torch in non-transferred mode).
5. Plasma jet exits through the constricting orifice at supersonic velocity (Mach 2–5).
6. The jet's thermal energy melts the metal locally; its kinetic energy ejects molten droplets through the kerf.
7. CNC moves the torch along the programmed path → continuous kerf is produced.

**Labelled Schematic (as drawn in SECTION 4.4 above):**
- Tungsten electrode (cathode)
- Swirl ring (gas inlet)
- Constricting orifice (copper nozzle)
- Water jacket (cooling)
- Plasma jet exit
- Shield gas outlet

**Closing statement:** PAM is essentially a *tamed lightning bolt* used as a cutting torch, capable of slicing any electrically conductive metal.

---

**(b)** List the major process parameters of PAM and explain how they affect cut quality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Major parameters and their effects:**

1. **Arc Current (30–800 A):** Higher current → thicker plate penetration and higher MRR, but also wider kerf, more dross and larger HAZ.
2. **Arc Voltage (80–200 V):** Higher voltage → wider kerf and greater bevel risk on cut edge.
3. **Gas Flow Rate (15–80 L/min):** Adequate flow → cleaner cut; excessive flow → turbulent plasma, unstable arc; insufficient flow → dross formation.
4. **Traverse Speed (0.5–6 m/min):** Optimum speed → dross-free vertical cut. Too slow → excessive HAZ and gouging. Too fast → incomplete cut and dross.
5. **Standoff Distance (3–15 mm):** Too close → nozzle double-arcing and damage. Too far → divergent jet, bevel edge, dross.
6. **Nozzle Diameter (0.8–3.5 mm):** Smaller orifice → narrower kerf, higher jet velocity, better precision.
7. **Gas Composition:** Ar–H₂ gives clean stainless-steel cuts; Air/O₂ for mild steel; N₂ for non-ferrous metals.

**Engineering trade-off:** Optimisation requires balancing speed (productivity) against edge quality (dross, bevel) and consumable life.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — *Numerical / Analytical Emphasis* `[KTU ESE — Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** A stainless-steel plate of 18 mm thickness is being cut by PAM at a traverse speed of 1.2 m/min. The arc voltage is 150 V and arc current is 300 A. Assuming heat efficiency of 65 %, calculate:
   (i) Arc power
   (ii) Useful heat input rate
   (iii) Material removal rate (take kerf width = 3.5 mm) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Arc Power:**

$$P = V \times I = 150 \times 300 = 45{,}000 \text{ W} = 45 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Useful Heat Input:**

$$Q_{\text{useful}} = \eta \times P = 0.65 \times 45{,}000 = 29{,}250 \text{ W} \approx 29.25 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

Convert traverse speed: $v = 1.2 \text{ m/min} = 1200 \text{ mm/min}$

$$\text{MRR} = v \times w \times d = 1200 \times 3.5 \times 18$$

Step 1: $1200 \times 3.5 = 4200$

Step 2: $4200 \times 18 = 75{,}600 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$

Convert to per-second basis:

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{75{,}600}{60} = 1260 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{P = 45 \text{ kW}, \;\; Q_{\text{useful}} = 29.25 \text{ kW}, \;\; \text{MRR} = 75{,}600 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 1260 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}}$$

---

**(b)** Compute the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) for the above process. Also, comment on whether PAM is more energy-efficient than EDM for which SEC = 60 J/mm³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{\text{MRR}} = \dfrac{29{,}250 \text{ J/s}}{1260 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}}$$

$$\text{SEC} \approx 23.21 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Comparison:**

Since SEC of PAM (≈ 23.2 J/mm³) is **much lower** than that of EDM (60 J/mm³), PAM is **significantly more energy-efficient** than EDM on a per-unit-volume-removed basis.

**Additional insight (for full marks):**
- However, EDM produces **much finer surface finish and tolerance** than PAM.
- So the comparison should be qualified: PAM is more energy-efficient, but EDM wins on precision.

$$\boxed{\text{SEC}_{\text{PAM}} \approx 23.2 \text{ J/mm}^3 < \text{SEC}_{\text{EDM}} = 60 \text{ J/mm}^3 \Rightarrow \text{PAM is more energy-efficient.}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in PAM questions:**
> 1. **Confusing transferred arc with non-transferred arc** — examiners specifically test whether you know *where the arc terminates*. Memorise the definition.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm³/min → mm³/s** when computing SEC. This unit-inconsistency is the **#1 reason students lose 2–3 marks** on numericals.
> 3. **Writing "PAM cuts all metals"** — WRONG. PAM cuts **electrically conductive** metals only. This is a classic trap; saying "PAM cuts everything" is a guaranteed 0.5 mark deduction.
> 4. **Drawing the torch free-hand** without labels — examiners award marks **only for labelled diagrams**. Even a block diagram with 5 labelled components scores full marks.
> 5. **Mixing up P and Q** — remember: $P = V \times I$ is *power*, $Q = 0.24 \cdot I \cdot V \cdot t$ is *heat energy* (uses 0.24 as Joules' constant).
> 6. **Ignoring HAZ in answers about quality** — every PAM discussion on cut quality must mention **HAZ (Heat Affected Zone)** as a quality-limiting factor.
> 7. **Stating wrong temperature range** — PAM operates at **16,000 °C – 30,000 °C**, NOT 3,000 °C. The latter is oxy-fuel flame.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Revise in 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Full form:** Plasma Arc Machining (PAM).
- ✅ **Type:** Thermal, non-traditional, advanced manufacturing process.
- ✅ **Temperature:** **16,000 °C – 30,000 °C** (one of the hottest industrial processes after LBM).
- ✅ **State of matter used:** Plasma — the **4th state of matter** (ionized gas).
- ✅ **Working gases:** Argon, Nitrogen, Hydrogen mixture (Ar-H₂-N₂); Air/O₂ for mild steel.
- ✅ **Two arc modes:**
  - **Transferred** → Electrode ↔ Workpiece (cutting).
  - **Non-transferred** → Electrode ↔ Nozzle (welding, surfacing).
- ✅ **Five PAM subsystems:** Power supply, Torch, Gas console, Cooling, CNC motion.
- ✅ **Key components in torch:** Tungsten electrode, swirl ring, constricting orifice, water jacket.
- ✅ **Jet velocity:** Mach 2 – Mach 5 (supersonic).
- ✅ **Standoff distance:** 3–15 mm (critical quality parameter).
- ✅ **MRR Formula:** $\text{MRR} = v \cdot w \cdot d$
- ✅ **SEC Formula:** $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot w \cdot d}$
- ✅ **Heat efficiency of PAM:** 60 – 70 %.
- ✅ **Conductive metals ONLY** — cannot cut wood, plastic, glass, ceramic.
- ✅ **Max thickness:** ~150 mm (industrial high-end systems).
- ✅ **Comparison anchors:**
  - vs **LBM:** PAM cheaper for thick cuts, lower precision.
  - vs **Oxy-fuel:** PAM works on SS/Al, oxy-fuel works only on mild steel.
  - vs **EDM:** PAM higher MRR, EDM higher precision.
- ✅ **HAZ present** → minor thermal distortion in cut edges.
- ✅ **Advantages:** High MRR, thick plate capability, all conductive metals, lower cost than laser.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Noisy (≥ 120 dB), UV radiation, hazardous fumes, nozzle wear.
- ✅ **Applications:** Shipbuilding, aerospace, structural fabrication, stainless-steel tanks, bevel cutting for weld prep.
- ✅ **Special variant:** Underwater plasma cutting for nuclear/ship-repair work.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Beam Machining (IBM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ion Beam Machining (IBM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ion Beam Machining (IBM)** is a non-traditional, thermal-free (athermal) material removal process belonging to the family of **Mechanical Energy-Based Advanced Machining Processes (MEAMP)**. In IBM, high-velocity ions of an inert gas (typically Argon) are accelerated through a potential difference of **0.5 kV to 5 kV** (commonly up to **50 kV** for micro-fabrication) and directed as a focused beam onto the workpiece inside a high-vacuum chamber. The kinetic energy of the accelerated ions is transferred to the surface atoms of the workpiece through a momentum-exchange process called **sputtering**, causing atomic-scale ejection of the workpiece material without any thermal melting, chemical reaction, or mechanical cutting force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** IBM is classified under the category of **ion beam / electron beam / laser beam processes** that use *directed kinetic energy* to remove material. The hallmark of IBM is that material removal occurs **atom-by-atom**, making it the most precise of all conventional and non-conventional machining processes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Atomic Billiards" Intuition

Imagine the world's smallest game of billiards. Picture a giant field of perfectly arranged glass marbles (representing the atoms of a metal workpiece). Now, imagine a tiny, ultra-fast steel ball (an Argon ion) being shot at this field. The marble struck by the steel ball is knocked out of its place, possibly knocking out another marble, and so on. The glass marbles that are ejected are removed material, and the indent left behind is the machined feature.

In real IBM:
- The **Argon ions** are the "steel balls" accelerated to speeds of tens to hundreds of kilometers per second.
- The **workpiece atoms** are the "glass marbles" being knocked out via momentum transfer.
- The process happens inside a **vacuum chamber** so that the ions do not collide with air molecules and lose energy before hitting the target.

This is fundamentally different from laser or electron beam machining because there is **no heat, no melting, and no vaporization** — material is literally *kicked out* of the surface by mechanical impact at the atomic scale.

## 1.3 Why IBM Exists — Engineering Necessity

Conventional machining struggles with:
1. **Hard and brittle materials** (ceramics, quartz, germanium, silicon, super-alloys)
2. **Sub-micron feature sizes** (micro-electronics, MEMS)
3. **Heat-sensitive materials** (biological samples, polymers with low melting points)
4. **Stress-free surfaces** (no HAZ, no recast layer, no micro-cracks)

IBM solves all four because:
- Sputtering depends only on the **momentum transfer cross-section**, not on thermal softening.
- Removal is **atomic-scale**, so feature resolution is governed by beam focus diameter (down to **< 10 nm** in Focused Ion Beam / FIB systems).
- Energy is carried by **kinetic mass particles**, not photons, so there is **no thermal damage zone**.
- Forces are microscopic, so there is **no mechanical stress, vibration, or chatter**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Process Metrics (KTU Reference Values):**
> - **Vacuum pressure:** 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr (≈ 1.33 × 10⁻² to 1.33 × 10⁻⁴ Pa)
> - **Beam voltage:** 0.5 kV – 50 kV (typical industrial: 0.5 – 5 kV)
> - **Beam current density:** 0.1 – 10 mA/cm²
> - **Material Removal Rate (MRR):** typically **0.01 – 0.1 mm³/min** (low — a major disadvantage)
> - **Sputtering Yield (S):** 0.1 – 20 atoms/ion (depending on ion energy, mass ratio, and incidence angle)
> - **Tolerance achievable:** ± 0.025 µm
> - **Surface roughness (Ra):** 0.01 – 0.1 µm

## 1.4 Historical and Industrial Context

IBM was first investigated in the **1960s** as a semiconductor patterning technique. It is the direct ancestor of the modern **Focused Ion Beam (FIB)** systems used in semiconductor failure analysis, TEM sample preparation, and ion lithography. The most commonly used ion is **Argon (Ar⁺)** because it is inert (does not chemically react with the workpiece) and has a favorable mass for momentum transfer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Working Principle

The IBM process is built on five sequential physical stages:

### Stage 1 — Ion Generation (Ion Source)
- A neutral inert gas (typically **Argon**) is introduced into a low-pressure chamber.
- Gas atoms are ionized by either:
  - **DC discharge** (cold-cathode or hot-cathode)
  - **RF discharge** (electrodeless, used in Kaufman-type sources)
  - **Electron bombardment** (duoplasmatron source)
- Result: a plasma containing Ar⁺ ions and free electrons.

### Stage 2 — Ion Extraction
- A pair of **extraction electrodes** (anode-cathode pair) draws the positively charged Ar⁺ ions out of the plasma region.
- The ions are pulled through a small aperture into a high-vacuum region.

### Stage 3 — Ion Acceleration
- The extracted ions pass through a high potential difference **V (in Volts)** between the extraction electrode and the accelerating electrode.
- Each ion of charge **q = +e (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C)** gains kinetic energy:

$$E_k = qV = eV$$

- This translates to a velocity of:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_i}}$$

where **m_i** is the mass of the ion (e.g., for Ar⁺, m_i = 39.948 amu = 6.64 × 10⁻²⁶ kg).

### Stage 4 — Beam Focusing and Neutralization
- **Electrostatic or electromagnetic lenses** focus the diverging ion beam into a tight spot (diameter from a few mm down to sub-micron in FIB).
- An **electron emitter (neutralizer filament)** injects electrons into the beam to maintain charge neutrality — otherwise the positive ion beam would repel itself (space-charge blow-up) and charge the workpiece surface, deflecting subsequent ions.

### Stage 5 — Sputtering at the Workpiece
- The high-velocity ions strike the workpiece surface inside the vacuum work chamber.
- Through elastic and inelastic nuclear collisions (described by the **Linear Collision Cascade theory** of Sigmund, 1969), surface atoms are ejected.
- Material is removed atom-by-atom — no heat, no melting, no chemical alteration.

## 2.2 Why Sputtering Occurs — The Physics of Energy Transfer

When a fast ion strikes a target atom, the maximum fraction of kinetic energy transferable to a surface atom (head-on elastic collision) is governed by the **Kinematic Factor (K)**:

$$K = \frac{4 M_i M_t}{(M_i + M_t)^2}$$

where:
- **M_i** = mass of the incident ion (kg or amu)
- **M_t** = mass of the target (workpiece) atom (kg or amu)

The **sputtering yield S** (atoms ejected per incident ion) depends on K and the binding energy of the target surface. Maximum momentum transfer (and thus maximum S) occurs when **M_i ≈ M_t** — this is why **Argon (Ar, mass ≈ 40 amu)** is preferred for many metals and semiconductors whose atomic masses fall in the 20–120 amu range.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU university exams. Memorize every formula, symbol, and unit.**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description / Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_k = qV$ | Kinetic energy gained by an ion of charge *q* accelerated through potential *V* | Joules (J) or eV |
| 2 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m_i}}$ | Final velocity of an ion after acceleration | m/s |
| 3 | $K = \dfrac{4 M_i M_t}{(M_i + M_t)^2}$ | Kinematic energy-transfer factor in elastic ion-atom collision | dimensionless (0 ≤ K ≤ 1) |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{N_{removed}}{N_{incident}}$ | Sputtering yield (atoms removed per incident ion) | atoms/ion (dimensionless) |
| 5 | $S \propto \dfrac{S_n(E)}{\, U_s\,}$ | Sputtering yield is proportional to nuclear stopping power, inversely proportional to surface binding energy | — |
| 6 | $MRR = \dfrac{I \cdot S \cdot M}{e \cdot \rho \cdot N_A}$ | Material Removal Rate based on beam current *I*, yield *S*, atomic mass *M*, density *ρ* | mm³/min or mg/min |
| 7 | $MRR = \dfrac{V_{acc} \cdot I \cdot S \cdot M}{\rho \cdot q \cdot N_A}$ | Alternate MRR form using acceleration voltage | mm³/min |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{q \cdot N_A} \cdot S$ | Total atoms removed over time *t* at current *I* | atoms or moles |
| 9 | $R_{roughness} \propto f(d_{beam}, \text{overlap})$ | Final surface roughness depends on beam spot diameter and scan overlap | µm |
| 10 | $\text{Resolution} \approx d_{beam} \approx \dfrac{\text{constant}}{\sqrt{I_{lens}}}$ | Smallest machinable feature ≈ focused beam diameter | µm or nm |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

IBM / FIB systems are essential in:
- **Semiconductor industry:** mask repair, circuit editing, failure analysis, TEM sample lift-out.
- **MEMS and Nanotechnology:** fabrication of nano-structures, nano-holes, atomic-scale tips.
- **Optics manufacturing:** precision figuring of optical components (X-ray mirrors, laser optics) at sub-nm accuracy.
- **Photonics:** writing waveguides and gratings in glass and lithium niobate.
- **Materials science research:** cross-sectional imaging, atom probe tomography sample prep.
- **Aerospace:** drilling micro-cooling holes in turbine blades where thermal methods cause recast.

## 2.5 Advantages and Limitations (Frequently Asked in KTU)

### Advantages
1. **No thermal damage** — no Heat Affected Zone (HAZ), no recast, no micro-cracks.
2. **No chemical alteration** — pure mechanical/atomic material removal.
3. **Sub-micron accuracy** — achievable feature size < 0.1 µm.
4. **Material independent** — works on hardest ceramics, glass, super-alloys, semiconductors.
5. **No tool wear** — the "tool" is a beam of ions, which does not physically wear out.
6. **No residual mechanical stress** — no cutting forces, no vibration.
7. **Excellent surface finish** — Ra 0.01 – 0.1 µm.

### Limitations
1. **Very low MRR** — typically 10³ to 10⁴ times slower than conventional machining.
2. **High capital cost** — vacuum system + ion source + electronics is expensive.
3. **Restricted to small workpieces** — vacuum chamber size limits part dimensions.
4. **Implantation contamination** — incident ions may get embedded (1–10 nm depth), a concern in semiconductor work.
5. **Beam divergence and charge buildup** — requires neutralization on insulating workpieces.
6. **High vacuum requirement** — adds operational complexity and cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Velocity of an Accelerated Ion

We start from energy conservation between the extraction electrode (where the ion is at rest, potential = 0) and the accelerating electrode (potential = V).

**Step 1 — Energy gained by an ion of charge q across potential difference V:**

$$W = qV$$

**Step 2 — Equate work done to kinetic energy gained (Work–Energy Theorem):**

$$\frac{1}{2} m_i v^2 = qV$$

**Step 3 — Solve for velocity v:**

$$v^2 = \frac{2 q V}{m_i}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 q V}{m_i}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute q = +e for a singly charged ion:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m_i}}$$

**Numerical sanity check for Argon ion, V = 1000 V:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times 1000}{6.64 \times 10^{-26}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-16}}{6.64 \times 10^{-26}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{4.82 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$v \approx 6.94 \times 10^{4} \text{ m/s} \approx 69.4 \text{ km/s}$$

This is far below the speed of light, so the non-relativistic treatment is valid for typical IBM voltages (< 50 kV).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Energy Transfer Factor (Kinematic Factor K)

Consider an elastic head-on collision between a moving ion (mass M_i, velocity v_i) and a stationary target atom (mass M_t).

**Step 1 — Conservation of linear momentum:**

$$M_i v_i = M_i v_i' + M_t v_t'$$

**Step 2 — Conservation of kinetic energy:**

$$\frac{1}{2} M_i v_i^2 = \frac{1}{2} M_i v_i'^{2} + \frac{1}{2} M_t v_t'^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system. The fraction of initial kinetic energy transferred to the target atom (E_t / E_i) equals K:**

$$K = \frac{E_t}{E_i} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} M_t v_t'^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} M_i v_i^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the conservation-law solutions, the classical result is:**

$$K = \frac{4 M_i M_t}{(M_i + M_t)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Boundary analysis:**

- If M_i ≪ M_t (light ion on heavy atom): K → 0, ion bounces back, little energy transferred.
- If M_i ≫ M_t (heavy ion on light atom): K → 0, ion ploughs through, light atom cannot absorb much.
- If M_i = M_t (equal masses): K = 1, *complete* energy transfer — ideal sputtering condition.

This is why Ar⁺ (40 amu) is favored for Fe (56 amu), Si (28 amu), Cu (64 amu), W (184 amu) — all are within a factor of ~5 in mass.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) in IBM

The MRR depends on the number of workpiece atoms removed per second. Each incident ion removes an average of *S* atoms.

**Step 1 — Number of ions striking the workpiece per second:**

$$\dot{N}_{ions} = \frac{I}{q} = \frac{I}{e}$$

where I is the beam current in Amperes.

**Step 2 — Number of workpiece atoms removed per second:**

$$\dot{N}_{atoms} = S \cdot \frac{I}{e}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of material removed per second:**

$$\dot{m} = \dot{N}_{atoms} \cdot M_a = S \cdot \frac{I}{e} \cdot M_a$$

where **M_a** is the atomic mass of the workpiece (kg/atom), also expressible as **M = (A_r × 10⁻³) / N_A** kg, with A_r the relative atomic mass.

**Step 4 — Divide by the workpiece density to get volume removed per second:**

$$MRR = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{S \cdot I \cdot M_a}{e \cdot \rho}$$

Expressed in mm³/min (multiply by 60 × 10⁹ for kg/m³ → mm³/min conversion):

$$MRR = \frac{60 \times 10^9 \cdot S \cdot I \cdot M_a}{e \cdot \rho} \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-mark derivation):**

> **Problem:** An IBM system uses Argon ions (A_r = 40) accelerated at 2000 V to machine a copper workpiece (A_r = 64, density ρ = 8960 kg/m³, atomic mass M_a = 64 × 10⁻³ / 6.022 × 10²⁶ = 1.063 × 10⁻²⁵ kg). The beam current is 5 mA and the sputtering yield S = 4 atoms/ion. Calculate (a) the velocity of Ar⁺ ions, (b) the kinematic energy-transfer factor K, and (c) the MRR.

**Solution:**

**(a) Velocity:**

$$m_i = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{6.022 \times 10^{26}} = 6.643 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2000}{6.643 \times 10^{-26}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{6.4 \times 10^{-16}}{6.643 \times 10^{-26}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{9.635 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$v \approx 9.82 \times 10^{4} \text{ m/s} = 98.2 \text{ km/s}$$

**(b) Kinematic factor:**

$$K = \frac{4 \times 40 \times 64}{(40 + 64)^2} = \frac{10240}{10816}$$

$$K = 0.947 \text{ (or 94.7\% energy transfer)}$$

This is excellent — Ar⁺ and Cu are close in mass.

**(c) MRR:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{S \cdot I \cdot M_a}{e} = \frac{4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.063 \times 10^{-25}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{2.126 \times 10^{-27}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.329 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$MRR = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{1.329 \times 10^{-8}}{8960} = 1.483 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

$$MRR = 1.483 \times 10^{-12} \times 60 \times 10^{9} = 0.0889 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

This very low MRR (~ 0.09 mm³/min) is *typical* of IBM and is a key reason IBM is used only for high-precision, low-volume work.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters and Their Effects (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Finish | Effect on Heat / Damage |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Beam voltage (V) | ↑ (S increases up to a peak ~ 50–100 keV, then decreases) | Slight degradation at high V due to deeper ion penetration | Negligible (athermal process) |
| ↑ Beam current (I) | ↑ (linear in I) | Slight degradation due to higher thermal load on neutralizer | Minimal |
| ↓ Beam spot size (focused) | ↓ MRR locally but ↑ precision | ↑ Finish (smaller spot = smoother cut) | No effect |
| Higher Ar mass match (M_i ≈ M_t) | ↑ (S increases with K) | Slight effect | No effect |
| ↑ Incidence angle (oblique) | ↑ S up to ~ 60–70°, then ↓ | Variable | No effect |
| ↑ Workpiece density (ρ) | ↓ MRR (more atoms per unit volume) | No effect | No effect |
| ↑ Vacuum level (lower pressure) | ↑ (less beam scatter) | ↑ (less contamination) | No effect |
| ↑ Time of exposure | ↑ linear removal depth | Deeper groove, possible redeposition | Minimal |

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: IBM vs EBM vs LBM (Common KTU Question)

| Feature | IBM (Ion Beam) | EBM (Electron Beam) | LBM (Laser Beam) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy carrier | Ions (mass particles) | Electrons (mass particles) | Photons (massless) |
| Mechanism | Momentum transfer (sputtering) | Kinetic + thermal vaporization | Thermal melting/vaporization |
| Vacuum required | Yes (mandatory) | Yes (mandatory) | No (often atmospheric) |
| MRR | Very low | Medium–high | High |
| Precision | Highest (sub-µm / nm) | High (few µm) | Moderate (10s of µm) |
| Thermal damage | None (athermal) | Possible (HAZ exists) | Significant (HAZ, recast) |
| Best for | Micro/nano-fabrication, mask repair | Welding, drilling, cutting | Cutting, drilling, engraving |
| Tool wear | None | Filament erosion | None |
| Workpiece | Any solid (insulator needs neutralizer) | Conductive (charge issues on insulators) | Almost any |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IBM System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete functional flow of an industrial Ion Beam Machining system, from gas input to material ejection at the workpiece. This is a **block-level functional architecture** (the KTU-preferred diagram type for processes that require vacuum chambers and complex physics).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GAS1[GAS SUPPLY]
        ArCyl[Argon Gas Cylinder]
        Reg[Pressure Regulator]
        FMass[Mass Flow Controller]
    end

    subgraph VAC1[VACUUM SYSTEM]
        VPump[Vacuum Pump Bank]
        VGauge[Vacuum Gauge]
        VLevel[Vacuum Level 10 to 4 torr]
    end

    subgraph ISRC1[ION SOURCE STAGE]
        Chamber1[Ion Source Chamber]
        Discharge[Plasma Discharge DC or RF]
        Ions[Ar Positive Ions]
    end

    subgraph ACC1[ACCELERATION STAGE]
        Extr[Extraction Electrode]
        Accel[Accelerating Electrode V 0 5 to 5 kV]
    end

    subgraph FOC1[FOCUSING STAGE]
        Lens1[Electrostatic Lens 1]
        Lens2[Electrostatic Lens 2]
        Neut[Neutralizer Filament Electrons]
    end

    subgraph WORK1[WORK CHAMBER]
        StageXY[X Y Z Workpiece Stage]
        WkPiece[Workpiece Cu or Si or Ceramic]
        Sputter[Sputtering Site Atoms Ejected]
        Redep[Redeposition Trap]
        Exh[Exhaust to Vacuum]
    end

    subgraph CTRL1[CONTROL AND POWER]
        HVPS[High Voltage Power Supply]
        LPS[Lens Power Supplies]
        CNC[CNC Motion Controller]
    end

    ArCyl --> Reg --> FMass --> Chamber1
    Discharge --> Chamber1
    Chamber1 --> Ions
    Ions --> Extr
    HVPS --> Accel
    Extr --> Accel
    Accel --> Lens1
    Lens1 --> Lens2
    LPS --> Lens1
    LPS --> Lens2
    Lens2 --> Neut
    Neut --> StageXY
    StageXY --> WkPiece
    WkPiece --> Sputter
    Sputter --> Redep
    Redep --> Exh
    VPump --> Exh
    VGauge --> VLevel
    CNC --> StageXY
    CNC --> Lens1
    CNC --> Lens2
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Material Removal Sequence

This second diagram explains the **physical mechanism** of sputtering at the atomic scale.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Argon Gas Atom Enters Source Chamber] --> B[High Voltage Discharge Ionizes Ar to Ar Plus]
    B --> C[Ar Plus Ion Extracted Through Aperture]
    C --> D[Accelerated by Potential V to Velocity v]
    D --> E[Focused by Electrostatic Lenses into Beam]
    E --> F[Neutralized by Electron Shower]
    F --> G[High Velocity Ar Plus Strikes Workpiece Surface]
    G --> H[Collision Cascade Inside Workpiece]
    H --> I[Surface Atom Receives Sufficient Energy Surfaces Binding Energy]
    I --> J[Ejected Atom Leaves Surface Sputtering]
    J --> K[Captured by Vacuum Pump or Redeposited on Trap]
    K --> L[Material Removal Achieved]
    L --> M[Workpiece Stage Repositions for Next Pass]
    M --> G
```

## 4.3 Reading the Diagrams (For the KTU Examiner Perspective)

When asked in an exam to "draw or describe the IBM setup," the examiner expects the following key functional blocks to appear:

1. **Gas supply and flow control**
2. **Vacuum system and chamber** (always mandatory)
3. **Ion source** with mention of plasma generation (DC/RF/duoplasmatron)
4. **Extraction-acceleration electrode pair** with a clearly stated voltage (0.5–5 kV typical)
5. **Focusing lenses** (electrostatic preferred for IBM; magnetic for EBM)
6. **Neutralizer** (critical for IBM to prevent charge buildup)
7. **Workpiece stage** (multi-axis, often CNC-controlled for pattern generation)
8. **Sputtered material collection / vacuum exhaust**

A diagram missing the **neutralizer** or the **vacuum system** will cost marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper Pattern]
**Define Ion Beam Machining. Mention any two distinguishing features that set it apart from Laser Beam Machining.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Ion Beam Machining (IBM)** is a non-traditional, vacuum-based material removal process in which high-velocity ions of an inert gas (usually Argon) are accelerated to kinetic energies of **0.5 to 5 keV** and directed at the workpiece; material is removed **atom-by-atom** through a momentum-exchange process called **sputtering**.

Two distinguishing features versus LBM:
1. IBM is a **purely mechanical (athermal) process** — material is sputtered by momentum transfer with **no melting, no HAZ, no recast layer**, whereas LBM is a **thermal process** that melts and vaporizes the material.
2. IBM operates inside a **high-vacuum chamber (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr)** and uses **massive ions** as energy carriers, giving **sub-micron precision** but very low MRR. LBM operates in air, uses massless photons, and has high MRR but coarser precision.

*(Award full 3 marks for clear definition + 2 distinct features.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary Pattern]
**State the significance of the neutralizer filament in an IBM system. Why is Argon the most commonly used gas?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **neutralizer filament** emits low-energy electrons into the ion beam to maintain **electrical charge neutrality**. Without it, the positively charged ion beam would (a) self-repel due to space-charge effects, causing the beam to diverge, and (b) charge up the workpiece surface (especially on insulating materials like ceramics and glass), which would electrostatically deflect subsequent ions and disrupt the process.

**Argon** is preferred because:
1. It is **chemically inert** — does not react with or contaminate the workpiece.
2. Its atomic mass (**40 amu**) is well-matched to most engineering metals and semiconductors (Fe 56, Cu 64, Si 28, Ti 48, W 184) — maximizing the kinematic energy-transfer factor K.
3. It is **abundant, cheap, and easily ionized** in a discharge.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two sub-parts per question — typically (a) 7 marks descriptive + (b) 7 marks numerical/derivation. Internal choice between Q-A and Q-B.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**

#### Q-A(a) [7 Marks]
**With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of an Ion Beam Machining (IBM) system. List its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
IBM is a non-traditional, thermal-free (athermal) material removal process in which accelerated ions of an inert gas (typically Argon) are directed at the workpiece in a vacuum chamber. Material removal occurs by **sputtering** — kinetic energy transfer from incident ions to workpiece atoms, ejecting them from the surface.

**Working Principle (3 marks) — refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1:**

1. **Gas supply and vacuum:** Argon gas is fed through a regulator and mass flow controller into the source chamber. The work chamber is maintained at a vacuum of **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr** using a diffusion pump + rotary pump combination.

2. **Ion generation:** A high-voltage **DC discharge** (Kaufman source) or **RF discharge** ionizes the Argon gas, creating a plasma of Ar⁺ ions and free electrons.

3. **Ion extraction:** A pair of electrodes extracts the Ar⁺ ions through a small aperture into the high-vacuum acceleration region.

4. **Acceleration:** A potential difference of **0.5 – 5 kV** is applied across the extraction-accelerating electrode pair. Each Ar⁺ ion gains kinetic energy **E_k = eV**, reaching velocities of **30 – 100 km/s**.

5. **Focusing:** Electrostatic (or sometimes electromagnetic) lenses focus the diverging beam to a spot of diameter 0.1 – 10 mm (industrial) or < 0.1 µm (FIB).

6. **Neutralization:** A hot tungsten filament injects electrons into the beam, making it electrically neutral.

7. **Sputtering:** The focused neutralized beam strikes the workpiece. Sputtering removes workpiece atoms. The CNC-controlled X-Y stage moves the workpiece to generate the desired pattern.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- No thermal damage (no HAZ, no recast)
- Sub-micron precision
- Material independent (works on ceramics, glass, super-alloys)
- No tool wear, no mechanical stress
- Excellent surface finish (Ra 0.01 – 0.1 µm)

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Very low MRR (0.01 – 0.1 mm³/min)
- High capital cost (vacuum + electronics)
- Limited to small workpieces
- Ion implantation contamination
- Requires neutralization on insulators

---

#### Q-A(b) [7 Marks]
**In an IBM setup, Argon ions (A_r = 40) are accelerated through 1500 V to machine a silicon workpiece (A_r = 28, density 2330 kg/m³). The beam current is 8 mA and the sputtering yield is 3.5 atoms/ion. Calculate:**
**(i) Velocity of Ar⁺ ions.**
**(ii) Kinematic energy-transfer factor K.**
**(iii) Material removal rate in mm³/min.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given data:**
- M_i (Ar) = 40 amu
- M_t (Si) = 28 amu
- V = 1500 V
- I = 8 mA = 8 × 10⁻³ A
- S = 3.5 atoms/ion
- ρ_Si = 2330 kg/m³
- e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- N_A = 6.022 × 10²⁶ /kg-mol (for atomic mass in kg, use M = A_r / N_A grams; here use 6.022 × 10²³ /mol and convert carefully)

**(i) Velocity of Ar⁺ ions [2 marks]:**

Mass of one Ar atom:

$$m_i = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 6.643 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m_i}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1500}{6.643 \times 10^{-26}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{4.8 \times 10^{-16}}{6.643 \times 10^{-26}}} = \sqrt{7.226 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$v = 8.50 \times 10^{4} \text{ m/s} = 85.0 \text{ km/s}$$

**(ii) Kinematic factor K [2 marks]:**

$$K = \frac{4 M_i M_t}{(M_i + M_t)^2} = \frac{4 \times 40 \times 28}{(40 + 28)^2} = \frac{4480}{4624}$$

$$K = 0.969 \text{ (96.9\% energy transfer — very efficient)}$$

**(iii) MRR [3 marks]:**

Mass of one Si atom:

$$M_a = \frac{28 \times 10^{-3}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 4.65 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$$

Mass removal rate:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{S \cdot I \cdot M_a}{e} = \frac{3.5 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.65 \times 10^{-26}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{1.302 \times 10^{-27}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 8.14 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg/s}$$

Volume removal rate:

$$MRR = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{8.14 \times 10^{-9}}{2330} = 3.49 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

Convert to mm³/min:

$$MRR = 3.49 \times 10^{-12} \times 60 \times 10^{9} = 0.210 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Final Answer:** v ≈ 85.0 km/s; K ≈ 0.969; MRR ≈ 0.21 mm³/min.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**

#### Q-B(a) [7 Marks]
**Explain the phenomenon of sputtering in Ion Beam Machining. Discuss the factors that affect the sputtering yield and how they influence the choice of process parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sputtering phenomenon (3 marks):**
Sputtering is the ejection of surface atoms of a solid target when bombarded by energetic ions (typically 0.5 – 5 keV Ar⁺). When a high-velocity ion strikes the surface, it undergoes a series of elastic and inelastic nuclear collisions with the lattice atoms (the **collision cascade**, described by Sigmund's Linear Collision Cascade theory, 1969). Through this cascade, momentum is redistributed within the solid. If a surface atom receives kinetic energy greater than the **surface binding energy U_s** (typically a few eV) and is directed outward, it escapes the surface — this is the sputtered atom. The process is **athermal**: the bulk of the energy is carried away by the sputtered atom and the back-scattered ion, leaving the target cold.

**Factors affecting sputtering yield S (4 marks):**

1. **Ion energy (V):** S rises from a threshold (~ 20–50 eV) to a peak at **50 – 100 keV**, then decreases as ions penetrate too deeply to transfer momentum back to the surface. KTU-typical operating range is **0.5 – 5 keV** (on the rising part of the S-vs-E curve).

2. **Ion-to-target mass ratio (M_i / M_t):** Maximum S occurs when M_i ≈ M_t (K → 1). Too light an ion bounces back; too heavy an ion embeds itself.

3. **Angle of incidence:** S increases as the incidence angle moves away from normal, reaching maximum at **60–70°**, then drops sharply near 90° (grazing) because the ion reflects off without depositing energy.

4. **Target binding energy U_s:** S is inversely proportional to U_s — materials with weaker surface binding sputter more easily.

5. **Ion species:** Inert gases (Ar, Kr, Xe) give clean sputtering without chemical reaction. Reactive ions (O₂⁺, CF₃⁺) cause **reactive ion etching**, which is faster but chemically alters the surface.

6. **Crystal orientation:** Sputtering yield is mildly anisotropic — close-packed directions sputter more slowly because the collision cascade can channel deeper.

**Influence on process parameter choice:**
- Choose **Ar** for general-purpose work, **Xe** for heavy targets (Au, W), **O₂** for reactive etching of polymers.
- Operate at **1 – 2 keV** for a balance between S and minimal ion implantation depth.
- Use **oblique incidence (30–60°)** to maximize MRR when geometry permits.

---

#### Q-B(b) [7 Marks]
**Compare Ion Beam Machining (IBM), Electron Beam Machining (EBM), and Laser Beam Machining (LBM) on the basis of: energy carrier, mechanism of material removal, MRR, surface finish, and typical applications. State two industrial applications of IBM.**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison table (5 marks) — refer to the Analytical Matrix in Section 3.5:**

| Basis | IBM | EBM | LBM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy carrier | Ar⁺ ions (massive) | Electrons (light) | Photons (massless) |
| Removal mechanism | Momentum transfer / sputtering | Kinetic + thermal vaporization | Thermal melting/vaporization |
| Vacuum required | Yes | Yes | No (usually atmospheric) |
| MRR | Very low (0.01 – 0.1 mm³/min) | Medium (1 – 10 mm³/min) | High (10 – 1000 mm³/min) |
| Surface finish | Excellent (Ra 0.01 – 0.1 µm) | Good (Ra 0.1 – 1 µm) | Moderate (Ra 1 – 10 µm) |
| Thermal HAZ | None (athermal) | Small HAZ | Significant HAZ + recast |
| Precision | Sub-µm to nm | Few µm | Tens of µm |

**Two industrial applications of IBM (2 marks):**

1. **Semiconductor failure analysis and mask repair** — Focused Ion Beam (FIB) systems are used to cut, image, and deposit material on integrated circuits at the nanometer scale without damaging adjacent features.

2. **Micro-hole drilling in aerospace turbine blades** — IBM drills micro-cooling holes (Ø 50–500 µm) in nickel super-alloy blades used in jet engines. The athermal nature prevents recast and micro-cracks that would reduce fatigue life.

*(Other acceptable applications: TEM sample preparation, X-ray optics figuring, photonics waveguide writing, MEMS/NEMS fabrication.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "sputtering" with "evaporation":** Sputtering is *mechanical* (momentum transfer) — never thermal. Any answer claiming IBM "melts" or "vaporizes" the material will lose 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the neutralizer:** The neutralizer filament is *the* distinguishing hardware of IBM (vs EBM, which does not need one because electrons are themselves the charge carriers). Omitting it costs 1–2 marks in descriptive answers.
> 3. **Unit errors in MRR:** Always show the conversion from m³/s → mm³/min by multiplying by **60 × 10⁹**. Students frequently leave the answer in m³/s, which is technically correct but loses 1 mark in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Wrong ion mass in v calculation:** Use the *atomic* mass of the ion species in kg, not the molar mass in grams. Converting: m_i = (A_r × 10⁻³) / (6.022 × 10²³) kg.
> 5. **Kinematic factor interpretation:** K is the *fraction* of energy transferred (0 to 1), not the velocity ratio. Conflating the two is a common mistake.
> 6. **Saying "no heat generated":** Strictly, IBM still produces a small amount of localized heat at the impact point (phonon energy). It is more accurate to say "no thermal *damage*" or "athermal in the engineering sense." Examiners may deduct for the absolute statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU revision — read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **IBM = Ion Beam Machining = Sputtering process = Athermal, non-chemical, vacuum-based material removal.**

- **Working principle:** Ar gas → ionized in source → extracted → accelerated (0.5–5 kV) → focused → neutralized → strikes workpiece → sputtering removes atoms.

- **Process characteristics:**
  - Vacuum: 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr
  - Beam voltage: 0.5 – 5 kV (industrial) / up to 50 kV (FIB)
  - MRR: 0.01 – 0.1 mm³/min (very low)
  - Surface finish: Ra 0.01 – 0.1 µm
  - Tolerance: ± 0.025 µm
  - No HAZ, no recast, no mechanical stress

- **Three MUST-MEMORIZE formulas:**
  1. $v = \sqrt{2eV / m_i}$ (ion velocity)
  2. $K = 4M_i M_t / (M_i + M_t)^2$ (kinematic factor)
  3. $MRR = S \cdot I \cdot M_a / (e \cdot \rho)$ (material removal rate)

- **Why Argon?** Inert + optimal mass match with most metals and semiconductors (maximizes K).

- **Why neutralizer?** Prevents beam divergence and workpiece charging (critical for insulators).

- **Advantages:** Precision, no thermal damage, no tool wear, material independent, excellent finish.

- **Limitations:** Low MRR, high cost, vacuum-restricted size, ion implantation.

- **Applications:** Mask repair, semiconductor failure analysis, TEM sample prep, MEMS, micro-hole drilling in turbine blades, photonics, X-ray optics.

- **Comparison short-cuts (memorize these 3 lines):**
  - IBM = *momentum* (athermal, vacuum, very precise, very slow)
  - EBM = *kinetic + thermal* (vacuum, conductive workpieces, medium MRR)
  - LBM = *thermal* (no vacuum, almost any material, high MRR, has HAZ)

- **Examiner hot-spots:** Kinematic factor derivation; MRR numerical; block diagram of IBM system; sputtering mechanism explanation; comparison table.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Velocity Forming of Metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** High Velocity Forming of Metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Velocity Forming (HVF)** is a class of non-traditional metal forming processes in which the workpiece is deformed at **extremely high strain rates**, typically in the range of **10² to 10⁴ s⁻¹**, using a sudden release of energy (chemical, electrical, magnetic, or pneumatic-mechanical) to drive the deformation. The forming event is completed in **microseconds to a few milliseconds**, in stark contrast to conventional forming which operates at strain rates of **10⁻³ to 10¹ s⁻¹**.

The energy source releases its stored energy almost instantaneously, generating a **high-amplitude pressure pulse** (often several hundred MPa to a few GPa) that accelerates the workpiece (or a driver medium such as water) at velocities of **50 to 1000 m/s**. The process exploits the **strain-rate sensitivity** of metals — most notably their **increased ductility and formability** under dynamic loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3**
> The four industrially-relevant HVF processes that a KTU student must know are:
> 1. **Explosive Forming (EF)**
> 2. **Electrohydraulic Forming (EHF)**
> 3. **Electromagnetic Forming (EMF)**
> 4. **Pneumatic–Mechanical Forming (e.g., Dynapak)**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Thunder-Clap Sheet Metal"

Imagine a calm lake (the *workpiece*). If you drop a small pebble into it (conventional forming), gentle ripples spread slowly and displace the water predictably. Now imagine detonating a firecracker **underwater** at the same spot (HVF). A violent shock wave races outward at the **speed of sound in water (~1480 m/s)**, slamming the sheet metal into a die with such force that it conforms to the die cavity in a fraction of a millisecond.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the metal behave better under HVF?**
> Under ultra-fast loading, dislocations in the metal lattice do **not have time to pile up** or to nucleate voids at inclusions. Instead, **inertial forces dominate** and the metal flows almost hydrodynamically. Result: less springback, higher limiting draw ratio, ability to form normally-brittle materials, and excellent dimensional accuracy on large parts.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Markers (to memorize)

| Metric | Conventional Forming | High Velocity Forming |
|---|---|---|
| Strain rate, $\dot{\varepsilon}$ | **10⁻³ – 10¹ s⁻¹** | **10² – 10⁴ s⁻¹** |
| Forming time | 0.1 – 10 s | **1 µs – 10 ms** |
| Peak pressure | 1 – 50 MPa | **100 – 5000 MPa** |
| Velocity of tool/punch | 0.01 – 1 m/s | **50 – 1000 m/s** |
| Springback | High | **Negligible** |
| Ductility of workpiece | Limited by strain-hardening | **Enhanced 2×–10×** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of HVF

### 2.1.1 The Physics Behind Strain-Rate Sensitivity

Most metals exhibit the **Constitutive (Flow) Equation**:

$$\sigma_f = K \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,m} \cdot \varepsilon^{\,n}$$

where:
- $\sigma_f$ = instantaneous flow stress (MPa)
- $K$ = strength coefficient (MPa)
- $\dot{\varepsilon}$ = strain rate (s⁻¹)
- $m$ = **strain-rate sensitivity exponent** (typically **0.01–0.20**)
- $\varepsilon$ = strain
- $n$ = strain-hardening exponent (typically **0.05–0.50**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $\dot{\varepsilon}$ increases by **3 to 4 orders of magnitude** in HVF, the flow stress $\sigma_f$ rises by 20–60 % (depending on $m$). The metal **stiffens**, but — paradoxically — its **uniform elongation to necking also increases** because higher $m$ postpones the onset of localized necking (Hart's criterion). Net effect: **more formable, not less.**

### 2.1.2 The Pressure-Pulse Generation Mechanism

Every HVF variant follows a common three-step energy chain:

1. **Energy Storage** — chemical (explosive), electrical (capacitor bank), pneumatic (compressed gas), or magnetic (current in coil).
2. **Rapid Conversion** — energy converted to a **kinetic shock front** in microseconds.
3. **Workpiece Acceleration** — shock front impacts the blank, accelerating it at **10⁴–10⁶ g** into the die.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board favourite equations** for Module 3 numerical problems. Memorize the **boxed** ones first.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Key Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v}{L}$ | Strain rate from velocity and gauge length | $v$ = velocity (m/s), $L$ = characteristic length (m) |
| 2 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Electrical energy in a capacitor (EHF, EMF) | $C$ = capacitance (F), $V$ = charging voltage (V) |
| 3 | $E = m \cdot Q$ | Chemical energy in an explosive (EF) | $m$ = mass of charge (kg), $Q$ = heat of detonation (J/kg) |
| 4 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{K \,\rho_w \,C_w^{2}}{8} \left(\dfrac{D}{r}\right)^{3}$ | **Birkofer peak-shock pressure** (underwater EF) | $\rho_w$ = water density (1000 kg/m³), $C_w$ = sound speed in water (1480 m/s), $D$ = charge diameter (m), $r$ = stand-off distance (m), $K \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ |
| 5 | $v_{blank} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,P\,x}{\rho_{b}\,t}}$ | Final blank velocity after travelling distance $x$ in fluid | $P$ = pressure (Pa), $\rho_b$ = blank density, $t$ = blank thickness |
| 6 | $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\, I^{2} \,\dfrac{dL}{dx}$ | **Magnetic force on a current-carrying coil** (EMF) | $I$ = current (A), $L$ = inductance (H), $x$ = coil–workpiece gap (m) |
| 7 | $P_{mag} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} = \dfrac{\mu_{0}\,H^{2}}{2}$ | **Magnetic pressure** (EMF) | $B$ = flux density (T), $H$ = field intensity (A/m), $\mu_0 = 4\pi\times10^{-7}$ H/m |
| 8 | $W = \displaystyle\int P\,dV$ | Plastic work done on the blank | $P$ = pressure, $V$ = swept volume |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{plastic}}{E_{input}} \times 100\%$ | **Energy-transfer efficiency** of the HVF process (typically 5–25 %) | — |
| 10 | $t_{c} = \dfrac{2 r}{C_w}$ | Contact / dwell time of shock on the die | $r$ = stand-off distance |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing $|x|$ or absolute value inside a derivation, **never** use the literal `|` character inside a markdown table cell — always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX form to avoid breaking the table.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of HVF

- **Aerospace** — large monolithic panels, rocket nose-cones, missile skins (one-shot forming up to 6 m × 2 m).
- **Shipbuilding & Nuclear** — heavy plate bulging, tube-expansion for heat-exchangers.
- **Automotive** — joining dissimilar metals (Al–steel) by electromagnetic pulse-welding.
- **Tube industries** — electromagnetic **compression** (crimping) and **expansion** of aluminium and copper tubes without internal mandrels.
- **Defence & R&D** — forming of normally-brittle tungsten, beryllium, and super-alloys.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are written out **exhaustively** — no skipped steps, no abbreviated algebra — so that a first-time KTU student can reproduce the entire solution in the answer script and secure full marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak Shock Pressure from an Underwater Explosive Charge (Birkofer Equation)

### Statement
A spherical high-explosive charge of diameter $D$ detonates at the centre of a water-filled tank at a stand-off distance $r$ from the workpiece. Derive the **peak shock-wave pressure** $P_{max}$ arriving at the blank.

### Step 1 — Energy Released by the Detonation
The chemical energy released when a mass $m$ of explosive detonates is

$$
E = m \cdot Q
$$

where $Q$ is the **heat of detonation** in J/kg (typical TNT: $Q \approx 4.2 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg).

### Step 2 — Energy Required to Generate the Shock Wave
The Rankine–Hugoniot shock relations give the specific energy density carried by a strong shock in water as

$$
e_{shock} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, P_{max} \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_0} - \dfrac{1}{\rho}\right)
$$

For a strong underwater shock, the compressed density is roughly $\rho \approx \tfrac{4}{3}\rho_w$, so

$$
\dfrac{1}{\rho_0} - \dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_w} - \dfrac{3}{4\rho_w} = \dfrac{1}{4\rho_w}
$$

Therefore

$$
e_{shock} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{8 \rho_w}
$$

### Step 3 — Geometric Spreading
The shock front sweeps outward as a sphere of radius $r$. The energy per unit area on the spherical wave front is

$$
\dfrac{E}{4\pi r^{2}} = e_{shock}
$$

Substituting $E = m Q$ and $e_{shock} = P_{max}/(8\rho_w)$:

$$
\dfrac{m Q}{4\pi r^{2}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{8 \rho_w}
$$

Solve for $P_{max}$:

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{2 \rho_w \, m Q}{\pi r^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Replace Mass with Charge Diameter
The mass of a spherical charge of density $\rho_{e}$ and diameter $D$ is

$$
m = \rho_{e} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{6}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{2 \rho_w \cdot \rho_{e} \cdot \pi D^{3} \cdot Q}{6 \, \pi \, r^{2}} = \dfrac{\rho_w \, \rho_{e} \, Q \, D^{3}}{3 \, r^{2}}
$$

### Step 5 — Introduce the Detonation-Velocity Term
The detonation velocity $C_D$ of the explosive is related to $Q$ through

$$
Q \approx \dfrac{C_D^{2}}{2 \left(\gamma_{e}^{2} - 1\right)}
$$

where $\gamma_{e}$ is the explosive's specific-heat ratio ($\approx 3$ for most high explosives). Substituting and grouping all explosive-dependent constants into a single coefficient $K$:

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{K \, \rho_w \, C_w^{2}}{8} \left(\dfrac{D}{r}\right)^{3}
$$

> This is the **Birkofer peak-shock pressure equation** (KTU-board favourite).
> $K \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ depending on explosive family (TNT, RDX, PETN, etc.).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Magnetic Pressure in an Electromagnetic Coil

### Step 1 — Energy Stored in an Inductor
The energy stored in the magnetic field of a coil carrying current $I$ is

$$
E_{mag} = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Work Done as Coil-Workpiece Gap Changes
If the workpiece moves a small distance $dx$ towards the coil, the inductance $L$ changes by $dL$, and the work done **on the workpiece** is

$$
dW = F \cdot dx = \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} \, dL
$$

Therefore the **instantaneous magnetic force** is

$$
F = \tfrac{1}{2}\, I^{2} \,\dfrac{dL}{dx}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert Force to Pressure
Dividing the force by the effective workpiece area $A$:

$$
P_{mag} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{I^{2}}{2A}\,\dfrac{dL}{dx}
$$

### Step 4 — Relate to Magnetic Field
Using the constitutive relation $B = \mu_0 H$ and the energy density of a magnetic field $u = B^{2}/(2\mu_0)$, the magnetic pressure is equivalently

$$
P_{mag} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_0}
$$

For $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, a field of $B = 10$ T generates a pressure of about **40 MPa**, sufficient to deform thin aluminium sheet.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Final Blank Velocity After a Pressure Pulse

### Step 1 — Equation of Motion
Treating the blank of mass $m_b$ as a rigid piston pushed by fluid at pressure $P(t)$:

$$
m_b \,\dfrac{dv}{dt} = P(t)\,A
$$

### Step 2 — Integrate over the Contact Time
Assuming $P$ is approximately constant over the very short forming time $\Delta t$:

$$
v_{final} = \dfrac{P\,A\,\Delta t}{m_b} = \dfrac{P\,\Delta t}{\rho_b \, t}
$$

where $t$ is the blank thickness and $\rho_b$ is the blank density. The contact time can be approximated by the dwell time of the shock on the die:

$$
\Delta t \approx \dfrac{2 r}{C_w}
$$

Substituting:

$$
v_{final} \approx \dfrac{2 P r}{\rho_b \, t \, C_w}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above expression is the **starting point for the KTU numerical** on "energy transferred to the blank" and "efficiency of explosive forming."

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HVF Processes

| Parameter | Explosive | Electrohydraulic | Electromagnetic | Pneumatic (Dynapak) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Chemical detonation | Capacitor discharge in water | Capacitor discharge in coil | Compressed gas (N₂/He) |
| Energy storage | $m Q$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | $P_0 V_0 \ln(V_1/V_0)$ |
| Driver medium | Water (or air) | Water | Coil (no medium) | Gas (He/N₂) |
| Peak pressure | 1 – 5 GPa | 0.1 – 1 GPa | 0.05 – 0.2 GPa | 0.05 – 0.3 GPa |
| Strain rate, s⁻¹ | 10³ – 10⁴ | 10³ | 10² – 10³ | 10² – 10³ |
| Repeatability | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good |
| Noise & safety | High (blasting licence) | Moderate | Low (silent) | Low |
| Typical part size | Very large (m²) | Medium | Small–medium | Medium |
| Tooling cost | Very low (one-shot die) | Moderate | Moderate (coil design) | High |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Marks are split: **Part A = 2 × 3 = 6 marks**, **Part B = 1 × 14 = 14 marks** (Module-internal choice provided).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 3 Marks
**Define High Velocity Forming (HVF). List any four processes that fall under this category and state the typical strain-rate range.**

**Model Answer:**

> High Velocity Forming (HVF) is a non-conventional metal forming process in which the workpiece is plastically deformed at **strain rates typically in the range 10² to 10⁴ s⁻¹**, with the forming event completed in **microseconds to a few milliseconds**. The high strain rate is generated by a sudden release of energy from a chemical, electrical, magnetic, or pneumatic source, producing a high-amplitude pressure pulse (100 MPa – 5 GPa) that accelerates the blank at 50–1000 m/s.
>
> **Four processes under HVF:**
> 1. Explosive Forming (EF)
> 2. Electrohydraulic Forming (EHF)
> 3. Electromagnetic Forming (EMF)
> 4. Pneumatic–Mechanical Forming (e.g., Dynapak)
>
> **Strain-rate range:** $\dot{\varepsilon} = 10^{2}$–$10^{4}$ s⁻¹.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of the Electrohydraulic Forming (EHF) process.**

**Model Answer:**

> In **Electrohydraulic Forming (EHF)**, a bank of capacitors is charged to a high DC voltage (typically 5–30 kV) and then discharged across a pair of electrodes immersed in a water-filled tank. The sudden electrical discharge vaporises a small quantity of water, creating a **high-pressure plasma vapour bubble** that expands radially as a **shock wave** in the surrounding water. The shock wave strikes the blank, which is clamped over a die, and forces it to conform to the die cavity in a few milliseconds. The process is essentially a **controllable, indoor version of underwater explosive forming** with the advantage of repeatable discharges.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3] — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Explosive Forming** process. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and typical industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** A spherical TNT charge of diameter **80 mm** is detonated at the centre of a water tank. The stand-off distance from the charge to the workpiece is **r = 600 mm**. Using the Birkofer equation, calculate the **peak shock pressure** arriving at the blank. Assume $K = 2.0$, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_w = 1480$ m/s. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Working Principle of Explosive Forming:**

A high explosive (TNT, RDX, PETN, or a plastic explosive) is positioned at a calculated stand-off distance from a sheet-metal blank that is clamped over a die. The die is placed inside a water tank (which acts as a **pressure-transmitting medium**). On detonation, the chemical energy of the explosive is released in **microseconds**, producing a **spherical shock wave** in the water. The shock wave strikes the blank, accelerating it at 10⁴–10⁶ g into the die cavity, where it plastically deforms and takes the shape of the die.

**Advantages:**
- Very low tooling cost (single-shot dies can be made of concrete, wood, or epoxy).
- Capable of forming **very large parts** (panels up to 6 m × 2 m) in one shot.
- Higher formability and lower springback than conventional forming.
- Suitable for normally-brittle materials (titanium, beryllium, magnesium).
- No heavy presses required.

**Limitations:**
- Low **repeatability** (charge mass and stand-off must be precisely controlled).
- **Safety hazard** — requires a licensed blasting area and trained personnel.
- Loud, destructive operation — not suitable for shop-floor environment.
- Low **energy-transfer efficiency** (5–15 %).
- Surface finish depends on water-tank cleanliness.

**Industrial Applications:**
- Aerospace: aircraft skin panels, missile nose-cones.
- Shipbuilding: hull plates, deck panels.
- Nuclear: large pressure-vessel end-caps.
- Forming of super-alloy and refractory-metal sheets.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given Data:**
- $D = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m
- $r = 600$ mm $= 0.600$ m
- $K = 2.0$
- $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³
- $C_w = 1480$ m/s

**Formula (Birkofer Equation):**

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{K \, \rho_w \, C_w^{2}}{8} \left(\dfrac{D}{r}\right)^{3}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $C_w^{2}$:**

$$
C_w^{2} = (1480)^{2} = 2.1904 \times 10^{6} \ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio $(D/r)^{3}$:**

$$
\dfrac{D}{r} = \dfrac{0.080}{0.600} = 0.13333
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{D}{r}\right)^{3} = (0.13333)^{3} = 2.370 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pre-factor:**

$$
\dfrac{K \, \rho_w \, C_w^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 1000 \times 2.1904 \times 10^{6}}{8}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{4.3808 \times 10^{9}}{8} = 5.476 \times 10^{8} \ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$
P_{max} = 5.476 \times 10^{8} \times 2.370 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P_{max} = 1.298 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa} \approx \mathbf{1.30 \ MPa}
$$

> **Answer:** $P_{max} \approx 1.30$ MPa.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3] — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Electromagnetic Forming (EMF)** process. Derive the expression for **magnetic pressure** acting on the workpiece. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** A capacitor of **600 µF** is charged to a voltage of **8 kV** and discharged through a forming coil. Calculate (i) the stored electrical energy, (ii) the magnetic force on the workpiece if the inductance gradient $\dfrac{dL}{dx} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, and (iii) the peak magnetic pressure on a blank of effective area **$A = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m²** (i.e., 40 cm²). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Working Principle of Electromagnetic Forming:**

In EMF, a bank of capacitors is charged to a high voltage and then discharged through a **forming coil** placed close to (or around) a conductive workpiece. The sudden pulse of current (rise time 10–100 µs, peak 50–500 kA) produces a **rapidly changing magnetic field** around the coil. By **Faraday's law**, this changing field induces **eddy currents** in the workpiece. The two opposing magnetic fields (coil and eddy current) **repel** each other (Lenz's law), and the resulting **Laplace force** propels the workpiece away from the coil at velocities of 50–300 m/s, forcing it to deform against a die.

**Derivation of Magnetic Pressure:**

Energy stored in the inductor:

$$
E_{mag} = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}
$$

Differential work done on the workpiece when the gap $x$ changes by $dx$:

$$
dW = F \cdot dx = \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} \, dL
$$

Instantaneous force:

$$
F = \tfrac{1}{2}\, I^{2} \,\dfrac{dL}{dx}
$$

Dividing by effective area $A$:

$$
P_{mag} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{I^{2}}{2A}\,\dfrac{dL}{dx}
$$

Using $B = \mu_0 H$ and the energy-density form:

$$
P_{mag} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \, H^{2}}{2}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given Data:**
- $C = 600 \ \mu F = 600 \times 10^{-6}$ F
- $V = 8 \ \text{kV} = 8000$ V
- $dL/dx = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- $A = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m²

**(i) Stored Electrical Energy:**

$$
E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 600 \times 10^{-6} \times (8000)^{2}
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.4 \times 10^{7} = 1.92 \times 10^{4} \ \text{J} = \mathbf{19.2 \ kJ}
$$

**(ii) Magnetic Force on the Workpiece:**

Peak current (assuming the coil is initially at zero current and the energy is entirely in the magnetic field at peak):

$$
E = \tfrac{1}{2} L_{peak} I_{peak}^{2} \quad \text{or, more directly, assume the limiting current:}
$$

For numerical purposes, using a typical discharge peak of $I_{peak} = 100$ kA (board-acceptable assumption when not given):

$$
F = \tfrac{1}{2}\, I^{2} \,\dfrac{dL}{dx} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times (1.0 \times 10^{5})^{2} \times 5 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 10^{10} \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 2.5 \times 10^{3} \ \text{N} = \mathbf{2.5 \ kN}
$$

**(iii) Peak Magnetic Pressure:**

$$
P_{mag} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{3}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.25 \times 10^{5} \ \text{Pa} = \mathbf{0.625 \ MPa}
$$

> **Answers:** (i) 19.2 kJ, (ii) 2.5 kN, (iii) 0.625 MPa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — High Velocity Forming**
> 1. **Unit mistakes in $C V^{2}$** — always convert µF to F and kV to V; mixing them gives a wrong answer by a factor of $10^{6}$.
> 2. **Confusing "standoff distance $r$" with "charge radius"** — the Birkofer equation uses **centre-to-blank distance $r$**, **not** the radius of the charge itself.
> 3. **Forgetting the cube** $(D/r)^{3}$ — many students write $(D/r)^{2}$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **EMF "Force" vs. "Pressure"** — always divide the magnetic force by the workpiece area before declaring "magnetic pressure."
> 5. **Strain-rate notation** — use $\dot{\varepsilon}$ (with a dot), not just $\varepsilon$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition** — HVF = forming at $\dot{\varepsilon} = 10^{2}$–$10^{4}$ s⁻¹, completed in µs–ms.
- **Four processes** — EF, EHF, EMF, Dynapak. **Memorize the energy-storage formula for each.**
- **Strain-rate equation** — $\dot{\varepsilon} = v / L$. Be ready to compute from given velocity and gauge length.
- **Birkofer peak-shock pressure** — $P_{max} = \dfrac{K \rho_w C_w^{2}}{8}\left(\dfrac{D}{r}\right)^{3}$. Most important numerical.
- **Explosive energy** — $E = m Q$ (TNT: $Q \approx 4.2 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg).
- **Capacitor energy** — $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$.
- **Magnetic force** — $F = \tfrac{1}{2} I^{2} (dL/dx)$.
- **Magnetic pressure** — $P_{mag} = B^{2}/(2\mu_0)$.
- **Why HVF works** — strain-rate sensitivity ($m$ exponent) and inertial stabilization delay necking.
- **Advantages** — low springback, higher formability, one-shot large parts, brittle materials.
- **Disadvantages** — poor repeatability (EF), safety/licence issues (EF), low efficiency (5–25 %).
- **Key applications** — aerospace panels, missile nose-cones, tube crimping, dissimilar-metal welding.
- **Critical units** — Pa (not MPa) when plugging into formulas; convert at the end.
- **Always cube the $(D/r)$ term** in Birkofer — common KTU trap.
- **Workpiece density $\rho_b$** — needed when computing blank velocity from pressure pulse.
- **Dwell time** — $\Delta t = 2r / C_w$ for underwater EF.
- **Process selection** — large parts → EF; repeatability → EMF (if conductive) or EHF; small/medium → Dynapak.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
