topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sheet Metal Forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sheet Metal Forming** is a class of manufacturing processes in which a metal sheet (blank or stock) is subjected to plastic deformation into a desired geometric shape by applying forces through tools (dies, punches, rollers) **without adding or removing material**, and without significantly changing the sheet's thickness or mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Sheet metal forming is a **cold working (or warm/hot) bulk deformation process** where stresses in the sheet remain **below the ultimate tensile strength (UTS)** but **above the yield strength (σy)** of the material, inducing permanent plastic strain.

The classification of sheet metal forming operations under the KTU 2024 scheme is:

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Nature of Stress** |
|---|---|---|
| Bending | V-bending, U-bending, Roll bending | Tensile + Compressive |
| Deep Drawing | Cylindrical, Rectangular, Square cups | Tensile (radial wall) + Compressive (flange) |
| Stretch Forming | Aircraft panels, automotive skins | Biaxial tensile |
| Spinning | Conical/cylindrical parts | Tensile + Shear |
| Embossing / Coining | Coins, panels | Localized compression |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine taking a **flat, thin A4 paper** and gently pushing your thumb into the middle of it while holding the edges down. The paper stretches and deforms into a cup-like shape — **without tearing it or cutting it**. That, in essence, is sheet metal forming on a large industrial scale.

- The **paper = sheet metal blank** (ductile, thin, flat).
- **Your thumb = punch** (tool applying force).
- **Fingers holding edges = die / blank holder** (constraint).
- The paper does **not break, melt, or get cut** — it only **reshapes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters industrially:** Modern cars, aircraft fuselages, kitchen sinks, beverage cans, and electronic casings are all products of sheet metal forming. It produces **lightweight, high-strength, near-net-shape components** with minimal material waste, making it a cornerstone of mass production.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Yield Strength (σy):** Stress at which plastic deformation begins (in **MPa** or **N/mm²**).
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, σu):** Maximum stress before fracture.
- **Strain Hardening Exponent (n):** Indicates formability. Higher *n* ⇒ better stretchability.
- **Plastic Anisotropy Ratio (R-bar / R̄):** Indicates deep-drawability. Higher R̄ ⇒ better resistance to thinning.
- **Forming Limit Curve (FLC):** Boundary between safe forming and failure (necking/fracture).
- **Thickness (t):** Blank thickness (typically **0.5 mm to 6 mm**).
- **Springback angle (Δθ):** Elastic recovery after load removal.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **Bending, Deep Drawing, and Stretch Forming** — both their **mechanics** and **typical products**. Always carry a clear distinguishing table to the exam hall.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Major Forming Operations

### 2.1.1 Bending

Bending is a forming process in which a sheet metal blank is deformed along a **straight axis** to produce an **angle, flange, or curved section**.

**Step-by-step logic of bending:**

1. The sheet is placed over a die and a punch applies a force at the bend line.
2. The outer fibers of the bend experience **tensile stress** (they stretch).
3. The inner fibers of the bend experience **compressive stress** (they compress).
4. There is a **neutral axis** (no stress, no strain) located roughly at the **mid-thickness**, but it shifts toward the inside of the bend due to thinning.
5. After punch release, the sheet **elastically recovers** — this is called **springback**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bend Allowance (BA):** The length of the neutral axis of the bend, used in flat-pattern layout.

**Key relations:**
- Neutral axis radius: $r_n = R + K_t$
- **Bend Allowance:** $BA = \theta \times \frac{\pi}{180} \times (R + K_t)$

Where $K$ is the **K-factor** (empirical constant, typically 0.33 for soft materials, 0.5 for harder ones).

**Springback (Δθ):** Elastic recovery angle after unloading.

$$ \Delta\theta = \theta_{formed} - \theta_{final} = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \times \frac{R}{t} \times \theta_{formed} \quad (\text{approx. for pure bending}) $$

**Why it matters:** Springback causes dimensional inaccuracy. Compensation is made by **overbending** (setting the punch angle smaller than desired).

### 2.1.2 Deep Drawing

Deep drawing is a process in which a flat sheet metal blank is **drawn into a die cavity by a punch** to form a **hollow cup, box, or shell**.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. A circular blank is placed over a die cavity.
2. A **blank holder** applies a controlled clamping force to prevent wrinkling.
3. The **punch descends**, pulling the sheet into the die to form a cup.
4. The **flange region** undergoes **radial tensile stress** (drawing) and **circumferential compressive stress** (causing thickening).
5. The **wall region** undergoes **uniaxial tensile stress** (thinning).

**Drawing Ratio (DR):** A critical parameter indicating drawability.

$$ DR = \frac{D_{blank}}{D_{punch}} $$

- If $DR \leq 2.0$: **Single draw** is feasible.
- If $DR > 2.0$: **Multiple draws** with intermediate annealing are required.

**Maximum Drawing Force:**

$$ F_{draw} = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{draw} $$

Where $\sigma_{draw}$ is the average drawing stress (work hardening + friction effects).

**Blank Holder Force (BHF):** Must be optimized — too low causes wrinkling, too high causes tearing.

$$ BHF_{optimal} \approx 0.015 \times \pi \times (D^2 - d^2) \times \sigma_y $$

### 2.1.3 Stretch Forming

A sheet is gripped at its edges and stretched over a **male die (form block)**. The sheet undergoes **biaxial tension** with no metal flow into the die.

- Used for **large, contoured panels** (aircraft skins, automotive door panels).
- Maximum strain occurs at the **pole** (apex of the die).
- Forming Limit Diagram (FLD) is used to predict failure.

### 2.1.4 Spinning

A sheet blank is rotated at high speed and **progressively forced against a mandrel** using a roller tool.

- Suitable for **axisymmetric conical, hemispherical, or cylindrical parts**.
- No wrinkling (no compressive flange).
- Typical products: satellite dishes, musical instrument bells, pressure vessel heads.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Symbols & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Bend Allowance | $BA = \theta \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} \cdot (R + K_t)$ | $\theta$: bend angle (°); $R$: inside bend radius (mm); $K$: K-factor; $t$: thickness (mm) |
| Minimum Bend Radius | $R_{min} = \frac{t \cdot (1 - 2\nu)}{2 \cdot (1 + \nu)} \cdot \frac{E}{\sigma_y}$ (approx.) | $E$: Young's modulus (MPa); $\nu$: Poisson's ratio |
| Springback Angle | $\Delta\theta \approx \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \frac{R}{t} \cdot \theta$ | $\theta$: formed angle (rad); $\sigma_y$: yield strength (MPa) |
| Drawing Ratio | $DR = \dfrac{D_{blank}}{D_{punch}}$ | Dimensionless; limit ≈ 2.0 (single draw) |
| Deep Drawing Force | $F_d = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d$ | $d_p$: punch diameter (mm); $t$: thickness (mm); $\sigma_d$: drawing stress (MPa) |
| Blank Holder Force | $BHF \approx 0.015 \cdot \pi \cdot (D^2 - d^2) \cdot \sigma_y$ | $D$: blank dia; $d$: punch dia; $\sigma_y$: yield strength |
| Reduction in Drawing | $r = \dfrac{D - d}{D} \times 100\%$ | $D$: blank dia; $d$: cup dia |
| Ironing Strain | $\varepsilon_i = \ln\left(\dfrac{t_0}{t_f}\right)$ | $t_0$: initial wall thickness; $t_f$: final wall thickness |
| Spinning Force (approx.) | $F_s = \sigma_y \cdot t \cdot L$ | $L$: contact length; $t$: thickness |
| Forming Limit Strain | $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 \leq FLC$ | $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$: principal strains |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hack:** Always include the **units** in your final answer. KTU valuators deduct marks for unitless numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bend Allowance

**Statement:** For a sheet of thickness *t* bent to an inside radius *R* through an angle *θ*, the developed length along the neutral axis is the bend allowance.

**Step 1 — Geometry of bending:**

When a sheet bends, the outer surface (radius $R + t$) is in tension and the inner surface (radius $R$) is in compression. The neutral axis lies at radius $r_n$.

$$ r_n = R + K \cdot t $$

where $K$ is the **K-factor** (empirical, based on material behavior). For most ductile metals under soft tooling, $K \approx 0.33$.

**Step 2 — Arc length along the neutral axis:**

The length of arc subtended by angle $\theta$ (in radians) at radius $r_n$ is:

$$ L_{arc} = r_n \cdot \theta_{rad} $$

**Step 3 — Convert angle from degrees to radians:**

$$ \theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the arc length equation:**

$$ L_{arc} = (R + K_t) \cdot \theta_{deg} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} $$

**Step 5 — Identify the result as the Bend Allowance:**

$$ \boxed{BA = \frac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180} \cdot (R + K_t)} $$

**Step 6 — Flat pattern length (developed length) of bent sheet:**

$$ L_{flat} = L_{straight,1} + BA + L_{straight,2} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Springback in Bending

**Statement:** After unloading, the bent sheet partially recovers elastically, resulting in an increased bend angle (the angle opens up slightly).

**Step 1 — Bending strain in the outer fiber:**

The maximum fiber strain at the outer surface of the bend is:

$$ \varepsilon_{max} = \frac{t}{2R + t} \approx \frac{t}{2R} \quad (R \gg t) $$

**Step 2 — Elastic recovery strain (using Hooke's law):**

When the moment is removed, the elastic portion of the strain is recovered:

$$ \varepsilon_{elastic} = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} $$

**Step 3 — Springback in terms of radius change:**

For pure elastic-perfectly-plastic bending, the change in radius of curvature upon unloading is:

$$ \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \left(\frac{R}{t}\right)^2 $$

**Step 4 — Springback in terms of angle change:**

The angle change $\Delta\theta$ for a given arc length $L$ is:

$$ \Delta\theta = \theta_{formed} - \theta_{final} = \frac{M \cdot L}{E \cdot I} $$

For a rectangular cross-section of width $w$ and thickness $t$:

$$ I = \frac{w \cdot t^3}{12} $$

$$ \Delta\theta \approx \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \frac{R}{t} \cdot \theta_{formed} $$

**Step 5 — Compensation in tooling:**

To achieve the final desired angle $\theta_f$, the punch must be set to a smaller angle:

$$ \theta_{punch} = \theta_f - \Delta\theta $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Drawing Force in Deep Drawing

**Statement:** Determine the maximum force required to draw a cylindrical cup without tearing the wall.

**Step 1 — Free body diagram of the cup wall:**

The drawing force is transmitted through the wall of the cup. The wall is in **uniaxial tension** (radial direction).

**Step 2 — Wall cross-sectional area:**

$$ A_{wall} = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t $$

where $d_p$ is the punch diameter and $t$ is the wall thickness.

**Step 3 — Drawing stress in the wall:**

The drawing stress $\sigma_d$ is the stress required to:
- Overcome **friction** at the die radius (≈ 30% increase).
- Perform **work hardening** of the material.
- Bend and unbend the sheet at the die entry radius.

A simplified empirical relation:

$$ \sigma_d = \sigma_y \cdot \left(1 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{d_p}{R_d} + \frac{2\mu F_{bh}}{A_{wall} \cdot \sigma_y}\right) \cdot e^{\mu \pi / 2} $$

For exam purposes, KTU typically uses a **simplified** form:

$$ \sigma_d \approx (1.1 \text{ to } 1.5) \cdot \sigma_y $$

**Step 4 — Drawing force:**

$$ F_d = \sigma_d \cdot A_{wall} = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d $$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (typical KTU problem):**

A circular blank of diameter **100 mm** is drawn to a cup of diameter **50 mm** in a single draw. Sheet thickness is **1.2 mm**; material yield strength is **250 MPa**.

Solution:

$$ DR = \frac{100}{50} = 2.0 \quad (\text{at the practical limit — single draw feasible}) $$

$$ F_d = \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1.2 \cdot (1.3 \cdot 250) $$

$$ F_d = \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 325 $$

$$ F_d = \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 390 = 61260 \text{ N} \approx 61.26 \text{ kN} $$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Defects in Sheet Metal Forming

| **Process** | **Defect** | **Cause** | **Remedy** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bending | Cracking at outer surface | Bend radius too small (R < R_min) | Increase R; anneal blank |
| Bending | Springback (angle change) | Elastic recovery after unloading | Overbend; bottoming the punch |
| Deep Drawing | Wrinkling of flange | Insufficient blank-holder force | Increase BHF; use draw beads |
| Deep Drawing | Tearing at punch shoulder | Excessive draw ratio, low BHF relief | Reduce DR; use multi-stage drawing |
| Deep Drawing | Earing (cup edge wavy) | Anisotropy in rolled sheet | Use balanced blanks; rotate orientation |
| Stretch Forming | Necking / fracture | Strain exceeds FLC | Lower strain rate; better lubrication |
| Spinning | Wrinkling | Insufficient roller pressure | Increase roller force; reduce feed rate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sheet Metal Forming Process Family

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **process selection logic** a manufacturing engineer follows when choosing a sheet metal forming operation based on geometry, production volume, and material properties.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Metal Blank Input] --> B{Geometry Required}
    B --> C1[Linear Angle Change]
    B --> C2[Hollow Cup or Shell]
    B --> C3[Large Contoured Panel]
    B --> C4[Axisymmetric Hollow Part]

    C1 --> D1[BENDING OPERATION]
    D1 --> D1a[V-Bending]
    D1 --> D1b[U-Bending]
    D1 --> D1c[Roll Bending]

    C2 --> D2[DEEP DRAWING OPERATION]
    D2 --> D2a[Single Stage Draw]
    D2 --> D2b[Multi Stage Draw with Annealing]

    C3 --> D3[STRETCH FORMING OPERATION]
    D3 --> D3a[Manual Stretch Forming]
    D3 --> D3b[Hydraulic Stretch Forming]

    C4 --> D4[SPINNING OPERATION]
    D4 --> D4a[Manual Spinning]
    D4 --> D4b[Power Spinning]

    D1a --> E[Inspection and Quality Check]
    D1b --> E
    D1c --> E
    D2a --> E
    D2b --> E
    D3a --> E
    D3b --> E
    D4a --> E
    D4b --> E
    E --> F[Final Formed Component Output]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Flow: Deep Drawing Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Flat Circular Blank] --> P2[Blank Holder Applied]
    P2 --> P3[Punch Descends]
    P3 --> P4{Stress State in Wall}
    P4 --> P41[Uniaxial Tension in Wall]
    P4 --> P42[Compression in Flange]
    P4 --> P43[Bending at Die Radius]
    P41 --> P5{Check Failure Modes}
    P42 --> P5
    P43 --> P5
    P5 --> P51[Wrinkling - Increase BHF]
    P5 --> P52[Tearing - Reduce DR or Add Lubricant]
    P51 --> P6[Finished Cup]
    P52 --> P6
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Flow: Bending with Springback Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start: Flat Sheet] --> B1[Position over Die]
    B1 --> B2[Apply Punch Force Fp]
    B2 --> B3[Plastic Deformation Induced]
    B3 --> B4[Formed Angle Theta Formed]
    B4 --> B5[Release Punch]
    B5 --> B6[Elastic Recovery Occurs]
    B6 --> B7[Final Angle Theta Final]
    B7 --> B8{Theta Final equals Theta Desired}
    B8 --> B9[Yes - Component Accepted]
    B8 --> B10[No - Adjust Punch Angle by Delta Theta]
    B10 --> B1
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Flow: Spinning Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sheet Blank Mounted on Mandrel] --> S2[Lathe Rotates Mandrel]
    S2 --> S3[Roller Tool Presses Blank onto Mandrel]
    S3 --> S4[Progressive Plastic Deformation]
    S4 --> S5[Final Axisymmetric Shape]
    S5 --> S6[Trim and Inspect]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above diagrams are **functional block-level architecture flows**, not physical engineering drawings. Mermaid cannot accurately depict 3D tooling (punches, dies, mandrels) — KTU examiners understand and accept this approach when the **process logic and decision flow** are clearly demonstrated.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define sheet metal forming. List any four sheet metal forming operations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sheet Metal Forming:** Sheet metal forming is a manufacturing process in which a metal sheet (blank) is plastically deformed into a desired shape by applying forces through tools (punches, dies, rollers) without adding or removing material, and with minimal change in mass or volume.

**Four Sheet Metal Forming Operations:**

1. **Bending** – producing angular deformations along a straight axis.
2. **Deep Drawing** – producing hollow cups/boxes from flat blanks.
3. **Stretch Forming** – stretching a sheet over a die to form large contoured panels.
4. **Spinning** – forming axisymmetric parts by rotating the blank against a mandrel using a roller.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for operations list with brief accuracy)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is springback in sheet metal bending? How is it compensated in tooling?**

**Model Answer:**

**Springback:** Springback is the elastic recovery of a bent sheet metal part after the forming load is released. When the bending force is removed, the elastic component of the total strain is recovered, causing the bend angle to **open up slightly** and the bend radius to **increase** marginally.

**Magnitude:** $\Delta\theta \approx \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R}{t} \cdot \theta_{formed}$

**Compensation in Tooling:**
- **Overbending:** The punch is set to a smaller angle than the desired final angle by an amount equal to the predicted springback.
- **Bottoming the punch:** The punch is designed to strike the bottom of the die cavity, inducing counter-bending that reduces springback.
- **Material selection:** Use of materials with lower $\sigma_y/E$ ratio (e.g., aluminum over spring steel).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for formula/cause + 1 for compensation methods)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**A. (a) Explain the deep drawing process with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for drawing force and discuss the defects in deep drawing. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Deep Drawing Process:**

In deep drawing, a flat circular (or rectangular) sheet metal blank is placed over a die cavity. A blank holder applies a controlled clamping force. A punch descends and pushes the sheet into the die, forming a hollow cup or shell. The flange region flows radially inward, while the wall region is stretched (thinned) in tension.

**Process Components:**
- **Punch:** Applies the drawing force.
- **Die:** Defines the cavity geometry.
- **Blank holder:** Prevents wrinkling of the flange.
- **Blank:** The input sheet.

**Derivation of Drawing Force:**

Consider a circular blank of diameter $D$ and thickness $t$ being drawn into a cup of diameter $d$.

The drawing force is transmitted through the **cup wall** (in uniaxial tension). The cross-sectional area of the wall is:

$$ A_{wall} = \pi \cdot d \cdot t $$

The drawing stress $\sigma_d$ in the wall must overcome:
- Yield stress of the material (with work-hardening factor).
- Friction at the die entry radius.
- Bending and unbending stresses.

A reasonable engineering approximation:

$$ \sigma_d = K \cdot \sigma_y \quad \text{where} \quad K = 1.3 \text{ to } 1.5 $$

Therefore, the **drawing force** is:

$$ F_{draw} = \sigma_d \cdot A_{wall} = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d $$

**Maximum safe drawing force** (limited by tearing at the punch shoulder):

$$ F_{max} = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u \cdot (0.7 \text{ to } 0.9) $$

**Defects in Deep Drawing:**

| **Defect** | **Cause** | **Remedy** |
|---|---|---|
| **Wrinkling** of flange | Insufficient blank-holder force; too thin blank | Increase BHF; add draw beads |
| **Tearing** at punch shoulder | Excessive draw ratio; excessive BHF | Reduce DR; add lubrication |
| **Earing** (wavy cup rim) | Anisotropy in rolled sheet | Use 45° orientation; balanced blanks |
| **Orange peel** | Coarse grain size | Use fine-grained material |
| **Surface scratches** | Poor die finish; inadequate lubrication | Polish die; apply proper lubricant |

*(7 marks: 2 for process + 2 for derivation + 3 for defects)*

---

**A. (b) A circular blank of 200 mm diameter and 2 mm thickness is to be deep drawn into a cup of 90 mm diameter. The yield strength of the material is 280 MPa. Calculate: (i) Drawing Ratio, (ii) Reduction, (iii) Drawing force, and (iv) Blank holder force. Assume drawing stress = 1.4 × σy. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D = 200$ mm, $d = 90$ mm, $t = 2$ mm
- $\sigma_y = 280$ MPa
- $\sigma_d = 1.4 \times 280 = 392$ MPa

**(i) Drawing Ratio:**

$$ DR = \frac{D}{d} = \frac{200}{90} = 2.222 $$

Since $DR > 2.0$, this is **at the upper limit of single-draw feasibility**; a second draw with annealing may be required.

**(ii) Reduction:**

$$ r = \frac{D - d}{D} \times 100\% = \frac{200 - 90}{200} \times 100\% = \frac{110}{200} \times 100\% = 55\% $$

**(iii) Drawing Force:**

$$ F_d = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d $$

$$ F_d = \pi \times 90 \times 2 \times 392 $$

$$ F_d = \pi \times 70560 $$

$$ F_d = 221671 \text{ N} \approx 221.67 \text{ kN} $$

**(iv) Blank Holder Force:**

$$ BHF \approx 0.015 \times \pi \times (D^2 - d^2) \times \sigma_y $$

$$ BHF = 0.015 \times \pi \times (200^2 - 90^2) \times 280 $$

$$ BHF = 0.015 \times \pi \times (40000 - 8100) \times 280 $$

$$ BHF = 0.015 \times \pi \times 31900 \times 280 $$

$$ BHF = 0.015 \times \pi \times 8932000 $$

$$ BHF = 420.85 \text{ kN} $$

*(7 marks: 1+1+2+3 distributed across the four sub-parts)*

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**B. (a) Explain the bending process in sheet metal. Define bend allowance. Derive the expression for bend allowance with a neat sketch. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Bending Process:**

Bending is a sheet metal forming process in which a straight axis of the sheet is plastically deformed to produce an angle, flange, or curved section. The punch applies a force at the bend line, causing the sheet to deform plastically over the die.

**Types of Bending:**
- **V-bending:** Sheet bent into a V-shape using V-die.
- **U-bending / Edge bending:** Sheet bent into a U-channel.
- **Roll bending:** Sheet passed through rollers to form large-radius curves.

**Bend Allowance (BA):**

**Definition:** Bend allowance is the length of the **neutral axis** of the sheet in the bent region. It is the dimension added to the flat-pattern layout to ensure that the bent part matches the design dimensions.

**Derivation:**

Consider a sheet of thickness *t* bent to an inside radius *R* through an angle *θ* (in degrees).

The neutral axis is located at radius $r_n$ from the center of curvature:

$$ r_n = R + K \cdot t $$

where $K$ is the **K-factor** (0.33 to 0.5, depending on material and tooling).

The arc length along the neutral axis for angle *θ* in radians is:

$$ L_{arc} = r_n \cdot \theta_{rad} $$

Converting $\theta_{deg}$ to radians:

$$ \theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} $$

Therefore:

$$ BA = r_n \cdot \theta_{rad} = (R + K_t) \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180} $$

$$ \boxed{BA = \frac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180} \cdot (R + K_t)} $$

**Flat Pattern Length:**

$$ L_{flat} = L_1 + BA + L_2 $$

where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the straight portions of the blank.

*(7 marks: 2 for process explanation + 1 for BA definition + 3 for derivation + 1 for diagram credit)*

---

**B. (b) A 3 mm thick aluminum sheet is to be bent to an included angle of 90° over a V-die. The inside bend radius is 6 mm. The yield strength of aluminum is 120 MPa and Young's modulus is 70 GPa. Calculate: (i) Bend Allowance (use K = 0.44), (ii) Springback angle. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $t = 3$ mm, $R = 6$ mm, $\theta = 90°$
- $K = 0.44$, $\sigma_y = 120$ MPa, $E = 70$ GPa = 70,000 MPa

**(i) Bend Allowance:**

$$ BA = \frac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180} \cdot (R + K_t) $$

$$ BA = \frac{\pi \times 90}{180} \times (6 + 0.44 \times 3) $$

$$ BA = \frac{\pi}{2} \times (6 + 1.32) $$

$$ BA = 1.5708 \times 7.32 $$

$$ BA = 11.50 \text{ mm} $$

**(ii) Springback Angle:**

The bend angle in radians: $\theta_{rad} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708$ rad

$$ \Delta\theta = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \frac{R}{t} \cdot \theta_{formed} $$

$$ \Delta\theta = \frac{120}{70000} \times \frac{6}{3} \times 1.5708 $$

$$ \Delta\theta = 0.001714 \times 2 \times 1.5708 $$

$$ \Delta\theta = 0.005386 \text{ rad} $$

Converting to degrees:

$$ \Delta\theta_{deg} = 0.005386 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.005386 \times 57.2958 = 0.3086° $$

**Therefore:** $\Delta\theta \approx 0.309°$ (or about **18.5 minutes** of arc)

**Compensation in punch angle:** $\theta_{punch} = 90° - 0.309° = 89.691°$

*(7 marks: 3 for BA + 4 for springback with full substitution and unit conversion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in bend allowance or springback formula is the **#1 cause of mark deduction**. Always check units of angle first.
> 2. **Mixing up inside radius (R) and neutral axis radius (R + Kt)** in the BA formula will lose **1 full mark** at minimum.
> 3. **Forgetting to use a multiplier (1.3 to 1.5) on σy for deep drawing stress** in force calculations results in an underestimation. KTU expects you to account for friction and work-hardening.
> 4. **Omitting units in final answers** (kN, MPa, mm) is a common 0.5-mark penalty.
> 5. **Not mentioning the practical limit DR ≈ 2.0** when the calculated DR exceeds it — KTU expects a comment on whether a single draw is feasible.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Sheet metal forming** = plastic deformation of sheet stock without material addition/removal; always operates between **σy and σuts** of the material.
- ✅ **Bending** is a **straight-axis deformation**; key formulas: $BA = \frac{\pi\theta}{180}(R+K_t)$ and springback $\Delta\theta \approx \frac{\sigma_y}{E}\cdot\frac{R}{t}\cdot\theta$.
- ✅ **Deep Drawing** uses drawing ratio $DR = D/d$ with a practical single-draw limit of **≈ 2.0**; drawing force $F_d = \pi d t \sigma_d$.
- ✅ **Stretch Forming** uses **biaxial tension** with no flange draw-in — suited for large contoured panels; FLD is the key design tool.
- ✅ **Spinning** is for **axisymmetric hollow parts**; roller pressure progressively deforms the rotating blank.
- ✅ **Defects to remember for exam:** wrinkling, tearing, earing (in deep drawing); cracking and springback (in bending); necking and fracture (in stretch forming).
- ✅ **Always carry the K-factor range** (0.33 for soft, 0.5 for hard) and the **springback compensation logic** in your mental cheat sheet.
- ✅ **Blank Holder Force (BHF)** in deep drawing is critical — too low = wrinkling; too high = tearing. KTU often tests this trade-off.
- ✅ **Forming Limit Diagram (FLD)** is the design boundary for safe stretch forming — strains above FLC cause localized necking.
- ✅ **Formability indicators:** higher **n (strain hardening exponent)** ⇒ better stretchability; higher **R-bar (plastic anisotropy)** ⇒ better deep-drawability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosive Forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Explosive Forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explosive Forming (EF)** is a *high-energy rate forming (HERF)* process belonging to the family of unconventional manufacturing techniques, in which the sudden, controlled detonation of a chemical explosive charge is utilized to generate a transient, high-amplitude shock wave that travels through a transmitting medium (typically water or air) and impacts a metallic blank, forcing it to conform plastically against a die cavity at velocities on the order of **50–300 m/s**.

This process is classified under **Dynamic Energy Forming** because the entire forming event is completed in **microseconds to milliseconds**, far below the time scale of conventional quasi-static operations. It is governed by the principles of *detonation physics*, *shock-wave mechanics*, and *high-strain-rate plasticity* (strain rates typically **10² to 10⁴ s⁻¹**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Thunderclap Pan"** 🍳
> Imagine placing a sheet of dough over a bowl, then instead of pressing it gently with your hands, you strike the air *just above* the dough with a thunderclap. The shock of compressed air pushes the dough into the bowl instantly. **Explosive Forming works on the exact same principle** — the explosion does not touch the metal; it pushes a "wall of compressed air or water" against it, slamming it into the die.

The metal behaves almost like a fluid for a fleeting instant. Because strain rates are so extreme, the yield strength of the metal rises sharply (a phenomenon called *strain-rate hardening* or *visco-plastic response*), but its *formability* actually *increases* in some respects because the strain distributes uniformly before localized necking can begin.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Detonation Velocity (D)** | **5000 – 9000 m/s** | Speed of shock front through explosive |
| **Strain Rate (ε̇)** | **10² – 10⁴ s⁻¹** | Deformation rate, orders of magnitude above conventional |
| **Forming Pressure** | **0.5 – 10 GPa** | Peak pressure at the blank |
| **Charge-to-Mass Ratio (C/M)** | **0.2 – 1.5** | Mass of explosive per unit mass of workpiece |
| **Stand-off Distance (S)** | **0.1 – 5 × blank diameter** | Distance between charge and workpiece |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Explosive forming is sometimes called **Detonation Forming**. The two terms are used interchangeably in KTU board examination papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The process unfolds in a tightly orchestrated chain of physical events:

1. **Chemical Initiation** → A detonator (typically an electric blasting cap) triggers the explosive charge. The detonation wave propagates through the charge at velocity **D**.
2. **Energy Conversion** → The chemical potential energy of the explosive (released heat of detonation ≈ **4.2 MJ/kg for TNT**) is converted almost instantaneously into the kinetic energy of the expanding detonation gases.
3. **Shock-Wave Transmission** → The detonation products drive a shock wave through the surrounding medium (air, water, or sand).
4. **Pressure Loading on Blank** → When the shock wave strikes the metal blank, it accelerates the blank to a velocity of **100–300 m/s** within microseconds.
5. **High-Velocity Plastic Deformation** → The blank impacts the die surface and wraps around the die cavity, conforming to its geometry.
6. **Energy Dissipation** → Kinetic energy is dissipated as heat, sound, and permanent plastic work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why water as a transmitting medium?** Water is essentially *incompressible* on the timescale of the shock, so it transmits pressure with almost no loss. Air, by contrast, allows a **1/r decay** of pressure, dramatically reducing efficiency. **Underwater explosive forming** is therefore the most common industrial variant.

## 2.2 Classification of Explosive Forming Processes

There are **three principal variants**, each a frequent KTU board question:

### (a) Stand-off (Free) Forming
- The charge is suspended at a *distance* above the blank.
- The shock wave travels through air and accelerates the blank into the die.
- **Used for:** Large panels, low-detail parts.

### (b) Contact (Driven / Cavity) Forming
- The charge is placed *inside* a hollow die, with the blank covering the open end.
- Detonation products drive the blank directly outward against the female die.
- **Used for:** Tubular parts, small precision components.

### (c) Underwater Forming
- Both charge and blank are submerged in a water tank.
- Water transmits the shock wave with minimal energy loss.
- **Used for:** Most industrial applications; highest efficiency.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation you need for KTU ESE problems on this topic. Pay special attention to units.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = m_{c} \cdot Q$ | Total released chemical energy; $m_c$ = charge mass, **Q** ≈ 4.2 MJ/kg (TNT) |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot D^{2}}{\gamma + 1}$ | **Chapman–Jouguet pressure** (detonation gas pressure); ρ = density, D = detonation velocity, γ ≈ 3 |
| 3 | $\sigma_{y,d} = \sigma_{y,s} \left(1 + \dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_{0}}\right)^{m}$ | **Cowper–Symonds strain-rate model**; dynamic yield increases with $\dot{\varepsilon}$ |
| 4 | $V = \dfrac{\sqrt{2E}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{M}{2C}}}$ | **Simplified Gurney equation** for fragment/blank velocity; C = charge mass, M = blank mass |
| 5 | $Z = \dfrac{S}{W^{1/3}}$ | **Scaled distance** (Hopkinson scaling) |
| 6 | $p \cdot S^{n} = \text{constant}$ | **Hopkinson cube-root law**; for similar pressure profiles, scaled distance is preserved |
| 7 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{V}{L}$ | Strain-rate estimate; V = impact velocity, L = characteristic length |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{released}}$ | Forming efficiency; typically **1–10 %** |
| 9 | $t_{f} = \dfrac{L}{V}$ | Forming time; typically **0.1 – 1 ms** |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Tip:** For "estimate the impact velocity" problems, the **Gurney equation** (Formula 4) is the single most-tested formula for explosive forming in KTU 2024 scheme papers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical Part Formed |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Wing-skin panels, large fuselage sections, missile nose cones |
| **Automotive (R&D)** | Prototype body panels, deep-drawn sheet metal |
| **Marine** | Submarine hull plates, sonar-dome covers |
| **Nuclear** | Large containment vessel end-caps |
| **Defence** | Armoured-plate shaping, ammunition casings |

Explosive forming excels where *part size* is too large for conventional presses, *tooling cost* must be minimized (only **one die half** is needed, the other is the water/air itself), and *lead time* is short.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Impact Velocity from the Gurney Equation

The **Gurney equation** gives the terminal velocity imparted to a metal blank (or fragment) by an explosive charge. The full derivation proceeds from conservation of momentum plus the assumption that the detonation products expand uniformly.

**Step 1 — Energy released by charge:**
$$ E = m_{c} \cdot Q $$

where $m_c$ is the charge mass and $Q$ is the heat of detonation per kg.

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy of the combined system (charge gases + blank):**

Assume the detonation gases move at average velocity $V_g$ and the blank moves at $V$. The kinetic energy of the gas is approximated by:
$$ KE_{gas} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} m_{c} \cdot V_{g}^{2} $$

**Step 3 — Empirical Gurney assumption:**
For a "sandwich" geometry (charge between two plates), Gurney showed experimentally that:
$$ V_{g} \approx \dfrac{V}{2} $$

This assumption reflects the fact that the gas cloud expands *symmetrically* about the charge centre.

**Step 4 — Energy balance:**
Total kinetic energy equals chemical energy:
$$ \tfrac{1}{2} m_{c} V_{g}^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} m_{b} V^{2} = m_{c} Q $$

Substituting $V_g = V/2$:
$$ \tfrac{1}{2} m_{c} \left(\dfrac{V}{2}\right)^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} m_{b} V^{2} = m_{c} Q $$

**Step 5 — Simplification:**
$$ \tfrac{1}{8} m_{c} V^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} m_{b} V^{2} = m_{c} Q $$

Factor out $V^{2}$:
$$ V^{2} \left( \tfrac{m_{c}}{8} + \tfrac{m_{b}}{2} \right) = m_{c} Q $$

$$ V^{2} \left( \dfrac{m_{c} + 4 m_{b}}{8} \right) = m_{c} Q $$

**Step 6 — Solve for V:**
$$ V = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \, m_{c} Q}{m_{c} + 4 m_{b}}} $$

This is the **Gurney equation for asymmetric (one-sided) geometry** — the form most often quoted for explosive forming where the blank is on one side of the charge.

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
- Charge: $m_c = 0.1$ kg of TNT, $Q = 4.2$ MJ/kg
- Blank: $m_b = 2.0$ kg steel plate
- Find impact velocity V:

$$ V = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 0.1 \times 4.2 \times 10^{6}}{0.1 + 4 \times 2.0}} $$

$$ V = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.36 \times 10^{6}}{8.1}} $$

$$ V = \sqrt{4.148 \times 10^{5}} \approx 644 \text{ m/s} $$

This far exceeds the **50–300 m/s** typical of forming, so in practice one uses a **smaller charge or larger stand-off** to reduce velocity.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Hopkinson (Cube-Root) Scaling Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This law is the **most-tested result** in explosive forming derivations at KTU. Commit it to memory.

Two explosive charges of different sizes but **geometrically similar** placement produce similar peak-pressure profiles if their **scaled distances** are equal.

**Step 1 — Dimensional reasoning:**
Pressure $p$ depends on charge mass $W$ and stand-off distance $S$:
$$ p = f(W, S) $$

The dimensions must yield pressure:
$$ [p] = \dfrac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = \dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume}} = \dfrac{M}{L T^{2}} $$

**Step 2 — Form dimensionless combination:**
The only combination of $W$ (kg) and $S$ (m) that has dimensions of length is:
$$ \dfrac{S}{W^{1/3}} \quad [\text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}] $$

Therefore:
$$ p = \phi\!\left(\dfrac{S}{W^{1/3}}\right) $$

For two charges producing **equal peak pressure** at their respective surfaces:
$$ \dfrac{S_{1}}{W_{1}^{1/3}} = \dfrac{S_{2}}{W_{2}^{1/3}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{S_{1}}{S_{2}} = \left(\dfrac{W_{1}}{W_{2}}\right)^{1/3} $$

This is the **cube-root scaling law**.

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A **1 kg** charge produces a peak pressure of 5 GPa at **0.5 m** stand-off. What stand-off distance for a **8 kg** charge gives the same pressure?

Using $S_{2} = S_{1} \cdot (W_{2}/W_{1})^{1/3}$:
$$ S_{2} = 0.5 \times (8/1)^{1/3} = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0 \text{ m} $$

The stand-off must **double** when the charge mass **octuples** to preserve the pressure profile.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Estimate of Strain Rate

Once velocity $V$ and characteristic forming length $L$ are known:

$$ \dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{V}{L} $$

For V = 200 m/s and L = 0.1 m (10 cm panel):
$$ \dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{200}{0.1} = 2000 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

This lies squarely in the **10² – 10⁴ s⁻¹** regime characteristic of HERF — confirming that explosive forming indeed qualifies as a high-energy-rate process.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Explosive vs. Other HERF Processes

| Feature | Explosive Forming | Electromagnetic Forming | Electrohydraulic Forming |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Chemical detonation | Pulsed magnetic field | Underwater electric arc |
| Energy released | **4.2 MJ/kg (TNT)** | **~0.1–1 MJ per pulse** | **~0.05 MJ per pulse** |
| Strain rate | 10²–10⁴ s⁻¹ | 10³–10⁴ s⁻¹ | 10²–10³ s⁻¹ |
| Max part size | **Very large (5 m+)** | Limited (magnetic coil size) | Moderate |
| Tooling cost | **Low** | High | Moderate |
| Noise level | **Extreme (~180 dB)** | Moderate | Moderate |
| Safety requirements | **Most stringent** | Moderate | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Architecture — Stand-off Underwater Forming

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Detonator] --> B[Explosive Charge]
    B --> C[Detonation Wave]
    C --> D[Shock Wave in Water]
    D --> E[Metal Blank]
    E --> F[Vacuum Chamber Below Blank]
    E --> G[Die Cavity]
    G --> H[Formed Part]
    F --> I[Atmospheric Pressure]
    D --> J[Water Tank]
    J --> K[Reinforced Walls for Safety]

    classDef energy fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef medium fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
    classDef part fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#fff5e5,stroke:#996600,color:#000

    class A,B,C energy
    class D,J medium
    class E,F,G,H,I part
    class K safety
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **detonator** initiates the **explosive charge**.
- The detonation wave creates a **shock wave in the water** (transmitting medium).
- The shock wave slams into the **metal blank**, which is held over a **die cavity**.
- A **vacuum chamber below the blank** ensures the blank moves toward the die rather than being trapped by air pressure.
- The blank wraps into the **die cavity** to form the final part.
- The entire operation is enclosed in a **reinforced tank** for safety.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Electrical Signal] --> P2[Detonator Activation]

    P5 --> P6[Pressure on Blank]
    P6 --> P7[Plastic Deformation]
    P7 --> P8[Part Conformity to Die]
    P8 --> P9[Quality Inspection]

    style P1 fill:#fff0d0,stroke:#aa6600

```
