# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM). List any four commonly used thermoplastic materials in FDM.**

**Model Answer:**

> FDM is a material extrusion–based additive manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is melted in a heated liquefier and extruded through a nozzle to deposit a bead layer-by-layer, which solidifies to form a 3D part directly from a CAD model.

**Four common FDM thermoplastics:**
1. **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** — biodegradable, easy to print, low warping.
2. **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** — tough, heat-resistant, requires heated bed and enclosed chamber.
3. **PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol)** — chemically resistant, food-safe variants.
4. **Nylon (PA12 / PA6)** — high strength, flexibility, requires high nozzle temperature (~260 °C).
*(Acceptable alternatives: TPU, Polycarbonate, PEEK, ASA.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is "stair-stepping" in FDM? Write the expression for stair-stepping error and explain how it can be minimized.**

**Model Answer:**

> Stair-stepping is the **staircase-like surface artifact** that appears on inclined or curved surfaces of an FDM part due to the discrete layer-by-layer deposition of material.

**Expression:**

$$\delta_s = t (1 - \cos\alpha)$$

where $t$ = layer thickness, $\alpha$ = angle of inclined surface from horizontal.

**Minimization methods:**
- Reduce layer thickness $t$ (e.g., from 0.2 mm to 0.1 mm).
- Orient the part so that critical surfaces are nearly vertical ($\alpha \to 90°$, where $\cos\alpha \to 0$ and $\delta_s \to 0$).
- Post-processing (sanding, acetone smoothing for ABS).
- Use of variable layer thickness (adaptive slicing).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **ESE Module 4 — Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]*

> **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)**. Describe the role of the **liquefier, nozzle, and build platform** in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A desktop FDM machine extrudes an ABS road of width $W = 0.5$ mm and layer thickness $t = 0.25$ mm at a print speed of $v = 50$ mm/s. The filament diameter is $d_f = 1.75$ mm and the density of ABS is $\rho = 1.04$ g/cm³. Calculate: **(i)** the cross-sectional area of the bead, **(ii)** the volumetric deposition rate, **(iii)** the mass flow rate, and **(iv)** the filament feed speed required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The FDM process follows the **material extrusion** principle (ISO/ASTM 52900). A thermoplastic filament is fed from a spool into a **liquefier** — a metal block containing a resistive heater and a thermistor. The heater raises the polymer to its melting point (typically 200–260 °C), converting the solid filament into a viscous melt.

This melt is extruded through a fine **brass nozzle** (diameter 0.2–1.0 mm) as a continuous molten bead. The nozzle is mounted on a **gantry system** driven by stepper motors, which position it precisely in the X and Y directions according to G-code generated by slicing software.

The molten bead is deposited onto a **heated build platform** (also called the print bed), which is maintained at a temperature below the polymer's glass transition temperature ($T_g$) — typically 60–110 °C for ABS — to ensure good first-layer adhesion and to minimize warping from differential thermal contraction. After each layer is completed, a **Z-axis stepper motor** lowers the platform by exactly one layer thickness $t$, and the next layer is deposited on top.

**Role summary:**
- **Liquefier:** Converts solid filament to molten polymer at controlled temperature.
- **Nozzle:** Defines the road's cross-sectional width and resolution.
- **Build platform:** Provides dimensional reference and thermal management for the first layer.

The bond between layers is achieved by **conductive re-melting** of the previously deposited layer's top surface, allowing polymer chain interdiffusion (Wool's reptation model) across the interface. This is why **FDM parts are anisotropic** — inter-layer strength is typically 30–60% of intra-layer strength.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $W = 0.5$ mm, $t = 0.25$ mm, $v = 50$ mm/s, $d_f = 1.75$ mm, $\rho = 1.04$ g/cm³.

**(i) Cross-sectional area of bead:**

$$A = W \cdot t = 0.5 \times 0.25 = 0.125 \text{ mm}^2$$

**(ii) Volumetric deposition rate:**

$$\dot{V} = A \cdot v = 0.125 \times 50 = 6.25 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate:**

Convert density: $\rho = 1.04$ g/cm³ = $1.04 \times 10^{-3}$ g/mm³ = $0.00104$ g/mm³.

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 0.00104 \times 6.25 = 6.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 6.5 \text{ mg/s}}$$

**(iv) Filament feed speed:**

Equating volumetric flow rate in filament to outflow:

$$\frac{\pi d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f = \dot{V}$$

$$v_f = \frac{4 \dot{V}}{\pi d_f^{\,2}} = \frac{4 \times 6.25}{\pi \times (1.75)^2} = \frac{25.0}{\pi \times 3.0625} = \frac{25.0}{9.6211}$$

$$\boxed{v_f \approx 2.598 \text{ mm/s}}$$

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

> **(a)** Explain the **key process parameters** of FDM that affect part quality. Discuss how **layer thickness, raster angle, and infill density** influence the mechanical and surface properties of an FDM part. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **stair-stepping error equation** for an inclined surface in FDM. A part has a feature inclined at $\alpha = 50°$ from the horizontal, printed with a layer thickness of $t = 0.20$ mm. Calculate the stair-stepping error. If the part is re-oriented such that the surface becomes vertical ($\alpha = 90°$), what is the new error? Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The FDM process has a large parameter space. The most influential parameters are:

**1. Layer Thickness ($t$):**
- Directly controls vertical resolution and the **stair-stepping error** $\delta_s = t(1 - \cos\alpha)$.
- Smaller $t$ (e.g., 0.10 mm) → smoother surface, better dimensional accuracy, longer build time.
- Larger $t$ (e.g., 0.30 mm) → faster build, visible layer lines, weaker inter-layer bond due to less re-melting per unit area.

**2. Raster Angle ($\theta_r$):**
- The orientation of the parallel roads within each layer relative to the part's loading axis.
- FDM parts are **anisotropic**: tensile strength parallel to the raster can be 2–3× higher than perpendicular.
- A common practice is to alternate $\theta_r$ between consecutive layers (e.g., 0°/90°/0°/90°) to balance properties, but this still leaves a weaker Z-direction.

**3. Infill Density ($\phi$):**
- The percentage of internal volume filled with material (vs. air gaps).
- Low infill (10–20%) → lightweight, fast, suitable for visual prototypes.
- High infill (80–100%) → strong, heavy, suitable for functional parts.
- Infill **pattern** (grid, triangular, honeycomb, gyroid) also affects stiffness-to-weight ratio.

**Other parameters:** Road width, nozzle temperature, bed temperature, print speed, build orientation, cooling fan speed.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation:**

Consider an inclined planar surface of angle $\alpha$ from the horizontal, built in layers of thickness $t$. In one layer, the surface advances horizontally by a run $r = t / \tan\alpha$ while the true surface drops by exactly $t$ vertically.

In the discrete layer, the top of the layer is held flat at the upper Z-level for the entire horizontal run $r$. At the trailing edge of that run, the **true** surface is at the lower Z-level, but the layer's flat top is still at the upper Z-level. The vertical gap between the true surface and the layer's flat top at that point is:

$$\delta_s = t - t\cos\alpha$$

Why $t\cos\alpha$? Because at angle $\alpha$, the **horizontal projection of one layer thickness** (the vertical drop) onto the surface normal direction is $t\cos\alpha$. Equivalently, if you project the layer thickness $t$ onto the surface itself, the vertical component of the chord is $t\cos\alpha$, and the leftover height is the error.

$$\boxed{\delta_s = t(1 - \cos\alpha)}$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

**Case 1:** $\alpha = 50°$, $t = 0.20$ mm.

$$\delta_s = 0.20 \times (1 - \cos 50°) = 0.20 \times (1 - 0.6428) = 0.20 \times 0.3572$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{s,1} \approx 0.0714 \text{ mm} = 71.4 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

**Case 2:** $\alpha = 90°$ (vertical surface).

$$\delta_s = 0.20 \times (1 - \cos 90°) = 0.20 \times (1 - 0) = 0.20 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{s,2} = 0.20 \text{ mm}}$$

**Comment:** The vertical surface gives a *larger* error in absolute terms because the error per layer is the full layer thickness. However, the **stair-step appears along the height**, not as a sloped artifact — so a vertical wall is geometrically "perfect" in profile (it is built up as a straight vertical line), while a $50°$ surface shows clear visible stairsteps. The practical takeaway: **smooth low-angle surfaces are the worst case for visible stair-stepping in FDM**, which is why critical curved or low-angle surfaces should be oriented vertically, or printed at very fine $t$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions.** Density in g/cm³ must be converted to g/mm³ (÷ 1000). Marks deducted for unit mismatch.
> 2. **Confusing $W$ and $d$.** Road width $W$ is the *deposited* bead width and is **slightly larger** than the nozzle diameter $d$ (the melt swells slightly). Many students use $d$ instead of $W$ — read the question carefully.
> 3. **Skipping the "why" in long answers.** A 7-mark part (a) requires both a diagram *and* the role of each component. A diagram without explanation earns only ~3 marks.
> 4. **Stair-stepping: wrong sign or no derivation.** Students often write $\delta_s = t \cdot \sin\alpha$ or $\delta_s = t \cdot \tan\alpha$ — both are wrong. The correct form is $\delta_s = t(1 - \cos\alpha)$, derived from the geometry of one layer.
> 5. **Ignoring anisotropic strength.** FDM parts are NOT isotropic. A statement like "FDM parts have uniform strength in all directions" will lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — FDM**

- ✅ **Definition:** FDM = Material Extrusion AM process; thermoplastic filament melted and deposited layer-by-layer (ASTM F2792 / ISO/ASTM 52900).
- ✅ **Inventor:** S. Scott Crump, 1989; commercialized by Stratasys in 1990.
- ✅ **Key sub-systems:** Filament feed → Liquefier → Nozzle → Build platform → Motion (X, Y, Z).
- ✅ **Most common materials:** PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon, TPU, Polycarbonate.
- ✅ **Critical process parameters:** Layer thickness $t$, Road width $W$, Nozzle temp $T_n$, Bed temp $T_b$, Print speed $v$, Raster angle $\theta_r$, Infill density $\phi$.
- ✅ **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - $A = W \cdot t$
  - $\dot{V} = W \cdot t \cdot v$
  - $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot W \cdot t \cdot v$
  - $\delta_s = t(1 - \cos\alpha)$
  - $v_f = \dfrac{4 \dot{V}}{\pi d_f^{\,2}}$
- ✅ **Inter-layer bonding mechanism:** Conductive re-melting of the previous layer's top surface → polymer chain interdiffusion (Wool's reptation theory).
- ✅ **Anisotropy:** FDM parts are weaker in Z than in X–Y (inter-layer bond < intra-layer bond by 30–60%).
- ✅ **Stair-stepping error:** Worst on low-angle surfaces; minimized by reducing $t$ or orienting the part so critical surfaces are vertical.
- ✅ **Build time scales as:** $T_{\text{build}} \propto N_{\text{layers}} \times L_{\text{per\,layer}} / v \propto H/(t \cdot v)$.
- ✅ **Support structures:** Required for overhangs > ~45°. In dual-extrusion FDM, soluble supports (PVA) can be used.
- ✅ **Comparison anchors:** FDM vs SLA (FDM is cheaper, stronger; SLA is smoother). FDM vs SLS (FDM is cheaper, lower temp; SLS is self-supporting).
- ✅ **Examiner's mnemonic — "F-L-N-B-X":** Filament → Liquefier → Nozzle → Build plate → X–Y–Z motion. Always describe the FDM process in this sequence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminated Object Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laminated Object Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)** is an **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** process classified under the **Sheet Lamination** category as per **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015** standards. In this process, successive sheets of feed material (paper, polymer, or metal foils) are bonded together using a heated roller or adhesive, and simultaneously a computer-controlled **laser cutter (or knife cutter)** traces and cuts the cross-sectional profile of the 3D object layer by layer. The un-bonded (waste) regions of each sheet are simultaneously cut into small cubes by a cross-hatch pattern, enabling easy removal after the build is complete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Rapid Prototyping):**
> LOM is one of the **Seven Primary AM Process Categories** defined by ASTM. It is grouped with **Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)** and **Selective Lamination Composite Object Manufacturing (SLCOM)** as the three sheet-lamination-based technologies.

**System Originator:** The original LOM system was commercialized by **Helisys Inc. (USA)** in 1991. After the company's dissolution, the technology was revived and rebranded as **"Paper Lamination Technology (PLT)"** by **Mcor Technologies** and **Solvay/Solonox** style derivatives.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stacked Cookie Cutter" Intuition

Imagine you are baking a giant 3D cookie in the shape of a complex sculpture:

1. You take **flat sheets of dough (paper with adhesive backing)**.
2. You lay down the first sheet on a tray.
3. You press and **heat-bond** the next sheet firmly on top (using a hot iron/roller — imagine an industrial waffle iron).
4. A **laser beam (like a precision stencil knife)** cuts the **outline of the desired cross-section** through the top layer.
5. It also cuts the **leftover dough** into a checkerboard grid of small squares, so the waste falls off like a perforated stamp.
6. You lower the tray by **one sheet thickness** and repeat — stacking cut paper like a wedding cake, one laser-traced layer at a time.

When finished, you pull out the bonded stack, snap off the waste cubes by hand (no chemicals needed!), and you have a **fully formed 3D solid object** carved from layered sheets — exactly like a topographic map carved from stacked paper pages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy works for KTU exams:** Examiners often expect students to explain LOM in 1–2 lines. A clear analogy instantly demonstrates conceptual understanding (Bloom's Level 2 — Comprehension).

## 1.3 Core Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Standard Layer Thickness ($\Delta z$) | **0.05 mm to 0.5 mm** (typical 0.1 mm) |
| Standard Feedstock | **Paper with adhesive backing, PVC, polymer foils, metal tapes** |
| Bonding Method | **Thermal pressure (heated roller) or adhesive backing** |
| Cutting Method | **CO₂ laser (10.6 µm wavelength) or Z-knife cutter** |
| Achievable Tolerance | **±0.1 mm (X,Y); ±0.3 mm (Z)** |
| Typical Build Volume | **250 × 250 × 250 mm** (small desktop) up to **800 × 550 × 500 mm** (industrial) |
| Surface Finish (as-built) | **Ra 10–25 µm** (rough; requires post-finishing) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Layer-by-Layer Logic

The LOM process follows a strict **repetitive cycle**, where each cycle produces exactly one bonded + cut layer. The cycle has **five sequential steps**:

### Step 1 — Sheet Feeding
A new sheet of material is fed from a **supply roll** onto the build platform by a **feed roller mechanism**. The sheet is positioned precisely so its leading edge aligns with the previous layer.

### Step 2 — Bonding (Heated Compaction)
A **heated roller (compaction roller)** moves across the freshly placed sheet. The combination of:
- **Pressure (typically 0.5–1.5 MPa)**
- **Temperature (typically 60–80 °C for paper, 150–200 °C for polymers)**

activates the adhesive backing and bonds the new sheet to the layer below.

### Step 3 — Laser Cutting
A **CO₂ laser (typically 25–50 W)** traces two distinct contours:
- **Object Contour:** The actual cross-section of the part (the geometry to be kept).
- **Cross-Hatch Pattern:** A checkerboard / grid of small rectangles (typically 10 mm × 10 mm) cut into the **waste region**. This fragmentation makes the unwanted material easy to remove.

### Step 4 — Platform Lowering (Z-Stage Descent)
The build platform descends by exactly **one layer thickness ($\Delta z$)** to create clearance for the next sheet. A sacrificial support base (often a laser-cut honeycomb grid) supports the part during the build.

### Step 5 — Repeat
Steps 1–4 repeat until the full Z-height of the part is built.

### Step 6 — Waste Removal (Decubing)
Once the build is complete, the operator **manually breaks away the waste cubes** ("decubing") from the part. The part is then separated from the support base using a hand-saw or wire cutter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic:** Notice that LOM is unique among AM processes because **the cutting action happens at the TOP of the part, not the bottom**. This means Z-axis accuracy depends on **roller pressure uniformity**, not laser focus depth.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Decision

- **Why use a heated roller instead of glue spray?** → Roller provides uniform pressure and consistent thermal contact → fewer delamination defects.
- **Why cross-hatch the waste?** → Continuous waste sheets would be impossible to remove from internal cavities. The grid allows waste to fall out like perforated paper.
- **Why paper as the standard material?** → Paper is the **cheapest, most dimensionally stable, and self-adhesive** (thermoplastic-coated) sheet material available → dramatically lowers running cost.
- **Why is Z-resolution poor compared to SLS/SLA?** → Z-resolution is limited by **paper thickness (≈ 0.1 mm)**, not by laser spot size.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of Layers ($n$)** | $n = \dfrac{Z_{\max}}{\Delta z}$ | $Z_{\max}$ = Build height (mm); $\Delta z$ = Layer thickness (mm) |
| 2 | **Total Build Time ($T_{build}$)** | $T_{build} = n \times (t_{feed} + t_{heat} + t_{cut} + t_{lower}) + t_{setup}$ | All $t$ in seconds; $t_{setup}$ ≈ 15–30 min |
| 3 | **Effective Material Volume ($V_{mat}$)** | $V_{mat} = L \times W \times Z_{\max} \times \rho$ | $L,W,Z$ in mm; $\rho$ = sheet bulk density (g/mm³) |
| 4 | **Part Volume vs Waste Volume** | $V_{waste} = V_{mat,total} - V_{part}$ | $V_{waste}$ is the decubed material |
| 5 | **Material Utilization Ratio ($\eta_{mat}$)** | $\eta_{mat} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{V_{mat,total}} \times 100\,\%$ | Expressed in %; ideally maximized |
| 6 | **Cross-Hatch Pitch ($p_{grid}$)** | $p_{grid} = 5\,\text{mm} \text{ to } 15\,\text{mm}$ (constant) | Trade-off: smaller $p$ → finer waste cubes but longer laser time |
| 7 | **Volumetric Build Rate (R)** | $R = \dfrac{L \times W \times \Delta z}{t_{cut}}$ | mm³/s; key productivity metric |
| 8 | **Linear Laser Cutting Speed ($v_{cut}$)** | $v_{cut} = \dfrac{P_{laser}}{k \times d \times \Delta z}$ | $P_{laser}$ = laser power (W); $d$ = cut depth per pass; $k$ = material constant |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LOM is **not** used for end-use functional metal parts. Its engineering utility lies in:

- **Sand Casting Patterns & Core Boxes:** LOM paper patterns burn out cleanly in the foundry → ideal for **one-shot prototypes**.
- **Investment Casting Wax Replacements:** LOM plastic sheets substitute wax patterns.
- **Architectural Scale Models:** Aesthetic, low-cost, easy to paint and sand.
- **Concept Models in Automotive Styling:** Used by Ford, GM in the 1990s for clay-replacement mockups.
- **Foundry Tooling & Master Patterns:** For short-run injection molding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Build Time for a LOM Part

We will derive the **closed-form expression for total build time** used in production planning.

### Given
- Part dimensions: $L \times W \times Z_{\max}$ (in mm)
- Layer thickness: $\Delta z$ (in mm)
- Per-cycle time components: $t_{feed}$, $t_{heat}$, $t_{cut}$, $t_{lower}$ (in seconds)
- Setup overhead: $t_{setup}$ (in seconds)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the number of layers in terms of part height and layer thickness.**

The part of height $Z_{\max}$ is built by stacking $n$ layers, each of thickness $\Delta z$. The total stacked height must equal $Z_{\max}$, so:

$$
Z_{\max} = n \times \Delta z
$$

Solving for $n$:

$$
n = \frac{Z_{\max}}{\Delta z}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the time for one complete cycle.**

Each cycle involves four sequential operations: feeding the sheet, heating/compaction, laser cutting, and platform lowering. These occur **in series** (one after another), so the total cycle time is the **arithmetic sum**:

$$
t_{cycle} = t_{feed} + t_{heat} + t_{cut} + t_{lower}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the cycle time by the number of layers.**

The build is fully repetitive: every layer takes the same time. Therefore:

$$
T_{build,core} = n \times t_{cycle}
$$

Substituting $n$ from Step 1:

$$
T_{build,core} = \frac{Z_{\max}}{\Delta z} \times (t_{feed} + t_{heat} + t_{cut} + t_{lower})
$$

**Step 4 — Add the setup time (one-time overhead).**

The setup time $t_{setup}$ is incurred **only once** at the start. Therefore:

$$
T_{build} = t_{setup} + \frac{Z_{\max}}{\Delta z} \times (t_{feed} + t_{heat} + t_{cut} + t_{lower})
$$

This is the **final, complete, production-ready** formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The total build time is **inversely proportional to layer thickness $\Delta z$**. Halving the layer thickness **doubles the build time**, which is why LOM is always slower than powder-bed AM processes at fine resolutions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Utilization Ratio ($\eta_{mat}$)

In LOM, every sheet that is fed contributes to the part **OR** the waste. Let us derive how much of the material is actually used.

### Step 1 — Define the part volume.**

The part is a near-rectangular solid of dimensions $L \times W \times Z_{\max}$:

$$
V_{part} = L \times W \times Z_{\max}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the total material volume (bounded by the build platform).**

The full sheet area covered is $A_{sheet} = L_{build} \times W_{build}$ where $L_{build}, W_{build}$ are the maximum build envelope dimensions. Hence:

$$
V_{mat,total} = L_{build} \times W_{build} \times Z_{\max}
$$

### Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\eta_{mat} = \frac{V_{part}}{V_{mat,total}} \times 100\,\% = \frac{L \times W \times Z_{\max}}{L_{build} \times W_{build} \times Z_{\max}} \times 100\,\%
$$

The $Z_{\max}$ cancels:

$$
\eta_{mat} = \frac{L \times W}{L_{build} \times W_{build}} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Practical implication:** For a small part placed in the center of a large platform, $\eta_{mat}$ can be as low as **5–10%**, which is why LOM cost depends strongly on platform utilization.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A LOM machine is used to build a part of dimensions $200 \times 150 \times 100$ mm. The layer thickness is $\Delta z = 0.1$ mm. The cycle times are: $t_{feed} = 2$ s, $t_{heat} = 3$ s, $t_{cut} = 12$ s, $t_{lower} = 1$ s. Setup time is 1200 s. Calculate:
- (a) The number of layers.
- (b) The total build time.
- (c) The material utilization ratio if the build envelope is $250 \times 250 \times 250$ mm.

### Solution

**(a) Number of layers:**

$$
n = \frac{Z_{\max}}{\Delta z} = \frac{100\,\text{mm}}{0.1\,\text{mm}} = 1000\,\text{layers}
$$

**(b) Total build time:**

First, compute the cycle time:

$$
t_{cycle} = t_{feed} + t_{heat} + t_{cut} + t_{lower} = 2 + 3 + 12 + 1 = 18\,\text{s/layer}
$$

Then the total core build time:

$$
T_{build,core} = n \times t_{cycle} = 1000 \times 18 = 18\,000\,\text{s}
$$

Add the setup time:

$$
T_{build} = t_{setup} + T_{build,core} = 1200 + 18\,000 = 19\,200\,\text{s}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
T_{build} = \frac{19\,200}{3600} \approx 5.33\,\text{hours}
$$

**(c) Material utilization ratio:**

$$
\eta_{mat} = \frac{L \times W}{L_{build} \times W_{build}} \times 100\,\% = \frac{200 \times 150}{250 \times 250} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
\eta_{mat} = \frac{30\,000}{62\,500} \times 100\,\% = 48\,\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Answer Format Tip:** Always show your formula first, then plug in the values. This is the valuation key's "Formula-Mark" — even if your arithmetic slips, you get partial credit for the correct formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the LOM Process Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete functional chain** of a LOM machine — from CAD input to the finished part — illustrating the **information flow** and the **physical material flow** separately. This is the diagram examiners expect to see in a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model STL File] --> A2[Slicer Software Layer Generation]
    A2 --> A3[Layer Contour Data Path]

    A3 --> B1[Laser Control Unit]
    A4 --> B2[Z Stage Controller]
    B1 --> C1[CO2 Laser Tube 50W]
    C1 --> C2[Galvanometer Mirror Scanner]
    C2 --> C3[Beam Focused on Sheet Surface]
    B2 --> C4[Build Platform Vertical Drive]
    A3 --> D1[Sheet Feed Mechanism]
    D1 --> D2[Supply Roll of Material]
    D2 --> D3[Sheet Feeder Roller]
    D3 --> D4[Heated Compaction Roller]
    D4 --> C3
    C3 --> E1[Object Contour Cut and Cross Hatch Cut]
    E1 --> E2[Waste Cubes Decubed Later]
    E1 --> E3[Laminated 3D Part on Platform]
    C4 --> E3
    E3 --> F1[Post Processing Sanding Sealing Painting]
    F1 --> F2[Finished Part]
```

## 4.2 LOM Machine — Component Block Diagram

This second diagram maps the **hardware architecture** of a typical LOM workstation, including the operator interface, motion control, and the safety enclosure.

```mermaid

    end

    end

```

## 4.3 Process Cycle State Diagram

The following diagram captures the **state transitions** during one build cycle. This is useful for writing "process sequence" answers in 7-mark questions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready
    Ready --> Feeding: Start Command
    Feeding --> Heating: Sheet Aligned
    Heating --> Cutting: Bonded to Previous Layer
    Cutting --> Lowering: Cut Complete
    Lowering --> Feeding: Platform Down by Delta z
    Cutting --> Done: All Layers Built
    Done --> Decubing: Manual Waste Removal
    Decubing --> PostProcessing: Trim from Base

    Ready --> [*]: Abort
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** When asked "Explain the LOM process with a diagram", draw the **Process Cycle State Diagram** (above) and label the layer thickness $\Delta z$ explicitly — examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**"What is Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)? Mention any two feedstocks used."**

**Model Answer (Expected ~40 words):**
LOM is a Sheet Lamination-based additive manufacturing process in which successive sheets of feed material are **thermally bonded** layer by layer, and a **laser cutter** traces the cross-sectional profile of the part while cutting the waste region into a removable cross-hatch grid. Common feedstocks include **paper with adhesive backing** and **PVC / polymer sheets**. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"State two advantages and two limitations of LOM."**

**Model Answer (Expected ~50 words):**
**Advantages:** (i) **Low running cost** — paper feedstock is cheap; (ii) **Minimal support structures** — the part is inherently supported by waste material. **Limitations:** (i) **Poor Z-axis surface finish** — staircase effect from 0.1 mm layers; (ii) **High material waste** — only 5–50% of the sheet becomes the part. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Past Pattern]

**"Explain the working principle of Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) with a neat block diagram. Discuss its process parameters, materials, advantages, limitations, and applications." (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle & Block Diagram (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Introduction (1 mark):**
LOM is an additive manufacturing process in which 2D sheets of material are bonded together and cut to shape using a laser, layer by layer, to form a 3D object. It belongs to the **Sheet Lamination** family of AM per **ISO/ASTM 52900**.

**(ii) Process Steps (4 marks — write all 6 steps):**
1. **Sheet feeding:** A new sheet is fed from a supply roll onto the build platform.
2. **Heated compaction:** A heated roller bonds the sheet to the previous layer using pressure (0.5–1.5 MPa) and temperature (60–80 °C for paper).
3. **Laser contour cutting:** A CO₂ laser (50 W) cuts the **object contour** and the **cross-hatch grid** in the waste region.
4. **Z-stage descent:** The platform lowers by one layer thickness $\Delta z$ (typically 0.1 mm).
5. **Repetition:** Steps 1–4 repeat for every layer.
6. **Decubing & post-processing:** After completion, waste cubes are broken off manually; the part is trimmed from the base and finished.

**(iii) Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Draw a labelled diagram showing: **Supply Roll → Feeder Roller → Heated Compaction Roller → Build Platform → CO₂ Laser → Galvo Scanner → Enclosure → Computer Controller**. (Refer to the diagrams in Section 4.)

#### Part (b) — Process Parameters, Materials, Advantages, Limitations, Applications (7 Marks)

**(i) Process Parameters (2 marks):**
- Layer thickness $\Delta z = 0.05$ – 0.5 mm
- Laser power = 25–50 W
- Heated roller temperature = 60–200 °C
- Pressure = 0.5–1.5 MPa
- Build tolerance = ±0.1 mm (XY), ±0.3 mm (Z)

**(ii) Materials (1 mark):**
Paper (with adhesive backing), PVC, polymer foils, metal tapes (steel, copper), ceramic tapes.

**(iii) Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Low cost (paper = cheapest AM feedstock)
- Large build volumes possible
- Minimal / no support structures needed
- No chemical post-curing required
- Suitable for sand-casting patterns

**(iv) Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Poor surface finish (staircase on Z faces)
- High waste material (5–50% utilization only)
- Slow build time (inversely proportional to $\Delta z$)
- Limited material options
- Wood-grain direction (in paper) causes anisotropy

**(v) Applications (1 mark):**
- Sand casting patterns & core boxes
- Investment casting patterns
- Architectural scale models
- Concept models in automotive design
- Master patterns for short-run injection molding

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"Derive an expression for the total build time of a LOM part. A LOM machine is set to build a part of size 250 × 200 × 150 mm with a layer thickness of 0.2 mm. Given the per-cycle times: $t_{feed} = 3$ s, $t_{heat} = 4$ s, $t_{cut} = 15$ s, $t_{lower} = 2$ s, and $t_{setup} = 1800$ s. Calculate the total build time. Comment on the effect of halving the layer thickness on build time." (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Build Time Formula (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

The derivation is exactly the one shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student should produce:

1. **Number of layers derivation (2 marks):**
$n = \dfrac{Z_{\max}}{\Delta z}$

2. **Cycle time identification (2 marks):**
$t_{cycle} = t_{feed} + t_{heat} + t_{cut} + t_{lower}$

3. **Multiplication and setup term (2 marks):**
$T_{build} = t_{setup} + n \times t_{cycle}$
$T_{build} = t_{setup} + \dfrac{Z_{\max}}{\Delta z} \times (t_{feed} + t_{heat} + t_{cut} + t_{lower})$

4. **Final boxed result (1 mark):**
The student must box the final equation for full marks.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation & Comment (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**(i) Number of layers (1.5 marks):**
$$
n = \frac{Z_{\max}}{\Delta z} = \frac{150\,\text{mm}}{0.2\,\text{mm}} = 750\,\text{layers}
$$

**(ii) Cycle time (1.5 marks):**
$$
t_{cycle} = 3 + 4 + 15 + 2 = 24\,\text{s/layer}
$$

**(iii) Core build time (1.5 marks):**
$$
T_{build,core} = 750 \times 24 = 18\,000\,\text{s}
$$

**(iv) Total build time with setup (1.5 marks):**
$$
T_{build} = 1800 + 18\,000 = 19\,800\,\text{s} = \frac{19\,800}{3600} = 5.5\,\text{hours}
$$

**(v) Effect of halving $\Delta z$ to 0.1 mm (1 mark):**
If $\Delta z$ is halved, the number of layers $n$ is **doubled** (since $n \propto 1/\Delta z$). Consequently, the total build time is **also doubled** (≈ 11 hours). The Z-axis surface finish improves (less staircase), but at the cost of **double the production time** and **double the material consumption**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting setup time:** Many students compute only $n \times t_{cycle}$ and forget to add $t_{setup}$. This costs **1.5 marks** every time. Always re-read the question to identify the overhead.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing mm and cm in the layer count. Express everything in **mm** consistently.
> 3. **Wrong direction of proportionality:** Stating that build time is *proportional* (not *inversely proportional*) to $\Delta z$. The correct relation is $T_{build} \propto 1/\Delta z$.
> 4. **Ignoring the trade-off:** When asked to "comment on halving the layer thickness", students often only mention the surface finish benefit. They must **also state that build time doubles** — this is the second half of the mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for LOM:**

- ✅ LOM stands for **Laminated Object Manufacturing** — falls under **Sheet Lamination** in ISO/ASTM 52900.
- ✅ Original system: **Helisys Inc., 1991**; modern derivatives: **Mcor, Solvay**.
- ✅ Standard feedstock: **Paper with thermoplastic adhesive backing** (cheapest AM material).
- ✅ Cutting tool: **CO₂ laser (10.6 µm wavelength, 25–50 W)**.
- ✅ Bonding: **Heated compaction roller** (pressure + temperature, no glue spray).
- ✅ Layer thickness $\Delta z$ = **0.1 mm (paper)**, **0.2 mm (polymer)**.
- ✅ Build time formula: $T_{build} = t_{setup} + \dfrac{Z_{\max}}{\Delta z} \times (t_{feed} + t_{heat} + t_{cut} + t_{lower})$.
- ✅ Material utilization: typically **5–50%** (high waste).
- ✅ Waste removal technique: **Cross-hatch cutting + manual decubing**.
- ✅ Unique feature: **No chemical post-curing, no support structures**.
- ✅ Primary application: **Sand-casting & investment-casting patterns**.
- ✅ Key limitation: **Poor Z surface finish + anisotropy from sheet stacking**.
- ✅ Build tolerance: **±0.1 mm XY, ±0.3 mm Z**.
- ✅ Trade-off: Halving $\Delta z$ → doubles build time, halves surface roughness.
- ✅ Examiner loves: "List advantages + limitations + applications" — always write **at least three points** in each category.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser-Engineered Net-Shaping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET502 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laser-Engineered Net-Shaping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Laser-Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)** is a **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** based additive manufacturing process that utilizes a high-power laser beam to melt and fuse metallic powder (or wire) feedstock, layer-by-layer, to fabricate fully dense, three-dimensional near-net-shape metal parts directly from a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) model.

As per the **ASTM F2792-12** standard, LENS falls under the broader category of **"Powder Bed Fusion"** with directed energy, but is more commonly classified under **"Directed Energy Deposition"** when the powder is injected coaxially through a multi-jet deposition head rather than spread from a bed. It belongs to the family of **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** processes defined by ISO/ASTM 52900.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LENS is a registered trademark technology originally developed by **Sandia National Laboratories** (USA) and commercialized by **Optomec Inc.** Students must remember that LENS is fundamentally distinct from Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) or Selective Laser Melting (SLM) because the powder is **injected through nozzles**, not spread on a bed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of LENS as a **"3D Welding Pen"**:
- Imagine you have a high-precision glue gun, but instead of glue, you feed it with fine metal powder, and instead of heating glue, you have an invisible laser beam that melts the metal powder the instant it touches the surface.
- The laser essentially acts like a "metallic fountain pen" that "draws" molten metal tracks on a substrate.
- The "ink" is the metal powder, the "heat source" is the laser, and the "paper" is the substrate or the previously deposited layer.
- The pen moves in the X-Y plane to draw a cross-section, then moves up in the Z-axis by one layer thickness, and repeats until the full 3D object is created.

## 1.3 Key Operational Metrics

The process is governed by the following standard metrics:

- **Laser Power (P):** Typically **100 W to 4 kW** (often Nd:YAG or fiber lasers).
- **Spot Diameter:** Usually between **0.5 mm and 5 mm**.
- **Deposition Rate:** Up to **0.5 kg/h** for powder, higher for wire-based variants.
- **Layer Thickness:** **0.1 mm to 0.5 mm**.
- **Build Envelope:** Typically **300 mm × 300 mm × 400 mm**.
- **Atmosphere:** Inert gas (Argon or Nitrogen) with **O₂ < 50 ppm** to prevent oxidation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Near-Net-Shape:** A part is called "near-net-shape" if it requires only minimal post-processing (like machining or grinding) to achieve final dimensions, thereby saving material and machining time compared to traditional subtractive manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Working Principle (Structured Logic Steps)

The LENS process follows a strict, deterministic sequence of operations:

1. **CAD Model Creation:** A 3D solid model of the desired part is created in CAD software and sliced into thin horizontal cross-sections (typically 0.1–0.5 mm thick) using slicing software.

2. **Path Planning:** The slicer generates a **toolpath** (G-code or similar) for each layer, defining the trajectory the deposition head must follow.

3. **Substrate Preparation:** A metallic substrate (often the same material as the powder, e.g., Ti-6Al-4V on a Ti plate) is placed on a build platform inside a sealed chamber. The chamber is purged with inert gas.

4. **Coaxial Powder Injection:** Metal powder is fed from a powder feeder (using argon as carrier gas) through **4 to 6 nozzles** arranged coaxially around the laser beam. This creates a focused "cone" of powder converging at the laser focal point.

5. **Laser Melting:** The high-power laser strikes the substrate/powder interface, creating a small **molten pool (melt pool)**. The powder particles are entrained into this pool and fully melt.

6. **Solidification:** As the laser head moves away, the molten metal rapidly solidifies, metallurgically bonding to the substrate or previous layer.

7. **Layer Stacking:** The Z-axis is incremented by one layer thickness, and the next cross-section is deposited on top.

8. **Repeat:** Steps 4–7 repeat until the entire 3D part is fabricated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why coaxial injection matters:** Because powder and laser emerge from the same axis, LENS can build parts in **any direction (5-axis deposition)**, not just vertical Z. This allows for **overhangs, cladding on curved surfaces, and repair of damaged components** — a major advantage over powder-bed systems.

## 2.2 Process Parameters (The 4 Pillars of LENS)

| Parameter Category | Specific Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Part Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laser** | Power (P) | 100 W – 4 kW | Higher P → deeper melt pool, wider track |
| **Laser** | Scan Speed (v) | 5 – 25 mm/s | Higher v → shallower melt, possible lack of fusion |
| **Powder** | Powder Feed Rate (ṁ) | 1 – 30 g/min | Higher ṁ → taller track, possible unmelted powder |
| **Geometric** | Hatch Spacing (h) | 0.3 – 1.0 mm | Smaller h → denser scan, more overlap |
| **Geometric** | Layer Thickness (t) | 0.1 – 0.5 mm | Defines Z resolution |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are **high-yield for KTU examinations** and must be memorized:

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** — the most critical parameter in LENS | J/mm³ |
| $R_{dep} = \rho \cdot v \cdot A_{track}$ | Theoretical Deposition Rate (ρ = powder packing density) | mm³/s |
| $A_{track} \approx w \cdot h$ | Cross-sectional area of single track (w = width) | mm² |
| $D_{melt} \approx \dfrac{2 P}{\pi \cdot v \cdot k \cdot (T_m - T_0)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha v}}$ | Approximate melt pool depth (semi-empirical) | mm |
| $R_{heat} = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_m - T_0) \cdot \sqrt{\pi \alpha v}}$ | Rosenthal's 2D heat-affected parameter (for T > T₀) | — |
| $\eta = \dfrac{m_{deposited}}{m_{fed}} \times 100\%$ | Powder catchment efficiency | % |
| $T_{cool} = \dfrac{E_d}{\rho \cdot c_p}$ | Approximate cooling rate per unit energy | K·mm³/J |

**Boundary Conditions (Process Windows):**
- $E_d < 20 \text{ J/mm}^3$ → **Lack of fusion** defects (porosity, voids)
- $E_d > 150 \text{ J/mm}^3$ → **Keyholing** or **vaporization** defects
- **Optimal window for Ti-6Al-4V:** $50 \leq E_d \leq 80 \text{ J/mm}^3$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Energy Density is King:** Just like in welding (Heat Input = P/v), the Energy Density governs whether the powder fully melts, whether porosity forms, and the resultant microstructure. It is the **single most-asked formula** in LENS exam questions at KTU.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LENS is not a laboratory curiosity — it has transformative industrial applications:

- **Aerospace:** Fabricating **Ti-6Al-4V** blisks (bladed disks) and structural brackets with high buy-to-fly ratio improvement (often from 20:1 to 1.5:1).
- **Biomedical:** Custom **patient-specific titanium implants** (cranial plates, spinal cages) using CT-scan-derived CAD models.
- **Defense:** Repair of high-value components like **turbine blades, landing gear, and gun barrels** by depositing material only where worn.
- **Tooling:** Adding wear-resistant **Stellite or Inconel clads** onto cutting tool surfaces.
- **Nuclear:** Fabrication of **functionally graded materials (FGM)** — e.g., a part that transitions from stainless steel to Inconel across its cross-section.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

The Volumetric Energy Density is derived from first principles of heat input in a moving laser source. The full derivation is shown below.

### Given:
- Laser Power: $P$ (in Watts = J/s)
- Scan Speed: $v$ (in mm/s)
- Hatch Spacing: $h$ (in mm)
- Layer Thickness: $t$ (in mm)

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Energy delivered per unit length of scan.**
The laser deposits energy as it moves. For a unit length $L$ traveled:
$$
E_{length} = P \cdot \frac{L}{v}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Power is energy per unit time. Time to travel length $L$ at speed $v$ is $L/v$. Multiplying gives energy per unit length (J/mm).

**Step 2: Energy per unit area of a single track.**
A single scan track has width approximately equal to the hatch spacing $h$. So the energy per unit area is:
$$
E_{area} = \frac{E_{length}}{h} = \frac{P \cdot L}{v \cdot h \cdot L} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Dividing the linear energy by the width distributes the energy over a 2D footprint.

**Step 3: Energy per unit volume (the VED).**
Multiplying layers, the 3D volume unit is $h \times L \times t$. Energy per unit volume is:
$$
E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The third dimension of the deposition (layer thickness $t$) is the volume denominator. Final units: $\frac{J/s}{(mm/s) \cdot mm \cdot mm} = \frac{J}{mm^3}$.

### Final Result:
$$
\boxed{E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A LENS system deposits **Inconel 718** powder with the following parameters:
- Laser Power, $P = 800$ W
- Scan Speed, $v = 10$ mm/s
- Hatch Spacing, $h = 0.5$ mm
- Layer Thickness, $t = 0.25$ mm

**Find:** (a) The Volumetric Energy Density. (b) Comment on whether the part will be defect-free for Inconel 718 (acceptable range: 60–100 J/mm³).

### Solution:

**Part (a): Calculation of $E_d$**

Substituting into the formula:
$$
E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

Insert the values:
$$
E_d = \frac{800}{10 \times 0.5 \times 0.25}
$$

Compute the denominator step by step:
$$
10 \times 0.5 = 5
$$

$$
5 \times 0.25 = 1.25
$$

Therefore:
$$
E_d = \frac{800}{1.25} = 640 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

**Part (b): Defect Analysis**

The calculated $E_d = 640$ J/mm³ is **far above the acceptable upper limit of 100 J/mm³** for Inconel 718.

Conclusion: At this energy density, the part will suffer from:
- **Keyholing** (vaporization of metal creating gas pockets)
- **Excessive dilution** with the substrate
- **Coarse grain microstructure** with reduced fatigue life
- **Possible warping** due to high residual stress

**Recommendation:** Increase scan speed $v$ to reduce $E_d$ into the acceptable range. Setting $v = 25$ mm/s gives:
$$
E_d = \frac{800}{25 \times 0.5 \times 0.25} = \frac{800}{3.125} = 256 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

Even higher speeds (around 50 mm/s) are needed to enter the 60–100 J/mm³ window.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Defect vs. Process Parameter Cause-Effect

| Defect Type | Root Cause | Process Parameter to Adjust | Direction of Correction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lack of Fusion (LoF)** | Insufficient energy to melt powder fully | $P \downarrow$, $v \uparrow$ | Increase $E_d$ |
| **Keyholing / Voids** | Excessive energy vaporizes metal | $P \uparrow$, $v \downarrow$ | Decrease $E_d$ |
| **Ballistic Particles** | Powder blown away by gas, not entering melt pool | Powder feed $\uparrow$ | Increase ṁ |
| **Porosity (Gas)** | Dissolved gas in powder escapes during solidification | Inert gas purity $\uparrow$ | Reduce O₂/H₂O |
| **Warping / Cracking** | High thermal gradient → residual stress | Pre-heat substrate $\uparrow$ | Reduce $\Delta T$ |
| **Delamination** | Poor inter-layer bonding | $t \downarrow$ | Use thinner layers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The "Golden Triangle" of LENS quality is **Power, Speed, and Feed Rate**. Always specify all three when discussing process optimization — never adjust one in isolation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LENS System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of a LENS system, showing the flow of materials, energy, and data.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[CAD Model Input] --> A2[Slicer Software]
    A2 --> A3[Toolpath Generation G-code]
    A3 --> A4[CNC Motion Controller]

    B1[Powder Feeder Hoppers x4] --> B2[Carrier Gas Argon]
    B2 --> B3[Convergent Nozzle Array]
    B4[High Power Laser Nd:YAG] --> B5[Optical Fiber Delivery]
    B5 --> B6[Coaxial Deposition Head]

    A4 --> C1[Multi Axis Motion Stage]
    B3 --> B6
    B6 --> C1

    C1 --> C2[Inert Chamber Argon Atmosphere]
    C2 --> C3[Substrate / Build Platform]
    C6[Layer by Layer Deposition] --> C3
    C3 --> C4[Molten Pool Formation]
    C4 --> C5[Rapid Solidification]
    C5 --> C6

    C6 --> D1[Real Time Monitoring Pyrometer]
    D1 --> D2[Closed Loop Feedback]
    D2 --> A4
    D2 --> B1
    D2 --> B4

    E1[Exhaust Gas Filtration] --> C2
    E2[Recycled Unmelted Powder] --> B1

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style A4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style B4 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000

```

## 4.2 Layer-by-Layer Deposition Sequence

This sequence diagram shows the temporal flow of the LENS process for a generic 3D part.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User/CAD
    participant S as Slicer
    participant M as Motion Controller
    participant L as Laser Source
    participant P as Powder Feeder
    participant H as Deposition Head
    participant W as Build Platform

    U->>S: Upload 3D CAD model
    S->>S: Slice into layers (0.25 mm)
    S->>M: Send G-code toolpath
    M->>L: Trigger laser ON (Power = P)
    M->>P: Activate powder flow
    P->>H: Deliver powder via carrier gas
    L->>H: Deliver laser beam via fiber
    H->>W: Deposit track 1 of layer N
    H->>W: Deposit track 2 of layer N
    H->>W: Continue until layer N complete
    W->>W: Cool and solidify
    M->>W: Increment Z by layer thickness
    Note over M,W: Repeat for layer N+1
    M->>L: Trigger laser OFF
    P->>H: Powder flow OFF
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Laser-Engineered Net Shaping (LENS) and mention two of its distinguishing features. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
LENS is a **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** based additive manufacturing process in which a high-power laser beam is used to melt metal powder injected coaxially through nozzles, building parts layer-by-layer directly from a CAD model.

Two distinguishing features:
1. **Coaxial powder injection** — allows 5-axis deposition on curved surfaces and overhangs.
2. **High deposition rate** — suitable for building large structural parts and repair applications, unlike powder-bed systems that are limited by bed size.

### Question 2: What is "Volumetric Energy Density" in LENS? Write its formula and state the unit. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**
Volumetric Energy Density ($E_d$) is the amount of laser energy delivered per unit volume of deposited material. It is the primary process parameter governing melt pool formation, porosity, and microstructure.

$$
E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

**Unit:** J/mm³ (Joules per cubic millimeter).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of Laser-Engineered Net Shaping (LENS) with a neat block diagram. List any **four process parameters** that affect the quality of the deposited part. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** With suitable equations, explain the concept of **Volumetric Energy Density**. A LENS machine operates at $P = 1200$ W, $v = 15$ mm/s, $h = 0.4$ mm, and $t = 0.3$ mm. Calculate the energy density and comment on the suitability for **Ti-6Al-4V** (acceptable range: 50–80 J/mm³). **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (Block Diagram in words):**
The LENS process begins with a **3D CAD model** of the desired part, which is sliced into thin horizontal layers (0.1–0.5 mm) by slicing software. The slicer generates a **toolpath** sent to the CNC controller of the LENS machine.

Inside a sealed **inert-gas chamber** (purged with Argon to keep O₂ < 50 ppm), a **high-power laser** (typically Nd:YAG or fiber laser, 100 W–4 kW) is delivered via optical fiber to a **coaxial deposition head**. Simultaneously, **metal powder** from a multi-hopper powder feeder is transported by carrier gas (Argon) through **3–6 nozzles** arranged concentrically around the laser axis.

The laser strikes the **substrate**, creating a small **molten pool** of about 1–3 mm diameter. Powder particles are entrained into this pool, melt, and fuse to the substrate. As the deposition head moves along the X-Y path, the molten track **solidifies** to form a dense metal line.

After one layer is complete, the **Z-axis is incremented** by one layer thickness, and the next layer is deposited on top. This continues until the full 3D part is built. The coaxial geometry permits **5-axis motion**, enabling build on curved substrates and overhanging features.

**Four Process Parameters Affecting Quality:**
1. **Laser Power (P)** — controls melt pool depth and track width.
2. **Scan Speed (v)** — controls heat input per unit length.
3. **Powder Feed Rate (ṁ)** — controls track height and deposition rate.
4. **Hatch Spacing (h)** — controls inter-track overlap and density.
5. *(Optional)* Layer Thickness (t) — controls Z-resolution and remelt depth.

#### Part (b) — Energy Density Calculation (7 Marks)

**Concept:**
Volumetric Energy Density ($E_d$) represents the laser energy supplied per unit volume of deposited material:

$$
E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

It combines the four primary process parameters into a single metric that predicts porosity, microstructure, and mechanical strength.

**Calculation:**

Given:
- $P = 1200$ W
- $v = 15$ mm/s
- $h = 0.4$ mm
- $t = 0.3$ mm

Step 1 — Compute the denominator:
$$
v \cdot h \cdot t = 15 \times 0.4 \times 0.3
$$

$$
15 \times 0.4 = 6
$$

$$
6 \times 0.3 = 1.8
$$

Step 2 — Compute $E_d$:
$$
E_d = \frac{1200}{1.8} = 666.67 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

**Comment on Suitability:**
The calculated $E_d = 666.67$ J/mm³ is **approximately 8× higher** than the upper acceptable limit (80 J/mm³) for Ti-6Al-4V. This energy density will cause:
- **Keyholing** and porosity due to metal vaporization
- **Coarse prior-β grain microstructure**
- **Excessive substrate dilution**
- **High residual stress** and distortion

**Recommendation:** Increase the scan speed to roughly $v \approx 100$ mm/s to bring $E_d$ into the optimal window:
$$
E_d = \frac{1200}{100 \times 0.4 \times 0.3} = \frac{1200}{12} = 100 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

This is still slightly high; $v \approx 125$ mm/s would yield $E_d = 80$ J/mm³ (the upper bound).

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q. (a)** What are the **main components of a LENS system**? Briefly explain the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of LENS compared to conventional (subtractive) manufacturing. Mention any **four industrial applications** of LENS. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Main Components of LENS (7 Marks)

1. **Laser Source** (Nd:YAG or Fiber Laser, 100 W–4 kW): Provides the high-energy coherent beam required to melt metallic powder. The wavelength (typically ~1064 nm) is well absorbed by most metals.

2. **Beam Delivery System** (Optical fiber + focusing lens): Transmits the laser from the source to the deposition head with minimal power loss, and focuses it to a spot of 0.5–5 mm diameter.

3. **Powder Feeder System** (Multi-hopper + carrier gas line): Stores and delivers metal powder at a precisely controlled mass flow rate (1–30 g/min) using Argon as carrier gas. Multiple hoppers enable **multi-material / functionally graded** builds.

4. **Coaxial Deposition Head** (Nozzle array + beam optics): The heart of the LENS system. Powder nozzles converge coaxially with the laser axis, allowing powder to enter the melt pool from all radial directions.

5. **Motion Control System** (CNC / 5-axis stage): Precisely positions the deposition head (or moves the substrate) along X, Y, Z, and rotary axes with micrometer accuracy.

6. **Inert-Gas Chamber**: An enclosed build environment purged with Argon (O₂ < 50 ppm) to prevent oxidation of reactive metals like Titanium and Aluminum.

7. **Process Monitoring & Control** (Pyrometer / CCD camera): Real-time closed-loop feedback to maintain stable melt pool temperature and detect defects like lack of fusion.

#### Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and Applications (7 Marks)

**Advantages over Conventional Manufacturing:**

1. **Near-Net-Shape Fabrication:** Material waste < 5% vs. up to 90% in machining (high buy-to-fly ratio improvement).
2. **Geometric Complexity:** Internal channels, lattice structures, and conformal features are possible.
3. **Multi-Material Capability:** Functionally graded materials (FGM) and bimetallic parts are feasible.
4. **Lead Time Reduction:** No tooling (molds, dies) required — ideal for prototypes and low-volume production.
5. **Repair Capability:** Damaged high-value parts (turbine blades) can be refurbished by local deposition.

**Limitations:**

1. **High Capital Cost:** LENS machines cost $0.5M–$2M, restricting adoption to high-value sectors.
2. **Residual Stress & Distortion:** Steep thermal gradients cause warping; post-process heat treatment (e.g., HIP) is often required.
3. **Limited Build Size:** Smaller build envelope than casting or forging.
4. **Surface Finish:** As-deposited roughness (Ra 10–20 μm) requires post-machining.
5. **Limited Material Library:** Powder must be flowable and laser-absorptive; some alloys are difficult to process.

**Four Industrial Applications:**

1. **Aerospace** — Ti-6Al-4V structural brackets, blisks, and engine components.
2. **Biomedical** — Patient-specific cranial and orthopedic implants from CT data.
3. **Defense** — Repair of gun barrels, landing gear, and rotorcraft components.
4. **Energy** — Inconel 718 turbine blade tips, nuclear reactor graded components.
5. *(Bonus)* Automotive — Prototype and low-volume specialty parts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT confuse LENS with SLM/SLS.** SLM uses a pre-spread powder bed; LENS injects powder through nozzles. This is a frequent 2–3 mark deduction.
> - **Always specify units** in energy density calculations. Writing "$E_d = 1200/1.8 = 666.67$" without "J/mm³" loses 1 mark.
> - **Mention coaxial geometry** when describing LENS — this is the single most distinguishing feature examiners look for.
> - **Never give a one-sided answer** in advantages/limitations — always compare to a reference process (subtractive or other AM).
> - **Do not forget the inert atmosphere** — Ti and Al are highly reactive; O₂ content must be mentioned.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist:

- **LENS = Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** — not powder-bed fusion.
- **Feedstock:** Coaxial powder injection (3–6 nozzles) or sometimes wire.
- **Heat source:** Nd:YAG or fiber laser, 100 W–4 kW.
- **Atmosphere:** Inert (Argon) chamber with O₂ < 50 ppm.
- **Key formula (must memorize):**
  $$
  E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{(J/mm}^3\text{)}
  $$
- **Optimal $E_d$ window for Ti-6Al-4V:** 50–80 J/mm³.
- **Below 20 J/mm³:** Lack of fusion defects.
- **Above 150 J/mm³:** Keyholing and vaporization.
- **5-axis capability** is LENS's biggest advantage over SLM/EBM.
- **ASTM F2792** is the standard classification.
- **Originally developed by Sandia National Labs; commercialized by Optomec Inc.**
- **Applications:** Aerospace (Ti blisks), biomedical (custom implants), defense (repair), tooling (cladding), nuclear (FGM).
- **Major defects:** Lack of fusion, keyholing, porosity, warping, cracking, delamination.
- **Post-processing required:** HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing) and finish machining.
- **Build envelope typical:** 300 × 300 × 400 mm.
- **Layer thickness:** 0.1–0.5 mm.
- **Melt pool size:** 1–3 mm diameter, monitored by pyrometer.
- **Powder catchment efficiency:** 60–90% (unmelted powder is recycled).
